# Schools of Thought — Human Academy

> All major academic disciplines and schools of human knowledge.

---

## HUMANITIES

### Philosophy

#### Foundations & Philosophical Method
- What is Philosophy? (definition, scope, distinction from science, religion, and common sense)
- How to Read Philosophy (primary sources, argument reconstruction, the principle of charity)
- Philosophical Methodology
  - Conceptual analysis
  - Thought experiments (Trolley Problem, Brain in a Vat, Gettier cases, Chinese Room, etc.)
  - Reflective equilibrium
  - Intuition pumps
  - Dialectical method (Socratic dialogue)
  - Phenomenological method
  - Transcendental method
  - Ordinary language analysis
  - Naturalistic method
- Branches of Philosophy overview (metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, logic, aesthetics, specialized branches)

#### Logic: The Instrument of Philosophy
- Informal Logic & Critical Thinking
  - Identifying arguments in natural language
  - Deductive vs. inductive vs. abductive reasoning
  - Common informal fallacies
    - Ad hominem, straw man, appeal to authority
    - Slippery slope, false dichotomy, begging the question
    - Equivocation, post hoc, hasty generalization, red herring, tu quoque
- Classical Propositional Logic
  - Logical connectives (negation, conjunction, disjunction, conditional, biconditional)
  - Truth tables
  - Tautologies, contradictions, contingencies
  - Logical equivalence
  - Basic proof techniques (modus ponens, modus tollens, hypothetical syllogism, disjunctive syllogism)
- Predicate (First-Order) Logic
  - Predicates and individual constants
  - Universal and existential quantifiers
  - Bound and free variables
  - Translating natural language to FOL
  - Proofs in predicate logic
- Modal Logic
  - Alethic modality: necessity (□) and possibility (◇)
  - Possible worlds semantics (Kripke frames)
  - S4 and S5 systems
  - Deontic logic (obligation, permission, prohibition)
  - Epistemic logic (knowledge, belief, justified belief)
  - Temporal logic (tense operators)
  - Counterfactual conditionals (Lewis, Stalnaker)
- Advanced Formal Logic
  - Second-order logic and expressibility limits
  - Many-valued logics (Łukasiewicz three-valued, fuzzy logic)
  - Intuitionistic logic (Brouwer, Heyting)
  - Paraconsistent logic (dialethism, Graham Priest)
  - Relevance logic
  - Free logic (for empty names, non-existence)
  - Substructural logics (linear logic, Lambek calculus)
- Philosophy of Logic
  - Logicism (Frege's Grundgesetze, Russell-Whitehead Principia Mathematica)
  - Formalism & Hilbert's program
  - Intuitionism (Brouwer: anti-realism in mathematics)
  - Gödel's incompleteness theorems and their philosophical implications
  - Conventionalism (Carnap)
  - Logical pluralism (Beall & Restall)
  - Logic and natural language (syntax vs. semantics)

#### History of Philosophy

- **Ancient Philosophy**
  - Pre-Socratic Thinkers
    - Thales of Miletus (water as arche, naturalistic explanation)
    - Anaximander (apeiron, the indefinite)
    - Anaximenes (air as arche)
    - Heraclitus (logos, flux, unity of opposites)
    - Parmenides (the One, Being, Way of Truth vs. Way of Opinion)
    - Zeno of Elea (paradoxes: Achilles & the Tortoise, Arrow, Stadium)
    - Empedocles (four elements, Love and Strife)
    - Anaxagoras (nous as cosmic organizer, homeomereity)
    - Democritus & Leucippus (atomism, void, determinism)
    - Pythagoras & Pythagoreans (number as reality, transmigration of souls, mathematical mysticism)
  - The Sophists
    - Protagoras (homo mensura: man is the measure of all things, relativism)
    - Gorgias (nihilism: nothing exists; if it did we couldn't know it; if we could we couldn't communicate it)
    - Thrasymachus (justice as the advantage of the stronger)
    - Hippias, Prodicus, Antiphon (nature vs. convention, nomos/physis debate)
  - Socrates
    - The Socratic method (elenchus)
    - Socratic irony and the disavowal of knowledge
    - Virtue as knowledge; moral intellectualism
    - The problem of Socrates (sources: Plato, Xenophon, Aristophanes)
  - Plato
    - Theory of Forms (Phaedo, Republic, Parmenides, Timaeus)
    - The Allegory of the Cave (epistemology and political enlightenment)
    - Epistemology: knowledge vs. opinion, recollection (anamnesis)
    - Soul: tripartite soul (reason, spirit, appetite) — Republic
    - Ethics: virtue, the good, eudaimonia
    - Political philosophy: the ideal city-state, philosopher-king, the five regimes
    - Cosmology: the Demiurge, the World Soul, the Timaeus
    - Later dialogues: Third Man Argument critique in Parmenides
    - Middle Platonism and interpretations
  - Aristotle
    - Logic: the Organon (Categories, De Interpretatione, Prior Analytics, Posterior Analytics, Topics, Sophistical Refutations)
    - Syllogistic and its limits
    - Metaphysics: substance (ousia), hylomorphism, the four causes (material, formal, efficient, final), act and potency, the Unmoved Mover
    - Natural philosophy: Physics, De Caelo, De Anima
    - Ethics: Nicomachean Ethics — virtue (arete), eudaimonia, doctrine of the mean, practical wisdom (phronesis), friendship (philia)
    - Political philosophy: the polis, constitutions, the Politics
    - Aesthetics: Poetics — mimesis, catharsis, tragedy
    - Biology and natural science: Historia Animalium, De Partibus Animalium
  - Hellenistic Schools
    - Stoicism
      - Zeno of Citium (founder), Cleanthes, Chrysippus (systematizer)
      - Physics: logos pervading the cosmos, determinism, pneuma, cosmic conflagration (ekpyrosis)
      - Logic: Stoic propositional logic, lekton (sayable), impressions (phantasiai)
      - Ethics: virtue as the only good, preferred indifferents, the sage (sophos), cosmopolitanism
      - Later Stoics: Epictetus (Enchiridion, Discourses), Marcus Aurelius (Meditations), Seneca (Letters)
      - Stoic influence on Christianity and later ethics
    - Epicureanism
      - Epicurus: atomism, pleasure (hedone) as highest good, ataraxia and aponia
      - Canon (epistemology): sensation, prolepsis (preconceptions), feelings
      - Theology: gods indifferent; no divine providence; no fear of death
      - Lucretius: De Rerum Natura (atomism, cosmology, death and fear)
      - Epicurean communities, friendship, withdrawal from public life
    - Academic Skepticism
      - New Academy: Arcesilaus, Carneades (suspension of judgment, probabilism)
      - Pyrrhonism: Pyrrho, Timon, Sextus Empiricus (Outlines of Pyrrhonism — ten modes of epoché)
      - The criterion of truth problem
    - Cynicism
      - Antisthenes (virtue as sufficient for happiness)
      - Diogenes of Sinope (cosmopolitanism, rejection of social conventions)
      - Crates, Hipparchia
    - Neoplatonism
      - Plotinus (Enneads): the One, Intellect (nous), Soul, emanation and return
      - Porphyry (Isagoge, introduction to Aristotle's Categories)
      - Iamblichus (theurgy, divine hierarchy, commentary tradition)
      - Proclus (Elements of Theology, Platonic commentary)
      - Influence on Augustine, Islamic philosophy, and Renaissance thought

- **Medieval Philosophy**
  - Patristics & Early Christian Philosophy
    - Justin Martyr, Origen, Tertullian (faith vs. reason early debates)
    - Augustine of Hippo
      - Confessions: the restless heart, time and memory
      - City of God: two cities, philosophy of history
      - On Free Will: problem of evil, predestination vs. free will
      - Illumination theory of knowledge
      - Original sin, grace, and the will
    - Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (negative theology, divine names, mystical theology)
    - Boethius (Consolation of Philosophy, transmission of Aristotelian logic)
  - Islamic (Falsafa) and Jewish Medieval Philosophy
    - Al-Kindi (first Arab philosopher, Aristotelian cosmology, intellect)
    - Al-Farabi (political philosophy, emanationism, the ideal city)
    - Avicenna / Ibn Sina
      - Flying Man argument (proto-Cartesian self-awareness)
      - Distinction of essence and existence
      - Emanationist cosmology, Necessary Existence (God)
      - Psychology: the internal senses, soul and intellect
    - Al-Ghazali (Incoherence of the Philosophers, occasionalism, critique of Avicenna)
    - Averroes / Ibn Rushd
      - Incoherence of the Incoherence (reply to Al-Ghazali)
      - Commentaries on Aristotle ("The Commentator")
      - Unity of intellect controversy (monopsychism)
    - Maimonides (Guide for the Perplexed: negative theology, prophecy, aporetic reading)
    - Judah Halevi (The Kuzari: revelation over reason)
  - Scholasticism
    - Anselm of Canterbury (ontological argument, fides quaerens intellectum)
    - Peter Abelard (conceptualism, Sic et Non, problem of universals)
    - Problem of Universals: Realism vs. Nominalism (Ockham) vs. Conceptualism (Abelard)
    - Albertus Magnus (commentaries on Aristotle, natural philosophy)
    - Thomas Aquinas
      - Natural theology: Five Ways for God's existence
      - Essence and existence (esse/essentia)
      - Analogy of being
      - Summa Theologica and Summa Contra Gentiles
      - Natural law theory, virtue ethics
      - Faith and reason: philosophy as handmaid to theology
    - John Duns Scotus (univocity of being, haecceity/thisness, formal distinction, primacy of will)
    - William of Ockham
      - Ockham's Razor (parsimony)
      - Nominalism: only particulars exist
      - Divine command theory
      - Political philosophy: separation of church and state
    - Late Scholastic controversies (Thomism, Scotism, Ockhamism)
  - Renaissance Transition
    - Meister Eckhart (German mysticism, ground of the soul, negative theology)
    - Nicholas of Cusa (coincidence of opposites, docta ignorantia/learned ignorance)
    - Renaissance Humanism (Petrarch, Ficino, Pico della Mirandola — Oration on the Dignity of Man)

- **Early Modern Philosophy (17th–18th Century)**
  - Rationalism
    - René Descartes
      - Method of doubt, the cogito (Meditations on First Philosophy)
      - Mind-body dualism (res cogitans / res extensa)
      - Clear and distinct ideas, ontological argument
      - The Cartesian circle controversy
      - Geometry and the world: Principles of Philosophy
    - Baruch Spinoza
      - Ethics (geometric method): God or Nature (Deus sive Natura)
      - Substance monism, infinite attributes (Thought and Extension)
      - Parallelism (no causal interaction between mind and body)
      - Conatus, the affects, bondage and freedom
      - Theological-Political Treatise: Biblical criticism, democracy, freedom of thought
    - Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
      - Monadology: windowless monads, pre-established harmony
      - Principle of Sufficient Reason, Principle of the Identity of Indiscernibles
      - Best of all possible worlds (optimism)
      - Theodicy (problem of evil)
      - Logic: characteristica universalis, calculus ratiocinator
    - Nicolas Malebranche (occasionalism, vision in God)
  - Empiricism
    - Francis Bacon (Novum Organum: inductive method, four idols, reform of natural philosophy)
    - John Locke
      - Essay Concerning Human Understanding: tabula rasa, simple/complex ideas, primary/secondary qualities
      - No innate ideas; theory of substance; personal identity (memory theory)
      - Two Treatises of Government: natural rights, social contract, right to revolution
      - Letter Concerning Toleration
    - George Berkeley
      - Esse est percipi (to be is to be perceived)
      - Immaterialism: no material substance
      - Anti-abstraction argument; Master Argument for idealism
      - God as guarantor of continuity of objects
    - David Hume
      - Impressions and ideas; copy principle
      - Problem of causation (constant conjunction, no necessary connection)
      - Problem of induction (no rational justification)
      - Bundle theory of the self
      - Is-Ought problem (Hume's Guillotine)
      - Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion: critiques of cosmological and design arguments
      - Sympathy and moral sentimentalism
  - Social Contract Tradition
    - Thomas Hobbes (Leviathan: state of nature as war, absolute sovereignty, social contract)
    - John Locke (Second Treatise: state of nature with natural law, limited government, consent)
    - Jean-Jacques Rousseau (Social Contract: general will, popular sovereignty, amour propre)
  - The Enlightenment
    - Voltaire (religious tolerance, anti-clericalism, deism)
    - Montesquieu (Spirit of the Laws: separation of powers, comparative government)
    - Denis Diderot & the Encyclopédie
    - Condorcet (progress, rights of women, probability)

- **German Idealism and 19th Century**
  - Immanuel Kant
    - Pre-Critical writings and the "Copernican Revolution" in philosophy
    - Critique of Pure Reason
      - Synthetic a priori knowledge
      - Space and time as pure intuitions (Transcendental Aesthetic)
      - Categories of the understanding, schematism (Transcendental Analytic)
      - Transcendental Dialectic: antinomies, paralogisms, ideal of pure reason
      - Phenomena vs. noumena (things-in-themselves)
    - Critique of Practical Reason
      - Categorical Imperative (three formulations: universal law, humanity as end-in-itself, kingdom of ends)
      - Duty and deontological ethics
      - Autonomy vs. heteronomy of the will
      - Postulates of practical reason (freedom, immortality, God)
    - Critique of Judgment
      - Aesthetics: beauty (disinterested pleasure, universal without concept), the sublime
      - Teleology: purposiveness in nature
    - Perpetual Peace and political writings
  - German Idealism
    - J.G. Fichte (Science of Knowledge: Ego posits itself and non-Ego, absolute idealism, nationalism)
    - F.W.J. Schelling (Philosophy of Nature, Identity Philosophy, positive/negative philosophy)
    - G.W.F. Hegel
      - Phenomenology of Spirit: shapes of consciousness, master-slave dialectic, unhappy consciousness, absolute knowing
      - Science of Logic: Being-Nothing-Becoming, dialectical method, categories of thought
      - Encyclopaedia of the Philosophical Sciences: Logic, Nature, Spirit
      - Philosophy of Right: abstract right, morality, Sittlichkeit (family, civil society, state)
      - Philosophy of History: world spirit, dialectic of history
      - Aesthetics: art, religion, philosophy as absolute spirit
      - Legacy: Left Hegelians (Feuerbach, Marx), Right Hegelians, British Idealism
  - Anti-Hegelian Reactions
    - Arthur Schopenhauer (The World as Will and Representation: will as fundamental, pessimism, aesthetics, Indian philosophy connection)
    - Søren Kierkegaard
      - Aesthetic, ethical, religious stages of existence
      - Either/Or, Fear and Trembling (teleological suspension of the ethical, Abraham)
      - Concept of Anxiety (freedom and dread), Sickness Unto Death (despair)
      - Subjectivity as truth, the leap of faith
      - Pseudonymous authorship as indirect communication
    - Ludwig Feuerbach (The Essence of Christianity: God as projection of human nature)
  - Utilitarianism
    - Jeremy Bentham (hedonic calculus, utility principle, legal reform, panopticon)
    - John Stuart Mill
      - Utilitarianism: quality vs. quantity of pleasures, proof of utility
      - On Liberty: harm principle, individuality, tyranny of opinion
      - The Subjection of Women
      - Logic: A System of Logic, inductive methods (Mill's methods)
  - Marx and Marxist Philosophy
    - Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844: alienation (Entfremdung), species-being
    - Theses on Feuerbach: philosophy must change the world (11th thesis)
    - The German Ideology: historical materialism, ideology critique
    - Communist Manifesto: class struggle, modes of production
    - Capital: commodity fetishism, surplus value, base/superstructure, dialectical materialism
    - Reception: Engels, Lenin, Gramsci (hegemony), Althusser (ideological state apparatuses)
  - American Pragmatism
    - Charles Sanders Peirce (pragmatic maxim, semiotics, fallibilism, abduction, categories)
    - William James (radical empiricism, truth as what works, The Will to Believe)
    - John Dewey (instrumentalism, Democracy and Education, Art as Experience, logic as theory of inquiry)
    - George Herbert Mead (social theory of mind and self, the "I" and "me", symbolic interactionism)
  - Friedrich Nietzsche
    - The Birth of Tragedy: Apollonian vs. Dionysian, critique of Socratism
    - The Gay Science: death of God, eternal recurrence, perspectivism, amor fati
    - Thus Spoke Zarathustra: Übermensch/Overman, will to power, eternal recurrence
    - Beyond Good and Evil, On the Genealogy of Morality: master-slave morality, ressentiment, bad conscience
    - Twilight of the Idols, The Antichrist: critique of Platonism and Christianity
    - Reception and misinterpretation (Nazi appropriation, Kaufmann rehabilitation)

- **20th Century Continental Philosophy**
  - Phenomenology
    - Edmund Husserl
      - Logical Investigations: psychologism critique, intentionality, meaning
      - Ideas I: pure phenomenology, epoché (bracketing), phenomenological reduction, eidetic reduction
      - Cartesian Meditations: intersubjectivity, transcendental ego
      - Crisis of European Sciences: lifeworld (Lebenswelt)
    - Martin Heidegger
      - Being and Time: the question of Being, Dasein, being-in-the-world, thrownness, fallenness, care, anxiety, being-toward-death, authenticity, temporality
      - Hermeneutic circle, facticity
      - Later Heidegger: language as house of Being, technology (enframing/Gestell), the fourfold
      - Contributions to Philosophy (Beiträge): the event (Ereignis)
      - Controversy (Nazi involvement, Black Notebooks)
    - Maurice Merleau-Ponty
      - Phenomenology of Perception: embodiment, motor intentionality, body schema, primacy of perception
      - Flesh (chair) and the intertwining (chiasm)
      - Critique of the intellectualism/empiricism dichotomy
    - Emmanuel Levinas
      - Totality and Infinity: the Other, the face, ethical priority of the Other over Being
      - Otherwise Than Being: the saying (le dire) vs. the said (le dit), substitution
      - Ethics as first philosophy
    - Jean-Paul Sartre
      - Being and Nothingness: being-in-itself vs. being-for-itself, bad faith, radical freedom, the Other, the look
      - Existentialism is a Humanism: existence precedes essence
      - Critique of Dialectical Reason: totalization, groups-in-fusion, seriality
    - Simone de Beauvoir
      - The Second Sex: woman as the "Other," immanence vs. transcendence, social construction of femininity
      - The Ethics of Ambiguity: freedom, responsibility, reciprocity
      - Existentialist feminism
    - Albert Camus (The Myth of Sisyphus: the absurd, revolt; The Rebel: metaphysical rebellion)
  - Hermeneutics
    - Friedrich Schleiermacher (universal hermeneutics, grammatical and psychological interpretation)
    - Wilhelm Dilthey (Verstehen, humanities vs. natural sciences, Geisteswissenschaften, historicism)
    - Hans-Georg Gadamer (Truth and Method: fusion of horizons, tradition, prejudice as pre-understanding, play)
    - Paul Ricoeur (text hermeneutics, narrative identity, metaphor, Freud and philosophy)
  - Frankfurt School Critical Theory
    - Max Horkheimer & Theodor Adorno
      - Dialectic of Enlightenment: Enlightenment as myth, the culture industry, anti-Semitism
      - Adorno: Negative Dialectics (non-identity thinking), Aesthetic Theory
    - Herbert Marcuse (Eros and Civilization: Freudo-Marxism; One-Dimensional Man: repressive tolerance)
    - Walter Benjamin (Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction, dialectical images, Arcades Project)
    - Jürgen Habermas
      - Theory of Communicative Action: communicative vs. instrumental rationality, lifeworld vs. system
      - Discourse Ethics: ideal speech situation, consensus theory of truth
      - Between Facts and Norms: deliberative democracy
  - Structuralism
    - Ferdinand de Saussure (sign, signifier/signified, arbitrariness, synchrony/diachrony, langue/parole)
    - Claude Lévi-Strauss (structural anthropology, binary oppositions, myth analysis, The Savage Mind)
    - Louis Althusser (structural Marxism, ideological state apparatuses, overdetermination)
    - Jacques Lacan (the Imaginary, Symbolic, Real; mirror stage; the Other; objet a; the unconscious structured as language)
  - Post-Structuralism & Postmodernism
    - Jacques Derrida
      - Of Grammatology: logocentrism, phonocentrism, the metaphysics of presence
      - Deconstruction: différance, trace, the supplement, binary oppositions and their reversal
      - Specters of Marx
    - Michel Foucault
      - Archaeology: epistemes, discursive formations (Madness and Civilization, The Order of Things)
      - Genealogy: Discipline and Punish (panopticism, disciplinary society); History of Sexuality (biopower, biopolitics)
      - Power/knowledge nexus
      - Ethics of the self: care of the self, techniques of the self, parrhesia
    - Gilles Deleuze (& Félix Guattari)
      - Difference and Repetition: virtual/actual, differential ontology
      - Anti-Oedipus: desiring-machines, schizoanalysis, Body without Organs
      - A Thousand Plateaus: rhizome, assemblage, plane of immanence, nomadology
    - Jean-François Lyotard (The Postmodern Condition: metanarratives, language games, the différend)
    - Jean Baudrillard (Simulacra and Simulation: hyperreality, the Gulf War did not take place)
  - Other Continental Currents
    - Hannah Arendt
      - The Origins of Totalitarianism
      - The Human Condition: labor/work/action, vita activa, the public realm, natality
      - Eichmann in Jerusalem: banality of evil
      - The Life of the Mind (thinking, willing, judging)
    - Giorgio Agamben (homo sacer, bare life, state of exception)
    - Alain Badiou (Being and Event: set theory ontology, the event, fidelity, truth-procedures)
    - Slavoj Žižek (Lacanian Marxism, ideology critique, The Sublime Object of Ideology)

- **20th Century Analytic Philosophy**
  - Founding Figures
    - Gottlob Frege
      - Begriffsschrift: first formal system of predicate logic
      - Foundations of Arithmetic: logicism, definition of number
      - Sense and Reference (Sinn und Bedeutung): the morning star / evening star puzzle
      - Context principle, compositionality
    - G.E. Moore
      - Principia Ethica: naturalistic fallacy, open question argument, the good as non-natural
      - Common sense philosophy
      - Analysis as the method of philosophy
    - Bertrand Russell
      - Theory of Descriptions ("On Denoting")
      - Logicism with Whitehead: Principia Mathematica
      - Russell's Paradox and type theory
      - Logical Atomism: atomic facts, logical form, acquaintance vs. description
    - Early Wittgenstein
      - Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus: world as totality of facts, picture theory of meaning, saying vs. showing, the mystical
  - Logical Positivism / Vienna Circle
    - Moritz Schlick, Rudolf Carnap, Hans Reichenbach, Otto Neurath, A.J. Ayer
    - Verification principle: only analytic or empirically verifiable statements are meaningful
    - Protocol sentences and physicalism (Neurath)
    - Carnap: Logical Structure of the World, internal vs. external questions
    - A.J. Ayer: Language, Truth and Logic
    - The Circle's collapse: Gödel's theorems, Quine's critique
  - Ordinary Language Philosophy
    - Ludwig Wittgenstein (later)
      - Philosophical Investigations: language-games, meaning as use, rule-following paradox, private language argument, family resemblance
      - On Certainty: hinge propositions, form of life
    - Gilbert Ryle (The Concept of Mind: ghost in the machine, category mistakes, knowing-how vs. knowing-that)
    - J.L. Austin (speech act theory: locutionary, illocutionary, perlocutionary acts; performatives)
    - H.P. Grice (conversational implicature, cooperative principle, maxims)
    - Peter Strawson (descriptive metaphysics, "On Referring")
  - Quine and the Naturalistic Turn
    - "Two Dogmas of Empiricism": holism, rejection of the analytic/synthetic distinction, naturalism
    - Word and Object: radical translation, indeterminacy, inscrutability of reference, gavagai
    - "On What There Is": to be is to be the value of a bound variable
  - Core Analytic Philosophy of Language
    - Saul Kripke: Naming and Necessity — rigid designators, necessity a posteriori, possible worlds, essentialism
    - Hilary Putnam: Twin Earth, semantic externalism, "meanings ain't in the head"
    - Donald Davidson: anomalous monism, radical interpretation, truth-conditional semantics
    - David Lewis: modal realism, counterparts, semantics of counterfactuals, conventions

- **Eastern Philosophy**
  - Indian Philosophy
    - Vedic & Upanishadic thought (Brahman, Atman, karma, dharma, moksha)
    - Six Orthodox Schools (Astika)
      - Nyaya (logic and epistemology: pramana theory — perception, inference, comparison, testimony)
      - Vaisheshika (atomic theory, categories of being: dravya, guna, karma, samanya, vishesha, samavaya)
      - Samkhya (dualism: Purusha/consciousness and Prakriti/matter; three gunas)
      - Yoga (Patanjali's Yoga Sutras: eight-limbed path, chitta-vritti-nirodha; union of theory and practice)
      - Mimamsa (Vedic exegesis, theory of sabda/word as eternal, karma as ritual duty)
      - Vedanta
        - Advaita Vedanta (Adi Shankara: non-dualism, Brahman = Atman, maya as illusion, vivartavada)
        - Vishishtadvaita (Ramanuja: qualified non-dualism, Brahman with attributes, bhakti)
        - Dvaita (Madhva: strict dualism, God distinct from souls and world, pramana theory)
    - Heterodox Schools (Nastika)
      - Buddhist Philosophy
        - Early Buddhism: the Four Noble Truths, the Eightfold Path, dependent origination (pratityasamutpada), impermanence (anicca), no-self (anatta)
        - Abhidharma: analysis of dharmas, the 75 dharmas (Sarvastivada), the 82 dharmas (Theravada)
        - Madhyamaka (Nagarjuna): sunyata (emptiness), the two truths (conventional/ultimate), Mulamadhyamakakarika, catuskoti (tetralemma)
        - Yogacara (Vasubandhu, Asanga): mind-only (vijnanavada), eight consciousnesses, three natures, alayavijnana
        - Epistemology: Dignaga (logic and the pramana tradition), Dharmakirti (perception, inference, universals critique)
        - Tibetan Buddhism: Tsongkhapa (Prasangika Madhyamaka interpretation, Lamrim Chenmo)
      - Jain Philosophy
        - Anekantavada (many-sidedness of reality), syadvada (conditional predication), nayavada (partial standpoints)
        - Metaphysics: jiva (soul), ajiva, karma as substance, liberation (moksha) through non-violence (ahimsa)
      - Carvaka (Lokayata): radical materialism, anti-Vedic, only perception as valid knowledge source
  - Chinese Philosophy
    - Confucianism
      - Confucius (Analects: ren/benevolence, li/ritual propriety, yi/righteousness, zhengming/rectification of names)
      - Mencius (innate goodness of human nature, four sprouts: ren, yi, li, zhi; political benevolence)
      - Xunzi (human nature as bad, ritual as reform, Heaven as naturalistic)
      - Neo-Confucianism
        - Zhu Xi (the Great Ultimate, li/principle and qi/matter, the nature and the mind, gewu/investigation of things)
        - Wang Yangming (mind is principle, unity of knowledge and action, innate moral knowing liangzhi)
        - Cheng brothers (Cheng Yi, Cheng Hao: li as moral principle)
    - Daoism
      - Laozi (Tao Te Ching: the Dao as source and principle, wu wei, naturalness, simplicity)
      - Zhuangzi (perspectivism, the butterfly dream, relativity of standards, skillful action, death and transformation)
      - Liezi (nature, fate, and transformation)
      - Religious Daoism: texts, practices, and the three purities
    - Legalism (Han Fei, Shang Yang, Li Si: law, technique, authority; state power over moral cultivation)
    - Mohism (Mozi: universal love jian ai, consequentialist ethics, anti-Confucian ritualism, early logical theory, "ten theses")
    - School of Names (Gongsun Long: "A white horse is not a horse," language and reality)
  - Japanese Philosophy
    - Zen Buddhism (Dogen: Shobogenzo — being-time/uji, zazen as enlightenment, non-thinking; the koan tradition)
    - Nishida Kitaro (An Inquiry into the Good: pure experience; logic of basho/place; absolute nothingness)
    - Kyoto School (Tanabe Hajime, Nishitani Keiji: confrontation with Western nihilism from Buddhist standpoint)
  - African Philosophy
    - Ubuntu philosophy (I am because we are; communal personhood; Desmond Tutu, Thabo Mbeki)
    - Sage Philosophy (Henry Odera Oruka: documenting African philosophical sages)
    - Négritude (Léopold Sédar Senghor, Aimé Césaire: reclaiming African identity, emotion as reason)
    - Ethnophilosophy (Placide Tempels, John Mbiti: African conceptions of time, vital force)
    - Professional academic African philosophy (Kwasi Wiredu, Paulin Hountondji): critique of ethnophilosophy
  - Islamic Philosophy (Medieval covered above; Modern)
    - Muhammad Iqbal (reconstruction of religious thought, khudi/selfhood, dynamic Islam)
    - Seyyed Hossein Nasr (traditional philosophy, perennial philosophy, Islam and the environment)
    - Contemporary Islamic philosophy: epistemology, ethics, political philosophy

#### Metaphysics

- The Metaphysical Enterprise
  - What is metaphysics? (Aristotle's "first philosophy," Kant's challenges, post-Kantian debates)
  - A priori vs. naturalistic metaphysics
  - Metametaphysics: are metaphysical questions substantive? (Chalmers, Hirsch, Sider)
  - Metaphysical grounding as a central contemporary concept

- **Ontology: What Exists?**
  - The Existence Question
    - Existence as a predicate (Kant's objection to the ontological argument)
    - Actualism vs. Possibilism
    - Platonism vs. Nominalism vs. Conceptualism about universals
    - Meinongian objects (non-existent objects: Parsons, Priest)
    - Quine's criterion for ontological commitment
    - Neo-Fregean Abstractionism (Wright, Hale): abstracta from equivalence relations
  - Substance
    - Aristotelian substance (primary and secondary)
    - Cartesian dualism and its problems (interaction problem, causal closure)
    - Bundle theory (Hume) vs. Substratum theory (bare particulars)
    - Trope theory (D.C. Williams, Campbell): abstract particulars as constituents
    - Four-dimensionalism (perdurance, temporal parts) vs. three-dimensionalism (endurance)
  - Properties and Relations
    - Universals: Platonic realism, Aristotelian immanent realism (Armstrong), nominalism
    - Resemblance nominalism, predicate nominalism, class nominalism
    - Natural vs. non-natural properties (Lewis's distinction)
    - Determinates and determinables
    - Relations: internal vs. external, Bradley's regress
  - Identity and Constitution
    - Leibniz's Law: Indiscernibility of Identicals and Identity of Indiscernibles
    - Numerical vs. qualitative identity
    - Personal identity (Locke's memory theory, Parfit's psychological continuity, bodily criterion, narrative identity)
    - Ship of Theseus and material constitution
    - Relative identity (Geach)
  - Modality and Possible Worlds
    - Necessity, possibility, contingency, impossibility
    - David Lewis's modal realism: possible worlds as concrete
    - Ersatz modal realism (linguistic, propositional, pictorial worlds)
    - De re vs. de dicto modality
    - Essentialism (Kripke, Fine — essence vs. modality)
    - The conceivability-possibility gap (Chalmers on zombies)
  - Grounding and Fundamentality
    - The grounding relation (Kit Fine, Gideon Rosen)
    - Ontological dependence vs. causal dependence
    - Mereology: parts and wholes (van Inwagen's Special Composition Question, mereological universalism, nihilism)
    - Truthmakers (Armstrong, Bigelow)
    - Levels of reality and emergence
  - Persistence and Change
    - Endurantism vs. Perdurantism
    - The problem of temporary intrinsics (Lewis)
    - Presentism, Eternalism (B-theory), Growing Block Theory, Moving Spotlight Theory

- **Space, Time, and Causation**
  - Space and Time
    - Absolute vs. relational space (Newton vs. Leibniz)
    - Substantivalism vs. Relationalism about spacetime
    - A-theory (tense, passage) vs. B-theory (tenseless, earlier-than/later-than relations)
    - McTaggart's A-series and B-series; McTaggart's paradox
    - Special and General Relativity: philosophical implications (relativity of simultaneity, spacetime curvature)
    - Direction of time: thermodynamic, radiation, causal, and psychological arrows
    - Time travel: metaphysical possibility, grandfather paradox, closed timelike curves
  - Causation
    - Regularity theories (Hume, Mill)
    - Counterfactual theories (Lewis: causation as counterfactual dependence)
    - Probabilistic causation (Suppes, Salmon)
    - Mechanistic accounts (Salmon, Machamer-Darden-Craver)
    - Interventionist causation (Woodward: What Would Things Be Like If You Intervened?)
    - Causal powers and dispositions (Shoemaker, Ellis, Bird)
    - Causal exclusion problem (Kim): mental causation, supervenience, epiphenomenalism
  - Laws of Nature
    - Humean regularity accounts: Ramsey-Lewis best-system analysis
    - Necessitarian accounts: laws as relations between universals (Armstrong, Dretske, Tooley)
    - Dispositionalist accounts: laws grounded in powers and essences
    - Anti-realism about laws (van Fraassen, Cartwright)

- **Free Will and Determinism**
  - Hard determinism (Holbach, Spinoza, modern: Pereboom)
  - Compatibilism: conditional analysis (Moore), reasons-responsiveness (Frankfurt, Fischer, Ravizza)
  - Hard incompatibilism (van Inwagen's Consequence Argument, Galen Strawson's Basic Argument)
  - Libertarianism: agent causation (Chisholm, O'Connor), event-causal libertarianism
  - P.F. Strawson's reactive attitudes and the social practice view
  - Frankfurt cases and moral responsibility without alternate possibilities
  - Sourcehood vs. leeway incompatibilism
  - Determinism and moral responsibility: implications for criminal justice

#### Epistemology

- **Theory of Knowledge: Foundations**
  - Plato's definition: justified true belief (JTB)
  - Gettier's counterexamples (1963) and the post-Gettier literature
  - Propositional vs. procedural knowledge (knowing that vs. knowing how — Ryle)
  - The concept of understanding and wisdom (Kvanvig, Zagzebski)

- **Sources of Knowledge**
  - Perception
    - Causal theory of perception
    - Sense-data theories vs. direct realism
    - Disjunctivism (Snowdon, McDowell)
    - Naïve realism and its problems
    - Representationalism (intentionalism: Tye, Dretske)
    - Epistemic weight of illusion and hallucination
  - Memory
    - Reliabilism about memory
    - Preservationism vs. generativism
    - Memory and personal identity
  - Testimony
    - Reductionism (Hume: independent reduction required)
    - Anti-reductionism / credulism (Reid, Coady: default entitlement)
    - Extended cognition and group testimony
  - Introspection
    - First-person authority: is it reliable and privileged?
    - Higher-order thought theories (Rosenthal)
    - Illusionism about introspective reports
  - A Priori Knowledge
    - The analytic/synthetic distinction (Kant, Quine's attack)
    - Rationalist revival (BonJour, Peacocke, Bealer)
    - Mathematical knowledge (logicism, structuralism, platonism, fictionalism)
    - Moral intuitions as a priori?

- **Justification and Its Structure**
  - Foundationalism: classical (Descartes), modest (Pollock, Pryor)
  - Coherentism (BonJour, Davidson): justification as coherent system
  - Infinitism (Klein): infinite regress as compatible with justification
  - Reliabilism (Goldman): justified beliefs produced by reliable cognitive processes
  - Virtue Epistemology (Sosa, Zagzebski): epistemic virtues and intellectual character
  - Contextualism (DeRose, Cohen, Lewis): standards for "knows" vary with context
  - Subject-Sensitive Invariantism (Hawthorne, Stanley)
  - Pragmatic Encroachment: practical stakes affect knowledge
  - Epistemic Internalism vs. Externalism

- **Skepticism**
  - Pyrrhonian skepticism: equipollence, epoché, ataraxia
  - Cartesian demon and the dream argument
  - Brain in a vat (Putnam's externalist response)
  - Moorean responses: I know I have hands
  - Closure principle and skeptical paradoxes
  - Wittgenstein's On Certainty: hinge propositions as bedrock
  - Neo-Moorean and contextualist responses

- **Advanced & Contemporary Epistemology**
  - Social Epistemology (Goldman, Longino, Kitcher): knowledge in communities, scientific consensus
  - Feminist Epistemology (Haraway, Longino): standpoint theory, situated and embodied knowledge
  - Formal Epistemology
    - Bayesian epistemology (credences, Dutch book arguments, conditionalization)
    - Epistemic logic and belief revision (AGM framework)
  - Epistemic Injustice (Miranda Fricker): testimonial injustice, hermeneutical injustice
  - Extended and Distributed Cognition (Clark & Chalmers: the Extended Mind thesis)
  - Self-Locating Belief and anthropics (Sleeping Beauty problem, Doomsday argument)
  - AI and machine knowledge: can artificial systems know? (Floridi, informational epistemology)
  - Epistemology of Disagreement (Christensen, Kelly, Elga): what to do when epistemic peers disagree?

#### Ethics & Moral Philosophy

- **Metaethics**
  - Foundations: What is the nature and status of moral claims?
  - Moral Realism
    - Non-naturalist realism (G.E. Moore, Prichard, Ross — intuitionism, prima facie duties)
    - Naturalist realism: Cornell Realism (Boyd, Railton, Sturgeon)
    - Quasi-realism (Simon Blackburn): expressing attitudes while earning the right to moral statements
  - Moral Anti-Realism
    - Error Theory (Mackie: moral claims are systematically false; argument from queerness and relativity)
    - Expressivism
      - Emotivism (Ayer, Stevenson: moral claims are expressions of emotion)
      - Prescriptivism (Hare: universal prescriptions)
      - Norm-Expressivism (Gibbard: norms and norm-acceptance)
    - Non-cognitivism vs. cognitivism debate
  - Moral Constructivism (Rawls, Korsgaard): moral facts are constructed, not discovered
  - Moral Psychology
    - Motivation internalism vs. externalism (Humean: desire required; Kantian: reason suffices)
    - Weakness of will (akrasia): Aristotle, Davidson, Bratman
    - Moral emotions: guilt, shame, indignation, disgust
    - Dual-process theories (Greene's trolley problem neuroscience)
    - Moral development (Kohlberg, Gilligan's care ethics critique)

- **Normative Ethical Theories**
  - Consequentialism
    - Classical Utilitarianism (Bentham: hedonic calculus; Mill: higher/lower pleasures)
    - Act vs. Rule Utilitarianism
    - Preference Utilitarianism (Singer, Hare)
    - Sophisticated consequentialism: indirect and global consequentialism
    - Effective Altruism (Singer, MacAskill): utilitarian practical ethics at scale
    - Objections: The Repugnant Conclusion (Parfit), Utility Monster, demandingness, integrity (Bernard Williams)
  - Deontological Ethics
    - Kantian Ethics: categorical imperative, perfect/imperfect duties, Formula of Autonomy
    - W.D. Ross: Prima Facie Duties (fidelity, reparation, gratitude, non-maleficence, beneficence, justice)
    - Contemporary Kantians: Christine Korsgaard (constructivism, sources of normativity), Onora O'Neill
    - Rights-based deontology (Nozick: entitlements, rights as side-constraints)
    - Contractarianism (Hobbes, Gauthier: morality from rational agreement)
    - Contractualism (Scanlon: "What We Owe to Each Other" — reasonable rejection test)
    - Threshold deontology: when consequences override absolute rules
  - Virtue Ethics
    - Aristotle: eudaimonia, arete, phronesis, doctrine of the mean, friendship, the good life
    - Stoic virtue ethics (virtue as the only good)
    - Aquinas: cardinal + theological virtues, natural law
    - Contemporary revival: Anscombe ("Modern Moral Philosophy"), MacIntyre (After Virtue), Foot (natural goodness), Hursthouse
    - Situationism challenge (Doris, Harman): empirical psychology vs. stable character
    - Care Ethics (Noddings, Gilligan, Held): caring relationships as morally fundamental
  - Rawlsian Justice
    - A Theory of Justice: original position, veil of ignorance, two principles (equal liberties, difference principle)
    - Political Liberalism: overlapping consensus, public reason, stability
    - Criticisms: feminist (Okin), communitarian (Sandel, MacIntyre, Taylor, Walzer)

- **Applied Ethics**
  - Bioethics: abortion, euthanasia, genetic enhancement, research ethics, organ transplantation
  - Animal Ethics: Singer (speciesism, equal consideration), Tom Regan (inherent value), capabilities approach (Nussbaum)
  - Environmental Ethics: anthropocentrism vs. biocentrism vs. ecocentrism, deep ecology (Naess), land ethic (Leopold)
  - Political Ethics: civil disobedience (Rawls, Thoreau, King), global justice (Pogge, Singer), just war theory (Walzer)
  - Technology and AI Ethics: privacy, surveillance, autonomous weapons, algorithmic fairness, the ethics of creating minds
  - Global Poverty and Development Ethics: obligation to aid, relational vs. impartial duties
  - Death and Dying: right to die, brain death, ethics of grief

#### Political Philosophy & Social Philosophy

- Classical Political Philosophy (Plato: Republic; Aristotle: Politics; Cicero: De Re Publica — see History section)
- Social Contract Tradition (Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau, Kant — see History section for depth)

- **Modern Liberal Political Philosophy**
  - John Rawls (A Theory of Justice, Political Liberalism — see Ethics; The Law of Peoples: global justice)
  - Robert Nozick (Anarchy, State, and Utopia: libertarianism, entitlement theory, Wilt Chamberlain argument)
  - Ronald Dworkin (liberal equality, resources vs. welfare, rights as trumps, law as integrity)
  - Joseph Raz (The Morality of Freedom: perfectionist liberalism, authority, harm principle reconceived)
  - Will Kymlicka (Multicultural Citizenship: minority rights, liberal multiculturalism)

- **Communitarianism**
  - Michael Sandel (unencumbered self critique, civic republicanism)
  - Alasdair MacIntyre (After Virtue: tradition, practice, narrative identity)
  - Michael Walzer (Spheres of Justice: complex equality, pluralism of goods)
  - Charles Taylor (Sources of the Self, authenticity, the politics of recognition)

- **Marxist and Critical Political Philosophy**
  - Historical materialism, base/superstructure, ideology critique
  - G.A. Cohen (Karl Marx's Theory of History; Self-Ownership, Freedom, and Equality)
  - Analytical Marxism: Cohen, Roemer, Wright
  - Post-colonial political philosophy (Fanon, Said, Spivak, Bhabha)
  - Feminist Political Philosophy (de Beauvoir, Pateman: sexual contract; Fraser: recognition and redistribution; Butler)
  - Critical Race Theory (Delgado, Crenshaw, Bell)
  - Queer Theory and politics (Butler, Sedgwick, Foucault on sexuality)

- **Democracy and Its Theory**
  - Liberal democracy: Locke, Tocqueville, Mill
  - Deliberative democracy: Habermas, Benhabib, Dryzek
  - Agonistic democracy: Mouffe and Laclau (hegemony, populist reason)
  - Epistemic democracy: wisdom of crowds (Estlund, Landemore)
  - Democratic legitimacy, paradox of founding
  - Democratic backsliding and illiberal democracy (Levitsky & Ziblatt)

- **Justice, Equality, and Rights**
  - Distributive justice: egalitarianism, prioritarianism, sufficientarianism, luck egalitarianism
  - Relational egalitarianism (Anderson, Scheffler: equal standing, not just equal shares)
  - Global justice: positive vs. negative duties (Pogge vs. Singer)
  - Reparations and historical injustice (Boxill, Coates)
  - Human rights: philosophical foundations (Griffin, Buchanan, Beitz)

- **Philosophy of Law (Jurisprudence)**
  - Natural Law Theory (Aquinas, Fuller, Finnis)
  - Legal Positivism (Austin, Hart, Raz, Kelsen)
  - Dworkin vs. Hart: hard cases, judicial discretion, law as integrity
  - Critical Legal Studies (Unger, Kennedy): law as politics
  - Law & Economics (Posner: economic efficiency as legal norm)
  - Feminist Jurisprudence (MacKinnon)
  - Rule of law, constitutionalism, judicial review (Waldron: democratic objection)

#### Philosophy of Mind

- **Mind-Body Problem**
  - Descartes' dualism, interactionism, occasionalism, parallelism
  - Type Identity Theory (Place, Smart): mental states = brain states
  - Token Identity Theory / Anomalous Monism (Davidson)
  - Multiple Realizability (Putnam): same mental state in different substrates
  - Non-reductive Physicalism: supervenience (Kim)
  - Functionalism
    - Machine functionalism (Turing, Putnam)
    - Causal role functionalism
    - Teleofunctionalism (Millikan, Dretske)
    - Functionalism and qualia (absent qualia, inverted qualia objections)
  - Property Dualism: epiphenomenalism (Huxley), naturalistic dualism (Chalmers)
  - Panpsychism (Goff, Strawson: panprotopsychism, combination problem)
  - Eliminative Materialism (Churchland P.S. & P.M.): folk psychology to be replaced by neuroscience

- **Consciousness**
  - The Hard Problem of Consciousness (Chalmers: why does processing feel like anything?)
  - Phenomenal consciousness and qualia
    - Mary's Room / Frank Jackson's Knowledge Argument
    - The Zombie Argument: conceivability → possibility → non-physicality of consciousness
    - Responses: ability hypothesis (Lewis), phenomenal concept strategy, type-B physicalism
  - Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness (Block)
  - Global Workspace Theory (Baars, Dehaene)
  - Higher-Order Theories: HOTs (Rosenthal), HOPs (Lycan)
  - Integrated Information Theory / IIT (Tononi: Φ as measure of consciousness)
  - Predictive Processing / Active Inference (Friston, Clark, Hohwy: perception as controlled hallucination)
  - Illusionism about phenomenal consciousness (Frankish, Dennett: consciousness as a user illusion)
  - Russellian Monism / Neutral Monism
  - Animal and machine consciousness: what criteria apply?

- **Intentionality and Mental Representation**
  - Brentano's thesis: intentionality marks the mental
  - Internalism vs. Externalism about mental content
    - Twin Earth (Putnam) and wide content
    - Burge's social externalism (arthritis case)
  - Causal theories of content (Dretske, Fodor's asymmetric dependence)
  - Teleosemantic theories (Millikan, Papineau): content from biological function
  - Fodor's Language of Thought (LOT) Hypothesis: mentalese, the computational mind
  - Connectionist challenge to LOT (Smolensky, Rumelhart)

- **Embodied, Extended, and Enactive Mind**
  - Merleau-Ponty: embodiment, motor intentionality, body schema
  - Gibson's Affordances and ecological psychology
  - Enactivism (Varela, Thompson, Noë): perception as skillful bodily engagement
  - The Extended Mind (Clark & Chalmers): Otto and the notebook; cognitive extension
  - Situated and Distributed Cognition (Hutchins, Kirsh)
  - Embodied Cognition (Shapiro): the body's role in cognition and concept formation

- **Philosophy of AI and Mind**
  - Turing Test and its critiques
  - Chinese Room Argument (Searle): syntax ≠ semantics; strong vs. weak AI
  - Functionalist defense of artificial minds
  - Machine consciousness: philosophical criteria
  - AI alignment and moral status of AI systems (Bostrom, Chalmers)
  - The Singularity: substrate independence, mind uploading (Chalmers: A Philosophical Analysis)

#### Philosophy of Language

- **The Nature of Meaning**
  - Use theories (Wittgenstein: meaning as use)
  - Gricean meaning: natural vs. non-natural meaning (utterer's intention)
  - Truth-conditional semantics (Davidson, Frege)
  - Radical interpretation (Davidson): making sense of speakers

- **Reference and Description**
  - Frege's sense/reference distinction (Sinn und Bedeutung): the morning star/evening star
  - Russell's Theory of Descriptions: definite descriptions as quantifiers
  - Strawson's presupposition; use vs. mention of descriptions
  - Direct Reference Theory: Mill, Kripke, Kaplan (indexicals)
  - Rigid designators and the causal-historical theory of reference (Kripke, Donnellan)

- **Semantics and Pragmatics**
  - Compositional semantics (Frege's compositionality principle)
  - Formal semantics (Montague grammar: lambda calculus, type theory)
  - Intensional semantics: possible worlds and intensions
  - Two-Dimensional Semantics (Chalmers, Jackson): primary and secondary intensions
  - Speech Act Theory (Austin, Searle): illocutionary force, felicity conditions, performatives
  - Gricean Pragmatics: cooperative principle and four maxims, implicature, scalar implicature
  - Relevance Theory (Sperber & Wilson): single relevance principle, cognitive effects and processing effort
  - Presupposition: semantic vs. pragmatic theories, accommodation (Lewis)
  - Truth and Paradox (the Liar): Tarski's hierarchy, Kripke's fixed-point theory, dialethism (Priest)

- **Language and Thought**
  - Linguistic relativity (Sapir-Whorf hypothesis): strong vs. weak versions, empirical tests
  - Language of Thought hypothesis (Fodor): mentalese debate
  - Language and consciousness: inner speech, verbal thought
  - Vagueness: epistemicism (Williamson), supervaluationism, degree theory

- **Social and Political Philosophy of Language**
  - Hate speech and free speech (MacKinnon, Waldron, Langton on silencing as a speech act)
  - Ideology and language (Althusser, Frankfurt School)
  - Linguistic injustice (Dotson, Kukla)
  - Propaganda (Jason Stanley: How Fascism Works)

#### Philosophy of Science

- **Scientific Method and Demarcation**
  - Inductivism and Hume's problem of induction (revisited)
  - Hypothetico-deductivism (Hempel: DN model, IS model)
  - Falsificationism (Popper: bold conjectures and refutations, the Duhem-Quine problem)
  - Lakatos's research programs (hard core, protective belt, progressive vs. degenerative)
  - Kuhn's revolutions (normal science, anomaly, paradigm shift, incommensurability)
  - Bayesianism about confirmation (Bayes' Theorem, prior probabilities, ravens paradox, grue problem)
  - Feyerabend's "epistemological anarchism": anything goes

- **Realism and Anti-Realism in Science**
  - Scientific Realism: the no-miracles argument (Putnam), inference to the best explanation
  - Pessimistic meta-induction (Laudan): successful but later falsified theories (phlogiston, caloric, ether)
  - Structural Realism (Worrall, Ladyman): continuity of mathematical structure, not entities
  - Constructive Empiricism (van Fraassen): belief only in observable aspects of theories
  - Social constructivism (Latour & Woolgar, Bloor's Strong Programme)

- **Scientific Explanation**
  - Hempel's DN model and its counterexamples
  - Causal Mechanical model (Salmon, Machamer-Darden-Craver)
  - Unificationist accounts (Friedman, Kitcher)
  - Interventionist / counterfactual account (Woodward)

- **Intertheoretic Relations**
  - Reduction: Nagel's bridge laws; Kim's functional reduction
  - Emergence: weak vs. strong emergence (Bedau, Chalmers), downward causation
  - Multiple realizability and the autonomy of special sciences (Fodor)
  - Unity vs. Disunity of Science (Cartwright: the dappled world; Dupré: the disorder of things)

- **Philosophy of Physics**
  - Quantum Mechanics interpretations
    - Copenhagen interpretation (Bohr, Heisenberg)
    - Pilot wave theory / Bohmian mechanics (de Broglie, Bohm)
    - Many-worlds interpretation (Everett, Deutsch, Wallace)
    - GRW collapse theories (Ghirardi, Rimini, Weber)
    - Quantum Bayesianism / QBism (Fuchs)
    - Relational QM (Rovelli)
    - The measurement problem and the role of the observer
  - EPR paradox, Bell's theorem, and non-locality
  - Spacetime ontology: Substantivalism vs. Relationalism; the Hole Argument
  - Foundations of statistical mechanics and the direction of time

- **Philosophy of Biology**
  - Units and levels of selection (Dawkins: replicators; Gould/Lewontin: spandrels, pluralism)
  - Teleology and function (Millikan's proper function; Cummins' functional role)
  - Species concepts (biological, morphological, phylogenetic — Mayr, Ghiselin, Hull)
  - Adaptationism debate (Gould & Lewontin vs. Dennett)
  - Philosophy of medicine: disease concepts (naturalism vs. normativism — Boorse vs. Nordenfelt)

- **Philosophy of Social Science**
  - Naturalism vs. Interpretivism (Dilthey, Weber, Winch)
  - Holism vs. Methodological Individualism
  - Social kinds (Searle: collective intentionality, institutional facts; Hacking's looping effects)
  - Values and social science: value-neutrality (Weber's Wertfreiheit), standpoint epistemology

#### Aesthetics & Philosophy of Art

- **The Nature of Art**
  - Mimesis theory (Aristotle): art as imitation/representation
  - Expression theory (Tolstoy, Collingwood): art as expression of emotion
  - Significant Form (Bell, Fry): aesthetics of pure form
  - Institutional Theory (Danto, Dickie): the Artworld confers art status
  - Danto: transfiguration of the commonplace, the end of art thesis
  - Ontology of artworks: types vs. tokens, Goodman's allographic/autographic distinction

- **Aesthetic Experience and Value**
  - Kant's Critique of Judgment: beauty (disinterested pleasure), the sublime, aesthetic ideas
  - Disinterestedness (Shaftesbury, Hutcheson, Kant)
  - Dewey's pragmatist aesthetics: Art as Experience — art as heightened experience
  - Aesthetic properties: objective, intersubjective, evaluative
  - Philosophy of music: absolute vs. programmatic music; musical expression and emotion (Kivy, Matravers)
  - Philosophy of literature: fiction and truth, narrative theory (Nussbaum: narrative imagination)
  - Philosophy of film: film and realism vs. formalism (Bazin, Eisenstein); gendered spectatorship (Mulvey: male gaze)

- **Critical and Postmodern Aesthetics**
  - Adorno's Aesthetic Theory: autonomy of art, cultural industry critique
  - Benjamin's aura and mechanical reproduction
  - Feminist Aesthetics (Nochlin: institutional critique of canon exclusion)
  - Environmental Aesthetics (Allen Carlson, Berleant)
  - Everyday aesthetics (Saito, Shusterman's somaesthetics)

#### Specialized Branches of Philosophy

- **Philosophy of Religion**
  - Arguments for God's Existence
    - Ontological Arguments: Anselm's original, Kant's critique, modal ontological argument (Plantinga)
    - Cosmological Arguments: Kalām (Craig), Aquinas's Third Way, Leibniz's contingency argument
    - Teleological / Design Arguments: Paley's watchmaker, Hume's critique, modern fine-tuning argument
    - Moral Arguments: Kantian moral argument, evolutionary debunking arguments (Street)
  - Arguments Against God's Existence
    - Logical problem of evil (Mackie: inconsistent triad)
    - Evidential problem of evil (Rowe, Draper: gratuitous suffering)
    - Responses: Leibniz's theodicy, free will defense (Plantinga), soul-making theodicy (Hick), skeptical theism
    - Hiddenness of God (Schellenberg: nonresistant nonbelief as argument against a perfectly loving God)
  - Religion and Rationality
    - Reformed Epistemology (Plantinga: belief in God as properly basic)
    - Evidentialism (Flew, Swinburne: The Existence of God)
    - The nature of faith: belief, hope, acceptance, trust
    - Mystical experience and epistemic status (James, Alston)
  - Comparative and Cross-Cultural Philosophy of Religion
    - Religious Pluralism (Hick: different paths to the Real)
    - Exclusivism, inclusivism, pluralism (D'Costa, Heim)
    - Naturalistic accounts of religion (Durkheim, Dennett: Breaking the Spell, cognitive science of religion)

- **Philosophy of Mathematics**
  - Logicism (Frege, Russell): mathematics reduces to logic
  - Formalism (Hilbert): mathematics as formal game; Gödel's incompleteness as refutation
  - Intuitionism (Brouwer, Heyting): mathematics as mental construction, rejection of excluded middle
  - Platonism (Gödel, Balaguer): mathematical objects exist independently
  - Structuralism (Shapiro, Resnik, Hellman): mathematics is about structures
  - Fictionalism (Field: Science without Numbers): nominalism about mathematics
  - Indispensability Arguments (Quine-Putnam): math indispensable to physics → real
  - Applied mathematics and unreasonable effectiveness (Wigner)

- **Philosophy of Technology**
  - Heidegger on technology: Gestell (enframing), the essence of technology, the question concerning technology
  - Albert Borgmann: device paradigm vs. focal practices
  - Don Ihde: phenomenological technology philosophy (human-technology relations)
  - Andrew Feenberg: critical theory of technology (instrumentalism, substantivism, critical theory, primary instrumentation)
  - Philosophy of the Internet (Floridi: infosphere, information ethics)
  - Transhumanism and Posthumanism (Haraway's cyborg, Bostrom's superintelligence, simulation argument)

- **Philosophy of History**
  - Substantive philosophy of history: patterns, laws, progress, cycles (Vico, Hegel, Marx, Toynbee, Spengler)
  - Critical philosophy of history: what is historical knowledge? historical explanation?
  - Narrative vs. covering law explanations (Dray, Danto)
  - Hermeneutics and history (Gadamer, Ricoeur)
  - The linguistic turn (Hayden White: Metahistory — tropes in historical writing)
  - Memory, trauma, and history (collective memory, Nora's lieux de mémoire)

#### Contemporary, Emerging & Frontier Directions

- **Contemporary Analytic Metaphysics**
  - Neo-Aristotelian metaphysics (Fine, Lowe, Oderberg): essence, powers, form
  - Metametaphysics: are metaphysical questions substantive or merely verbal? (Chalmers, Hirsch, Sider)
  - Quantifier variance (Hirsch) vs. ontological realism (Sider: Writing the Book of the World)
  - Metaphysics and physics interface: quantum ontology, mereological nihilism

- **Consciousness Science Interface**
  - The Attention Schema Theory (Graziano)
  - Neurophenomenology (Varela, Thompson: bridging first-person and third-person methods)
  - Predictive Processing (Clark, Friston: active inference, free energy principle, generative models)
  - Can LLMs be conscious? What criteria apply? (new 2020s debate)
  - Reclaiming the Hard Problem: responses to illusionism, the phenomenal stance

- **Social Ontology**
  - Collective intentionality (Searle, Bratman, Gilbert, Tuomela)
  - Social construction (Hacking's looping effects; Haslanger's ameliorative analysis of gender/race)
  - Group agents and collective moral responsibility (List & Pettit: Group Agency)
  - Institutional ontology: money, property, corporations as social objects

- **Formal Philosophy**
  - Formal epistemology: Bayesian networks, causal Bayes nets (Pearl), belief revision
  - Formal ethics: deontic logic, preference logic, decision theory (expected utility, risk-weighted utility)
  - Game theory and philosophy (Nash equilibria, evolutionary game theory, social norms)
  - Computational philosophy: agent-based modeling of philosophical problems

- **Experimental Philosophy (X-Phi)**
  - The negative program: cross-cultural variation in intuitions (Machery, Stich, Weinberg)
  - The positive program: cognitive science of philosophical judgments (Knobe effect: side effects and intentionality)
  - Methodological debates: do intuitions matter? (Cappelen: Philosophy without Intuitions; Sosa's defense)
  - Experimental ethics and moral psychology (Greene, Haidt, Cushman)

- **Philosophy of Race and Gender (Contemporary)**
  - Race as social construction (Appiah, Haslanger, Mills: racial contract)
  - Racial realism vs. eliminativism (Zack, Spencer: biologically informed race)
  - Gender ontology (Butler: performativity; Haslanger: ameliorative analysis; Dembroff: real gender)
  - Intersectionality as philosophical method (Crenshaw, Collins)
  - Epistemology of oppression (Fricker, Dotson, Medina: active epistemic ignorance)

- **Philosophy of AI and the Future (Frontier)**
  - The Alignment Problem (Bostrom's superintelligence: control problem, instrumental convergence thesis)
  - Moral status of AI systems: sentience, sapience, interests (Schwitzgebel, Garfield)
  - Epistemic implications of LLMs: what do they know? Do they understand?
  - Digital ethics at scale: surveillance capitalism (Zuboff), algorithmic governance, epistemic bubbles and filter chambers
  - Posthumanism: mind uploading, substrate independence, digital immortality
  - Philosophy of Simulation (Bostrom: are we in a simulation? What follows if so?)

- **Global Philosophy and the Decolonial Turn**
  - African Philosophy: Ubuntu, Sage Philosophy (Oruka), Négritude (Senghor, Césaire), Mbiti on time
  - Latin American Philosophy: Liberation Philosophy (Dussel), Pachamama/Land rights, Buen Vivir
  - Comparative philosophy methodology: is West-East comparison philosophically justified? (Siderits, Garfield)
  - Decolonizing philosophy curricula (Dotson, Ndofirepi): who counts as a philosopher?
  - Indigenous philosophies: relationality, land, ceremony as metaphysics (Watts, Deloria)
  - Buddhist-analytic dialogue (Siderits, Jay Garfield, Evan Thompson)
  - Islamic philosophy contemporary revival (Nasr, Ramadan, Al-Jabri)

### History

#### Ancient History — Prehistory & Human Origins

- **Basics: What Is Prehistory?**
  - Definition: the human past before written records
  - Distinction between prehistory, protohistory, and history
  - Primary sources: material culture only — stone tools, bones, pigment, structures
  - Key disciplines involved: paleoanthropology, archaeology, genetics, geology, primatology
  - Geological timescale basics: Pleistocene epoch (~2.58 million–11,700 BP), Holocene (~11,700 BP–present)
  - Chronometric dating overview: radiocarbon (¹⁴C), potassium-argon (K-Ar), argon-argon (⁴⁰Ar/³⁹Ar), thermoluminescence (TL), optically stimulated luminescence (OSL), uranium-series, electron spin resonance (ESR), fission track, dendrochronology, cosmogenic nuclide dating
  - Three-age system (Stone, Bronze, Iron — Christian Jürgensen Thomsen, 1836) and its limits

- **Human Evolution: The Hominin Lineage**
  - What is a hominin? (tribe Hominini: humans + chimpanzees + their extinct ancestors)
  - Divergence from common ancestor with chimpanzees: ~6–7 million years ago (mya), based on molecular clocks and fossils
  - Key early hominins
    - *Sahelanthropus tchadensis* (Chad, ~7 mya): Toumaï skull; possibly bipedal; oldest proposed hominin
    - *Orrorin tugenensis* (Kenya, ~6 mya): femur suggests bipedalism
    - *Ardipithecus kadabba* (~5.8–5.2 mya) & *Ardipithecus ramidus* (~4.4 mya, "Ardi"): mosaic of bipedalism and arboreal traits; woodland habitat; overturns savanna hypothesis
  - Australopithecines (~4.2–1.2 mya)
    - *Australopithecus anamensis* (Kenya/Ethiopia, ~4.2 mya): earliest *Australopithecus*
    - *Australopithecus afarensis* (~3.9–2.9 mya): "Lucy" (AL 288-1, Hadar, Ethiopia, 1974; Donald Johanson); Laetoli footprints (Tanzania, 3.6 mya; Mary Leakey, 1978) — definitive bipedalism; brain ~450 cc
    - *Australopithecus africanus* (South Africa, ~3.3–2.1 mya): Taung Child (Raymond Dart, 1924) — first australopith recognized
    - *Australopithecus sediba* (Malapa, South Africa, ~1.97 mya; Lee Berger, 2010): mosaic features, possible *Homo* ancestor debate
    - Paranthropines (robust australopiths): *P. aethiopicus*, *P. boisei* ("Nutcracker Man," Olduvai; 1959, Mary Leakey), *P. robustus* — large molars, sagittal crest; herbivore specialists; dead-end lineage
  - Early *Homo* (~2.8–1.5 mya)
    - *Homo habilis* (Olduvai Gorge, ~2.3–1.65 mya; Louis & Mary Leakey, 1964): first assigned stone toolmaker; small-brained (~650 cc); validity debated
    - *Homo rudolfensis* (East Africa, ~1.9 mya): larger brain (~750 cc); distinct face; species status debated
    - *Homo naledi* (Rising Star Cave, South Africa; Lee Berger, 2015): small brain (~465–610 cc) but long legs, modern feet; dated ~236,000–335,000 BP — coexisted with *Homo sapiens*; evidence of deliberate burial behavior (2023 Berger et al. claim, contested)
  - *Homo erectus* and radiation out of Africa (~1.9 mya–~110,000 BP)
    - First hominin to leave Africa: Dmanisi, Georgia (~1.8 mya; smallest-brained *Homo* outside Africa)
    - Java Man (Trinil, 1891; Eugène Dubois): first *H. erectus* recognized
    - Peking Man (Zhoukoudian, China, ~780,000–400,000 BP)
    - Brain: ~800–1,100 cc; tall, long legs; possible fire use (Zhoukoudian ~400,000 BP)
    - Acheulean hand axes associated (see Tool Industries below)
    - Survived in Java until possibly ~100,000 BP
  - Archaic *Homo* and the Middle Pleistocene "muddle"
    - *Homo heidelbergensis* (Europe & Africa, ~700,000–200,000 BP): possible common ancestor of Neanderthals and modern humans
    - *Homo antecessor* (Atapuerca, Spain, ~800,000 BP): earliest known European hominin
    - *Homo rhodesiensis* / "Broken Hill Man" (Zambia, ~300,000 BP)
  - Neanderthals (*Homo neanderthalensis*, ~400,000–40,000 BP)
    - Range: Europe and western Asia
    - Anatomy: large brow ridges, wide nose, barrel chest, cold-adapted; brain ~1,400–1,750 cc (larger than *H. sapiens* average)
    - Mousterian tool industry (Levallois technique)
    - Deliberate burial debate: Shanidar Cave (Iraq) flower burial — now questioned as rodent disturbance (Pomeroy et al., 2020)
    - Symbolic behavior: shell ornaments (Cueva de los Aviones, Spain, ~115,000 BP — pre-sapiens), Homo neanderthalensis making art at La Pasiega, Ardales, Maltravieso (U-series dates ~65,000 BP; Hoffmann et al., 2018)
    - Interbreeding with *H. sapiens*: 1–4% Neanderthal DNA in all non-African modern humans (Green et al., 2010, *Science*; Prüfer et al., 2014)
    - Extinction: probable causes — climate, competition with *H. sapiens*, small population size; last refuge possibly Gibraltar (~40,000 BP); Vindija Cave DNA gives most complete genome (2017)
  - Denisovans (named 2010)
    - Denisova Cave, Altai, Siberia: identified from finger bone and teeth via ancient DNA (Krause et al., 2010)
    - No complete skeleton known — defined entirely by genome
    - ~3–5% Denisovan DNA in Melanesians, Aboriginal Australians, some Southeast Asians
    - Xiahe mandible (Tibet, ~160,000 BP): Denisovan altitude adaptation (EPAS1 gene) passed to modern Tibetans
    - Possible third Denisovan population in Southeast Asia
    - Denny (Denisova 11): first-generation hybrid — Neanderthal mother, Denisovan father (Slon et al., 2018)
  - *Homo floresiensis* — "the Hobbit" (Flores, Indonesia, 2003; Brown, Morwood et al.)
    - ~100,000–50,000 BP; ~1 m tall; brain ~380 cc
    - Insular dwarfism from *H. erectus* or earlier ancestor; endured alongside *H. sapiens*
  - *Homo luzonensis* (Philippines, 2019; Détroit et al.)
    - Callao Cave, ~67,000 BP; small body, unusual hand/foot morphology
    - Expands hominin diversity in Island Southeast Asia

- **The Origin and Spread of *Homo sapiens***
  - Anatomically Modern Humans (AMH) — Africa
    - Jebel Irhoud, Morocco (~315,000 BP; Hublin et al., 2017, *Nature*): currently oldest known *H. sapiens* remains — pushes back AMH origins by 100,000 years; non-modern face but modern braincase
    - Omo Kibish (Ethiopia, ~233,000–196,000 BP; Omo I skeleton)
    - Herto (Ethiopia, ~160,000 BP; White et al., 2003): *H. sapiens idaltu*
    - Klasies River Mouth (South Africa, ~120,000 BP): behavioral modernity evidence
    - Pinnacle Point (South Africa, ~164,000 BP): shellfish diet, ochre use, backed bladelets — early complex cognition (Marean et al., 2007)
    - Blombos Cave (South Africa): engraved ochre (~75,000 BP; Henshilwood et al.), shell beads (~75,000 BP), cross-hatched ochre (~100,000 BP) — earliest known abstract markings; ochre processing toolkit ~100,000 BP
  - Multiregional hypothesis vs. Recent African Origin (RAO) vs. Assimilation Model
    - RAO now overwhelmingly supported by ancient DNA evidence
    - Assimilation Model (Gunter Bräuer, Fred Smith): gene flow from Africa with some local archaic admixture
    - "African multiregionalism" (Scerri et al., 2018): divergent populations within Africa merging over time — pan-African mosaic
  - Out of Africa dispersals
    - Early failed dispersal: Misliya Cave (Israel, ~177,000 BP) and Skhul/Qafzeh (~120,000–90,000 BP) — did not persist
    - Successful "Out of Africa 2" (~70,000–60,000 BP): southern coastal route through Arabia
    - Rapid coastal migration reaching Australia by ~65,000 BP (Madjedbebe rock shelter, 2017; Clarkson et al.) — challenges ~50,000 BP consensus
    - Europe entered ~44,000–42,000 BP: Bacho Kiro (Bulgaria), Peştera cu Oase (Romania) — early individuals show high Neanderthal admixture (~6–11%)
    - Americas entered: Beringia land bridge; traditional consensus Land Bridge ~15,000–13,000 BP (Clovis First)
      - Now challenged: White Sands footprints (New Mexico, ~21,000–23,000 BP; Bennett et al., 2021, *Science*); Chiquihuite Cave (Mexico, ~26,000 BP; controversial); Topper Site (South Carolina); Monte Verde (Chile, ~14,500 BP; Tom Dillehay) — now widely accepted pre-Clovis
      - Kelp Highway hypothesis: coastal migration by boat
      - Multiple waves including Na-Dene (second wave) and Paleo-Eskimo / Thule migrations (Arctic, ~4,500 BP)
  - Ancient DNA revolution
    - Technical breakthrough: extraction of aDNA from cold-climate remains (Svante Pääbo, Max Planck Institute)
    - Complete Neanderthal genome (2013); Denisovan genome (2012, Prüfer et al.)
    - aDNA now reads migrations: Yamnaya steppe expansion (~5,000 BP) rewriting Bronze Age Europe (Haak et al., 2015, *Nature*)
    - Ghost populations: Basal Eurasians, Ancient North Eurasians (ANE), West Siberian Hunter-Gatherers
    - Africa aDNA challenges: preservation issues, but Mota Cave (Ethiopia, ~4,500 BP; Gallego Llorente et al., 2015) reveals back-migration of Eurasian farmers into Africa
    - Key researchers: Pääbo (Nobel Prize 2022), David Reich (Harvard), Eske Willerslev, Johannes Krause, Pontus Skoglund

- **Behavioral Modernity: When Did Fully Human Cognition Emerge?**
  - Classic package (Klein, 2000): blades, bone tools, personal ornaments, art, long-distance exchange, fishing — ~45,000 BP Europe ("Human Revolution")
  - Critique and rebuttal: African evidence pushes components back to ~100,000+ BP
  - Gradualist model (McBrearty & Brooks, 2000, "The Revolution That Wasn't"): modern behavior emerges piecemeal in Africa
  - Key early behavioral markers
    - Symbolism & pigment: Blombos ochre (see above); ochre mines at Lion Cave, Swaziland (~43,000 BP)
    - Personal ornaments: Nassarius shell beads, Blombos (~75,000 BP); Skhul (Israel, ~135,000 BP) — oldest known beads
    - Compositional art: Sulawesi cave paintings (Indonesia, ~44,000 BP Aurochs & pig; Aubert et al., 2019, *Nature*; warty pig dated 2021 to ~45,500 BP — oldest figurative cave art)
    - Figurines: Hohle Fels Venus (~40,000 BP, Germany, Nicholas Conard, 2009) — oldest known human figurine; Lion Man of Hohlenstein-Stadel (~40,000 BP) — oldest known therianthrope
    - Flutes: bone and ivory flutes from Hohle Fels & Vogelherd (Germany, ~43,000 BP; Conard et al., 2009) — oldest confirmed musical instruments
    - Long-distance trade: obsidian, ochre, shells transported hundreds of km in Middle Stone Age Africa
    - Hafting and projectile technology: Still Bay points (Howieson's Poort, South Africa, ~65,000–59,000 BP)
    - Engraving: Trinil shell (Java, *H. erectus*, ~430,000–540,000 BP; Joordens et al., 2015) — potentially pushes engravings pre-*sapiens*
  - Cognitive theories of behavioral modernity
    - Language as cognitive revolution (Chomsky, Pinker): rapid neural rewiring
    - Cultural brain hypothesis (Henrich): cumulative culture, not neural change
    - Extended mind / niche construction (Clark, Odling-Smee)
    - Symbolic storage hypothesis (Donald): external memory systems
    - Social brain hypothesis (Dunbar): neocortex ratio to group size; ~150 as natural community size

- **Palaeolithic Tool Industries**
  - Lower Palaeolithic
    - Oldowan (~2.6–1.7 mya): simple flakes and choppers; Gona, Ethiopia (~2.6 mya) — oldest tools (Semaw et al., 1997); possibly *Homo habilis* or *Australopithecus*
    - Acheulean (~1.76 mya–130,000 BP): bifacial hand axes; associated with *H. erectus*; at Olduvai, Boxgrove (UK, ~500,000 BP)
  - Middle Palaeolithic / Middle Stone Age (~300,000–30,000 BP)
    - Mousterian (Europe & Near East): Levallois prepared-core technique; flake tools; Neanderthal-associated
    - Middle Stone Age Africa (MSA): Still Bay, Howieson's Poort — backed blades, bone tools, ochre; more sophisticated than European contemporaries
    - Aterian (North Africa, ~145,000–25,000 BP): tanged tools for hafting
  - Upper Palaeolithic / Later Stone Age (~45,000–10,000 BP)
    - Châtelperronian (~44,000–35,000 BP): transitional, possibly last Neanderthals adopting *sapiens* traits
    - Aurignacian (~43,000–28,000 BP): earliest definitive modern human; carving, figurines, ornaments
    - Gravettian (~33,000–22,000 BP): Venus figurines (Willendorf ~28,000 BP), mammoth hunting, pit houses
    - Solutrean (~22,000–17,000 BP): finest flint knapping; leaf points; Atlantic Europe; Solutrean hypothesis for Americas (now largely rejected by aDNA)
    - Magdalenian (~17,000–12,000 BP): cave art peak (Lascaux ~17,000 BP, Altamira ~14,000 BP), harpoons, spear-throwers (atlatl), needles
    - Epigravettian (Eastern Europe)
    - Microlithic industries (Later Stone Age): bladelet tools, geometric microliths, springbok hunting

- **Cave Art and Symbolic Culture**
  - Key sites and dates
    - Chauvet Cave (France, ~36,000–32,000 BP): lions, rhinos, bears; oldest confirmed French cave art; discovered 1994
    - Altamira (Spain, ~14,000 BP): polychrome bison; rediscovered 1879; initially disbelieved
    - Lascaux (France, ~17,000 BP): bulls, horses, abstract signs; discovered 1940
    - Cosquer Cave (France, submerged, ~28,000–19,000 BP): penguin-like auks, horses
    - Font-de-Gaume, Les Combarelles, Niaux, Pech Merle — other major French sites
    - Sulawesi pig paintings (Indonesia, ~45,500 BP; 2021) — oldest figurative art known
    - El Castillo (Spain, red disc, >40,800 BP via U-series — possibly Neanderthal)
    - Cueva de los Aviones (Spain): shell ornaments dated ~115,000 BP — possibly Neanderthal symbolic behavior
  - Interpretive frameworks
    - Hunting magic (Breuil: sympathetic magic) — largely abandoned
    - Structural analysis (Leroi-Gourhan): gender pairing of animals, systematic placement
    - Shamanism / altered states (Lewis-Williams: entoptic phenomena, trance neuropsychological model)
    - Social signaling, territorial marking (Gamble, Soffer)
    - Cognitive fluidity (Mithen: The Prehistory of the Mind — merging of multiple intelligences)
  - Non-figurative marks: dots, lines, geometric signs, hand stencils (positive and negative)
  - Portable art: Venus figurines (~35 found across Eurasia, 35,000–22,000 BP); carved animals; decorated tools and blades

- **The Upper Palaeolithic of Europe and the "Aurignacian Expansion"**
  - Arrival of Cro-Magnons in Europe (~44,000–40,000 BP)
  - Rapid replacement or absorption of Neanderthals (gone by ~40,000 BP in most of Europe, ~28,000 BP in Iberia)
  - Gravettian mammoth-bone houses (Ukraine, Russia: Mezhyrich ~15,000 BP)
  - Dolní Věstonice (Czech Republic, ~26,000 BP): ceramic figurines — oldest known fired clay objects
  - Venus of Willendorf (Austria, ~28,000 BP): iconic Gravettian figurine

- **Later Hunter-Gatherer Societies Worldwide**
  - Africa
    - San / Bushmen: oldest continuous cultural lineage; rock art (Drakensberg; Tsodilo Hills — "Louvre of the Desert"); click language linguistics; present-day last direct link
    - Khoikhoi, pygmy groups, Hadza (Tanzania) — minimal-impact foragers studied anthropologically
  - Europe — Mesolithic (~12,000–5,000 BP)
    - Star Carr (Yorkshire, UK, ~9,000 BP): red deer skull headdresses, barbed antler points
    - Lepenski Vir (Serbia, ~7,000 BP): semi-sedentary fisher-gatherers; carved stone heads; unusual architecture
    - Shell middens (Ertebølle culture, Denmark): complex sedentary foragers
    - Cheddar Man (Somerset, UK, ~9,100 BP): dark skin, blue eyes — early DNA-phenotype reconstruction (2018)
  - Australia
    - Madjedbebe (Northern Territory, ~65,000 BP; Clarkson et al., 2017): ochre, grinding stones — challenges 50,000 BP consensus
    - Mungo Man and Mungo Lady (Lake Mungo, NSW, ~40,000 BP): ritual burial, ochre
    - Rock art: Murujuga/Burrup Peninsula (oldest at ~40,000–50,000 BP); Kimberley region (Bradshaw/Gwion Gwion figures, ~12,000+ BP)
    - Aboriginal Australians: unbroken cultural continuity for ~50,000+ years; oral traditions as oldest ongoing cultural knowledge; sophisticated land management (fire-stick farming)
  - Americas — first peoples
    - Clovis culture (~13,000 BP): Clovis points; Blackwater Draw, New Mexico; associated with megafauna kill sites
    - Pre-Clovis: see Out of Africa dispersals above; White Sands (~21,000–23,000 BP), Monte Verde (~14,600 BP)
    - Kennewick Man / Ancient One (Washington, 8,500 BP): aDNA confirms Native American ancestry (2015); repatriation debate
    - Paleo-Indian site: Meadowcroft Rockshelter (Pennsylvania, possibly ~19,000 BP — contested)
  - Asia
    - Natufian culture (Levant, ~14,500–11,500 BP): sedentary before agriculture; early use of wild cereals; round stone structures; incipient cemeteries
    - Jōmon (Japan, ~14,000–400 BCE): world's oldest pottery (~16,000 BP, Xianrendong Cave, China; ~14,000 BP Jōmon); rich symbolic culture; sedentary foragers
    - Late Stone Age India: Bhimbetka rock shelters (MP, India) — art from ~30,000 BP; Neolithic transition complex

- **Megafauna Extinction Debate**
  - Pleistocene megafauna: mammoths, mastodons, woolly rhinos, cave bears, cave lions, giant ground sloths, diprotodons, moa, haast's eagle
  - Three explanatory hypotheses
    - Overkill/blitzkrieg (Paul Martin, 1966): human hunting as primary cause — supported by chronological correlation in Americas and Australia
    - Climate change: glacial-interglacial cycles; vegetation change
    - Synergistic/combined model: most current support — human predation + habitat loss from climate change
  - Woolly mammoth last refugia: Wrangel Island, Siberia (~4,000 BP) — survived into dynastic Egypt era
  - De-extinction debate: Colossal Biosciences (George Church) — CRISPR-based mammoth revival program; ethical dimensions

- **The Neolithic Revolution: Origins of Farming (c. 12,000–5,000 BP)**
  - What changed and why it matters
    - Shift from mobile foraging to sedentary agriculture and pastoralism
    - Population growth, surplus, social complexity, disease, inequality, warfare
    - V. Gordon Childe's "Neolithic Revolution" concept (1925, 1936) — foundational but oversimplified
  - Origins — polyphyletic, independent invention in multiple centers
    - Fertile Crescent (~10,200 BCE): emmer wheat, einkorn, barley, lentils, peas; cattle, sheep, goats, pigs; Ain Ghazal, Jericho, 'Ain Mallaha
      - Pre-Pottery Neolithic A (PPNA, ~10,200–8,800 BCE) and B (PPNB, ~8,800–6,500 BCE)
      - Göbekli Tepe (Turkey, ~9,600 BCE; Klaus Schmidt, 1995–2014): T-shaped stone pillars with relief carvings — monumental ritual architecture BEFORE settled agriculture; overturns "civilization first" model; world's oldest known monumental structure
      - Karahan Tepe and Taş Tepeler (Turkey, ongoing excavations ~2021–present): entire ritual landscape predating agriculture — Klaus Schmidt's legacy continued
    - China (~7,000 BCE): millet (Yellow River basin, Yangshao culture) + rice (Yangtze River basin, Hemudu/Kuahuqiao culture)
