    - New Guinea highlands (~8,000–6,500 BCE): taro, bananas, yams — independent; Kuk Swamp evidence
    - Sub-Saharan Africa: sorghum, pearl millet, teff (multiple centers, ~5,000–4,000 BCE)
    - Mesoamerica (~8,000–5,000 BCE): maize (teosinte domestication, Balsas region) + squash + beans ("Three Sisters")
    - Andes (~8,000 BCE): potatoes, quinoa, llama, guinea pig
    - Eastern North America (~4,000–3,000 BCE): sunflower, goosefoot, marsh elder — independent domestication
  - The domestication process
    - Unconscious selection: humans selecting for non-shattering rachis, bigger seeds, reduced dormancy
    - Animal domestication: dog first (~15,000–40,000 BP, multiple events; latest: Bonn-Oberkassel dog burial ~14,000 BP); then sheep/goat, cattle, pig, horse (~3,500 BCE, Botai culture, Kazakhstan)
    - Genetic evidence of domestication: population bottlenecks in crop and animal genomes
  - Consequences of agriculture
    - Nutritional decline: reduced dietary diversity, shorter stature, dental decay, nutritional deficiencies (Iron Age skulls)
    - Epidemic disease: zoonosis from domesticated animals (smallpox, measles, influenza)
    - Social stratification: surplus → storage → chiefs → warfare
    - Human microbiome shift: from fiber-rich hunter-gatherer diet
    - Demographic explosion: Neolithic demographic transition (NDT)
  - Spread of farming
    - Europe: Anatolian farmers migrate ~7,000 BCE; LBK culture (Linear Band Keramik, ~5,500 BCE, Central Europe); aDNA confirms replacement of European hunter-gatherers (Bramanti et al., 2009; Haak et al., 2015)
    - Africa: Bantu expansion (~3,000 BCE–1,000 CE)
    - Americas: maize agriculture spreads northward, reaching SW USA ~2,000 BCE and Eastern Woodlands ~1,000 CE

- **Advanced & Specialist Topics**
  - Paleodemography
    - Methods: skeletal age-at-death, dental wear, Harris lines, isotopes
    - Life tables, paleodemographic reconstruction
    - Forager vs. farmer health comparison (skeletal stress markers)
  - Bioarchaeology
    - Stable isotope analysis (δ¹³C, δ¹⁵N, δ¹⁸O, ⁸⁷Sr/⁸⁶Sr): diet, migration, weaning, climate
    - Palaeopathology: evidence of violence, infection, healed fractures, cancer in ancient bones
    - Violence prehistory: Nataruk (Kenya, ~10,000 BP; Lahr et al., 2016) — massacre site; questions peaceful pre-state narrative
  - Landscape archaeology and environmental reconstruction
    - Palynology (pollen analysis): vegetation change, deforestation, agriculture onset
    - Phytolith analysis: plant microremains in sediment
    - Zooarchaeology: faunal assemblages; NISP and MNI calculations; taphonomy
    - Geoarchaeology: sediment micromorphology, stratigraphy
    - LiDAR revolution: Caracol (Belize, 2010 — first major LiDAR survey of Maya landscape); Angkor Wat environs; Amazon geoglyphs (Acre, Brazil ~2010s); Oaxaca region (2024 surveys revealing new sites); La Corona, Guatemala
  - Cognitive archaeology
    - Material evidence for theory of mind, planning depth, symbolic thought
    - Thomas Wynn & Frederick Coolidge: working memory and Acheulean hand axes
    - Merlin Donald: stages of cognitive evolution (episodic → mimetic → mythic → theoretic)
  - Archaeogenomics (frontier)
    - Whole-genome ancient DNA enabling reconstruction of migrations, admixture events, population size changes
    - "Ghost populations" detected only in DNA signal
    - Tools: ADMIXTURE, HAPLOGREP, BEAST, TreeMix, D-statistics (Patterson's D)
    - The 1000 Genomes Project and its ancient DNA companion projects
    - Frontiers: ancient pathogens in aDNA (plague, hepatitis B, *Yersinia pestis* in Bronze Age samples)
  - Niche construction theory
    - Humans as ecosystem engineers; coevolution with domesticated species and cultivated environments
    - Odling-Smee, Laland, Feldman: niche construction alters selective environments
  - Ongoing excavations and discoveries (2020s)
    - Karahan Tepe and Taş Tepeler complex (southeast Turkey, 2021–present): expanding the Göbekli ritual world
    - Dragon Man / *Homo longi* (China, 2021; Ji et al.): ~146,000 BP skull; possibly closer to *H. sapiens* than Neanderthals; challenges Asian hominin taxonomy
    - Nesher Ramla *Homo* (Israel, 2021): ~120,000–140,000 BP archaic morphology; potential source of Neanderthal gene flow into early modern humans in Levant
    - Tam Pà Ling cave (Laos): ~68,000–86,000 BP modern human fossils — evidence of early *H. sapiens* in mainland Southeast Asia (2023)
    - Extensive rock art sites discovered via drone/satellite in Arabia (2021–2024): Hima and ancient camel sculptures, Saudi Arabia (~8,000 BP)
    - White Sands National Park (USA, 2021): trackways in hardened mud dated ~21,000–23,000 BP — reopened Americas-peopling debate
    - aDNA from southern Africa now accessible due to new methods (Skoglund et al.) — pre-Bantu populations characterized

- **Frontier & Contemporary Research Directions**
  - Palaeoproteomics: protein analysis from ancient teeth enamel survives longer than DNA; *Homo antecessor* proteins show it as sister group to Neanderthal-*sapiens* clade (Welker et al., 2020)
  - Sedimentary ancient DNA (sedaDNA): extracting hominin DNA from cave sediments without skeletal finds (Slon et al., 2017; Vernot et al., 2021 — identifying Denisovan occupations at Baishiya Karst Cave via sedaDNA)
  - Multispecies ethnography and deep-time anthropology: rethinking human uniqueness
  - The deep ancestry of language: does the ~70,000 BP bottleneck relate to language emergence?
  - Debating the Sapient Paradox (Colin Renfrew): why 40,000 years between modern anatomy and behavioral modernity?
  - Computational modeling of prehistoric migrations (agent-based models, ABC methods)
  - Ethics of ancient DNA: NAGPRA repatriation law (USA), consent for indigenous aDNA (Tsosie et al., 2021), decolonizing archaeogenomics
  - Climate and human evolution nexus: pulsed variability hypothesis (Potts); African humid periods (green Sahara) enabling migrations
  - The Toba catastrophe: super-eruption ~74,000 BP (Sumatra) — population bottleneck hypothesis (Ambrose, 1998) now challenged by survival evidence in India (Petraglia et al., 2007) and South Africa; debate ongoing
  - Quantum archaeology (speculative frontier): theoretical reconstruction of past events from physical traces — at conceptual boundary of science

#### Ancient History — Mesopotamia & the First Cities

- **Basics: The Mesopotamian World**
  - Mesopotamia: Greek for "land between the rivers" — Tigris and Euphrates
  - Geographic scope: modern Iraq, northeastern Syria, southeastern Turkey, southwestern Iran
  - Why here first? Annual river flooding → silt deposits → highly fertile alluvial plain → food surplus → population density → specialization → cities
  - Key ecological zones: southern alluvium (Sumer), northern rain-fed agriculture (Assyria/Akkad), piedmont (Zagros foothills), Syrian steppe
  - Primary scholarly disciplines: Assyriology, archaeology, epigraphy, cuneiform studies
  - Key institutions: British Museum, Oriental Institute (Chicago), Vorderasiatisches Museum (Berlin), Iraq Museum (Baghdad), Louvre
  - Core chronological framework
    - Ubaid period (~6,500–3,800 BCE): first temple towns, pottery, irrigation
    - Uruk period (~4,000–3,100 BCE): first cities, first writing, first monumental architecture
    - Jemdet Nasr period (~3,100–2,900 BCE): proto-literate, cylinder seals
    - Early Dynastic period (~2,900–2,350 BCE): city-states, warfare, Gilgamesh cycle
    - Akkadian Empire (~2,334–2,154 BCE): first empire
    - Third Dynasty of Ur / Ur III (~2,112–2,004 BCE): bureaucratic renaissance
    - Old Babylonian (~2,004–1,595 BCE): Hammurabi's Code
    - Kassite Babylonia (~1,595–1,155 BCE)
    - Middle and Neo-Assyrian Empire (~1,350–609 BCE)
    - Neo-Babylonian Empire (~626–539 BCE): Nebuchadnezzar, Hanging Gardens, destruction of Jerusalem
    - Persian conquest: Cyrus the Great takes Babylon 539 BCE

- **Ubaid Culture (~6,500–3,800 BCE): The Proto-Urban Foundation**
  - Origins in southern Mesopotamia; spread across the entire ancient Near East
  - Eridu (Tell Abu Shahrain): considered by Sumerians their oldest city; excavated by Fuad Safar & Seton Lloyd (1946–1949); 18 superimposed temple levels — continuous sacred site from ~5,400 BCE
  - Ubaid pottery: distinctive painted geometric designs; distributed as far as the Persian Gulf coasts — evidence of early long-distance exchange
  - Irrigation agriculture: managing unpredictable river floods with canals, levees
  - Social hierarchy beginning: large houses vs. small houses in same settlements
  - T-shaped temples on raised platforms: precursor to ziggurats
  - Ubaid "lizard-head" figurines: distinctive elongated skull figures — purpose debated (religious? ethnic marker?)
  - "Ubaidization" of the Gulf: evidence of Ubaid contact with nomadic/fishing communities along Persian Gulf and Oman — Dalma Island, Kuwait, Qatar sites

- **The Uruk Period (~4,000–3,100 BCE): World's First Cities**
  - Uruk (modern Warka, Iraq): key site; excavated by Deutsche Orient-Gesellschaft from 1912; possibly largest city in the world by 3,000 BCE (~50,000–80,000 inhabitants)
  - Key architectural complexes at Uruk
    - Eanna precinct: temple complex of Inanna; White Temple on ziggurat; Limestone Temple; Cone Mosaic Temple (walls decorated with millions of clay cones)
    - Anu ziggurat: massive mudbrick platform
    - World's first monumental public architecture
  - The "Uruk Expansion" (~3,400–3,100 BCE)
    - Uruk trading colonies established across Anatolia, Syria, Iran: Habuba Kabira (Syria), Arslantepe (Turkey), Godin Tepe (Iran)
    - First "world-system"? (Algaze's Uruk World System thesis, 1993): extracting raw materials (metal, timber, stone) absent in southern alluvium
    - Debate: colonies vs. trade enclaves vs. migration
  - Invention of writing (~3,200 BCE)
    - Proto-cuneiform clay tablets; Uruk IV–III levels; W series tablets (Uruk): administrative records — grain, animals, personnel
    - Writing as accounting technology first: tokens → bullae → impressed tablets → incised signs
    - Earlier token system (~8,000–3,200 BCE): clay tokens for trade accounting (Denise Schmandt-Besserat's research — transformative; debated in detail)
    - First identifiable author: Kushim — a beer accountant named on Uruk tablets (~3,100 BCE); debated as first named individual
    - Simultaneous or near-simultaneous script inventors: Proto-Elamite (Susa, Iran) script; Egyptian hieroglyphics (~3,200 BCE) — independent invention vs. stimulus diffusion
    - Note: Vinča symbols (southeast Europe, ~5,500–4,000 BCE) and Tartaria tablets (~5,500 BCE, Romania) — predated Uruk; classified as proto-writing/symbols, not true script (contested)
    - Recent claim: Jiahu symbols (China, ~6,600 BCE) and Blombos ochre (~75,000 BP) — precludes simple priority claims
  - Cylinder seals: rolled to leave impressions on clay — signature, property mark, administrative control; unique art form and historical source
  - Uruk period legacy: urban life, writing, bureaucracy, monumental religion all originate here

- **Sumer and the Early Dynastic Period (~2,900–2,350 BCE)**
  - The Sumerian question: who were the Sumerians? Language isolate (unrelated to any known family) — arrived when? Possibly pre-Uruk; possibly Ubaid continuity; "Sumerian problem" unresolved
  - City-states system: Uruk, Ur, Eridu, Lagash, Nippur, Girsu, Umma, Kish, Shuruppak, Adab — each with own patron deity, temple, king
  - Kingship ideology: "kingship descended from heaven" — the Sumerian King List (Prism of Weld-Blundell, Ashmolean Museum); lists rulers before and after the Great Flood with impossibly long reigns
  - The Flood Tradition: Ziusudra (Sumerian), Utnapishtim (Akkadian Gilgamesh) — parallels to Genesis; Flood geology at Ur (Leonard Woolley, 1920s flood deposit) dated ~2,900 BCE; probably local event mythologized
  - Nippur: religious capital of all Sumer; Enlil's temple (Ekur); not politically dominant but religious legitimacy center — kings from all cities sought Nippur's sanction
  - War between city-states: Lagash vs. Umma border wars documented in "Stele of the Vultures" (~2,450 BCE, Louvre) — earliest known historical monument depicting warfare
  - Royal Standard of Ur (~2,600 BCE, British Museum): war and peace panels; mosaic of shell, lapis lazuli, red limestone — depicts army, banqueting, chariots — extraordinary social document
  - Royal Graves of Ur (Leonard Woolley, 1922–1934)
    - 1,800+ graves; 16 "Royal Tombs" with spectacular wealth: gold helmets, lyres, jewelry
    - "Death Pits": attendants sacrificed to accompany royalty — up to 74 bodies in one pit
    - Queen Puabi (c. 2,600 BCE): intact burial; gold headdress, lapis lazuli; possibly queen or high priestess
    - Gold harp/lyre with bull-headed soundbox
    - Ram in a Thicket (actually a goat, c. 2,600–2,400 BCE) — iconic artifact
  - Lugal vs. en vs. ensi: different types of rulers; lugal = "great man" (king), en = lord/high priest, ensi = city governor
  - Enmebaragesi of Kish: earliest ruler confirmed by contemporary inscriptions (~2,600 BCE)
  - Gilgamesh of Uruk: semi-legendary; listed in King List; now thought to be historical (~2,700 BCE); his city wall Uruk confirmed archaeologically
  - Early Dynastic art: votive statues (Tell Asmar hoard); "Warka Vase" (Uruk, ~3,200–3,000 BCE) — alabaster narrative ritual vessel — among world's oldest narrative art

- **The Invention and Evolution of Writing (Cuneiform)**
  - Cuneiform ("wedge-shaped"): impressed with reed stylus on wet clay
  - Evolution: pictographic → logographic → syllabic → alphabetic borrowings
  - Sumerian language: agglutinative, ergative-absolutive morphology; no living relatives
  - Akkadian language: Semitic; borrowed cuneiform from Sumerians; became ancient Near East's lingua franca
  - Other languages in cuneiform: Elamite, Hittite, Hurrian, Ugaritic (adapted to alphabet), Luwian, Urartian, Old Persian
  - Decipherment history
    - Grotefend (1802): first decipherment of Old Persian cuneiform
    - Henry Rawlinson: Behistun Inscription (Darius I, 519 BCE) — the "Rosetta Stone of cuneiform"; trilingual (Old Persian, Elamite, Babylonian)
    - Rawlinson and Edward Hincks cracked Babylonian by 1850s
    - Edward Hincks identified Sumerian as separate language (1850s)
  - Content of cuneiform tablets (~500,000 known tablets; only fraction published)
    - Administrative: rations, land, labor, livestock, trade
    - Literary: myths, epics, hymns, lamentations, proverbs
    - Legal: contracts, wills, adoption, debt tablets
    - Scientific: mathematical tablets, astronomical observations (ephemerides), medical diagnosis texts
    - Letters: thousands of letters between individuals, diplomats, kings — deeply human and personal
    - Lexical lists: school texts, sign lists, first dictionaries (Sumerian-Akkadian bilinguals)
  - The Eduba (Tablet House): Sumerian school system; scribal training documented in school texts ("A Schoolboy's Day" — most complete ancient school record)
  - Libraries: Ashurbanipal's Library at Nineveh (~668–627 BCE): 30,000+ tablets; first systematically collected library; discovered by Austin Henry Layard and Hormuzd Rassam (1849–1851); includes Gilgamesh epic, Enuma Elish, Atrahasis
  - Proto-Elamite script (~3,100–2,900 BCE): still largely undeciphered; 1,000+ tablets; possibly most numerous early script; decipherment efforts ongoing (2024)

- **The Epic of Gilgamesh**
  - Oldest major work of literature; 12 tablets; Standard Babylonian Version compiled ~1,200 BCE by Sin-leqi-unninni (first known named editor/author)
  - Earlier Sumerian poems: "Gilgamesh and Humbaba," "Gilgamesh and the Bull of Heaven," "Gilgamesh, Enkidu and the Underworld," "The Death of Gilgamesh"
  - Key themes: friendship (Gilgamesh and Enkidu), heroism, mortality, the futility of seeking immortality, the flood (Tablet XI)
  - Discovery of Flood Tablet: George Smith, British Museum, 1872 — caused international sensation; public lecture drew PM Gladstone
  - Mesopotamian flood tradition: Atrahasis Epic (~1,600 BCE) — even fuller flood narrative with Creation and flood as continuous story
  - Enkidu: created from clay by Aruru; civilized through sexual encounter with Shamhat (the harlot) — civilization vs. nature theme
  - Utnapishtim's flood: parallels Genesis in boat construction, birds sent out, resting on mountain; biblical scholarship implications
  - George Smith tablet fragment now in British Museum; copies in Istanbul, Baghdad, Louvre
  - The "Tavern Keeper Speech" (Siduri): "When the gods created mankind, they allotted death to mankind, but life they retained in their own keeping" — Mesopotamian perspective on mortality
  - Recent scholarship: Andrew George's critical edition (2003, 2 volumes) — definitive current reference

- **The Akkadian Empire (~2,334–2,154 BCE): The World's First Empire**
  - Sargon of Akkad (~2,334–2,279 BCE): founder; rose from cupbearer of king of Kish; conquered all of Sumer, Syria, possibly Anatolia and the "four quarters" of the world
  - Capital Akkad/Agade: location still unknown — searched for archaeologically without success
  - Legend of Sargon's birth: exposed as infant, saved by gardener, raised to power — parallels Moses, Romulus; "birth legend" genre
  - First truly imperial administration: appointed Akkadian governors over Sumerian cities; standardized weights and measures
  - Naram-Sin (~2,254–2,218 BCE): Sargon's grandson; called himself "God of Akkad" — first Mesopotamian ruler to claim divinity in life; Victory Stele of Naram-Sin (Louvre) — masterpiece of ancient art showing mountain campaign against Lullubi
  - Akkadian language spreads as administrative tongue across Near East
  - Collapse: Gutian invasions from Zagros mountains + internal rebellion; Akkadian empire falls ~2,154 BCE; debated role of "4.2 kiloyear event" (climate event ~2,200 BCE) — Harvey Weiss (Yale) drought hypothesis; now supported by geophysical data
  - The Curse of Akkade: Sumerian literary composition blaming Naram-Sin's hubris for the collapse — first literary "fall of empire" narrative

- **Third Dynasty of Ur / Ur III Empire (~2,112–2,004 BCE)**
  - Ur-Nammu (~2,112–2,095 BCE): founder; builder of the famous Ziggurat of Ur (best-preserved in Iraq; partly restored by Saddam Hussein 1980s)
  - Ur-Nammu Law Code: possibly older than Hammurabi's; earliest surviving law code (silver and barley fines, not "eye for eye")
  - Shulgi (~2,094–2,047 BCE): most celebrated Ur III king; ran empire 48 years; claims to be a god; standardized the entire Mesopotamian administrative and scribal system
  - Ur III bureaucracy: most thoroughly documented ancient economy; tens of thousands of tablets recording every ox, laborer, grain ration
  - Collapse: Amorite invasions from the west, Elamite attack from east; 2004 BCE the Elamites sack Ur and carry its last king captive — commemorated in Lamentations of Ur (literary masterpiece)

- **Old Babylonian Period (~2,004–1,595 BCE): Law, Commerce, and Empire**
  - Amorite dynasties: tribal groups became kings in all major cities; Isin-Larsa rivalry; Mari kingdom on Euphrates
  - Mari (Tell Hariri, Syria): excavated by André Parrot from 1933; palace of Zimri-Lim (~1,775–1,761 BCE) — 300 rooms; 25,000 tablets including diplomatic archive and private letters; destroyed by Hammurabi
  - Hammurabi of Babylon (~1,792–1,750 BCE): conquered all of Mesopotamia; created short-lived Babylonian empire
  - Code of Hammurabi: 282 laws carved on 2.25m black diorite stele (discovered Susa, 1901; Louvre); not the oldest code but most complete; divine authority from Shamash sun-god
    - Content: property, commerce, family law, wages, fees for services, false accusation, adoption
    - Famous lex talionis passages: "if a man puts out the eye of another man, his eye shall be put out"
    - Actually more nuanced: penalties vary by social class (free man, freedman, slave)
    - Precursors: Ur-Nammu code, Lipit-Ishtar code, Laws of Eshnunna
  - Old Babylonian trade: the Old Assyrian Trade Network (Kültepe/Karum Kanesh, Anatolia): Assyrian merchant families ran enormous long-distance trade in tin and textiles; thousands of tablets (kārum = trading post) — most documented ancient private commercial enterprise
  - Enheduanna (~2,285–2,250 BCE): daughter of Sargon; High Priestess of Nanna at Ur; first named author in history (predates Kushim question); composed Hymn to Inanna ("Lady of Largest Heart") and In-nin-sa-gura — poetry of extraordinary power
  - Old Babylonian mathematics: Plimpton 322 tablet (~1,800 BCE, Columbia University): Pythagorean triples — evidence of advanced mathematics 1,000+ years before Pythagoras; debate whether trigonometric table (Mansfield & Wildberger, 2017) or standard problem set
  - Yale Babylonian Collection, Schøyen Collection: key repositories of tablets
  - Kassite conquest (~1,595 BCE): Hittites sack Babylon; Kassites (mountain people, unknown origin) fill vacuum and rule Babylonia ~400 years as feudal dynasty

- **Assyria: The Military Empire (~900–609 BCE)**
  - Origins: Ashur city-state on the Tigris; patron deity Ashur; Old Assyrian trading network (above)
  - Middle Assyrian period (~1,350–1,050 BCE): Tukulti-Ninurta I (~1,244–1,208 BCE) — first king to conquer Babylon
  - Neo-Assyrian Empire (~911–609 BCE): largest empire the ancient world had yet seen
  - Key kings
    - Ashurnasirpal II (~883–859 BCE): extreme cruelty documented in own annals; new capital Kalhu (Nimrud); Northwest Palace — first Assyrian palace with massive relief sculptures; banquet stele documenting feast for 69,574 guests at palace dedication
    - Shalmaneser III (~858–824 BCE): Battle of Qarqar (853 BCE) — first mention of Israel in Assyrian records (Ahab of Israel in coalition against Assyria); Black Obelisk shows Jehu of Israel paying tribute
    - Tiglath-Pileser III (~745–727 BCE): administrative reform; mass deportations as policy; first professional standing army
    - Sargon II (~722–705 BCE): conquered Samaria 722 BCE; destroyed Kingdom of Israel; deported 27,290 Israelites ("Lost Tribes"); Dur-Sharrukin (Khorsabad) — new capital; Paul-Émile Botta excavated 1843 — first major Mesopotamian excavation
    - Sennacherib (~705–681 BCE): destroyed Babylon 689 BCE (shocking to Mesopotamians); Nineveh new capital; siege of Jerusalem (701 BCE) — documented in Taylor Prism and Bible (2 Kings 18–19); 185,000 Assyrian soldiers struck dead (divine intervention — possibly plague, or legendary); Lachish reliefs at British Museum
    - Esarhaddon (~681–669 BCE): rebuilt Babylon; conquered Egypt 671 BCE; most extensive empire yet
    - Ashurbanipal (~668–627 BCE): sacked Thebes (663 BCE); collected the great library of Nineveh; last great Assyrian king; lion hunt reliefs — pinnacle of Assyrian art
  - Assyrian art: colossal human-headed bull/lion guardians (lamassu); narrative stone relief panels; deportation scenes; siege warfare documentation; lion hunt sequence
  - Deportation policy: 4+ million people forcibly relocated across the empire — created multi-ethnic imperial subjects; ended ethnic homelands; biblical resonance (Babylonian Captivity, Assyrian Captivity)
  - Fall of Assyria: Babylonian-Mede coalition; Nineveh falls 612 BCE; Harran 610 BCE; Carchemish 605 BCE — rapid total collapse; cities never reoccupied at scale
  - Why did Assyria fall? Overextension, palace instability, stretched military, Babylonian-Median alliance (no single cause)

- **Neo-Babylonian Empire (~626–539 BCE): Babylon at Its Zenith**
  - Nabopolassar (~626–605 BCE): founded dynasty; allied with Medes to destroy Assyria
  - Nebuchadnezzar II (~604–562 BCE): greatest Neo-Babylonian king
    - Defeated Egypt at Battle of Carchemish 605 BCE; dominated the entire Near East
    - Deportation of Jews: first (~597 BCE) and second (~586 BCE) deportations; destruction of Solomon's Temple; Babylonian Captivity — foundational event for Judaism
    - Rebuilt Babylon to greatest city in the ancient world: population possibly 150,000–200,000
    - Processional Way and Ishtar Gate (lapis lazuli glazed brick, dragon and bull reliefs; reconstructed in Pergamon Museum, Berlin)
    - Etemenanki ziggurat: possibly the historical inspiration for the Tower of Babel (Genesis 11); ~90m tall
    - Hanging Gardens: listed as one of Seven Wonders; ancient sources describe them; no positive archaeological identification at Babylon; Stephanie Dalley (Oxford) proposed they were actually at Nineveh (Sennacherib's garden) — ongoing debate
    - Babylon's layout: double walls (Herodotus — "chariots could turn on top"), Esagila temple complex of Marduk, paved roads
  - Nabonidus (~556–539 BCE): last king; devoted to moon god Sin; went to Tayma (Arabia) for 10 years; son Belshazzar regent ("Belshazzar's Feast," Daniel 5 — historically confirmed name)
  - Cyrus the Great of Persia: conquers Babylon 539 BCE without battle; issues Cyrus Cylinder (British Museum) — first "declaration of human rights"? (modern claim; actually standard Mesopotamian royal propaganda, but notable for permitting return of exiled peoples)
  - Persian rule: Mesopotamia becomes satrapy of Persia; cuneiform continues; Babylon remains major city under Alexander the Great (died there 323 BCE)

- **Mesopotamian Religion and Cosmology**
  - Polytheistic pantheon: hundreds of deities; each city has patron deity
  - Major deities
    - An (Sumerian) / Anu (Akkadian): sky god, king of gods
    - Enlil: wind/breath god; lord of earth; gave kingship; terrifying; destroyed humanity with flood (Atrahasis)
    - Enki/Ea: god of wisdom, fresh water, magic, crafts; protector of humanity; tricks other gods to save humans
    - Inanna (Sumerian) / Ishtar (Akkadian): love, war, sex, fertility; most complex deity of ancient world; "Descent of Inanna" — journey to underworld; hymns by Enheduanna
    - Nanna/Sin: moon god; patron of Ur
    - Utu/Shamash: sun god; justice, law
    - Marduk: patron of Babylon; elevated to king of gods in Babylonian theology (Enuma Elish)
    - Nergal: god of death, plague, underworld
    - Ninlil, Ninhursag, Ereshkigal (queen of underworld), Tiamat (primordial chaos)
  - Cosmological texts
    - Enuma Elish ("When on high…"): Babylonian creation epic; Marduk slays Tiamat and creates world from her body; humans made from blood of slain god Kingu to serve gods; recited at Akitu (New Year) festival
    - Atrahasis Epic: creation + flood in one narrative; humans too noisy, gods send flood to reduce population
    - Descent of Inanna/Ishtar: Inanna descends through seven gates to underworld; parallels to persephone myth and resurrection symbolism
  - Temple (ziggurat) economy: temples were economic engines — land, labor, livestock, workshops
  - Divination practices: extispicy (reading sheep livers — clay liver models found), astrology (first systematic; MUL.APIN compendium ~1,000 BCE), dream interpretation, oil on water (lecanomancy), smoke (libanomancy), bird behavior (ornithomancy)
  - Babylonian astronomy: systematic observational records from ~800 BCE; predict eclipses using Saros cycle; calculate planetary periods (Venus, Jupiter, Saturn, Mars); ephemeris texts; zodiac system (12 signs) invented ~400 BCE; direct precursor of Greek astronomy
  - Medicine: diagnostic texts (Sa-gig Series — 40 tablet canonical compendium); two types of healer: āšipu (exorcist, magical) and asû (physician, herbal); rational and magical intertwined; knowledge of many diseases, drugs
  - Law: not secular — divine authority; king as intermediary of divine justice; widows, orphans, the weak nominally protected

- **Daily Life, Society, and Economy**
  - Agriculture: barley primary grain (wheat too salt-sensitive in south); sesame, dates; irrigation by gravity canals; labor organized by temples and palaces
  - Trade: Mesopotamia lacks metal, timber, stone — imports everything via trade; exports textiles, grain, crafted goods
  - Social hierarchy: king → nobles/officials → free citizens → debt-servants → slaves; women could own property, run businesses (Old Babylonian period especially); female scribes attested
  - Marriage and family law: contracts; bride price and dowry; divorce possible (more easily by husband); widows protected
  - Beer: ubiquitous; "Hymn to Ninkasi" (~1,800 BCE) — world's oldest beer recipe; rations paid in beer and bread
  - Urban layout: Babylon's streets, markets, residential quarters documented; ground plans recovered archaeologically
  - Education: scribal schools (edubba); elite literacy; standardized curriculum survives in lexical lists
  - Money: no coins until Persian period; silver weighed by shekel standard; barley used for daily transactions; credit and interest documented (interest on loans ~20% — regulated by Hammurabi)

- **Advanced & Specialist Topics**
  - Assyriology methodology
    - Cuneiform reading: 600+ signs; complex sign values; requires specialist training; major dictionaries: CAD (Chicago Assyrian Dictionary, 21 volumes, completed 2010) and AHw (Akkadisches Handwörterbuch)
    - CDLI (Cuneiform Digital Library Initiative): online database of 350,000+ tablets; open access; transforming the field
    - Tablet forgeries: market for unprovenanced tablets (Hobby Lobby / Museum of the Bible scandal — smuggled Iraqi tablets 2019-2021)
  - The Looting Crisis
    - Gulf War 1991: Baghdad Museum looting
    - Iraq War 2003: Iraq Museum looting (15,000 objects stolen; many recovered)
    - ISIS/ISIL destructions (2014–2017): Nimrud, Nineveh, Hatra — deliberate iconoclasm; also systematic looting for sale
    - International law: UNESCO 1970 Convention on cultural property; 1954 Hague Convention
  - Archaeoacoustics and the Nippur sacred landscape (ongoing research)
  - Computational approaches: AI cuneiform tablet joining; algorithmic sign recognition; MTAAC project (UCLA)
  - The "4.2 kiloyear event" and societal collapse
    - Climate event ~2,200 BCE: prolonged drought across much of the Northern Hemisphere
    - Harvey Weiss (Yale): linked to collapse of Akkadian Empire, Egyptian Old Kingdom, Indus Valley phase shift, Chinese Neolithic transitions
    - Evidence: Tell Leilan (Syria) abandonment; dust in deep-sea cores; speleothems (cave deposits); pollen records
    - Debate: is the correlation causation? Were societies already stressed? How regional was the drought?

- **Frontier & Contemporary Research**
  - ASOR Cultural Heritage Initiatives: documenting and recovering looted Iraqi sites post-2003 and post-ISIS
  - LiDAR and satellite archaeology: mapping ancient canal systems; identifying unexcavated sites across Iraq (many thousands still unexcavated); CORONA satellite imagery (declassified 1990s) reveals pre-looting landscapes
  - Nippur environs survey (Oriental Institute): canal networks, settlement patterns across millennia
  - Southern Iraq marshlands (Ahwar): UNESCO World Heritage 2016; ancient landscape now partially restored; Eden-like landscape proposed as Garden of Eden setting
  - Erbil Citadel (Iraqi Kurdistan): continuously inhabited for 6,000+ years; UNESCO World Heritage; excavations revealing Assyrian and earlier layers
  - Girsu-Lagash Project (British Museum & Iraqi SBAH): 2022–present; major new excavations at Temple of Ningirsu; cuneiform archive recovery
  - Digital Hammurabi: high-resolution imaging of cuneiform tablets; multispectral imaging recovering erased signs
  - Repatriation of tablets to Baghdad: Cornell University, Yale, British Museum ongoing returns of tablets legally acquired but now recognized as looted
  - Understanding Proto-Elamite: computational approaches to decipherment (still unsolved as of 2026)
  - Genetics of ancient Mesopotamians: tell Arpachiyah aDNA; Chalcolithic Zagros; Bronze Age Iraq — population histories being reconstructed
  - Archaeoastronomy: reconstructing Babylonian observational programs; testing predictions of eclipse records against astronomical software (Ossendrijver, Steele)
  - New mathematical tablet analysis: Friberg (2007), Robson (2008): reinterpreting Old Babylonian mathematical curriculum; challenge to "discovery of Pythagorean theorem" claim for Plimpton 322

#### Ancient History — Mesopotamia & the First Cities — Key Figures
- Enheduanna (~2,285–2,250 BCE): first named author in history; High Priestess; poet
- Sargon of Akkad (~2,334 BCE): first empire-builder
- Shulgi of Ur III: greatest bureaucrat-king of ancient world
- Hammurabi (~1,792–1,750 BCE): law code, Babylonian empire
- Ashurbanipal (~668–627 BCE): library-builder; last great Assyrian king
- Nebuchadnezzar II (~604–562 BCE): destroys Jerusalem; Babylon at zenith
- George Smith (1840–1876): discovered Flood Tablet; newspaper-sensation archaeology
- Henry Rawlinson (1810–1895): deciphered cuneiform via Behistun Inscription
- Leonard Woolley (1880–1960): excavated Royal Graves of Ur
- André Parrot (1901–1980): excavated Mari
- Paul-Émile Botta (1802–1870): first excavator of Assyrian palace (Khorsabad)
- Austen Henry Layard (1817–1894): Nineveh, Nimrud — foundational Assyriology
- Andrew George (contemporary): definitive Gilgamesh edition
- Harvey Weiss (contemporary): climate collapse hypothesis
- Eleanor Robson (contemporary): Babylonian mathematics scholarship
- Francesca Rochberg (contemporary): Babylonian astronomy and science studies

#### Ancient History — Ancient Egypt: Predynastic through New Kingdom

- **Basics: Geography as Destiny**
  - The Nile Valley: ~6,650 km river; longest in the world; runs north through desert to Mediterranean
  - "Gift of the Nile" (Herodotus): annual inundation deposits rich black silt (kemet = "black land") on a narrow strip averaging 10–20 km wide; flanked by red desert (deshret)
  - Upper Egypt: Nile Valley (south, upstream — counterintuitive to modern maps); Lower Egypt: the Delta (north)
  - Geographic isolation: deserts east and west, sea north, cataracts south — natural defenses; fostered stable, insular civilization
  - The Nile as highway: almost all transport by boat; no roads needed; unified administration possible
  - Agricultural calendar: Akhet (inundation, June–September), Peret (planting/growing, October–February), Shemu (harvest, March–May)
  - Resources: fertile soil, papyrus, fish, wildlife; lacked timber (import from Lebanon), metals (trade/conquest for gold, copper, turquoise, lapis lazuli)
  - Chronological framework
    - Predynastic (~5,000–3,100 BCE)
    - Early Dynastic / Protodynastic (Dynasties 1–2, ~3,100–2,686 BCE)
    - Old Kingdom (Dynasties 3–6, ~2,686–2,181 BCE): Age of Pyramids
    - First Intermediate Period (Dynasties 7–11, ~2,181–2,055 BCE)
    - Middle Kingdom (Dynasties 11–13, ~2,055–1,650 BCE)
    - Second Intermediate Period / Hyksos (Dynasties 14–17, ~1,650–1,550 BCE)
    - New Kingdom (Dynasties 18–20, ~1,550–1,069 BCE): Empire; Tutankhamun; Akhenaten; Ramesses
    - Third Intermediate Period (Dynasties 21–25, ~1,069–664 BCE)
    - Late Period (Dynasties 26–31, ~664–332 BCE)
    - Ptolemaic Period (332–30 BCE)
    - Roman Egypt (30 BCE–395 CE)
  - Key Egyptologists: Jean-François Champollion (decipherment), Flinders Petrie (stratigraphy), Howard Carter (Tutankhamun), Zahi Hawass (contemporary), Kara Cooney (gender, death), Salima Ikram (mummification), Mark Lehner (Giza), Egyptological institutions: Egypt Exploration Society, IFAO (French), DAI (German), Oriental Institute

- **Predynastic Egypt (~5,000–3,100 BCE): Before the Pharaohs**
  - Badarian culture (~5,000–4,000 BCE): earliest Nile Valley farming culture; fine brown pottery with rippled surface; copper use; burials with grave goods; Badari region (Middle Egypt)
  - Naqada (Nagada) culture (~4,400–3,100 BCE): three phases
    - Naqada I (Amratian, ~4,400–3,500 BCE): painted pottery with geometric and figurative designs; stone palettes; female figurines
    - Naqada II (Gerzean, ~3,500–3,200 BCE): large settlements; long-distance trade (lapis lazuli from Afghanistan, obsidian from Ethiopia); cylinder seal imports from Mesopotamia; "Decorated Ware" pottery with boats and standards — earliest Egyptian iconography
    - Naqada III (~3,200–3,100 BCE): proto-states; large elite tombs; hieroglyphs emerging; Abydos tomb U-j (~3,150 BCE) — oldest Egyptian writing (labels, possibly king "Scorpion 0")
  - Hierakonpolis (Nekhen): largest Predynastic city; pottery kilns; the Painted Tomb (~3,200 BCE) — oldest painted tomb in Egypt; the Main Deposit (found 1898): Narmer Palette, Scorpion Macehead, ivory figurines
  - Abydos: most important Predynastic and early Dynastic site; cemetery of early rulers
  - Merimda Beni Salama (Delta, ~4,800–4,200 BCE): earliest known village in Egypt; Northern (Lower) Egyptian culture — separately developing from Upper Egyptian Naqada
  - Maadi culture (Lower Egypt, ~3,600–3,000 BCE): trade hub with Levant/Canaan; copper smelting
  - Unification of Egypt: long process; Upper Egypt gradually absorbed Lower Egypt; "Two Lands" symbolism pervades pharaonic ideology
  - The "Followers of Horus" (Shemsu Hor): legendary semi-divine rulers before Menes in Egyptian tradition — possibly reflecting memory of late Predynastic rulers

- **Protodynastic & Early Dynastic (~3,100–2,686 BCE): State Formation**
  - Narmer (~3,100 BCE): first king to rule unified Egypt? Narmer Palette (Cairo Museum) — ceremonial greywacke palette ~63 cm; both sides show king smiting enemies, wearing both crowns; earliest royal narrative monument; found at Hierakonpolis 1898 (James Quibell)
  - "Menes": traditional founder of unified Egypt in later lists (Manetho); possibly = Narmer, or his successor Aha, or composite figure
  - Scorpion I and Scorpion II: pre-unification Upper Egyptian rulers; Scorpion Macehead shows ritual/warfare scenes
  - Manetho (~3rd century BCE): Egyptian priest who wrote history of Egypt in Greek for Ptolemy II; divided rulers into 30 dynasties (structure still used today — no original survives, only excerpts in later authors)
  - Abydos royal cemetery: tombs of Dynasty 1–2 kings; excavated by Flinders Petrie (1899–1900) and Émile Amélineau; re-excavated by Günter Dreyer (German Institute, 1985–2017); still ongoing
    - Dog and lion burials around royal tombs; servant/retainer sacrifice in early Dynasty 1 (diminishes by Dynasty 2)
    - Tomb Uj (~3,150 BCE): ~400 small clay labels with signs — oldest Egyptian writing (Dreyer's claim, 1998); debate whether truly writing or administrative marks
  - Memphis: traditional capital founded by Menes/Narmer at apex of Delta; remains under modern Cairo — minimally excavated
  - Saqqara: necropolis of Memphis; enormous mastaba tombs (bench-shaped) of early officials; some possibly real royal tombs (Emery's large Dynasty 1 mastabas — Egyptologists debate whether kings buried at Abydos or Saqqara or both)
  - Writing development: hieroglyphic script (~3,100 BCE) — simultaneously formal monumental script and hieratic (cursive) developing alongside; logographic + phonetic signs; not directly derived from cuneiform (parallel invention or stimulus diffusion from Mesopotamia via trade contact — still debated)
  - Serekh: earliest royal name frame — palace façade topped by Horus falcon; later evolves into cartouche (oval ring enclosing royal name)
  - Key Early Dynastic developments: royal titulary (five-part name), administrative nomarchs (provincial governors), taxation, redistribution economy, canonical art style emerging

- **Old Kingdom (~2,686–2,181 BCE): The Age of Pyramids**
  - Political structure: divine kingship; pharaoh as living Horus, dead Osiris; intermediary between gods and humans; absolute authority but practically constrained by priests, officials
  - Dynasty 3 (~2,686–2,613 BCE)
    - Djoser (~2,667–2,648 BCE): first stone monumental architecture in history; Step Pyramid at Saqqara
    - Imhotep: architect, physician, sage; designed Step Pyramid complex; later deified; Greeks equated with Asclepius; Step Pyramid = first large-scale stone structure anywhere (~62 m high, six superimposed mastabas)
    - Step Pyramid complex: revolutionary — entire funerary city in stone; dummy buildings, serdab (statue chamber), Heb-Sed court
  - Dynasty 4 (~2,613–2,494 BCE): the classical pyramid age
    - Sneferu (~2,613–2,589 BCE): built three pyramids — Meidum Pyramid (converted from step to true pyramid, then partially collapsed), Bent Pyramid at Dahshur (angle changes midway — engineering adjustment), Red Pyramid at Dahshur (first fully successful true pyramid); more pyramid stone than any other pharaoh
    - Khufu / Cheops (~2,589–2,566 BCE): Great Pyramid of Giza
      - Original height: ~146.5 m (now ~138.5 m); base 230 m per side; ~2.3 million limestone blocks averaging 2.5 tonnes; total mass ~5.75 million tonnes
      - Constructed ~2,560 BCE; most precisely oriented large structure on earth — aligned to true north within 0.05 degrees
      - Interior: King's Chamber (granite); Queen's Chamber; Grand Gallery; relieving chambers above King's Chamber
      - Boat pits: Khufu Solar Boat (1954 discovery; 43.6 m; dismantled; largely reassembled; second boat being excavated in situ with Japanese technology)
      - Workers: not slaves — paid labor force; bakeries, breweries, medical facilities found at workers' village (Mark Lehner and Zahi Hawass, 1990s; AERA — Ancient Egypt Research Associates)
      - New shaft/void discoveries:
        - ScanPyramids project (2015–present): muon tomography, infrared thermography, 3D reconstruction
        - "Big Void" announced 2017 (Morishima et al., *Nature*): large cavity (~30 m) above Grand Gallery — purpose unknown
        - North Face Corridor: new corridor discovered 2023 (ScanPyramids + Egyptian Ministry of Antiquities): ~9 m corridor behind chevron blocks at north face entrance — announced February 2023; purpose still under investigation; possibly pressure-relieving, possibly original entrance passage
        - Ground-penetrating radar (GPR) surveys of plateau: ongoing; possible undiscovered chambers beneath
      - Pyramid complex: mortuary temple, causeway, valley temple, satellite pyramids (queens), queen burials
      - Khufu's only confirmed portrait: tiny 7.5 cm ivory figurine (Cairo Museum, 1903, Abydos)
    - Khafre (~2,558–2,532 BCE): second pyramid at Giza; slightly smaller but appears larger (higher ground); preserved casing stones at apex; Valley Temple (granite-lined, best preserved); Great Sphinx
    - The Great Sphinx
      - ~73 m long, ~20 m high; carved from single limestone outcrop with added blocks; human head on lion body; faces due east (equinox sunrise)
      - Traditionally attributed to Khafre (his face?); no contemporary inscription directly naming it; Thutmose IV's Dream Stele (~1,400 BCE) — first textual reference (as "Horem-akhet")
      - "Sphinx Water Erosion Hypothesis" (John Anthony West, Robert Schoch, 1990s): proposes water weathering on sphinx enclosure predates Khafre by thousands of years (possibly ~7,000–9,000 BCE) — rejected by mainstream Egyptology (erosion = wind/sand + chemical weathering, geologists broadly disagree with Schoch's reading, though debate not fully closed)
      - 2024 GPR survey (Eman Ghoneim et al.): possible ancient channel or geological feature beneath/beside Sphinx — ongoing interpretation
      - Sphinx temple and valley temple: limestone/granite; cult of solar worship
    - Menkaure (~2,532–2,503 BCE): third and smallest Giza pyramid; three subsidiary pyramids; unfinished; Menkaure triads (greywacke sculptures of king with deities — masterpieces)
    - Hetepheres I: mother of Khufu; tomb shaft found at Giza 1925 (Reisner); spectacular jewelry; empty sarcophagus — body missing; mystery unexplained
  - Dynasty 5 (~2,494–2,345 BCE)
    - Sun temples: Abu Ghurab (Niuserre's sun temple, best preserved); cult of Ra dominant
    - Sahure: Abusir pyramid complex; causeway reliefs — first detailed military/naval campaigns shown
    - Unas (~2,375–2,345 BCE): last Dynasty 5 king; smallest pyramid at Saqqara; interior walls — first Pyramid Texts (oldest religious texts in the world; spells for royal afterlife; ~228 utterances)
  - Dynasty 6 (~2,345–2,181 BCE)
    - Teti: first Dynasty 6 king; possibly assassinated (Manetho claims)
    - Pepi I: long reign; copper statue (oldest surviving large metal sculpture); Sinai expeditions; Byblos trade
    - Pepi II (~2,278–2,184 BCE): possibly longest reign in history (~94 years — debated); building quality declines; power devolving to nomarchs (provincial governors)
    - Autobiography of Weni: carved on limestone; describes military campaigns, canal building, court intrigue — key historical document of Old Kingdom
    - Autobiography of Harkhuf: four expeditions to Nubia/Yam; brought a dancing dwarf from Africa for young Pepi II (letter of excited king survives — one of most charming ancient documents)
  - Old Kingdom collapse (~2,181 BCE)
    - Decentralization: nomarchs accumulate power; elaborate provincial tombs replacing centralized pyramid fields
    - Climate: the 4.2 kiloyear event (~2,200 BCE) drought; failure of Nile floods; famine evidence in texts (Ankhtifi of Moalla boasts of feeding his province during famine)
    - Stele of Ipuwer ("Admonitions of Ipuwer"): literary text describing social chaos, inversion of order — "The servant is now master" — possibly composed during or after First Intermediate Period; debated relation to historical events
  - Old Kingdom art and architecture: canonical style established — hieratic frontality, register scenes, proportion grids; ka statues; mastaba and pyramid as resurrection machines; role of ka (vital force) and ba (personality-soul) developed

- **Middle Kingdom (~2,055–1,650 BCE): Reunification and Classical Culture**
  - First Intermediate Period (~2,181–2,055 BCE)
    - Fragmentation; multiple competing dynasties; local rulers; cultural creativity in provinces; more individualized art
    - Coffin Texts develop from Pyramid Texts — afterlife access democratized beyond royalty; painted coffins; spell collections
    - Herakleopolis (Dynasties 9–10) vs. Thebes (Dynasty 11) — civil war ending with Theban victory
  - Mentuhotep II (~2,055–2,004 BCE): reunifier; 51-year reign; mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri (later model for Hatshepsut's); "Great" — multiple statues show evolution of portraiture
  - Dynasty 12 (~1,985–1,795 BCE): classical age of Egyptian literature
    - Amenemhat I (founder; possibly assassinated; "Instruction of Amenemhat" — warns son from experience)
    - Senusret I: campaigns into Nubia (Second Cataract fortresses); White Chapel at Karnak (limestone, finest relief work)
    - Senusret III (~1,870–1,831 BCE): centralized power; broke nomarch system; campaigns into Nubia (definitive conquest to Semna); new realistic portrait style — brooding, careworn royal face; possibly the historical basis for the Sesostris legend (Herodotus)
    - Amenemhat III: intensive exploitation of Fayum; Hawara pyramid and mortuary temple ("Labyrinth" — marveled at by Herodotus and Strabo as wonder of the world; now largely destroyed)
  - Classical Middle Kingdom Literature
    - Story of Sinuhe: courtier flees to Canaan after Amenemhat I's death; lives abroad; eventual return; exploration of Egyptian identity, loyalty, mortality — most beloved ancient Egyptian narrative
    - Eloquent Peasant: peasant makes nine eloquent speeches to official for justice; celebrates rhetoric and ma'at
    - Tale of the Shipwrecked Sailor: adventure story; possible prototype of "Sinbad the Sailor"
    - Wisdom literature: Instructions of Ptahhotep, Instructions of Amenemhat — ethical and practical advice
    - "Dialogue of a Man and his Ba": debate about suicide and the afterlife — existential despair
  - Administration: vizier system; two viziers (Upper and Lower Egypt) from New Kingdom; Papyrus Bulaq 18 — administrative accounts
  - Fayum: hydraulic agricultural project; reservoir; canal linking Nile to Fayum depression; massive reclamation
  - Egypt-Nubia relations
    - Chain of mud-brick fortresses at Second Cataract (Buhen, Mirgissa, Semna, Kumma): military and trade control
    - "Execration Texts": pottery/figurines with names of enemies ritually smashed — magical diplomacy; lists Nubian chiefs, Asiatic rulers, Egyptians — important historical source
  - Lahun (Kahun): Middle Kingdom workers' village preserved; papyri found (medical, veterinary, mathematical, legal) — rare window into daily life; "Kahun gynaecological papyrus" — earliest known medical text focused on women's health
  - Mathematical papyri: Rhind Mathematical Papyrus (~1,650 BCE copy of ~1,850 BCE original; British Museum): 87 problems — fractions, geometry, algebra; calculates area of circle using π ≈ 3.16; Moscow Mathematical Papyrus (~1,850 BCE): includes calculation of volume of truncated pyramid

- **Second Intermediate Period & the Hyksos (~1,650–1,550 BCE)**
  - Hyksos ("rulers of foreign lands" — Egyptian heka khasut): people from Canaan/Levant; gradual infiltration then takeover
  - Avaris (Tell el-Dab'a, Delta): Hyksos capital; Austrian excavations (Manfred Bietak, 1966–present): Minoan-style frescoes found — extraordinary cultural contact evidence; massive bronze age city
  - Dynasty 15 (main Hyksos): ruled Lower Egypt; brought horse-drawn chariots, composite bow, bronze weapons, vertical loom, new musical instruments, olive and pomegranate cultivation to Egypt
  - Dynasty 17 (Theban): ruled Upper Egypt; paid tribute to Hyksos initially
  - Seqenenre Tao: started war of liberation; mummy shows severe axe wounds to skull — possibly died in battle
  - Kamose: continued war; Carnarvon Tablet stele describes campaigns against Hyksos
  - Ahmose I (~1,550 BCE): expelled Hyksos; reunified Egypt; founded Dynasty 18 and the New Kingdom; his admiral Ahmose son of Ibana's autobiography documents the campaigns

- **New Kingdom (~1,550–1,069 BCE): Egypt's Imperial Age**
  - Political and ideological transformation: from defensive to imperial; professional army; international diplomacy; vast wealth from tribute and trade
  - Capital: Thebes (modern Luxor) in south; Memphis remains administrative hub; Pi-Ramesses under Ramesses II
  - Valley of the Kings (Wadi el-Muluk): royal necropolis; ~64 known tombs; why secret rock-cut tombs instead of visible pyramids? Anti-robbery rationale; stone quality of West Bank; solar symbolism of western horizon
  - Dynasty 18 (~1,550–1,295 BCE)
    - Ahmose I: expelled Hyksos; reorganized military; campaigns into Nubia and Canaan
    - Amenhotep I: deified after death; patron of Deir el-Medina workmen; oracle cult
    - Thutmose I: first burial in Valley of the Kings; campaigns reached Euphrates — commemorated with stele
    - Thutmose II: short reign; married Hatshepsut
    - Hatshepsut (~1,473–1,458 BCE)
      - First woman to rule as pharaoh with full titulary (not simply regent); assumed male kingship iconography (false beard, male pronouns in inscriptions — inconsistently)
      - Mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri (Djeser-Djeseru): three-tiered colonnade; finest New Kingdom architecture
      - Punt expedition: reliefs document trading mission to "Land of Punt" (probably Eritrea/Somalia region) — frankincense trees, ivory, ebony, live animals, gold; unique biodiversity documentation in ancient art
      - Obelisks at Karnak: still standing; tallest ancient obelisks remaining; electrum-tipped
      - Post-death defacement: Thutmose III eventually had many of her images erased — motives debated (political legitimacy? personal hatred? theological? delayed royal action — not begun until ~20 years after her death)
      - Rediscovery: Herbert Winlock (Metropolitan Museum): pieced together smashed Hatshepsut statues dumped in pit; Kara Cooney's contemporary biography "The Woman Who Would Be King" (2014)
    - Thutmose III (~1,479–1,425 BCE): "Napoleon of Egypt"; 17 military campaigns into Canaan and Syria; Battle of Megiddo (~1,457 BCE) — first battle in history with detailed written tactical account (Annals of Thutmose III, Karnak); empire stretched from Euphrates to Fourth Cataract; Karnak Botanical Garden reliefs; greatest military pharaoh
    - Amenhotep II: chariot archer; brutal campaigns into Canaan; boasts of personally hanging prisoners
    - Thutmose IV: Dream Stele between Sphinx paws — cleared sphinx of sand; diplomatic marriage with Mitanni princess
    - Amenhotep III (~1,390–1,352 BCE): reign of luxury and diplomacy; married Tiye (commoner — remarkable); Amenhotep III was also the father of Akhenaten; Colossi of Memnon (remaining elements of his mortuary temple); Malkata palace; diplomatic correspondence: Amarna Letters (clay tablets in cuneiform — diplomatic archive found 1887 by peasant woman at Tell el-Amarna; correspondence between pharaoh and kings of Babylonia, Assyria, Mitanni, Cyprus, Hatti, Canaan city-states)
    - Akhenaten (~1,352–1,336 BCE)
      - Born Amenhotep IV; changed name to Akhenaten ("Servant of the Aten/sun disk")
      - Atenism: monotheistic (or monolatrous) religious revolution — sole worship of Aten (sun disk, not sun god Amen-Ra); closed temples of other gods; redistributed their wealth
      - Amarna (Akhetaten): new capital built from scratch in Middle Egypt in second year; abandoned after his death; excavated since 1890s (EES, DAIK); extensive planned city grid; workers' village; elite tombs with Amarna art
      - Amarna art style: elongated skulls, exaggerated limbs, naturalistic intimate scenes (royal family playing, Akhenaten bouncing daughters); radical departure from 1,500 years of canonical style; debated whether realistic portrayal or ideological statement
      - "Great Hymn to the Aten": composed by Akhenaten; strikingly similar to Psalm 104 — academic debate on direction of influence vs. common source
      - Nefertiti: "Great Royal Wife"; possibly co-regent or successor; famous bust (Painted Limestone, Neues Museum Berlin — smuggled to Germany 1913 by Ludwig Borchardt; repatriation demanded by Egypt but refused by Germany; ongoing controversy)
      - Nefertiti as Pharaoh? Theory (explored by Aidan Dodson, Joyce Tyldesley, Joann Fletcher): Nefertiti may have ruled briefly as Neferneferuaten after Akhenaten's death
      - Smenkare: shadowy co-regent/successor; identity debated
      - Aftermath: temples restored; Akhenaten's name erased; "the heretic"
    - Tutankhamun (~1,336–1,327 BCE)
      - Minor king; died young (~19); historical importance primarily from intact tomb discovery
      - Tomb KV62: discovered November 4, 1922 by Howard Carter and team; funded by Lord Carnarvon
      - Contents: ~5,398 objects; golden death mask (~11 kg solid gold); golden shrine; calcite ('alabaster') canopic chest; chariots; weapons; food; musical instruments; two mummified fetuses (his daughters?); throne with Amarna-style scene of Nefertiti applying perfume to Tutankhamun — possible evidence of lingering Atenist influence
      - "Curse of the Pharaohs": media invention exploiting Lord Carnarvon's death (April 1923, blood poisoning from infected mosquito bite); Carter lived 16 more years; no statistical support for curse
      - Cause of death: multiple analyses — genetic inbreeding (parents were siblings — aDNA 2010: Hawass et al., *JAMA*); malaria (two strains in mummy); bone disease (Köhler disease in foot); possible chariot accident (not now favored); most recent analysis (2022): complex multiple ailments including sickle cell variants and malaria
      - DNA surprise: he and his father Akhenaten's mummies connected; probable grandmother = Tiye; probable mother = unidentified "Younger Lady" (KV35) — not Nefertiti; full family tree established via aDNA
      - Face reconstruction: multiple teams; forensic facial reconstruction shows broad-cheeked, somewhat protruding-toothed youth
      - Treasures still yield new research: neutron diffraction of iron dagger (2016) revealed meteoritic iron; XRF analysis of artifacts; CT scanning of mummy
    - Ay (~1,327–1,323 BCE): succeeded Tutankhamun; possibly grand vizier; brief reign; married Tutankhamun's widow Ankhesenamun (documented in desperate letter she may have sent to Hittite king requesting a Hittite prince as husband — Hittite prince killed en route or on arrival)
    - Horemheb (~1,323–1,295 BCE): general; erased Akhenaten's family from records; began Amarna erasure systematically; legal decrees against corruption
  - Dynasty 19 (~1,295–1,186 BCE)
    - Ramesses I: brief reign; founder; former general
    - Sety I (~1,294–1,279 BCE): KV17 — finest decorated tomb in Valley of the Kings; Battle of Qadesh against Hittites begun; Abydos temple and Abydos King List (chronological list of 76 predecessors)
    - Ramesses II ("the Great," ~1,279–1,213 BCE): 66-year reign; ~100+ children; most statues of any pharaoh
      - Battle of Qadesh (~1,274 BCE): against Hittites under Muwatalli II at Qadesh (Syria); arguably the largest chariot battle in history (~5,000–6,000 chariots); Egyptian tactical near-disaster; Ramesses' counter-attack; tactical draw; propagandized as great Egyptian victory in five temple inscriptions (most prolifically recorded battle in ancient world)
      - Egyptian-Hittite Peace Treaty (~1,259 BCE): oldest surviving international peace treaty; copies in both hieroglyphic and cuneiform; UN Security Council chamber has replica; Hattusili III and Ramesses II exchange silver tablets
      - Abu Simbel: rock-cut temples in Nubia; four colossal statues ~21 m; solar alignment — twice yearly equinox-adjacent alignment illuminates inner sanctuary (February 22 and October 22); modern relocation 1968 due to Aswan High Dam flooding — UNESCO-coordinated international rescue
      - Pi-Ramesses: vast Delta capital; now largely under modern city of Qantir
      - Building program: additions to Karnak, Luxor, Abydos; Ramesseum mortuary temple; massive propaganda machine
      - Ramesses II as Exodus pharaoh? Most historians identify Ramesses II as the pharaoh of the Exodus if the Exodus occurred; no Egyptian records of Exodus; some scholars dismiss the Exodus as historically non-literal; others propose a smaller historical kernel (James Hoffmeier, Israel Finkelstein debates)
    - Merneptah (~1,213–1,203 BCE): Merneptah Stele (1208 BCE) — "Israel is laid waste, its seed is no more" — first and only mention of "Israel" in an Egyptian text; confirms Israel existed as a people in Canaan by 1208 BCE
    - Sety II, Amenmesse, Siptah, Tawosret: chaotic end of Dynasty 19
  - Dynasty 20 (~1,186–1,069 BCE)
    - Setnakht: founded dynasty; brief reign
    - Ramesses III (~1,184–1,153 BCE): last great pharaoh; repelled two waves of "Sea Peoples" (land and sea battles, Medinet Habu reliefs — most detailed ancient naval battle depiction); Sea Peoples identities debated (Peleset=Philistines? Tjeker, Shekelesh, Denyen, Weshesh — possibly from Aegean, Anatolia, Cyprus displaced by Bronze Age Collapse); also repelled Libyan invasions
      - "Harem Conspiracy": assassination plot by secondary wife Tiye and conspirators; "Judicial Papyrus of Turin" — trial records survive; Ramesses III may have died from the conspiracy or its aftermath; 2012 CT scan of mummy revealed cut throat under bandages; 2012 forensic study confirmed he was murdered
    - Ramesses IV–XI: declining power; Theban high priests of Amun increasingly powerful; tomb robberies documented (Valley of the Kings robbery papyri — Papyrus Abbott, Papyrus Amherst — extraordinary documents of police investigations into royal tomb robberies); end of New Kingdom

- **The Valley of the Kings: Archaeology in Depth**
  - Location: West Bank of Nile at Thebes (Luxor); two valleys (KV = Kings' Valley, WV = West Valley)
  - Known tombs: 64+ as of 2024; numbered KV1–KV64 (plus some unnumbered)
  - Most significant beyond KV62 (Tutankhamun)
    - KV17 (Sety I): longest and deepest decorated tomb; discovered 1817 by Giovanni Belzoni; remarkable astronomical ceiling
    - KV2 (Ramesses IV): pharaoh's tomb; ancient "tourist" graffiti from Greek and Roman period — ancient visitors
    - KV5 (sons of Ramesses II): largest tomb in Valley; 120+ rooms; found by Howard Carter 1905; rediscovered by Kent Weeks (Theban Mapping Project, 1987); at least 52 sons buried; still being excavated
    - KV55 (Amarna Cache): disarticulated remains possibly of Akhenaten or Smenkhkare; coffin and canopic jars adapted for mysterious royal occupant
    - KV35 (Amenhotep II): cache of royal mummies; "Elder Lady" = Queen Tiye (DNA confirmed); "Younger Lady" = Tutankhamun's mother
    - KV63: embalming cache; discovered 2005 — most recent substantial new tomb discovery
    - KV64: uninscribed coffin; found 2011 (Basel University team)
  - Deir el-Medina: workers' village for tomb builders; ~1,500 years of occupation; thousands of ostraca (inscribed pottery sherds) give unprecedented view of working-class ancient Egyptian life
    - Complaints about pay, work stoppages, arguments, love poetry, medical issues, dreams, magic spells
    - First recorded labor strike in history (~1170 BCE, Ramesses III reign): workers stopped work and sat down demanding overdue rations
    - Community of literate artisans: unusual for ancient world; men and women could read/write
  - New Valley of the Kings discoveries: ground-penetrating radar surveys showing possible undiscovered chambers; Nicholas Reeves (2015) proposed KV62 (Tutankhamun's tomb) conceals entrance to Nefertiti's burial behind plastered walls — Egyptian authorities conducted radar and non-invasive tests; results inconclusive/negative; debate continues

- **The Bronze Age Collapse (~1,200–1,150 BCE) and Its Effect on Egypt**
  - Simultaneous collapse of interconnected Late Bronze Age civilizations: Hittites, Ugarit, Mycenaean Greece, Cypriote cities, Canaan city-states
  - Sea Peoples as both symptom and cause: displaced people from collapsed regions invading as refugees/raiders
  - Eric Cline (*1177 BC: The Year Civilization Collapsed*, 2014): systems collapse model — no single cause; drought + earthquakes + disease + internal rebellion + trade network disruption + migration
  - Egypt survives but weakened; Third Intermediate Period follows; New Kingdom never recovered

- **Ancient Egyptian Religion: Core Structure**
  - Polytheism with henotheistic tendencies (elevation of one god without denying others)
  - Ma'at: fundamental cosmic principle of truth, justice, balance, order — opposed by isfet (chaos)
  - The pharaoh as guarantor of ma'at between humanity and the gods
  - Key deities
    - Ra/Re: sun god; creator; daily solar journey; merged with Amun as Amun-Ra (Theban state religion)
    - Amun: "the hidden one"; wind; Thebes; empire's chief god; priests of Amun eventually rival royal power
    - Osiris: death, resurrection, agriculture; killed by Set; resurrected by Isis; rules the dead
    - Isis: magic, motherhood, healing; sister-wife of Osiris; mother of Horus; protection of the dead
    - Horus: sky, kingship; living pharaoh is Horus; avenged father Osiris against Set
    - Set: chaos, storms, desert, foreigners; evil in later tradition; earlier a legitimate deity
    - Thoth: wisdom, writing, the moon; arbiter of the gods; scribal patron; records the weighing of the heart
    - Anubis: embalming, mummification; guides the dead; jackal-headed
    - Hathor: love, music, beauty, fertility, the sky; cow goddess
    - Ptah: craftsmen, creation through speech; patron of Memphis
    - Sekhmet: war, disease, healing; lion-headed; consort of Ptah
    - Sobek: crocodiles, the Nile, fertility
    - Nut: sky goddess; swallows sun at dusk, gives birth at dawn; depicted arching over earth (Geb)
    - Nefertum: lotus, primordial creation, perfume
  - Cosmogonies
    - Heliopolitan (Heliopolis/Iunu): Atum (or Ra-Atum) emerges from primordial mound (Nun); creates Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture); then Geb (earth) and Nut (sky); then Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys — the "Ennead of Heliopolis"
    - Memphite theology: Ptah creates through his heart (thought) and tongue (spoken word) — intellectual creation
    - Hermopolitan (Khmunu): the Ogdoad — eight primordial deities in four pairs representing precreation chaos; Thoth associated
    - Theban: Amun as self-created hidden power behind all creation
  - Death and the afterlife
    - Mummification: preserving body so ka (vital force/double) can return; process took 70 days; natron salt desiccation; organ removal into canopic jars (lungs-Hapi, liver-Imsety, stomach-Duamutef, intestines-Qebehsenuef); heart left in body (weighed at judgment); brain removed via nostrils (discarded — not considered seat of thought)
    - The Book of the Dead (Pert em hru — "Coming Forth by Day"): collection of spells; spell 125 = the "Negative Confession" before 42 assessors; "I have not stolen," "I have not killed," etc.; the "Weighing of the Heart" against the Feather of Ma'at; Ammit devours the unworthy
    - Earlier texts: Pyramid Texts (Old Kingdom; only for royalty) → Coffin Texts (Middle Kingdom; extended to nobles) → Book of the Dead (New Kingdom; available to anyone who could afford a copy)
    - Other funerary books: Amduat ("What is in the Underworld"), Book of Gates, Book of Caverns, Book of the Earth, Book of the Heavenly Cow
    - The Field of Reeds (Aaru): Egyptian paradise; continuation of earthly life; agricultural abundance
  - Temples: "house of the god" (not congregation worship space); priests perform rituals for resident deity statue; public excluded from inner sanctum; festivals when deity statue carried outside in procession (Great Opet Festival, Valley Festival at Thebes)
  - Oracle tradition: divine statues consulted for decisions; legal disputes resolved; succession potentially determined by oracle

- **Egyptian Hieroglyphics and Writing Systems**
  - Three scripts
    - Hieroglyphic ("sacred carving"): formal monumental; ~750–1,000 signs (Middle Kingdom); carved or painted
    - Hieratic: cursive administrative/literary script; written on papyrus; used simultaneously from Dynasty 1
    - Demotic ("popular script"): administrative/legal; ~650 BCE onward; very different from hieroglyphic
    - Coptic: Egyptian language in Greek alphabet + 7 Demotic-derived letters; final stage of Egyptian language; still liturgical language of Coptic Christians
  - The Rosetta Stone (~196 BCE; discovered 1799 at Rosetta/Rashid by French soldiers)
    - Decree of Ptolemy V in three scripts: hieroglyphic, demotic, ancient Greek
    - At British Museum since 1802 (Egypt repeatedly requests return)
    - Decipherment process: Thomas Young (1819): identified cartouche as royal name; identified some signs; Champollion's breakthrough: Jean-François Champollion (1822, September 27 — "Letter to M. Dacier"): realized hieroglyphics not purely symbolic but phonetic/alphabetic; key insight from bilingual Philae Obelisk and Ptolemy/Cleopatra cartouche comparison
  - Direction of reading: right-to-left OR left-to-right OR top-to-bottom; faces of signs face the beginning
  - Determinatives: unpronounced signs clarifying word class (man, woman, seated divinity, papyrus roll, abstract concept) — crucial for reading
  - Middle Egyptian: classical literary language; used for 2,000+ years as prestige written form even after spoken evolution
  - Epigraphy: Chicago House Method (Oriental Institute's Epigraphic Survey at Luxor) — photographic documentation of temple reliefs

- **Egyptian Science, Mathematics, and Technology**
  - Mathematics: decimal system (no zero or place value initially); unit fractions (all Ahmes/Rhind problems use fractions with numerator 1 except 2/3); practical geometry
  - Rhind Mathematical Papyrus: calculation of area, volumes, slope of pyramid (seked), arithmetic progressions; value of π ~3.16
  - Moscow Mathematical Papyrus: volume of truncated pyramid — most sophisticated formula in ancient Egyptian math
  - Astronomy: 365-day civil calendar; 12 months of 30 days + 5 epagomenal days; decans (36 groups of stars rising 10 days apart) → basis of 24-hour day; Merkhet (shadow clock/sundial) for time measurement; star clocks on coffin lids
  - Nilometer: measuring Nile flood levels; predicting harvest taxation; oldest at Elephantine — still in use in Roman times
  - Medicine: Edwin Smith Papyrus (~1,600 BCE copy of ~2,600 BCE original; only surgical text from ancient world): 48 case studies in head-to-toe order; rational diagnosis rather than magic; describes brain, meninges, cerebrospinal fluid — first known reference to the brain; wound treatment, sutures, honey as antiseptic
  - Ebers Papyrus (~1,550 BCE): 877 prescriptions; extensive herbal pharmacopeia; describes heart, blood vessels, digestive system; conception of metu (channels) throughout body (possibly vessels/nerves/tendons)
  - Construction technology
    - Pyramid construction methods: still debated — ramp theories (straight frontal, spiral wrap-around, internal zigzag); Mark Lehner's internal ramp hypothesis; sledges on wetted sand (friction reduction — depicted in Djehutihotep quarry painting ~1,880 BCE); no cranes; labor organized in named gangs
    - Obelisk quarrying: Unfinished Obelisk at Aswan (~3.4 m wide, would have been heaviest obelisk ever at ~1,168 tonnes) — shows dolerite pounding technique; cracked during quarrying; left in situ
    - Faience: glazed quartz/sand material; turquoise blue-green; first synthetic material?; Predynastic period onward; widespread in jewelry, shabtis, architectural decoration

- **Advanced & Specialist Topics**
  - Egyptological methods
    - Seriation (Flinders Petrie, Sequence Dating): ceramic typology to establish relative chronology; foundational archaeological method
    - Absolute chronology problems: Egyptian chronology tied to astronomy (Sothic cycle — heliacal rising of Sirius); key dates debated; New Kingdom chronology reasonably secure; Old Kingdom less so; multiple variant chronologies (high, middle, low) in academic use
    - The Turin King List (Papyrus of Turin): most complete ancient Egyptian king list; ~300 rulers; damaged; in fragmentary state (Museum of Egyptian Antiquities, Turin)
    - Palimpsests and papyrus conservation
  - Current debates in Egyptology
    - Race and identity: ancient Egyptians' ethnic/racial identity — legacy of 19th-century racist historiography still distorts popular discussion; aDNA studies show predominantly "Near Eastern and African" ancestry mix varying by period and region (Schuenemann et al., 2017, *Nature Communications* — 90 ancient Egyptian mummies: closest to ancient Levantines and Anatolians; sub-Saharan African ancestry increases in later periods); Afrocentric scholarship (Cheikh Anta Diop) claims Black African identity — mainstream Egyptology disagrees with categorical claims but acknowledges African roots; the debate reflects modern political concerns as much as ancient reality
    - The Exodus Question: is it historical? When? Who was the pharaoh? No Egyptian records; Merneptah Stele confirms "Israel" in Canaan by 1208 BCE; some scholars propose a smaller-scale historical event underlying the narrative; others see it as literary construction; Israel Finkelstein and Neil Asher Silberman ("The Bible Unearthed") argue against large-scale historical Exodus
    - Pyramid construction: ramp debates (Lehner, Hawass, Jean-Pierre Houdin's internal ramp theory supported by some GPR anomalies); labor organization (workers' village confirms organized state labor — not slaves per se — but complexity of managing 20,000+ workers)
    - New chambers in the Great Pyramid: ScanPyramids Big Void (2017) and North Face corridor (2023) — precise function and connection to known architecture being modeled
  - Mummification science
    - CT scanning of mummies: resolving cause of death, disease, age, family relations without unwrapping
    - Proteomics and metabolomics of mummy tissues: new biomarker data
    - Salima Ikram's mummy research: non-human mummies; millions of animal mummies (cats, ibises, crocodiles, baboons) — vast resource for Nile ecology, disease, trade; some found to be fraudulent offerings (empty wrappings)
  - Papyrus discoveries (ongoing)
    - Saqqara Papyri (2022): found in shaft beneath Step Pyramid complex; Book of the Dead papyrus for a man named Pwkhaef, 90-foot scroll — first Book of the Dead found at Saqqara in over a century; announced by Egyptian Ministry of Antiquities
    - Wadi el-Jarf Papyri (discovered 2013, published 2017): oldest papyri ever found (~2,560 BCE, reign of Khufu); harbor logbook of inspector Merer; describes transport of white limestone from Tura to Giza for great pyramid; confirms pyramid construction organization; Pierre Tallet excavation; remarkable primary source
  - Amarna City ongoing research: EES Amarna Project (Barry Kemp); skeletal evidence of stress and violence in workmen's cemetery; Amarna letters digital publication; strontium isotopes reveal non-local origins of some inhabitants

- **Frontier & Contemporary Research Directions**
  - DNA and the ancient Egyptians: expanding aDNA datasets; obtaining samples from hot climate mummies technically challenging; nuclear DNA degradation faster than in cold climates; new methods improving recovery; key question: how much Sub-Saharan African vs. Near Eastern vs. Mediterranean admixture changed over time?
  - ScanPyramids and non-invasive archaeology: muon tomography; thermal imaging; GPR; drone photogrammetry — applied to all Egyptian monuments; model for non-destructive investigation globally
  - LiDAR and satellite archaeology: CORONA, WorldView-3, Pleiades satellites; Sarah Parcak ("space archaeologist") — discovered ~3,100 possible ancient Egyptian sites from space; looting detection; previously unknown pyramid candidates
  - Digital Egyptology: 3D modeling temples and tombs (Karnak 3D project; Virtual Abydos; RTI — Reflectance Transformation Imaging for faded texts)
  - New site excavations: Luxor/Thebes West Bank — ongoing surprise discoveries; 2021 "Golden City" (Aten) at Luxor: large administrative/industrial settlement of Amenhotep III and Akhenaten; well-preserved; one of most significant discoveries in decades (Zahi Hawass's team)
  - Abydos royal boat burials: 14 wooden boats buried near Dynasty 1 tombs (~3,000 BCE); among oldest planked boats known; ongoing conservation
  - Climate history: paleoclimate reconstructions from speleothems, pollen, lake sediments — correlating Nile flood variability with political history; "Green Sahara" and population movements into the Valley
  - Saqqara ongoing discoveries: Egyptologist Mostafa Waziry leading joint Egyptian-German-French missions; new tombs, sarcophagi, ushabtis, animal mummies found annually 2019–present; Saqqara yielding extraordinary quantity of New Kingdom and Late Period objects
  - Decolonization of Egyptology: challenging the history of extraction and exploitation; debates over return of artifacts (Rosetta Stone, Nefertiti bust, Dendera Zodiac, Elgin equivalents); Egyptian scholars leading their own heritage narratives

#### Ancient History — Ancient Egypt: Third Intermediate Period, Late Period, Ptolemaic Period & Roman Egypt

- **Basics & Scope**
  - Covers ~1,069 BCE (end of New Kingdom) to 641 CE (Arab conquest): roughly 1,700 years
  - Third Intermediate Period (TIP): 21st–25th Dynasties (~1069–664 BCE) — political fragmentation, multiple simultaneous ruling houses
  - Late Period: 26th–31st Dynasties (~664–332 BCE) — native renaissance, then Persian domination (twice)
  - Macedonian/Ptolemaic Period: 332–30 BCE — Greek-Egyptian cultural synthesis, Hellenistic cosmopolitanism
  - Roman Egypt: 30 BCE–641 CE — province of Rome, then Byzantine empire, Christianization, Arab conquest
  - Primary sources: king lists (Manetho's *Aegyptiaca*, Ptolemaic-era; Palermo Stone; Turin King List); temple inscriptions; papyri (Demotic, Greek, Coptic); classical authors (Herodotus, Diodorus Siculus, Plutarch, Strabo); Church Fathers
  - Key journals: Journal of Egyptian Archaeology (JEA), Göttinger Miszellen, Bulletin de l'Institut français d'archéologie orientale (BIFAO), Enchoria (Demotic studies)
  - Major museums: Egyptian Museum Cairo, British Museum, Louvre, Museo Egizio Turin, Metropolitan Museum New York, Berlin Neues Museum
  - Key reference works: Cambridge Ancient History vols. III–XIV; Alan Lloyd (ed.) *A Companion to Ancient Egypt*; Naphtali Lewis *Life in Egypt under Roman Rule*; Roger Bagnall *Egypt in Late Antiquity*; David Frankfurter *Religion in Roman Egypt*

- **Third Intermediate Period (~1069–664 BCE)**
  - **Overview**
    - Begins with death of Ramesses XI, end of 20th Dynasty (~1069 BCE)
    - Egypt politically fragmented; no single ruler controls the entire country simultaneously
    - Two main ruling centers: Tanis (Delta — pharaonic titulature, secular authority) and Thebes (High Priests of Amun — theocratic authority)
    - Characterized by Libyan immigration and eventual Libyan dynasties; later Nubian/Kushite 25th Dynasty
    - Weakened military power; mercenary armies; loss of Nubian territories and Levantine empire
    - Personal piety emphasis in religion; Osirian funerary tradition intensifies; elaborate individual Books of the Dead
    - Economy: still functional but increasingly localized; inter-regional trade reduced
  - **21st Dynasty: Tanite Dynasty (~1069–945 BCE)**
    - Founded by Smendes I (Nesbanebdjed I) at Tanis after Ramesses XI
    - Capital: Tanis (ancient Djanet) in the northern Delta — new royal burial ground replacing Thebes
    - **Tanis Royal Tombs**: discovered by Pierre Montet 1939–1940 beneath temple precinct; intact burials of Psusennes I, Amenemope, Shoshenq II; silver coffins, gold masks, exquisite jewelry; "Egypt's hidden treasure" — discovery overshadowed by WWII; largely unknown to general public
    - Psusennes I's silver inner coffin (gold unobtainable in this period) and turquoise/lapis/gold funerary mask among finest pieces of ancient goldsmithing; Shoshenq II's pectoral with winged scarab
    - High Priests of Amun at Thebes: Herihor, Piankh, Masaharta, Menkheperre, Pinedjem I, Pinedjem II — held real power in Upper Egypt; some used full royal cartouches
    - Herihor (~1080 BCE): first High Priest to use royal cartouche; military general; de facto co-ruler of Upper Egypt; depicted in Khonsu Temple reliefs at Karnak larger than the pharaoh
    - Pinedjem I: High Priest who assumed full pharaonic titulature and ruled Upper Egypt as a parallel king
    - Tanite pharaohs and Theban High Priests linked by marriage alliances — a controlled duopoly
    - **The Wenamun Papyrus**: literary/historical text of early 21st Dynasty; official Wenamun sent to Byblos (Lebanon) to procure cedar for Amun's sacred barque; merchants demand payment up front; Byblos prince questions Egyptian authority; vivid demonstration of Egypt's diminished international prestige; contrast with New Kingdom texts where Egypt dictates terms
    - Egypt retracted from Nubia and Canaan; no campaigns; diplomatic relations maintained but military leverage gone
  - **22nd–23rd Dynasties: Libyan Pharaohs (~945–715 BCE)**
    - 22nd Dynasty (Bubastite Dynasty) founded by Shoshenq I (~945 BCE): of Libyan Meshwesh tribal origin; family had served as military commanders since 20th Dynasty
    - Capital: Bubastis (Tell Basta) in Eastern Delta
    - **Shoshenq I** (~943–922 BCE): most powerful ruler of the entire TIP; campaigned in Canaan ~925 BCE; biblical "Shishak" who raided Jerusalem temple (1 Kings 14:25–26; 2 Chronicles 12); his Bubastite Portal at Karnak lists ~150 conquered Canaanite cities (Megiddo, Gaza, Taanach, Raphia); provides key absolute Egyptian-Biblical chronological anchor
