    - **Pan-Hellenic Festivals**: Pythian Games (Delphi), Nemean Games (Nemea), Isthmian Games (Corinth); all four together = *periodos* (grand slam circuit); festival victors won olive/laurel/celery crowns; Pindar's odes commissioned for these victories

- **The Hellenistic Period (~323–31 BCE)**
  - **Overview**
    - Begins with Alexander's death; ends with Octavian's defeat of Cleopatra and Antony at Actium
    - Greek language and culture spread across a vast territory from Egypt to Bactria (Afghanistan)
    - New centers of learning and power: Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon
    - Major philosophical schools: Epicureanism, Stoicism, Skepticism; each offering personal paths to happiness in a fragmented political world
    - Scientific achievements arguably surpass Classical period (Eratosthenes, Archimedes, Aristarchus, Hipparchus)
  - **The Wars of the Successors (Diadochi, ~323–281 BCE)**
    - After Alexander's death: his generals fought for 40 years over his empire
    - Main figures: **Ptolemy** (secured Egypt); **Seleucus** (Mesopotamia, Syria, Persia, parts of India); **Antigonus** (Asia Minor; killed at Ipsus 301 BCE); **Cassander** (Macedonia); **Lysimachus** (Thrace); **Pyrrhus of Epirus** (eventually invaded Italy and fought Rome — a "Pyrrhic victory" is one won too dearly)
    - By 281 BCE three main successor kingdoms crystallized: **Ptolemaic Egypt**, **Seleucid Empire**, **Macedonian Antigonid Kingdom**
    - **Seleucid Empire**: largest but most unstable; lost India to Chandragupta Maurya (~305 BCE); Bactria and Parthia broke away ~250 BCE; eventually reduced to Syria and Mesopotamia; absorbed by Rome 63 BCE
    - **Kingdom of Pergamon**: minor kingdom in NW Asia Minor under Attalid dynasty; became major power after defeating Galatians (~241 BCE); great patrons of art and scholarship (**Pergamon Library** — 200,000 volumes, rival to Alexandria; vellum/parchment allegedly developed here when Egypt cut off papyrus supply — *charta pergamena* → "parchment"); **Altar of Pergamon** (2nd c. BCE; giant monumental frieze showing gigantomachy — gods vs. giants; now Berlin Pergamon Museum; controversy over display of looted cultural heritage); art style of heightened emotional realism (*Hellenistic Baroque*): most famous: *Laocoön and his Sons* (~100 BCE or Roman copy), *Dying Gaul*, *Winged Victory of Samothrace*
  - **Epicureanism**
    - **Epicurus (~341–270 BCE)**: founded Garden school at Athens; admitted women and slaves; *tetrapharmakos* (four-fold remedy): "Don't fear god; don't fear death; what is good is easy to get; what is terrible is easy to endure"
    - **Ataraxia** (tranquility of mind) + **aponia** (freedom from pain) = happiness; pleasure is the absence of pain, not hedonistic excess; gods exist but are indifferent to human affairs; death = dissolution of atoms = nothing to fear ("where death is, I am not; where I am, death is not"); the *clinamen* (random atom swerve) gives free will
    - **Lucretius** (~99–55 BCE): Roman Epicurean; *De Rerum Natura* (On the Nature of Things) — didactic poem; atomism, cosmology, anthropology, psychology; manuscript lost for centuries; discovered at Fulda monastery 1417 by Poggio Bracciolini; its rediscovery helped launch the Scientific Revolution (Stephen Greenblatt *The Swerve*, 2011)
  - **Stoicism**
    - **Zeno of Citium (~334–262 BCE)**: Phoenician merchant; founded Stoic school at the Stoa Poikile ("Painted Porch") in Athens' Agora ~300 BCE; hence "Stoicism"
    - **Core doctrine**: live according to nature and reason; virtues (wisdom, justice, courage, temperance) are the only good; externals (health, wealth, reputation) are *adiaphora* (indifferent); passions are false judgments (*katalepsis* = secure cognition); *logos* (divine reason) pervades the cosmos; cosmopolitan brotherhood of all rational beings (*kosmopolites*)
    - Successors: Cleanthes (~330–230 BCE); **Chrysippus of Soli (~279–206 BCE)**: the "second founder"; systematized Stoic logic and physics; ~700 works (all lost except fragments); his propositional logic and conditionals anticipate modern logic
    - Roman Stoics: **Seneca** (statesman, playwright, essayist); **Epictetus** (former slave; *Enchiridion* = "handbook"); **Marcus Aurelius** (*Meditations*: personal journal of an emperor struggling to live Stoically; written in Greek; one of the most read philosophical texts in history); Stoicism had profound influence on early Christianity (Logos theology, universal brotherhood, natural law theory)
  - **Skepticism**
    - **Pyrrho of Elis (~360–270 BCE)**: traveled with Alexander to India; influenced by Indian philosophy (Gymnosophists — naked philosophers); suspended judgment (*epochē*) on all questions; *ataraxia* (tranquility) results from neither affirming nor denying; no criterion of truth is reliable
    - **Academic Skepsis**: Arcesilaus (~316–241 BCE) and Carneades (~214–129 BCE) turned Plato's Academy to skeptical direction; argument against any criterion of knowledge; *probabilism* (we can act on what seems probable without certainty)
    - **Pyrrhonism systematized**: Aenesidemus (~80–10 BCE): Ten Modes for suspending judgment; **Sextus Empiricus** (~200 CE): *Outlines of Pyrrhonism* and *Against the Mathematicians* — preserved Pyrrhonian arguments; rediscovery in 16th c. (Montaigne; Descartes' *cogito* as response to Pyrrhonian challenge)
  - **Hellenistic Science**
    - *See Library of Alexandria section above (Eratosthenes, Aristarchus, Herophilus, Erasistratus)*
    - **Archimedes of Syracuse** (~287–212 BCE): greatest mathematician and physicist of antiquity; *Eureka* story (king's crown and water displacement — testing gold purity using buoyancy principle); **Archimedes Principle** (buoyancy); calculated area of parabola, volume of sphere; *Method* — uses geometric intuition (later called "Cavalieri's Principle") approaching calculus; *Sand Reckoner*: number system to express grains of sand filling the universe; invented an early form of integral calculus; machines for Syracuse's defense (catapults, claw grabbing ships, possibly a burning mirror — *Newton heat ray*); killed by Roman soldier at siege of Syracuse 212 BCE ("Do not disturb my circles")
    - **Hipparchus of Nicaea** (~190–120 BCE): greatest observational astronomer of antiquity; star catalogue (850 stars with positions and magnitudes); discovered **precession of the equinoxes** (Earth's axis wobbles slowly over ~26,000 years); improved mathematical models of moon and sun; trigonometry; influenced Ptolemy's *Almagest*
    - **Apollonius of Perga (~240–190 BCE)**: *Conics* (8 books; mostly surviving): defined ellipse, parabola, hyperbola — curves that Kepler would use 1,800 years later for planetary orbits
    - **Heron of Alexandria** (~10–70 CE): aeolipile (steam engine prototype — rotary steam ball); programmable mechanical puppet theatre; surveying instruments; first formulas for triangle area from side lengths (*Heron's formula*)

- **Key Themes and Topics**
  - **Greek Art**
    - Archaic sculpture: *kouroi* (nude male youth) and *korai* (draped female); Archaic smile; rigid frontal pose (Egyptian influence); Dipylon Master in pottery
    - Early Classical ("Severe Style" ~480–450 BCE): **Riace Warriors** (2 full-size bronze warriors found in Ionian Sea 1972; Reggio Calabria Museum; extraordinary preservation); **Temple of Zeus at Olympia** (west pediment: Lapiths vs. Centaurs; east: Olympia chariot race); **Critius Boy** (~480 BCE; Acropolis Museum; contrapposto begins)
    - Classical: **Polykleitos** (~450–420 BCE): *Doryphoros* (Spear-Bearer) as *kanon* (embodiment of mathematical ideal proportions); treatise *The Canon*; **Pheidias**: Parthenon sculptures, Zeus at Olympia, Athena Parthenos; **Praxiteles** (~364–330 BCE): *Aphrodite of Knidos* — first full female nude in monumental art (revolutionary); *Hermes with the Infant Dionysus* (possibly original); red-figure pottery (Euphronios, Exekias, Berlin Painter; scenes from daily life and mythology)
    - Hellenistic: emotional extremes; *Laocoön*, *Dying Gaul*, *Winged Victory of Samothrace*, *Boxer at Rest* (Terme Museum, Rome); Alexander portraits (Lysippus); increased portraiture and landscape
    - Greek architecture: Doric, Ionic, Corinthian orders; post-and-lintel; precise optical refinements; temple as house of the god's statue; temples listed: Parthenon (Athens), Temple of Hephaestus (Athens), Temple of Zeus at Olympia (with the Wonder), Temple of Hera at Paestum (best-preserved archaic Doric, S. Italy), Temple of Apollo at Corinth, Erechtheion, Temple of Apollo Epicurius at Bassae (Ictinus; unusual features)
  - **Gender and Sexuality in Ancient Greece**
    - **Athenian women**: legally minors under male guardian (*kyrios*); confined to household (*oikos*); could not own property, make contracts, attend most public events (possibly exception: theater, religious festivals); primary roles: marriage (~14–16), childbearing, wool-working; *Xenophon's Oeconomicus* — husband teaches wife household management; Lysias (speeches) and Demosthenes reveal glimpses of real women's lives in lawcourt contexts
    - **Spartan women**: much more freedom; owned/managed property; exercised publicly; delayed marriage (18); more say in domestic decisions; seen as shockingly free by other Greeks
    - **Hetairai**: high-class courtesans who gave men intellectual and sexual companionship; only women present at *symposia*; some achieved fame: **Aspasia of Miletus** (Pericles' companion; said to have helped write the Funeral Oration; taught rhetoric; met Socrates; subject of *Aspasia* dialogues); **Phryne** (reportedly acquitted of impiety charges because her lover Hyperides revealed her beautiful body to jury — anecdote may be apocryphal)
    - **Pederasty (*paiderastia*)** as social institution: adult male (*erastēs*) and adolescent boy (*erōmenos*); understood as educational mentoring combined with erotic attraction; cultural norm in elite Athenian and Spartan contexts; hotly debated modern moral evaluation; pedagogic framing vs. power exploitation; **Sacred Band of Thebes** — 150 pairs of male lovers as military unit
    - **Sappho** (see above): female same-sex desire; Lesbian as adjective from Lesbos
  - **Alexander the Great** *(see end of Near East section above for campaigns)*
    - His extraordinary multicultural fusion goals: married Roxana (Bactrian) and Stateira II (Persian princess); encouraged 80 of his officers to marry Persian noblewomen (Susa mass wedding 324 BCE); distributed Persian administrative roles to local elites; wore mixed Macedonian-Persian dress; adopted *proskynesis* (prostration) — Macedonians resented this; *Philotas Conspiracy* and killing of Cleitus the Black (Clitus: saved Alexander's life at Granicus; Alexander killed him drunk at dinner 328 BCE — lifelong regret)
    - Alexander as founder: ~70 cities named Alexandria or something similar; Alexandria in Egypt the most enduring; Alexandria Eschate ("the Farthest") = modern Khujand, Tajikistan; Alexandropolis (earliest, from boyhood)
    - **Alexander's tutor: Aristotle** (~343–340 BCE); perhaps the most significant teacher-pupil pair in history; Alexander carried a copy of the *Iliad* annotated by Aristotle; slept with it under his pillow with a dagger

- **The Antikythera Mechanism and Frontier Research**
  - **Antikythera Mechanism** (~150–100 BCE; possibly earlier): found in shipwreck by sponge divers off small island of Antikythera 1901; bronze object in wooden case; identified as astronomical calculator by Rehm (1905), Price (1974 *Gears from the Greeks*), Wright (2002–2006), and Antikythera Mechanism Research Project (2005–present)
    - Contains at least 37 meshing bronze gears; predicted positions of sun and moon; eclipses (Metonic cycle, Saros cycle); Olympiad cycle (dates of games); possibly 5 planetary positions (controversial 2021 UCL model — Tony Freeth et al.); **nothing comparable appears until European mechanical clocks ~1,300 years later**; demonstrates Bronze-like technical sophistication of Hellenistic craftsmanship not previously imagined; still not fully decoded; 2023–2026: new CT scanning efforts and pixel-super-resolution imaging of remaining fragmented surfaces
    - **Herculaneum Scrolls AI Decipherment (2023–2026)**: Vesuvius Challenge; charred library scrolls from Villa of the Papyri (Herculaneum, buried 79 CE); AI model reads the carbonized papyri through CT scans; 2023: Youssef Nader, Luke Farritor, Julian Schilliger won $700,000 prize for first substantial text revealed; Platonic philosophy text, philosophy of pleasure (possibly from Philodemus); 2024–2025: further progress; many scrolls contain Epicurean philosophical texts; some may contain lost works of Aristotle; major ongoing project; Greek philosophical knowledge may expand dramatically
    - **New Sappho fragments** (see above) continue to shape understanding of archaic lyric
    - **Derveni Papyrus** (~340 BCE; discovery 1962): oldest surviving European book; partially carbonized scroll found at Derveni, northern Greece; Orphic-philosophical commentary on a poem about cosmogony; extraordinary convergence of Orphism, pre-Socratic philosophy, allegorical interpretation; *editio princeps* (Tsantsanoglou, Papadimitriou, Kouremenos 2006)
    - **Ancient DNA studies of Greeks**: 2017–2025 aDNA from Mycenaean and Minoan skeletons; 2017 Klaus Harrer et al. (*Nature*): Minoans and Mycenaeans genetically similar to each other and to modern Greeks; Minoans not primarily from Africa (as Evans speculated) but from Anatolian Neolithic farmer stock; Mycenaeans had additional steppe ancestry (~Yamnaya) confirming Indo-European migration origin; expansion of Bronze Age Greek island aDNA ongoing 2022–2025
    - **Underwater archaeology**: Antikythera shipwreck new systematic excavations (2012–present; Greek Ephorate of Underwater Antiquities + WHOI + Argo remotely operated vehicle): found human remains 2016 (skull, long bones with teeth — possible eDNA extraction attempted); discovered more bronze fragments 2022–2023
    - **Digital Humanities applied to Greek texts**: *Classics CLEF* projects; **SAWS project** linking ancient Greek manuscripts; **Perseus Digital Library** (Tufts); **Open Greek and Latin** project; computer-assisted papyrus fragment joining; **Oxford Computational Papyrology** — neural networks reading fragmentary papyri
    - **Sparta revisited**: new archaeology at Sparta by Greek Archaeological Service (Sparta was unfortified, so little monumental architecture survives — "the Spartan mirage" in physical form); but new sanctuary excavations and the Spartan Theater being studied more carefully; Paul Cartledge *Sparta and Lakonia* (new revised ed. 2023) synthesizing latest work
    - **New excavations at Olympia**: German Archaeological Institute ongoing; 2021–2025: new Bronze Age material found beneath Classical levels; hydraulic survey of the Kladeos and Alpheios rivers that flooded and buried ancient Olympia; digital reconstruction of the sanctuary published 2022

#### Ancient History — Ancient Rome

- **Basics & Scope**
  - Ancient Rome: conventional span ~753 BCE (traditional founding) to 476 CE (Romulus Augustulus deposed, Western Empire) or 1453 CE (fall of Constantinople, Eastern Empire/Byzantium)
  - For ancient history purposes the core arc is: Regal Period (~753–509 BCE) → Roman Republic (~509–27 BCE) → Principate/Early Empire (~27 BCE–235 CE) → Crisis of the Third Century (235–284 CE) → Dominate/Late Empire (284–476 CE)
  - Rome's legacy: Roman law (foundation of civil law for ~60% of the world today), Romance languages (200+ million speakers), Christianity's institutional form, town planning, calendar, engineering (aqueducts, roads, concrete), political vocabulary (*republic*, *senate*, *dictator*, *consul*, *veto*, *census*, *province*)
  - Primary sources: Livy (*Ab Urbe Condita*, 142 books, ~35 survive), Polybius (Books 1–6 complete; invaluable for Republic and Punic Wars), Sallust, Julius Caesar (*Gallic War*, *Civil War*), Cicero (~900 surviving letters + speeches + philosophical works), Suetonius (*Lives of the Twelve Caesars*), Tacitus (*Annals*, *Histories*, *Germania*, *Agricola*), Dio Cassius, Appian, Plutarch (*Parallel Lives*), Josephus, Pliny the Elder (*Natural History*), Pliny the Younger, Ammianus Marcellinus (Late Antique), Augustine (*City of God*, *Confessions*)
  - Major reference works: Cambridge Ancient History (vols. 7–14 cover Rome); T. Cornell *The Beginnings of Rome* (1995); Andrew Lintott *The Constitution of the Roman Republic* (1999); Adrian Goldsworthy *Caesar* (2006), *Augustus* (2014); Mary Beard *SPQR* (2015 — bestselling scholarly synthesis); Fergus Millar *The Emperor in the Roman World* (1977); Peter Heather *The Fall of the Roman Empire* (2006); Bryan Ward-Perkins *The Fall of Rome and the End of Civilization* (2005)
  - Key journals: *Journal of Roman Studies* (JRS), *Journal of Roman Archaeology* (JRA), *Britannia*, *Papers of the British School at Rome* (PBSR), *American Journal of Philology*, *Zeitschrift für Papyrologie und Epigraphik* (ZPE)
  - Key excavation sites: Rome (Forum Romanum, Palatine, Colosseum/Flavian Amphitheatre, Circus Maximus), Pompeii and Herculaneum (Vesuvius 79 CE), Ostia Antica, Carthage, Masada, Vindolanda (Hadrian's Wall), bath complexes across the Empire

- **The Regal Period (~753–509 BCE)**
  - **Traditional Founding**: Romulus and Remus legend; twin sons of Mars and Rhea Silvia; suckled by she-wolf (*lupa*); Romulus killed Remus (dispute over which hill to found city on or who saw more birds as omen); date traditionally *ab urbe condita* (AUC 1) = 753/754 BCE by Varro's calculation; modern archaeology places Iron Age settlement on Palatine Hill from ~900–800 BCE, becoming a proto-urban center ~750–700 BCE — consistent with tradition
  - **Seven Kings**: Romulus, Numa Pompilius, Tullus Hostilius, Ancus Marcius, Tarquinius Priscus, Servius Tullius, Tarquinius Superbus (the "Proud"; last king; expelled 509 BCE)
  - **Etruscan influence**: 7th–6th c. BCE Rome profoundly shaped by Etruscans (Tarquins were Etruscan or of Etruscan cultural affiliation); Etruscan alphabet → Latin alphabet; augury, *fasces* (bundle of rods with axe — symbol of magistrate power), triumph ceremony, gladiatorial combat, toga, arch construction techniques — many derived from or mediated through Etruscan culture
  - **Servian Constitution** (attributed to Servius Tullius): centuriate assembly (*comitia centuriata*); citizens divided into 5 property classes supplying different numbers of voting/military centuries; heavily weighted toward wealthy — foundational structure of Republican assemblies
  - **Tarquinius Superbus expelled 509 BCE**: violent rape of Lucretia by Sextus Tarquinius; she told her husband and father, then killed herself; Lucius Junius Brutus (feigning stupidity — *brutus* = "stupid") led revolution; kings replaced by two annually elected consuls; the *founding myth of the Republic* — liberty over tyranny; Lucretia story told by Livy and Dionysius of Halicarnassus; probably contains legendary elements but the end of monarchy ~509 BCE is historically plausible and supported by archaeological evidence (Etruscan goods disappear from Roman sites ~500 BCE)

- **The Roman Republic (~509–27 BCE)**
  - **Constitution**
    - **SPQR**: *Senatus Populusque Romanus* (the Senate and People of Rome) — the eternal formula of Roman public authority
    - Designed with separation/balancing of powers: **magistrates** (executive), **Senate** (deliberative/advisory body of ~300 then ~600 then ~900 ex-magistrates; controlled finances, foreign policy, provinces), **assemblies** (legislative/electoral)
    - **Magistracies** (in ascending *cursus honorum*): quaestor (financial), aedile (public facilities), praetor (courts/military), **consul** (2 per year; highest ordinary magistracy; supreme civil and military authority; years named after them), **censor** (every 5 years; enrolled citizens; supervised public morals; contracted public works); **dictator** (6-month emergency magistrate; appointed by a consul; commanding all); interrex, tribunes of the plebs (see below)
    - **Checks and balances**: annual tenure; collegiality (2 consuls could veto each other); successive offices; *provocatio ad populum* (right of appeal to people against magistrate punishment — basis of Roman liberty)
    - **Senate**: technically advisory but effectively controlled policy; senators held office for life; traditional Roman deference to *mos maiorum* (ancestral custom) made the Senate nearly sovereign in practice
  - **The Struggle of the Orders (~494–287 BCE)**
    - Conflict between patricians (hereditary aristocratic class who monopolized early magistracies, priesthoods, and legal interpretation) and plebeians (everyone else, including many wealthy non-nobles)
    - **First Secession of the Plebs ~494 BCE**: plebeians withdrew to Sacred Mountain, threatening to found own city; patricians capitulated; created **tribunes of the plebs** (*tribuni plebis*): 10 tribunes annually; their persons sacrosanct (inviolable); could veto any magistrate action (*intercessio*); convened *concilium plebis* (plebeian assembly)
    - **Twelve Tables** (~451–450 BCE): first written Roman law code; produced by the Decemviri (board of ten); published in Forum; made law accessible to all — not just patrician priests who previously controlled legal interpretation; originals lost (destroyed when Gauls sacked Rome 390 BCE) but contents reconstructed from fragments and citations; include property, inheritance, debt, delict law
    - **Licinio-Sextian Laws 367 BCE**: one consul must be plebeian; opened highest magistracy to non-nobles
    - **Lex Hortensia 287 BCE**: *plebiscita* (decisions of plebeian assembly) binding on all Romans without Senate ratification; nominal end of the Struggle; in practice a wealthy plebeian aristocracy (*nobilitas*) had merged with patricians into a governing oligarchy
  - **Roman Religion**
    - Polytheistic; state religion a civic duty (*pietas* = proper fulfillment of religious obligations); gods of the Roman state (*di indigetes* and *di novensiles*); no personal salvation doctrine or creed; *pax deorum* (peace of the gods) maintained by correct ritual
    - **Main gods**: Jupiter (Optimus Maximus — king of gods; Capitoline Triad with Juno and Minerva), Mars (war; also agriculture), Janus (beginnings; two-faced; January), Vesta (hearth fire; Vestal Virgins — 6 priestesses sworn to celibacy for 30 years; tending eternal flame of Rome; profound sacral importance; death penalty for violating vows), Neptune, Vulcan, Diana, Venus, Mercury, Ceres, Bacchus (Dionysus)
    - **Priesthoods**: pontifices (led by Pontifex Maximus — Caesar then emperors took this title; *pope* derives from it), augures (read omens — bird flight, animal entrails), haruspices (Etruscan-derived; read livers of sacrifice animals), fetiales (war/treaty rituals), flamines (dedicated priests of specific deities)
    - **Roman calendar**: originally lunar (~10 months); reformed by Julius Caesar (Julian Calendar, 46 BCE — solar year of 365.25 days; essentially our modern calendar until Gregorian reform 1582); month names preserve old deities and emperors (January/Janus, March/Mars, July/Julius Caesar, August/Augustus)
    - **Augury and auspices** (*auspicium* = watching birds): magistrates took auspices before major decisions; *ex avibus* (from birds); a bad omen could halt proceedings; deeply embedded in constitution
    - **Lares and Penates**: household gods; family religion alongside public; small shrines in every home (*lararium*); the Roman pater familias conducted daily domestic cult
    - **Roman mythology**: largely borrowed and adapted from Greek; Virgil's *Aeneid* (19 BCE) connected Rome to Troy (Aeneas, Trojan hero, ancestor of Romans and of Julius Caesar's gens Iulia via Venus); Ovid's *Metamorphoses* (8 CE) — systematic poetic mythology; Livy's narratives blending history and foundation myths
  - **Early Roman Expansion in Italy**
    - **Samnite Wars (~343–290 BCE)**: three wars against Samnites (fierce mountain warriors of central Italy); Caudine Forks 321 BCE — entire Roman army humiliated, forced to "pass under the yoke"; eventual Roman victory extended control across Italian peninsula
    - **Latin Wars (~340–338 BCE)**: Rome defeated Latin League; dissolved it; individual Latin cities bound to Rome by separate treaties (divide and rule); some given full citizenship, others *Latin rights* (partial citizenship)
    - **Pyrrhic War (~280–275 BCE)**: **Pyrrhus of Epirus** (Alexander's cousin; brilliant tactician) invaded Italy to support Tarentum against Rome; won battles (Heraclea 280 BCE, Asculum 279 BCE) at such heavy cost he reportedly said "Another such victory and I am undone" — origin of "Pyrrhic victory"; departed for Sicily; Romans rebuilt armies; Pyrrhus returned, defeated at Beneventum 275 BCE; left Italy; Rome mastered entire Italian peninsula by ~265 BCE
    - **Roman alliance system**: *foedus* (treaty) — allied cities contributed troops but retained local autonomy; Rome owned the foreign policy; allied troops (*socii*) eventually constituted most Roman armies; Social War 91–87 BCE when allies demanded citizenship
  - **The Punic Wars (~264–146 BCE)**
    - Three wars against Carthage (dominant maritime power of western Mediterranean; Phoenician colony in N. Africa, modern Tunisia; ~500,000 population; empire in Spain, Sardinia, Sicily)
    - **First Punic War (~264–241 BCE)**: began in Sicily; Rome built first major navy almost from scratch; used *corvus* (boarding bridge) to neutralize Carthaginian seamanship advantage; won Battle of the Egadi Islands 241 BCE; Carthage ceded Sicily (Rome's first province); Hamilcar Barca sent to Spain to rebuild Carthaginian power
    - **Second Punic War (Hannibalic War, ~218–201 BCE)**:
      - **Hannibal Barca** (~247–183 BCE): greatest general of antiquity (with Alexander); son of Hamilcar; crossed Alps with ~37,000 infantry, 8,000 cavalry, 37 war elephants in winter 218 BCE; three massive Roman defeats: Trebia 218 BCE, Lake Trasimene 217 BCE (~15,000 Romans killed), **Cannae 216 BCE** (~50,000–70,000 Romans killed in one afternoon — possibly the most studied battle in military history; double envelopment tactic; **model for Schlieffen Plan 1905**)
      - Rome refused to surrender; sent Fabius Maximus as dictator — "Fabian" strategy of attrition, avoiding battle; Hannibal devastated Italy for 15 years but could not take Rome (no siege equipment); Capua and other Italian cities defected to Hannibal; Syracuse fell to Rome (Archimedes killed defending it 212 BCE); Spain contested
      - **Scipio Africanus** (~236–183 BCE): captured Carthago Nova (Spain) 209 BCE; won Battle of Ilipa (Spain) 206 BCE; took war to Africa; forced Hannibal to return; **Battle of Zama 202 BCE**: Scipio defeated Hannibal; Carthage surrendered — lost Spain, paid war indemnity, navy reduced; Hannibal fled to east, took poison when extradition threatened
      - **Polybius** (~200–118 BCE): Greek hostage taken to Rome; eyewitness witness of Scipio Aemilianus; wrote *Histories* explaining how Rome conquered the Mediterranean in 53 years; key concept: Roman **mixed constitution** (consuls = monarchy, Senate = aristocracy, assemblies = democracy) in balanced harmony prevents decay; most important ancient historian for understanding Republican Rome
    - **Third Punic War (~149–146 BCE)**: Cato the Elder's relentless "Carthago delenda est" (Carthage must be destroyed); Rome found pretext; Carthage besieged 3 years; **Scipio Aemilianus** razed Carthage 146 BCE; enslaved survivors; salted the earth (legend — possibly not literally true); same year: Rome sacked Corinth; Rome master of Mediterranean world
  - **Roman Engineering and Infrastructure**
    - **Roads** (*via*): All roads lead to Rome — literally; ~400,000 km of roads eventually; military, commercial, administrative arteries; **Via Appia** (Appian Way, ~312 BCE): first great road, Rome → Brindisi (Adriatic); typical construction: *statumen* (stone foundation), *rudus* (rubble), *nucleus* (concrete), *summum dorsum* (polygonal stone paving, slightly cambered for drainage); milestones (*miliarium*) marking distance from Golden Milestone (*miliarium aureum*) in Forum
    - **Aqueducts**: Rome eventually had 11 major aqueducts supplying ~1 million cubic meters/day; greatest engineering achievement before the industrial age; **Aqua Appia** (312 BCE, first), **Aqua Marcia** (144 BCE, best quality), **Aqua Claudia** (52 CE); gravity-fed from mountains; underground channels, arched bridges spanning valleys (Pont du Gard, France — the most visually stunning surviving example, 49 m high); Frontinus *De Aquis Urbis Romae* (97 CE): commissioner of water supply describes the system with modern-sounding bureaucratic precision; Rome's freshwater abundance enabled dense urban population and public bathing culture
    - **Roman concrete** (*opus caementicium*): volcanic ash (*pozzolana*) + lime + sea water = hydraulic concrete; set underwater; incredibly durable; enabled Pantheon's 43.4 m unreinforced dome (still the world's largest); harbors like Caesarea Maritima built with concrete piers underwater; modern scientists analyzing Roman concrete as model for carbon-reduced, ultra-durable modern construction (MIT study 2023 — seawater interaction crystallizes *tobermorite* mineral, self-healing cracks)
    - **Arch and vault**: key to Roman monumental construction; triumphal arches (Titus, Septimius Severus, Constantine); barrel vaults; groined vaults; the dome; Pantheon's coffered concrete dome (125 CE): 43.4 m diameter; oculus (9 m opening); cast without formwork by removing rotating support; still in use as a church; mathematically proportioned (sphere fits inside the structure)
    - **Baths** (*thermae*): Roman social infrastructure; Baths of Caracalla (~216 CE): 1,600 bathers simultaneously; mosaics, libraries, gardens; Baths of Diocletian: even larger; admission free or minimal; open to all classes; social equalizer; hot room (*caldarium*), warm (*tepidarium*), cold (*frigidarium*); underfloor heating (*hypocaust*)
    - **Colosseum** (Flavian Amphitheatre, 70–80 CE): inaugurated by Titus; ~50,000–80,000 spectators; travertine limestone, tufa, brick, concrete; 80 arched entrances (*vomitoria*) for crowd flow management; *velarium* (awning); subterranean *hypogeum* (chambers, lifts, trapdoors for animals and fighters); gladiatorial games (*munera*), animal hunts (*venationes*), occasional mock sea battles (*naumachiae*); operational ~500 years
  - **The Late Republic and the Fall of the Republic (~133–27 BCE)**
    - **Tiberius and Gaius Gracchus (~133–121 BCE)**: tribunes who attempted land reform (*lex agraria*) redistributing ager publicus (public land illegally occupied by wealthy); broke Senate norms by going directly to assemblies; Tiberius killed in mob violence 133 BCE (first political assassination in Republic since Regal period); Gaius killed 121 BCE; marks the beginning of Rome's long political breakdown
    - **Marius and Sulla (~107–78 BCE)**:
      - **Gaius Marius** (~157–86 BCE): seven consulships (unprecedented); rewrote Roman army — **Marian Military Reforms**: eliminated property qualification; soldiers provided equipment by state; legions tied to individual commanders; made soldiers loyal to general rather than state; **long-term consequence**: civil wars; first march on Rome 88 BCE (by Sulla, reaction to Marius's political maneuver)
      - **Lucius Cornelius Sulla** (~138–78 BCE): besieged Athens; defeated Mithridates; **first march on Rome with army 88 BCE** (first time a Roman used his legions against the city); second civil war 83–82 BCE; won; became dictator; proscriptions (published death/confiscation lists — ~1,500 senators and knights killed); set Senate back to 600; resigned dictatorship 79 BCE (unique in Roman history); died 78 BCE
    - **Pompey, Crassus, Caesar — the Late Republic**
      - **Pompey (Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus, ~106–48 BCE)**: extraordinary military career — conquered Asia Minor, Syria, Judaea (entered Temple of Jerusalem 63 BCE — "desecration" or curious inspection, he found nothing behind Holy of Holies curtain); cleared Mediterranean of pirates in 67 BCE (in 3 months); commanded East; dominated Rome
      - **Marcus Crassus (~115–53 BCE)**: richest man in Rome; suppressed Spartacus slave revolt 73–71 BCE (**Spartacus**: Thracian gladiator; led ~70,000 slaves; defeated multiple Roman armies; final battle Lucania 71 BCE; 6,000 crucified along Via Appia from Capua to Rome — Crassus's display); died at **Battle of Carrhae 53 BCE** (disastrous invasion of Parthia; Parthian cavalry destroyed Roman legions with arrows; Crassus killed; 10,000 Romans captured — some reached Chinese sources as mercenaries, per debated "Liqian" hypothesis)
      - **Julius Caesar (~100–44 BCE)**:
        - Early career: quaestor, aedile (spent lavishly; incurred enormous debt; financed by Crassus); praetor; governed Spain; triumphant return; sought consulship 59 BCE
        - **First Triumvirate 60 BCE**: informal alliance of Caesar, Pompey, Crassus (not constitutional; *amicitia* = personal relation); Caesar got consulship 59 BCE; passed Pompey's land grants for veterans and other legislation by force; governorship of Gaul arranged
        - **Gallic Wars (~58–50 BCE)**: *Commentarii de Bello Gallico* (7 books by Caesar, 8th by Hirtius); masterpiece of Latin prose; self-promotion as official dispatch; conquered all of Gaul (modern France, Belgium, parts of Germany, Switzerland); two expeditions to Britain (55, 54 BCE — reconnaissance, not conquest); **Battle of Alesia 52 BCE**: besieged Gallic chieftain Vercingetorix in Alesia; simultaneously repulsed huge Gallic relief army; textbook siege and counter-siege; 1 million Gauls alleged killed, 1 million enslaved (Caesar's own figures, probably inflated); modern archaeological verification of Alesia fortifications by aerial photography + excavation
        - **Crossing the Rubicon January 49 BCE**: Rubicon River was legal boundary of Caesar's province; bringing army across = declaration of war on Republic; Pompey and Senate fled Italy; Caesar pursued; **civil war 49–45 BCE**: Spain, Greece (Battle of Pharsalus 48 BCE — decisive defeat of Pompey), Egypt (met Cleopatra VII; Battle of the Nile; Caesar's son Caesarion), Africa (Thapsus 46 BCE), Spain (Munda 45 BCE)
        - **Dictator perpetuo** (dictator in perpetuity) — early 44 BCE; unprecedented; raised fears of kingship (offered diadem at Lupercalia festival — refused?); planning enormous wars (Parthia, Danube)
        - **Ides of March, 15 March 44 BCE**: assassinated by ~60 conspirators (senators) including Brutus and Cassius; 23 stab wounds; fell at foot of Pompey's statue in Theatre of Pompey; *Sic semper tyrannis* (never recorded as being said); Senate chamber where he was killed — confirmed by archaeological excavation 2012 at the Largo di Torre Argentina site (the *damnatio memoriae* — memory damnation — of the spot)
      - **Caesar's reforms**: Julian Calendar (46 BCE); land grants for veterans; planned massive public works; expanded Senate to 900 (diluting its prestige); Romanization of provinces; colonization of Carthage and Corinth; remarkable clemency to defeated enemies — politically, this allowed his assassins to survive and organize
    - **Mark Antony, Octavian, and the Last Civil Wars (~44–31 BCE)**
      - **Second Triumvirate 43 BCE**: formal constitutional commission (unlike First); Octavian (Caesar's adopted heir), Mark Antony, Lepidus; **Proscriptions** renewed — Cicero killed on Antony's orders (his hands and head nailed to Rostra in Forum; Antony's wife Fulvia reportedly pierced his tongue with hairpin)
      - **Philippi 42 BCE**: Brutus and Cassius defeated; committed suicide; Republic de facto ended
      - **Conflict divides**: Octavian (West), Antony (East with Cleopatra VII); Antony married Cleopatra; donated Roman territories to her children (*Donations of Alexandria* 34 BCE); Octavian propaganda made Antony's Cleopatra entanglement seem like surrender of Roman identity to Oriental queen
      - **Battle of Actium 31 BCE**: Octavian's general Agrippa defeated Antony and Cleopatra's fleet off Greece; Antony and Cleopatra fled to Egypt; committed suicide 30 BCE; Egypt became Roman province; last Hellenistic kingdom absorbed; Mediterranean world united under one ruler

- **The Principate (~27 BCE–235 CE)**
  - **Augustus (~63 BCE–14 CE; ruled 27 BCE–14 CE)**
    - Born Gaius Octavius; adopted by Julius Caesar's will; renamed C. Julius Caesar Octavianus; eventually called **Augustus** ("revered": honorific voted by Senate January 27 BCE — the formal date of the Principate beginning)
    - **Constitutional settlement 27 BCE**: preserved all Republican forms; Augustus held *imperium proconsulare maius* (supreme military command over all provinces with armies) + *tribunicia potestas* (tribunician power = personal inviolability + power to veto + convene assembly and Senate) annually renewed; called himself *princeps* (first citizen — the "Principate") not *rex* or *dictator*; the fiction was brilliantly effective — Romans could pretend the Republic still existed
    - **Pax Romana**: ~200 years of Mediterranean peace under imperial system (with intermittent civil wars and frontier wars); dramatic population and economic growth; trade from Britain to India to China
    - **Augustus's building program**: "I found Rome a city of brick and left it a city of marble" (Suetonius); Forum of Augustus with Temple of Mars Ultor (Mars the Avenger); Theatre of Marcellus; restored 82 temples; Ara Pacis Augustae (Altar of Augustan Peace, 9 BCE — marble bas-relief masterpiece; exquisite procession frieze; reassembled in modern museum cover 1938)
    - **Virgil (70–19 BCE)**: *Aeneid* — Latin national epic; 12 books; Trojan hero Aeneas founds Rome; Augustan ideology embedded in myth; written at Augustus' implicit commission; Virgil died before it was finished; asked it be burned; Augustus had it published; *Georgics* (praise of Italian agriculture), *Eclogues* (pastoral poetry)
    - **Horace (65–8 BCE)**: Carmen Saeculare (Secular Hymn for the Ludi Saeculares 17 BCE); *Odes*, *Satires*, *Epistles*; *Ars Poetica*; "carpe diem" (seize the day, Odes 1.11)
    - **Ovid (43 BCE–17/18 CE)**: *Metamorphoses* (transformation mythology); *Ars Amatoria*; *Amores*; exiled by Augustus to Tomis (Black Sea) in 8 CE — reasons unclear (probably *Ars Amatoria* + some personal scandal connected to Julia's affair); died in exile; his exile as instance of Augustan cultural control
    - **Livy (59 BCE–17 CE)**: *Ab Urbe Condita* (From the Foundation of the City): 142 books covering 753–9 BCE; 35 survive; magnificent historical narrative; moral exempla driving narrative more than critical analysis; Tacitus later contrasts his own darker vision with Livy's patriotic optimism
    - **Augustus's succession problem**: no sons; adopted Tiberius (stepson) as heir; but manipulated many potential successors; complex dynastic politics; **Julio-Claudian Dynasty** founded
  - **The Julio-Claudian Emperors (14–68 CE)**
    - **Tiberius (14–37 CE)**: capable administrator; withdrawn; retired to Capri 27 CE; **Sejanus** (Praetorian Prefect) exercised enormous power during his absence; treason trials (*maiestas*) multiplied; Sejanus fell 31 CE (executed); Tiberius died 37 CE on Capri; Suetonius's scandalous accounts of his vices on Capri — reliability disputed
    - **Caligula (37–41 CE)**: popular at first; apparently suffered serious illness ~37 CE that may have neurologically changed him; reportedly declared himself a god; humiliated senators; made his horse a consul (probably satirical rather than literal); assassinated by Praetorian tribunes 41 CE after 4 years of increasing terror and irrationality; mental illness vs. historical exaggeration — modern debate (Aloys Winterling *Caligula: A Biography*)
    - **Claudius (41–54 CE)**: acclaimed emperor by Praetorians after Caligula's death; had slight physical disability (limp, stammer) that excluded him from public life → made him a voracious scholar; excellent administrator; conquered Britain 43 CE; extended citizenship; used freedmen as powerful administrative secretaries (Narcissus, Pallas) — resented by aristocrats; poisoned by wife Agrippina the Younger 54 CE (mushrooms); **Robert Graves** *I Claudius* (1934) — masterpiece historical novel
    - **Nero (54–68 CE)**: first 5 years (*quinquennium Neronis*) praised by even Trajan as a good period — guided by Burrus and Seneca; then: killed mother Agrippina 59 CE, killed wife Octavia 62 CE, married Poppaea Sabina (kicked her to death while pregnant 65 CE), **Great Fire of Rome 64 CE** (burned 10 of 14 districts; Nero blamed Christians — first Roman persecution; Tacitus *Annals* 15.44: earliest pagan reference to Christ and Christians and Pontius Pilate); sang on stage (shocking); Golden House (*Domus Aurea*): enormous palace complex covering 100 hectares; committed suicide 68 CE "What an artist dies in me!"
  - **The Year of the Four Emperors (69 CE)**
    - After Nero's death: Galba, Otho, Vitellius, and Vespasian each briefly emperor; shows political fragility — soldiers make and break emperors; Vespasian wins
  - **The Flavian Dynasty (69–96 CE)**
    - **Vespasian (69–79 CE)**: down-to-earth, practical soldier; restored finances; began Colosseum (Flavian Amphitheatre)
    - **Destruction of Jerusalem 70 CE**: Jewish Revolt (66–73 CE); Vespasian campaigns; Titus completes siege; Temple destroyed (never rebuilt; Western Wall surviving retaining wall); Arch of Titus (Forum) shows Menorah carried in triumph; mass enslavement of Jews; **Masada** (73/74 CE): last holdout; Sicarii Jews committed mass suicide rather than surrender — Jewish historical memory; Josephus *Bellum Judaicum* (*Jewish War*) — invaluable source; written to explain the Jewish defeat to a Roman and Jewish audience
    - **Pompeii and Herculaneum destroyed 79 CE**: Vesuvius eruption; Pliny the Younger's letters to Tacitus (*Epistles* 6.16, 6.20) — two most important first-hand disaster narratives from antiquity; Pliny the Elder (natural historian; uncle) died observing the eruption; **lava/pyroclastic surge** preserved both cities; systematic excavation since ~1748; extraordinary finds: complete town plan, paintings, mosaics, graffiti (*writing is everywhere* — gladiator ratings, personal insults, election notices, philosophical quotes on tavern walls), **Pompeii as archaeological laboratory for Roman daily life**; ongoing **New Pompeii Project** (**Grande Progetto Pompeii**): since 2012, major new excavations; 2018–2026 spectacular finds
    - **Domitian (81–96 CE)**: Titus's brother; efficient administrator; aggressive frontier policy (Dacian wars, Agricola's conquest of much of Britain per Tacitus); became increasingly absolutist; executed senators; killed by household conspiracy; Senate declared *damnatio memoriae* (official condemnation of memory); Pliny the Younger, Tacitus, Suetonius wrote under his shadow — their hostile accounts color tradition
  - **The "Five Good Emperors" — Nerva-Antonine Dynasty (96–180 CE)**
    - Gibbon (*Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire*, 1776–1789): "the period in the history of the world during which the condition of the human race was most happy and prosperous" — criticized as Eurocentric but reflects real stability
    - **Nerva (96–98 CE)**: elderly senator chosen to end Domitianic terror; established adoption as succession principle; adopted Trajan
    - **Trajan (98–117 CE)**: *optimus princeps* (best emperor); Spanish-born — first non-Italian emperor; **Dacian Wars** (101–102, 105–106 CE): defeated Dacians; created province of Dacia (modern Romania); **Trajan's Column** (113 CE): 30 m spiral narrative relief sculpture chronicling the wars; one of the greatest visual narratives from antiquity; shows siege techniques, river crossings, Dacian prisoners; **Forum of Trajan** (Apollodorus of Damascus): grandest of all Imperial Fora; **Trajan's Markets** (multi-story commercial complex; possibly world's first "shopping mall"); **Parthian campaign 116 CE**: reached Persian Gulf — maximum Roman territorial extent; territory largely abandoned by Hadrian
    - **Hadrian (117–138 CE)**: architect-emperor; consolidated rather than expanded; **Hadrian's Wall** (122–128 CE): 117 km coast to coast across northern Britain; most important surviving Roman frontier monument; **Pantheon** rebuilt (120–124 CE): as we know it; **Hadrian's Villa (Tivoli)**: enormous complex expressing his personal aesthetic and philosophical interests; traveled extensively; Greek cultural devotee; unofficial capital briefly at Athens; **Antinous**: his young Greek lover; drowned in Nile 130 CE; grief-stricken Hadrian deified him, founded city *Antinoopolis*, created cult — most famous and explicit expression of Roman imperial same-sex love
    - **Antoninus Pius (138–161 CE)**: peaceful, prosperous reign; **Antonine Wall** (Scotland, ~140 CE — brief occupation, abandoned ~160 CE); the empire's most stable decades
    - **Marcus Aurelius (161–180 CE)**: philosopher-emperor; Stoic; *Meditations* (personal journal in Greek; 12 books; "never written for publication" — closest thing to a saint's inner life from antiquity); fought continuous **Marcomannic Wars** on Danube 166–180 CE (German tribal invasions triggered by Hunnic pressure from east — early sign of coming migrations); **Antonine Plague** (~165–180 CE): smallpox epidemic killing ~5 million; Marcus died of plague 180 CE on campaign at Vindobona (Vienna); broke with adoption succession — left empire to his son Commodus (*a mistake that ended the golden age*, Gibbon)
    - **Commodus (180–192 CE)**: increasingly identified himself with Hercules; renamed Rome *Colonia Commodiana*; fought in arena as gladiator (senators compelled to watch); assassinated by wrestling partner on New Year's Eve 192 CE
  - **The Severan Dynasty and the Third Century Crisis**
    - **Septimius Severus (193–211 CE)**: African-born (Leptis Magna, Libya); won civil war Year of the Five Emperors; **militarized the monarchy** — "enrich the soldiers, ignore everyone else" (deathbed advice to sons); doubled legionary pay; Arch of Septimius Severus in Forum; died at Eboracum (York, Britain) while campaigning
    - **Caracalla (211–217 CE)**: killed brother Geta; **Constitutio Antoniniana 212 CE** — extended Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants of the Empire; massive fiscal measure (more people paying citizenship taxes); Baths of Caracalla (~216 CE); assassinated by Praetorian soldier while urinating on campaign
    - **Elagabalus (218–222 CE)** and **Alexander Severus (222–235 CE)**: increasing instability; Elagabalus's transgressive behavior (reportedly wore women's clothes, offered self as prostitute, installed Syrian sun god as supreme deity)

- **The Crisis of the Third Century (235–284 CE)**
  - Near-collapse of the Roman state; ~50 emperors in 49 years (most died violently); barbarian invasions on multiple frontiers simultaneously; plague (**Plague of Cyprian** ~250–262 CE: estimated 5,000 deaths/day in Rome at peak); economic disruption (coin debasement → inflation); **Empire temporarily fragmented**: Gallic Empire (West), Palmyrene Empire (East) under Queen Zenobia (~270–272 CE), Roman Empire (center)
  - **Aurelian (270–275 CE)** "Restorer of the World" (*Restitutor Orbis*): reunified empire militarily; built Aurelian Walls around Rome (~271–275 CE: 19 km, 13 m high — still largely standing); defeated Zenobia; assassinated; his wall-building shows how destabilized even Rome itself had become
  - **Economic crisis**: silver content of *denarius* fell from ~85% under Nero to ~2.5% by 268 CE; hyperinflation; collapse of long-distance trade; urban populations fell; villa economy replaced market economy in much of West

- **The Dominate and Late Empire (284–476 CE)**
  - **Diocletian (284–305 CE)**: bureaucratic reformer; expanded bureaucracy enormously; reorganized provinces (doubled their number); **Tetrarchy** (rule of four): 2 Augusti + 2 Caesares governing different regions — attempt to solve succession and frontier defense; **Edict on Maximum Prices 301 CE** (attempt to control inflation; failed — merchants hid goods); **Great Persecution of Christians** (303–311 CE) — last and most severe Roman persecution; abdicated 305 CE (unique among emperors who died in office)
  - **Constantine I (306–337 CE)**:
    - Won civil wars against other tetrarchs; sole ruler by 324 CE
    - **Battle of Milvian Bridge 312 CE**: defeated Maxentius at Rome's northern approach; reportedly saw cross-in-sky vision (*In hoc signo vinces* = "In this sign you will conquer"); painted Chi-Rho on soldiers' shields; won; became champion of Christianity
    - **Edict of Milan 313 CE**: co-issued with Licinius; granted religious tolerance throughout Empire; restored confiscated Christian property; Christianity no longer persecuted
    - **Council of Nicaea 325 CE**: first Ecumenical Council; settled Arian controversy (*Arianism*: Christ was a created being, subordinate to God the Father — Arius of Alexandria; **Homoousios** vs. **Homoiousios** debate — whether Christ was "of the same substance" or merely "of similar substance" to the Father); **Nicene Creed** established; Arianism condemned; but persisted among Germanic tribes as they were converted by Arian missionaries → later theological conflict
    - **Constantinople founded 330 CE**: on site of Byzantium (Greek colony on Bosphorus); called "New Rome"; extraordinary strategic position (Europe-Asia crossing; defensible peninsula); built at enormous speed; endowed with monuments, Senate, grain dole; eventually became largest city in medieval Europe; survived until 1453
    - **Church building program**: **Hagia Sophia** (1st version), **Church of the Holy Sepulchre** (Jerusalem), **St. Peter's Basilica** (Rome) — all Constantinian foundations; enormous patronage; Christianity transformed from persecuted sect to state church
  - **Theodosius I (379–395 CE)**:
    - **Edict of Thessalonica 380 CE**: Nicene Christianity made the official state religion; paganism progressively banned; temples closed
    - Defeated last pagan pretender (Eugenius, 394 CE) — final Pagan-Christian civil war
    - Died 395 CE; **divided Empire between his sons**: Honorius (West) and Arcadius (East) — never formally reunited
    - **Olympic Games ended ~393–394 CE** (Theodosius banned pagan festivals)
  - **The Fall of the Western Empire**
    - **Sack of Rome 410 CE by Visigoths** (under Alaric): psychological earthquake for the entire Mediterranean world; Augustine began writing *City of God* in response; Rome had not been sacked for 800 years (since Gauls 390 BCE)
    - **Attila the Hun** (~406–453 CE): led Hunnic Empire from central Asia / Pannonia; extorted enormous tribute from Eastern Empire; invaded West 451 CE; **Battle of the Catalaunian Plains (Châlons) 451 CE**: Aëtius + Visigoths stopped Attila in Gaul — "the last victory of Rome"; Attila invaded Italy 452 CE but spared Rome (negotiations with Pope Leo I — "Scourge of God" rhetoric); died 453 CE (nosebleed on wedding night); Hunnic Empire collapsed
    - **Odoacer 476 CE**: Germanic chieftain deposed last Western emperor Romulus Augustulus; no new emperor appointed; sent Western imperial regalia to Constantinople; **conventional date for Fall of Western Roman Empire**; debate: Gibbon blamed Christianity; Peter Heather blames external barbarian pressure; Bryan Ward-Perkins emphasizes genuine civilizational collapse in material culture; *Eastern Empire (Byzantium) continued until 1453*

- **Roman Law**
  - **Twelve Tables** (~451–450 BCE): see above; foundational codification
  - **Roman legal development**: by Late Republic, enormous body of juristic interpretation (*ius civile*); **praetor's edict** (*ius honorarium*): each year incoming praetor published edicts of rules he would apply; accumulated into standard (*edictum perpetuum* codified by Hadrian ~130 CE); **ius gentium** (law of peoples): applied to non-citizens; more flexible; became basis of international law concepts
  - **Classical jurisprudence (1st–3rd c. CE)**: greatest jurists: **Gaius** (*Institutiones* ~161 CE: foundational teaching text); **Papinian** (~142–212 CE: greatest jurist; Caracalla killed him); **Ulpian** (~170–223 CE: "Justice is the constant and perpetual will to render to each his due" — definition of law still cited); **Paul** (~died after 235 CE); enormous commentaries on the praetor's edict; *Digest* later compiled from their writings
  - **Justinian's Codification (529–534 CE)**:
    - Eastern Emperor Justinian I; commission led by Tribonian
    - **Codex Justinianus** (529, revised 534): imperial constitutions; **Digest** (*Pandectae*, 533): 50 books; extracted texts from 39 classical jurists (~3 million words condensed to 150,000); **Institutes** (533): introductory law text based on Gaius; **Novels** (*Novellae*): new legislation after 534
    - Together: **Corpus Juris Civilis** (the Body of Civil Law); rediscovered in Bologna ~1070; taught at Bologna University (first European university); the foundation of **civil law systems** covering continental Europe, Latin America, Quebec, Louisiana, Scotland, sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia; one of the most consequential documents in human history

- **Roman Daily Life**
  - **Population**: Rome ~1 million at peak (Augustus–Marcus Aurelius era); only premodern city to reach this size until 19th century London; Alexandria and Carthage ~500,000; Antioch and Ephesus ~300,000
  - **Social structure**: senators (600 families; property requirement 1 million *sestertii*); equestrians (*equites*: business/military class; 400,000 *sestertii* requirement); **decurions** (local municipal elite); **common people** (*plebs urbana*); **slaves** (~30–40% of Italian population; ~15–20% of Empire-wide; slave trade integral to economy); **freedmen** (*liberti*): freed slaves became Roman citizens but retained obligations to former masters; could rise very high — imperial freedmen held enormous administrative power (see Claudius's secretaries above)
  - **Housing**: wealthy → *domus* (urban townhouse with atrium, garden, *triclinium* dining room); very wealthy → *villa* (country estate); poor → *insulae* (apartment blocks of 5–8 stories; ground floor commercial/wealthy; upper floors crowded and dangerous — fire risk; collapse risk; no running water above ground floor); Pompeii and Ostia reveal insula archaeology
  - **Food**: Mediterranean diet staples (bread, oil, wine, fish sauce *garum*, legumes); cheap street food from *thermopolia* (fast food counters — 80+ found at Pompeii); grain dole (*annona*) to ~200,000+ citizens in Rome; massive grain imports from Sicily, North Africa, Egypt; Roman cookbook: Apicius *De Re Coquinaria* (~4th–5th c. CE compilation of earlier recipes)
  - **Slavery and Economy**: M.I. Finley *The Ancient Economy* (1973) — Rome had a "pre-market" economy (status and politics driving economic decisions, not profit maximization); heavily contested by modern scholars; Keith Hopkins on growth, trade, and taxes; recent Roman economy scholarship: more sophisticated market behavior than Finley allowed; new quantitative data from lead pollution in Greenland ice cores tracking Roman economic activity

- **Key Figures Summary**
  - **Military/Political**: Scipio Africanus, Marius, Sulla, Pompey, Julius Caesar, Augustus, Trajan, Hadrian, Marcus Aurelius, Diocletian, Constantine, Theodosius
  - **Literary/Intellectual**: Cicero, Virgil, Horace, Ovid, Livy, Lucretius, Seneca, Pliny the Elder, Pliny the Younger, Tacitus, Suetonius, Juvenal (satires), Martial (epigrams), Apuleius (*The Golden Ass* — only complete surviving Latin novel), Ammianus Marcellinus
  - **Historical/Philosophical**: Polybius, Josephus, Plutarch, Dio Cassius, Augustine, Boethius (*Consolation of Philosophy* — written while awaiting execution 524 CE; last major work of classical Latin literature)

- **Frontier/Contemporary Research (2020–2026)**
  - **Vindolanda Tablets (Hadrian's Wall)**: annual excavations at Vindolanda fort; wooden writing tablets preserved in anaerobic soil; ~2,000 recovered since 1973; world's oldest surviving handwritten Latin documents; reveal everyday military life — duty rosters, supply requests, personal letters; **2017**: tablet with earliest known map of a Roman road in Britain; **2022–2025**: new tablet finds each season; Alan Bowman and David Thomas editions; British Museum
  - **Pompeii New Excavations (2018–2026)**:
    - **Regio V** systematic excavations: 2018: "Frozen in time" fast-food shop with intact food in containers; 2019: electoral propaganda frescos, chariot; 2020: **Thermopolium of Regio V** fully excavated — duck bones, pork, snails, fish in serving containers; 2021: bakery with chained slaves' quarters (evidence of forced labour in baking); 2022: 2 victims in the House of Civitas near the Herculaneum Gate — sheltered from eruption then killed by pyroclastic surge; **sacred room with ritual objects** (miniature amphorae, terracotta figurines); 2023: stunning fresco of Helen and Paris in a banquet room; 2024: new atrium house excavation; intricate garden with charred wooden furniture; **Pompeii continues to rewrite daily Roman life understanding every year**
  - **Roman concrete research**: 2023 MIT study (Marie Jackson team, *Science*): seawater reaction with volcanic ash created *phillipsite* and *tobermorite Al* crystals — self-healing concrete; Roman harbour concrete from Caesarea Maritima, Pozzuoli analyzed; insight for modern sustainable concrete design; Roman concrete contains no steel (no rebar) yet structural strength rivals or exceeds modern concrete for certain applications
  - **Ancient DNA of Roman-era populations**: 2019 Vagene et al. *Current Biology*: first aDNA analysis of ancient Rome's multi-ethnic population — 127 individuals spanning ~12,000 years; Iron Age Romans resembled other European farmers; Imperial Rome showed influx of Near Eastern ancestry from massive population movements; Late Antique period: partial reversion toward earlier European profiles as Empire contracted; reveals Roman Empire's astonishing demographic cosmopolitanism; 2022–2025: ongoing refinement with larger samples; aDNA from provincial sites (Britain, North Africa, Danubian provinces)
  - **New Roman shipwrecks**: Mediterranean archaeology; photogrammetry and survey robots locating hundreds of previously unknown wrecks; amphorae cargo analysis revealing trade networks; Sicilian shelf surveys
  - **Digital Humanities**: **ORBIS** (Stanford network model of Roman world — travel time and cost between any two cities; http://orbis.stanford.edu); **Epigraphik-Datenbank Clauss-Slaby** (~800,000 Latin inscriptions); **Epigraphic Database Roma** (EDR); **EAGLE Network** — European digital Latin epigraphy
  - **Roman army studies**: *Vindolanda Tablets Online*; new papyri from Egypt (military pay records, lists of soldier origins); 2021 Roman cavalry tombstone found Carlisle (UK): includes text identifying Lucius Junius Victorinus, first direct physical evidence of Hadrianic-era unit deployment; LiDAR mapping of Roman marching camps across Scotland revealing planned network of Agricola's advance
  - **Late Antique and Christian archaeology**: ongoing excavations at Constantinople before Istanbul construction projects; survey of Roman roads in Jordan, Tunisia; Dura-Europos fresh analysis of earliest Christian church fresco (c. 230 CE); discoveries at Baelo Claudia (Spain), Volubilis (Morocco), Leptis Magna (Libya — security permitting)
  - **Historiographic debates**:
    - **Fall of Rome debate**: Gibbon (Christianity + internal decay) vs. Heather (*The Fall of the Roman Empire* 2006 — external barbarian pressure primary) vs. Ward-Perkins (*The Fall of Rome and the End of Civilization* 2005 — genuine civilizational collapse, contra "transformation" scholars) vs. Walter Goffart (*Barbarians and Romans* 1980 — "accommodation" model: barbarians invited in, not invasive) vs. **Bryan Ward-Perkins** environmental-economic collapse model; **Kyle Harper** *The Fate of Rome* (2017): climate change (Roman Climate Optimum followed by Late Antique Little Ice Age) + pandemic disease as major causal factors; rich ongoing debate incorporating environmental proxies, aDNA, pollen records
    - **Roman economy**: Moses Finley *The Ancient Economy* (1973) "primitivist" model vs. modern "neo-modernist" approach (more market behavior, long-distance trade, growth); Scheidel/von Reden compendia
    - **Gender and sexuality in Rome**: shift from reading Roman texts as straightforward sources to interrogating constructions of gender; Craig Williams *Roman Homosexuality* (2nd ed. 2010); Judith Hallett on Roman women; recent work on enslaved people's bodies and consent in Roman law

#### Ancient History — Ancient India: Indus Valley, Vedic, Mauryan, Gupta

- **Basics & Scope**
  - Ancient India: ~7000 BCE (Mehrgarh, earliest Indus predecessor) to ~550 CE (end of Gupta period)
  - Core arc: Indus Valley Civilization (~3300–1300 BCE) → Vedic Age (~1500–500 BCE) → Mahajanapadas and the rise of heterodox religions (~600–320 BCE) → Mauryan Empire (~322–185 BCE) → Post-Maurya fragmentation and Sangam South India → Gupta Golden Age (~320–550 CE)
  - Indian civilization's global legacy: zero and decimal notation (transmitted through Arab scholarship to Europe), yoga, chess (precursor *chaturanga*), cotton textiles, Sanskrit grammar (Pāṇini — described as the first modern linguistics), Buddhist and Hindu philosophy (shaped East and Southeast Asia), caste system (still structuring social life for ~1.4 billion), the Mahabharata and Ramayana (longest epics ever composed), Ayurveda, Nalanda University (world's first residential university)
  - Primary sources: archaeological (Indus Valley — no texts deciphered); Rigveda (oral composition ~1500–1200 BCE; written Vedic texts later); Upanishads; Pāṇini's *Ashtadhyayi* (~4th c. BCE); Arthashastra (attributed Kautilya/Chanakya, compiled ~3rd–2nd c. BCE); Ashokan edicts (rock and pillar, ~268–232 BCE — Brahmi and Kharosthi scripts; bilingual in Greek at Kandahar); Jataka tales; Buddhist Pali Canon (oral tradition, written down ~1st c. BCE in Sri Lanka); Tamil Sangam literature (~3rd c. BCE–3rd c. CE); Kalidasa's plays and poems (~4th–5th c. CE); *Panchatantra* (~3rd c. CE); Puranas
  - Major reference works: Romila Thapar *Early India: From the Origins to AD 1300* (2002); D.D. Kosambi *The Culture and Civilisation of Ancient India* (1965); A.L. Basham *The Wonder That Was India* (1954; still valuable); Shereen Ratnagar *Understanding Harappa* (2001); Patrick Olivelle translations of *Arthashastra* (2013) and Upanishads (1996); Wendy Doniger *The Hindus: An Alternative History* (2009); Georg Feuerstein *Yoga: The Technology of Ecstasy*; Sheldon Pollock *The Language of the Gods in the World of Men* (2006 — on Sanskrit cosmopolis)
  - Key journals: *Indian Economic and Social History Review*, *South Asian Studies*, *Ancient India* (ASI), *Journal of the Economic and Social History of the Orient*, *Man and Environment* (South Asian archaeology)

- **Indus Valley Civilization (~3300–1300 BCE)**
  - **Discovery and early excavation**
    - Seals with unknown script noticed by British officers in 19th c.; Alexander Cunningham (first Director-General of the Archaeological Survey of India) investigated Harappa briefly 1872–1873
    - **Systematic excavations**: John Marshall, Daya Ram Sahni and Madho Sarup Vats at Harappa (1920–1934); Marshall and Ernest Mackay at Mohenjo-daro (1922–1934); Marshall's announcement 1924 in *Illustrated London News* revealed a hitherto unknown civilization contemporary with Sumer and Egypt
    - IVC now recognized as the **largest Bronze Age civilization by territorial extent**: ~1.25 million km² — greater than Mesopotamia and Egypt combined; ~1,500+ sites identified stretching from Baluchistan to western Uttar Pradesh, from northern Afghanistan to Gujarat coast
  - **Phases of the IVC**
    - **Early Phase (Ravi / Hakra, ~7000–2600 BCE)**: **Mehrgarh** (Bolan Pass, Baluchistan): earliest pre-Indus farming site in South Asia; ~7000 BCE; mud-brick houses; wheat, barley, domesticated cattle and sheep/goat; dentistry — bowed drill holes in teeth 7500 BCE (world's earliest dental work per Coppa et al. 2006 *Nature*); manufactured clay figurines, beads; cotton cultivation evidence ~5000–4000 BCE (Mehrgarh); direct predecessor of urban IVC
    - **Mature Harappan Phase (~2600–1900 BCE)**: peak urban civilization; ~5–6 million population estimate
    - **Late / Post-Urban Phase (~1900–1300 BCE)**: decline of major urban centers; regional successor cultures; overlap and interaction with Vedic culture debated
  - **Key Sites**
    - **Mohenjo-daro** (Sindh, Pakistan; "Mound of the Dead" in Sindhi): largest city; ~400 hectares occupied; population ~40,000–80,000 at peak; excavated Marshall, Mackay, Wheeler (1944–1945); UNESCO World Heritage Site; critically endangered by saltwater damage to mudbrick; major conservation crisis
    - **Harappa** (Punjab, Pakistan): gave name to the civilization; excavated extensively; multiple periods; large granary complex (Wheeler's interpretation as state granary now questioned); workers' quarters
    - **Dholavira** (Gujarat, India; Kutch): most recently excavated major city; spectacularly preserved; 10 large water reservoirs; stadium-like structure; unique "signboard" of Indus signs; multi-layered city planning; UNESCO World Heritage Site 2021
    - **Rakhigarhi** (Haryana, India): larger than Mohenjo-daro by area (~350 hectares); major ongoing excavations since 1997; focus of intense study; 2019 Nature article on aDNA (see Frontier below)
    - **Lothal** (Gujarat): major port city; brick-lined dock; warehouse; bead factory; Egypt-India trade contact evidenced
    - **Kalibangan** (Rajasthan): fire altars; evidence for earliest ploughed fields in South Asia; pre-Harappan levels
    - **Sutkagen-dor** (Baluchistan coast): westernmost Harappan trading post; Oman/Gulf trade
    - **Shortugai** (NE Afghanistan, on Amu Darya / Oxus): northernmost Indus site; lapis lazuli trade with Mesopotamia via this station
  - **Urban Planning and Technology**
    - **Grid-pattern city planning**: streets oriented NE-SW and NW-SE; major streets up to 10 m wide; remarkable consistency across the entire civilization's territory
    - **Architecture**: standardized kiln-fired bricks in consistent 1:2:4 ratio (width:height:length) across the entire civilization — extraordinary standardization over 1.25 million km²; two-story houses; private wells in homes; ground-floor rooms probably for storage/commerce, upper for living
    - **Great Bath (Mohenjo-daro)**: central public structure; 12×7 m pool, 2.4 m deep; waterproofed with asphalt; surrounding rooms possibly for ritual bathing; widely interpreted as ceremonial/religious; debates about "priestly class" or proto-Hindu ritual bathing (*snan*); may be the world's oldest public water tank
    - **Drainage system**: covered brick-lined drains running beneath streets; latrines in homes connected to drains; more sophisticated municipal sanitation than contemporary Mesopotamia or Egypt; some drains had inspection holes at intervals; extraordinary public health infrastructure
    - **Weights and measures**: standardized cuboid stone weights in binary/decimal ratios (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, etc.); standard length measure (the "Indus inch" ~1.68 cm); consistency across the entire civilization's trading network
    - **Craft production**: fine wheel-turned pottery (including black-on-red painted ware); **steatite seals** (~3,500 found): square seal with short-horned unicorn (*urus*? mythological? ritual?) most common motif; also elephant, zebu bull, rhinoceros, tiger, crocodile; each seal has Indus script inscription; used for administrative/commercial identification; fired to high gloss; extraordinary craft
    - **Indus script**: ~400–600 distinct signs on seals, pottery, tablets, copper plates; direction: right-to-left (main direction); 26 basic signs + composite signs; **completely undeciphered** despite many attempts; too short (average inscription ~5 signs) for statistical analysis; no bilingual key (no Rosetta Stone equivalent found); Asko Parpola (Finland) argues it encodes a Dravidian language (proto-Dravidian) — the most argued scholarly position; Steven Farmer, Richard Sproat, Michael Witzel (2004 *Electronic Journal of Vedic Studies*) controversial claim it may not even be writing in the linguistic sense (possibly logographic or non-linguistic semasiographic system); debate unresolved; **decipherment of Indus script is one of the great unsolved problems of archaeology**
    - **Trade network**: Gulf trade (Mesopotamian texts mention Meluhha = probably IVC; Dilmun = Bahrain; Magan = Oman); semi-precious beads (carnelian etched, lapis, agate) exported West; Indus seals found in Mesopotamia; copper, ivory, lumber, cotton goods exported; gold and silver imported
    - **Bronze Age metallurgy**: copper and bronze tools, weapons, ornaments; lost-wax casting; **"Dancing Girl" figurine** (Mohenjo-daro; bronze; ~2300 BCE): naturalistic dancing female with bangles; extraordinary artistic achievement; possibly a cult dancer or royal attendant
    - **Priest-King sculpture (Mohenjo-daro)**: steatite bust of bearded male wearing trefoil-pattern robe; half-closed meditative eyes; interpreted as ruler or priest; one of the most famous archaeological objects from South Asia
  - **Decline (~1900–1300 BCE)**
    - Multiple theories; current scholarly consensus: **multi-causal**
    - **Monsoon weakening / drought**: paleoclimatic data (Giosan et al. 2012 *PNAS*; Dixit et al. 2014 *Geology*) shows significant aridification beginning ~2000 BCE; Ghaggar-Hakra river system (possibly the Rigvedic Sarasvati River) drying up — loss of major water source
    - **Tectonic activity**: earthquakes may have altered river courses; Indus floods intensified at Mohenjo-daro
    - **Disease**: skeletal evidence at Mohenjo-daro "massacre" event — bodies in streets; Wheeler proposed Aryan conquest; now attributed to disease and social collapse rather than invasion
    - **Not an "Aryan invasion"**: the discredited Wheeler theory (IVC destroyed by Aryan invaders ~1500 BCE) was politically motivated and archaeologically unsupported; IVC declined over ~400 years before Vedic culture emerged; interaction rather than violent replacement now the consensus
    - Population movement toward east (Gangetic Plain) and south (Gujarat) as Indus ecology collapsed; cultural continuities in pottery, weights, religious iconography into successor cultures

- **The Vedic Age (~1500–500 BCE)**
  - **Indo-Aryan Migration**
    - **Indo-European speakers** entered South Asia from Central Asian steppe (~Bronze Age Yamnaya/Sintashta cultural sphere); linguistically related to Iranian, Greek, Latin, Germanic, Celtic, Slavic, Baltic, Anatolian, Tocharian branches — the **Proto-Indo-European family**
    - **Migration timeline**: Sintashta culture (~2100–1800 BCE, southern Urals) → Andronovo culture → BMAC (Bactria-Margiana Archaeological Complex) interaction → entry into South Asia ~1800–1500 BCE; arriving in northwest (Punjab) and spreading east and south over centuries
    - **Not a violent "invasion"**: modern aDNA (Narasimhan et al. 2019 *Science* — 524 ancient South Asian genomes): steppes ancestry increases in South Asia after ~2000 BCE; IVC individuals lack steppe ancestry; post-IVC individuals show steppe mixture → confirms migration but gradual, complex interaction not single military invasion
    - **Dravidian retreat**: pre-existing Dravidian-speaking populations (ancestors of modern Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam speakers) gradually pushed south by Indo-Aryan expansion; Brahui language (Baluchistan) is a relic Dravidian pocket in the northwest
  - **The Four Vedas**
    - **Rigveda**: 10 books (*mandalas*); 1,028 hymns (*suktas*); ~10,580 verses; composed ~1500–1200 BCE; oral transmission (extraordinary precision — not written for ~2,500 years); hymns to Agni (fire god), Indra (warrior-storm god; ~250 hymns — the most; soma ritual hymns), Varuna (cosmic order/*rita*), Mitra, Nasatyas (divine twins), Ushas (dawn goddess); **Book 10.90**: Purushasukta cosmogonic hymn — universe from sacrifice of primordial giant *Purusha*; varnas (social classes) emerge from his body: Brahmin from mouth, Kshatriya from arms, Vaishya from thighs, Shudra from feet — earliest textual basis for varna system
    - **Samaveda**: musical arrangement of Rigvedic verses for chanting; basis of Indian classical music raga system according to tradition
    - **Yajurveda**: prose formulas for sacrifice ritual; two recensions (Black = older prose+verse; White = pure prose)
    - **Atharvaveda**: spells, charms, healing, magic; more "popular" religion than ritual orthodoxy; cosmological and philosophical hymns; later addition to the canonical four
    - Transmission: four priestly schools (*shakha*); mnemonic techniques of extraordinary complexity (word-by-word, syllable-by-syllable, alternating recitations ensuring exact preservation); UNESCO Memory of the World 2008; Vedic chanting tradition
  - **Vedic Society**
    - Semi-nomadic pastoralists gradually settling; cattle = primary wealth unit; horse central to elite identity (the *ashvamedha* horse sacrifice — royal power ritual); iron introduced ~1000 BCE (enabling forest clearing, plow agriculture, spread east into the Gangetic Plain)
    - **Varna system**: four social categories (not yet rigid hereditary castes): **Brahmins** (priests, specialists in sacred knowledge), **Kshatriyas** (warriors, rulers), **Vaishyas** (herders, farmers, merchants), **Shudras** (those serving the other three); **later**: fifth category of *avarnas* or *Dalits* ("untouchables" — those outside the varna system doing polluting work)
    - Key distinction: **varna** (the four-fold classification, theoretically based on function) vs. **jati** (endogamous birth-groups, of which there are ~3,000+ in modern India — the *actual* operative caste system); the conflation and rigidification of varna-jati differentiated by birth is a historical development, not present in earliest Vedic texts
    - **Vedic polity**: *jana* (tribe) → *rashtra* (land/realm); *sabha* (assembly of elders) and *samiti* (popular assembly); *raja* (king/chief) as war-leader; sacrificial *yajna* rituals central to royal power (Rajasuya = royal consecration; Ashvamedha = horse sacrifice asserting territorial sovereignty)
  - **The Upanishads (~800–200 BCE)**
    - **"Sitting near" a teacher**: *upanishad* = secret/confidential teaching; ~200 texts; the *principal Upanishads* (13 major ones); compositions in forest hermitages by sages and their disciples; text Brihadaranyaka, Chandogya (oldest ~800–600 BCE), Katha, Mundaka, Mandukya, Taittiriya, Aitareya, Kaushitaki, Kena, Isha, Shvetashvatara, Prashna, Maitri
    - **Core concepts**:
      - **Brahman**: the ultimate impersonal cosmic reality; the ground of all being; identical with spacetime energy; beyond all attributes
      - **Atman**: the individual self/soul; ultimately *identical* with Brahman (the central Upanishadic insight); "Tat tvam asi" (That art thou — Chandogya 6.8.7): the deepest teaching; the individual self is the universal self
      - **Maya**: the cosmic illusion or creative power that makes the one Brahman appear as the many; the phenomenal world is not unreal but is not the deepest reality
      - **Karma** (action and its consequences): actions create tendencies that shape future rebirths; moral causality operating across lifetimes
      - **Samsara**: the cycle of death and rebirth (*transmigration*); driven by desire and karma; the goal of spiritual life is **moksha** (liberation from samsara)
      - **Jnana** (knowledge/wisdom): knowing the identity of atman and Brahman produces liberating insight
    - **The six orthodox schools of Hindu philosophy** (*darshanas*, "viewpoints") all engage with the Upanishads:
      - **Samkhya**: dualist; consciousness (*purusha*) and matter (*prakriti*); oldest school; Kapila (mythological founder); provides metaphysical framework for Yoga
      - **Yoga**: practical/meditative path; systematized in **Patanjali's *Yoga Sutras*** (~2nd c. BCE–4th c. CE): eight-limbed path (*ashtanga*): yama (restraints), niyama (observances), asana (posture), pranayama (breath), pratyahara (sense withdrawal), dharana (concentration), dhyana (meditation), samadhi (absorption/enlightenment)
      - **Nyaya**: logic and epistemology; valid means of knowledge (*pramana*); inference, perception, comparison, testimony; Gautama's *Nyaya Sutras*
      - **Vaisheshika**: atomism and categories of reality; Kanada's *Vaisheshika Sutras*; universe of atoms + souls + God
      - **Mimamsa**: hermeneutics of Vedic ritual; Jaimini's *Purva Mimamsa Sutras*; primacy of ritual action
      - **Vedanta**: interpretation of the Upanishads; most important school; three main sub-schools: **Advaita** (non-dualism, Shankaracharya ~788–820 CE — all reality is Brahman; world is maya); **Vishishtadvaita** (qualified non-dualism, Ramanuja ~1017–1137 CE); **Dvaita** (dualism, Madhvacharya ~1238–1317 CE)
  - **The Mahabharata and Ramayana**
    - **Mahabharata**: attributed to Vyasa; ~100,000 *shlokas* (200,000+ lines of verse) — the longest poem in world literature (8 times longer than Homer's combined); core narrative: war between Pandavas and Kauravas (the Kurukshetra War, set ~1000–800 BCE?); embedded philosophical discourses including the **Bhagavad Gita** (18 chapters; Krishna counsels Arjuna before battle; synthesizes karma yoga, jnana yoga, bhakti yoga; the most important text of Hinduism; compared by T.S. Eliot to Dante); Mahabharata contains virtually all of Hindu mythology; "whatever is here is found elsewhere; what is not here is nowhere"
    - **Ramayana**: attributed to Valmiki; ~24,000 verses (7 books); Rama (divine king, avatar of Vishnu), his wife Sita (ideal of wifely virtue), Hanuman (loyal monkey-warrior), Ravana (demon king of Lanka); abduction of Sita, war in Lanka, rescue; foundational text of bhakti devotion; spread across South and Southeast Asia (Thai Ramakien, Indonesian Ramayana)
    - Both epics: oral composition over many centuries; written versions crystallized ~400 BCE–400 CE; composite texts with multiple layers; contain extraordinary variety of narrative, law, genealogy, cosmology, philosophy
  - **The Mahajanapadas, Jainism, and Buddhism (~600–320 BCE)**
    - **Sixteen Mahajanapadas**: the great realms of northern India by ~600 BCE; included Magadha (Bihar — eventually Mauryan heartland), Koshala (Oudh), Vatsa (Allahabad region), Avanti (Malwa); oligarchic gana-sanghas (republics) and monarchies; complex interstate politics; riverine trade on Ganges network; rise of towns (*nagara*)
    - **Jainism**:
      - Founded (or reformed) by **Vardhamana Mahavira** (~599–527 BCE or ~549–477 BCE — dating disputed); 24th *tirthankara* (ford-finder); a Kshatriya prince who renounced wealth, became an ascetic; achieved *kevalajnana* (omniscience) after 12 years of extreme austerity; taught for 30 years; died by *sallekhana* (ritual fasting unto death)
