    - Military genius: Austerlitz (1805, "Battle of the Three Emperors"), Jena-Auerstedt (1806), Wagram (1809) — defeated every major European power
    - **Napoleonic Code (Code Civil, 1804)**: unified French civil law; enshrined legal equality (for men), property rights, secular civil authority, contract freedom — spread across conquered Europe; influenced legal systems in Latin America, Middle East, East Asia; still basis of French law
    - Continental System (1806): blockade of British trade — attempted economic warfare; ultimately unsustainable; contributed to conflicts with Russia
    - Administrative reforms: meritocratic bureaucracy, prefectoral system, centralised education (lycées), concordat with papacy (1801), Bank of France
    - Empire at greatest extent (1811): France, Low Countries, western Germany, Italy, Illyria, Spain (under Joseph Bonaparte); satellite states and forced allies across Europe
  - **Resistance and coalition wars**
    - Six Coalition Wars against France (various combinations of Britain, Austria, Prussia, Russia, Spain, others)
    - **Peninsular War (1808–1814)**: Spanish and Portuguese resistance with British support (Wellington); guerrilla warfare (origin of the term); "the Spanish ulcer"
    - **Russian Campaign (1812)**: Grande Armée of ~600,000 invaded Russia; Battle of Borodino; occupation of Moscow (burned); catastrophic retreat; ~400,000 dead; beginning of Napoleon's downfall
    - Battle of Leipzig / "Battle of the Nations" (1813): largest battle in European history before WWI; Napoleon defeated by combined coalition
    - First abdication (1814); exile to Elba; escape and **Hundred Days** (March–June 1815)
    - **Battle of Waterloo (18 June 1815)**: Wellington and Blücher defeated Napoleon; second abdication; exile to Saint Helena; death 1821
  - **Congress of Vienna (1814–1815)**
    - Klemens von Metternich (Austria), Viscount Castlereagh (Britain), Tsar Alexander I (Russia), Karl August von Hardenberg (Prussia), Talleyrand (France)
    - Principles: legitimacy (restoring old dynasties), compensation, balance of power
    - Territorial settlement: redrew map of Europe; enlarged Prussia and Kingdom of the Netherlands; created German Confederation; Poland partitioned again; Concert of Europe as mechanism for diplomatic crisis management
    - Holy Alliance (Alexander I): somewhat mystical pact of Christian solidarity — mocked by Castlereagh but symbolised conservative order
    - **Concert of Europe**: great-power system of congresses and consultations to prevent revolution and war — maintained relative peace until Crimean War (1853)
    - Critique: suppressed liberal and nationalist aspirations; Metternich's system as reactionary constraint; but also prevented major European war for 40 years
  - **Napoleonic legacy**
    - Spread of revolutionary ideals: legal equality, meritocracy, civic nationalism, secularism
    - Stimulated nationalism in both allied and conquered peoples (German, Italian, Spanish, Polish, Russian)
    - Napoleonic myth: romanticism of the "great man"; propaganda via memoirs (Memorial of Saint Helena); debated endlessly

- **The Industrial Revolution**
  - **British industrialisation (c. 1760–1840)**
    - **Why Britain first?** — debated for 200+ years
      - Robert Allen, The British Industrial Revolution in Global Perspective (2009): high wages + cheap energy (coal) made labour-saving machinery profitable
      - Joel Mokyr, The Enlightened Economy (2009): Enlightenment culture of useful knowledge, tinkering, and open communication
      - Kenneth Pomeranz, The Great Divergence (2000): before 1800, Europe and China comparably developed; divergence driven by coal access and New World colonial resources
      - Institutional factors: property rights, patent system (Statute of Monopolies 1624), parliamentary governance, relatively free markets, rule of law
      - Geographic: abundant coal and iron; navigable rivers and canals; island geography favouring naval trade
      - Agricultural Revolution (18th c.): enclosure movement, crop rotation (Townshend, "Turnip"), selective breeding (Robert Bakewell) → increased food production, freed labour for factories
    - **Key technologies**
      - **Textile industry**: spinning jenny (James Hargreaves, c. 1764), water frame (Richard Arkwright, 1769), spinning mule (Samuel Crompton, 1779), power loom (Edmund Cartwright, 1785); cotton as first mass-produced commodity
      - **Steam power**: Thomas Newcomen's atmospheric engine (1712, mine pumping); **James Watt's** improved steam engine (separate condenser, 1769) with Matthew Boulton — rotary motion enabled factory power
      - **Iron and steel**: Abraham Darby's coke-smelting of iron (1709); Henry Cort's puddling and rolling process (1784); later Bessemer process (1856) and Siemens-Martin open-hearth
      - **Coal**: foundation of industrial energy; deep mining expanded; Humphry Davy's safety lamp (1815)
    - **Transport revolution**
      - Canals: "Canal Mania" (1790s); James Brindley, Duke of Bridgewater's canal (1761)
      - **Railways**: George Stephenson's Rocket (1829); Liverpool and Manchester Railway (1830) — first intercity passenger railway; railway mania (1840s); by 1850 ~6,600 miles of track in Britain
        - Railways as transformative: collapsed time and distance, created national markets, standardised time (Railway Time → Greenwich Mean Time), reshaped landscape, spawned new industries and professions
      - Steamships: SS Great Western (1838, Brunel) — Atlantic crossing; SS Great Eastern; iron-hulled steamers replaced wooden sailing ships; transformed global trade
    - **Factory system**
      - Shift from domestic/putting-out system to centralised factory production
      - Arkwright's Cromford Mill (1771): model factory; water-powered, disciplined labour, shift work
      - Factory discipline: clocks, overseers, fines, child labour, long hours (12–16 hour days typical)
      - Factory Acts: 1833 (banned child labour under 9 in textile mills, limited hours for children); 1847 (Ten Hours Act); gradual legislative improvement
  - **Social consequences of industrialisation**
    - **Urbanisation**: Manchester grew from ~25,000 (1772) to ~300,000 (1851); London exceeded 2 million by 1850; unprecedented urban growth
    - Living conditions: overcrowding, disease (cholera epidemics: 1831–32, 1848–49, 1854), inadequate sanitation, pollution
      - Edwin Chadwick, Report on the Sanitary Condition of the Labouring Population (1842): documented appalling conditions; catalyst for public health reform
      - John Snow: identified Broad Street pump as cholera source (1854) — foundational for epidemiology
    - **Working class formation**
      - E.P. Thompson, The Making of the English Working Class (1963): working class not just product of industrialisation but actively "made" through shared experience, culture, and political action — foundational social history text
      - Luddite movement (1811–1816): machine-breaking by skilled textile workers facing technological displacement; suppressed by military force
      - Trade unions: combination acts (banned 1799–1800, partially repealed 1824–1825); Grand National Consolidated Trades Union (1834); Trades Union Congress (TUC, 1868)
      - Chartism (1838–1857): mass working-class political movement; People's Charter demanded universal male suffrage, secret ballot, annual parliaments, equal constituencies, payment of MPs, abolition of property qualification; petitions rejected (1839, 1842, 1848); all demands except annual parliaments eventually enacted
    - **Child and women's labour**
      - Children as young as 5 in mines and factories; climbing boys (chimney sweeps); piecer's and scavengers in cotton mills
      - Women: textile workers, domestic service, pit-brow lasses (coal mines); exclusion from many trades
      - Reform: Mines Act (1842, banned women and children under 10 from underground work); Education Acts (from 1870)
    - **Middle class rise**: professionals, factory owners, merchants, clerks — new bourgeois culture of respectability; reform movements; evangelicalism
  - **Industrialisation beyond Britain**
    - **Belgium**: first continental European country to industrialise (1820s–1840s); coal, textiles, railways; John Cockerill's Seraing ironworks
    - **France**: slower, more gradual industrialisation; silk (Lyon), luxury goods; railways expanded from 1840s; less coal-dependent; more artisanal production persisted
    - **Germany**: rapid industrialisation from 1850s; Zollverein (customs union, from 1834); Ruhr Valley coal and steel; Krupp steelworks (Essen); by 1900 Germany rivalled or surpassed Britain in steel and chemicals
    - **United States**: from 1820s (New England textiles — Lowell Mills); railways (transcontinental, 1869); steel (Andrew Carnegie, Pittsburgh); oil (John D. Rockefeller, Standard Oil); by 1900 world's largest industrial economy
    - **Russia**: industrialisation from 1880s–1890s under Finance Minister Sergei Witte; railways (Trans-Siberian, begun 1891); still overwhelmingly agrarian; emancipation of serfs (1861) as precondition
    - **Japan**: Meiji industrialisation (see below)
    - **Absence / alternative paths**: much of Asia, Africa, Latin America deindustrialised under colonial pressure or remained agrarian — debate over why (Prasannan Parthasarathi, Why Europe Grew Rich and Asia Did Not, 2011)

- **The age of revolution and nationalism (1815–1871)**
  - **1820s revolutions**
    - Spain (1820): Liberal revolt restored 1812 constitution briefly; suppressed by French intervention (1823)
    - Naples and Piedmont (1820–1821): constitutionalist revolts; suppressed by Austrian intervention
    - Greek War of Independence (1821–1829): against Ottoman Empire; philhellenic movement (Lord Byron died at Missolonghi, 1824); Treaty of Adrianople (1829); independent Greek kingdom (1832)
    - Latin American independence: Simón Bolívar (Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia); José de San Martín (Argentina, Chile, Peru); Mexican independence (1821); Brazilian independence (1822, uniquely monarchical); Monroe Doctrine (1823) — US warned European powers against recolonisation
  - **1830 revolutions**
    - **July Revolution (France, 1830)**: overthrew Charles X (ultra-royalist); installed Louis-Philippe ("Citizen King") — constitutional monarchy; celebrated by liberals across Europe
    - Belgium (1830): independence from Kingdom of the Netherlands; constitutional monarchy; Leopold I
    - Poland (1830–1831): "November Uprising" against Russian rule; brutally suppressed; Polish emigration (Great Emigration: Chopin, Mickiewicz, Czartoryski)
    - Reform in Britain: Great Reform Act (1832) — expanded franchise to middle-class men; reformed rotten boroughs; peaceful reform rather than revolution as British pattern
  - **1848 — "The Springtime of Peoples"**
    - Wave of revolutions swept Europe: France, German states, Austria, Hungary, Italy, Poland, Denmark, Wallachia
    - **France**: February Revolution overthrew Louis-Philippe; **Second Republic** declared; universal male suffrage; June Days uprising (working-class revolt in Paris, suppressed by General Cavaignac — ~1,500+ killed); Louis-Napoleon Bonaparte elected president (December 1848); coup d'état (1851); **Napoleon III** — Emperor of the Second Empire (1852–1870)
    - **German states**: Frankfurt Parliament (1848–1849) — first elected all-German parliament; debated "greater" vs. "lesser" Germany; offered crown to Prussian King Frederick William IV — refused ("a crown from the gutter"); parliament dissolved; revolution suppressed
    - **Austrian Empire**: revolution in Vienna (Metternich fled); Hungarian revolution under Lajos Kossuth; Czech and Italian revolts; all suppressed by Austrian and Russian forces (1849); young Emperor Franz Joseph (r. 1848–1916)
    - **Italy**: revolutions in Milan, Venice, Rome (Mazzini's Roman Republic, defended by Garibaldi); all suppressed by Austria and France
    - **Marx and Engels**: The Communist Manifesto published February 1848 (coincidental timing with revolutions); foundational text of communist movement
    - **Why 1848 "failed"**: liberal and nationalist goals conflicted with each other and with working-class demands; revolution demanded both freedom AND order; monarchies recovered because revolutionaries fragmented; army loyalty to old order; but lasting consequences: serfdom abolished in Habsburg Empire; constitutions survived in Piedmont-Sardinia (Statuto Albertino) and elsewhere; issues of 1848 dominated next decades
  - **Unification of Italy (1859–1871)**
    - **Giuseppe Mazzini (1805–1872)**: republican nationalist ideologue; Young Italy movement; inspirational but repeatedly failed in practice
    - **Count Camillo di Cavour (1810–1861)**: Prime Minister of Piedmont-Sardinia; Realpolitik; allied with Napoleon III against Austria (1859); Franco-Austrian War; Lombardy gained
    - **Giuseppe Garibaldi (1807–1882)**: revolutionary hero; **Expedition of the Thousand** (1860): invaded and conquered Kingdom of the Two Sicilies with ~1,000 red-shirted volunteers — one of 19th century's most dramatic military exploits; handed southern Italy to Victor Emmanuel II
    - Kingdom of Italy proclaimed (1861); Venetia gained (1866, after Austro-Prussian War); **Rome captured (1870)** — became capital 1871; Pope confined to Vatican; "Roman Question" unresolved until Lateran Treaty (1929)
  - **Unification of Germany (1862–1871)**
    - **Otto von Bismarck (1815–1898)**: Prussian Junker; Minister-President from 1862; "Iron and Blood" speech (1862)
    - Three wars of unification:
      - Danish War (1864): Prussia and Austria vs. Denmark; seized Schleswig-Holstein
      - **Austro-Prussian War (1866)**: "Seven Weeks' War"; Battle of Königgrätz/Sadowa — decisive Prussian victory; Austria excluded from German affairs; North German Confederation (1867)
      - **Franco-Prussian War (1870–1871)**: Bismarck provoked war through Ems Dispatch; French defeated at Sedan (Napoleon III captured); Siege of Paris; Paris Commune (1871, see below)
    - **German Empire proclaimed (18 January 1871)**: Hall of Mirrors, Versailles; Wilhelm I as Kaiser; Bismarck as Chancellor
    - Bismarckian system: Realpolitik diplomacy; alliance system (Triple Alliance with Austria-Hungary and Italy); Kulturkampf (anti-Catholic campaign, 1871–1878); Anti-Socialist Laws (1878–1890) but also pioneering social insurance (health 1883, accident 1884, old age 1889 — world's first welfare state measures)
  - **Paris Commune (March–May 1871)**
    - Radical socialist/anarchist government in Paris after French defeat and siege
    - Decrees: separation of church and state, women's rights, workers' self-management, remission of rents
    - "Bloody Week" (21–28 May 1871): government troops from Versailles crushed Commune; ~10,000–20,000 Communards killed; mass deportations
    - Legacy: Marx celebrated it as first working-class government; Lenin studied it closely; remains potent symbol in left-wing politics

- **Ideologies of the 19th century**
  - **Liberalism**
    - Core tenets: individual rights, constitutional government, rule of law, free markets, limited state, religious toleration
    - John Stuart Mill (1806–1873): On Liberty (1859) — "harm principle" as limit of state authority; Utilitarianism (1863); The Subjection of Women (1869, with Harriet Taylor Mill) — feminist liberalism
    - Alexis de Tocqueville (1805–1859): Democracy in America (1835/1840) — analysis of American democratic society; "tyranny of the majority"; civil associations; prophetic on racial issues
    - Classical liberals: Bastiat (France), Cobden and Bright (British free trade — Anti-Corn Law League, repeal 1846); Herbert Spencer (Social Darwinism — survival of the fittest applied to society)
  - **Conservatism**
    - Burke as foundational (see Enlightenment section); tradition, organic society, hierarchy, suspicion of radical change
    - Metternich: conservative statecraft; repression of liberalism and nationalism
    - Benjamin Disraeli (1804–1881): "One Nation" conservatism; social reform; empire; Reform Act 1867 (extended franchise to urban working men)
    - Bismarck: pragmatic conservatism — used nationalism and social reform to neutralise liberalism and socialism
  - **Socialism and communism**
    - **Utopian socialists** (term coined by Marx and Engels to distinguish from their "scientific" socialism):
      - Robert Owen (1771–1858): New Lanark mills (improved worker conditions); New Harmony (Indiana, utopian community — failed); cooperative movement
      - Henri de Saint-Simon (1760–1825): industrial society should be governed by scientists and industrialists; technocratic planning
      - Charles Fourier (1772–1837): phalansteries (cooperative communities); utopian social organisation; remarkably original (and eccentric) thinker
    - **Karl Marx (1818–1883) and Friedrich Engels (1820–1895)**
      - The Communist Manifesto (1848): "the history of all hitherto existing society is the history of class struggles"; bourgeoisie vs. proletariat; inevitable revolution
      - **Das Kapital** (Vol. I, 1867; Vols. II–III posthumously): labour theory of value, surplus value, exploitation, commodity fetishism, tendency of the rate of profit to fall; analysis of capitalism as dynamic but self-destructive system
      - Historical materialism: economic base determines social and political superstructure; dialectical progression through modes of production (feudalism → capitalism → socialism → communism)
      - First International (International Workingmen's Association, 1864–1876): Marx vs. Bakunin (anarchist); split over state role in revolution
    - **Anarchism**
      - Pierre-Joseph Proudhon (1809–1865): "property is theft"; mutualism; opposed both capitalism and state socialism
      - Mikhail Bakunin (1814–1876): revolutionary anarchism; abolish the state; clashed with Marx in First International
      - Peter Kropotkin (1842–1921): anarcho-communism; Mutual Aid (1902) — cooperation as evolutionary factor
    - Second International (1889–1914): socialist and labour parties of Europe; debates over reform vs. revolution; May Day established; collapsed over WWI (most parties supported their nations)
    - **Social democracy**: Eduard Bernstein (1850–1932): Evolutionary Socialism (1899) — argued Marx was wrong about capitalism's imminent collapse; socialism achievable through gradual parliamentary reform; "revisionism" debate split socialist movement
  - **Nationalism**
    - Most powerful ideology of the century: drove Italian and German unification, Greek and Balkan independence, Polish and Irish movements, Zionism, and anti-colonial stirrings
    - Romantic nationalism: Herder, Fichte (Addresses to the German Nation, 1808); language, folk culture, and historical myth as basis of national identity
    - Civic nationalism: French model — nation as community of citizens bound by political values
    - Ethnic nationalism: nation defined by blood, language, race — more exclusionary; dominant in Central and Eastern Europe
    - Pan-movements: Pan-Slavism, Pan-Germanism, Pan-Turkism, Pan-Africanism (late 19th c.)
    - Ernest Renan, "What Is a Nation?" (1882): "a daily plebiscite" — nation as act of collective will and shared memory/forgetting
  - **Feminism — first wave**
    - Mary Wollstonecraft's Vindication (1792) as precursor
    - Seneca Falls Convention (1848): Declaration of Sentiments (Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Lucretia Mott) — demanded women's suffrage and legal equality
    - John Stuart Mill: The Subjection of Women (1869); parliamentary motion for women's suffrage (1867)
    - Suffrage movements: National American Woman Suffrage Association; British suffragists (Millicent Fawcett) and suffragettes (Emmeline Pankhurst, WSPU — militant tactics)
    - New Zealand: first country to grant women national suffrage (1893); Australia 1902; Finland 1906
  - **Social Darwinism and scientific racism**
    - Herbert Spencer (1820–1903): coined "survival of the fittest"; applied evolutionary metaphor to society — laissez-faire economics, opposition to welfare, racial hierarchy
    - Francis Galton (1822–1911): coined "eugenics" (1883); hereditary genius; measuring human traits — led to horrific 20th-century applications
    - Scientific racism: racial classifications (Blumenbach, Morton, Gobineau — Essay on the Inequality of the Human Races, 1853–1855); used to justify slavery, imperialism, and discrimination
    - Critique: modern genetics has thoroughly debunked biological race as a meaningful category; but the ideologies shaped policy and caused immense suffering

- **Abolitionism and emancipation in the 19th century**
  - British abolition: slave trade (1807), slavery in empire (1833) — see previous section
  - **United States**
    - Abolitionist movement: Frederick Douglass (1818–1895, Narrative, 1845), William Lloyd Garrison (The Liberator), Harriet Beecher Stowe (Uncle Tom's Cabin, 1852), Harriet Tubman (Underground Railroad)
    - **American Civil War (1861–1865)**
      - Secession of 11 Southern states forming the Confederacy; slavery as central cause (despite "Lost Cause" revisionism emphasising states' rights)
      - Abraham Lincoln: Emancipation Proclamation (1 January 1863, freed slaves in rebel states); **13th Amendment (1865)**: abolished slavery; 14th Amendment (1868): citizenship and equal protection; 15th Amendment (1870): voting rights regardless of race
      - Approximately 620,000–750,000 soldiers died — bloodiest American conflict
      - Reconstruction (1865–1877): brief period of Black political participation in South; Ku Klux Klan terrorism; ended with Compromise of 1877; Jim Crow era of segregation and disenfranchisement followed
    - Eric Foner, Reconstruction: America's Unfinished Revolution (1988): definitive modern account
  - **Brazil**: last Western Hemisphere country to abolish slavery — Lei Áurea (Golden Law, 1888); ~4 million enslaved freed; abolished without civil war but also without reparation or integration
  - **Russian serfdom**: Emancipation Reform (1861, Alexander II) — freed ~23 million serfs; land allocated but with redemption payments; remained impoverished; insufficient reform contributed to later revolutionary pressures
  - **Ottoman Empire**: gradual abolition through 19th century; slave trade formally banned 1847 (circassians), 1857 (africans); Tanzimat reforms

- **The New Imperialism (c. 1870–1914)**
  - **Drivers of imperialism**
    - Economic: markets for industrial goods, raw materials (rubber, palm oil, copper, diamonds, gold, cotton), investment opportunities (J.A. Hobson, Imperialism: A Study, 1902; Lenin, Imperialism, the Highest Stage of Capitalism, 1917)
    - Strategic: naval bases, coaling stations, canal routes (Suez Canal, 1869; Panama Canal, 1914); Great Power competition
    - Ideological: "civilising mission" (mission civilisatrice), "White Man's Burden" (Kipling, 1899), Social Darwinism, racial supremacism, Christian missionary impulse
    - Technological: quinine (malaria prophylaxis), Maxim gun, steamship, telegraph, railways — vastly amplified European military advantage
    - Domestic politics: national prestige, redirecting social tensions outward, popular imperialism and jingoism
  - **The Scramble for Africa**
    - Before 1880: European presence largely limited to coastal trading posts (except Algeria, Cape Colony, Boer republics)
    - **Berlin Conference (1884–1885)**: European powers (no African representatives) established rules for colonial claims; "effective occupation" requirement; triggered accelerated partition
    - By 1914: virtually entire African continent colonised; only Ethiopia (defeated Italy at Battle of Adwa, 1896 — Menelik II) and Liberia remained independent
    - **Major colonial territories**:
      - British: Egypt (1882, nominally Ottoman), Sudan, Nigeria, Gold Coast, Sierra Leone, Kenya, Uganda, Rhodesia (Cecil Rhodes), South Africa (Cape Colony, Natal; Boer Wars)
      - French: Algeria (from 1830), West Africa (AOF), Equatorial Africa (AEF), Madagascar, Tunisia, Morocco
      - Belgian: **Congo Free State (1885–1908)** — King Leopold II's personal colony; rubber extraction through forced labour, mutilation, terror; estimated millions of deaths (Adam Hochschild, King Leopold's Ghost, 1998; exact figures debated — possibly 1–10 million); international outcry → Belgian state took over (1908) but exploitation continued
      - German: Tanganyika, South-West Africa (Namibia — **Herero and Nama genocide, 1904–1908**: first genocide of 20th century; Germany formally recognised it in 2021), Cameroon, Togo
      - Portuguese: Angola, Mozambique, Guinea-Bissau, Cape Verde — oldest European colonial presence in Africa
      - Italian: Eritrea, Libya (1911), attempted Ethiopian conquest (defeated at Adwa 1896)
    - **African resistance**: Zulu resistance (Battle of Isandlwana, 1879 — defeated British force; Rorke's Drift); Asante wars; Samori Ture's Mandinka resistance to France; Maji Maji Rebellion (German East Africa, 1905–1907); Ethiopian victory at Adwa
  - **British India**
    - EIC rule → British Crown rule after **Indian Rebellion / "Mutiny" of 1857**: sepoy revolt triggered by cultural grievances (greased cartridges controversy); broader participation; brutal suppression; EIC dissolved; British Raj established (1858); Queen Victoria as Empress of India (1876)
    - Economic impact: deindustrialisation of Indian textile manufacturing; forced into raw material export economy; famines (Bengal, Madras, Orissa famines) exacerbated by export policies — Mike Davis, Late Victorian Holocausts (2001)
    - Railways, telegraph, English education (Macaulay's Minute, 1835); Indian Civil Service; legal codification
    - Indian responses: Indian National Congress (founded 1885) — initially moderate, sought reform within empire; Dadabhai Naoroji's "drain of wealth" theory; Bal Gangadhar Tilak's more militant nationalism
    - Swami Vivekananda (1863–1902): Hindu reformer; represented Hinduism at Parliament of the World's Religions (Chicago, 1893); cultural nationalism
  - **China in the age of imperialism**
    - **Opium Wars**: First (1839–1842, Treaty of Nanjing — Hong Kong ceded, treaty ports, extraterritoriality); Second (1856–1860, sack of Summer Palace, further concessions)
    - "Century of humiliation" in Chinese national memory
    - **Taiping Rebellion (1850–1864)**: Hong Xiuquan's quasi-Christian millenarian movement; controlled much of southern China; estimated 20–30 million dead — deadliest civil war in history
    - Self-Strengthening Movement (1861–1895): attempted modernisation within traditional Qing framework; arsenals, railways, translation bureaus; failed to achieve fundamental reform
    - Sino-Japanese War (1894–1895): China defeated by Japan; ceded Taiwan; revealed weakness; intensified imperialist scramble for concessions
    - Boxer Rebellion (1899–1901): anti-foreign, anti-Christian uprising; Eight-Nation Alliance intervention; humiliating Boxer Protocol
  - **Japan: Meiji Restoration and modernisation**
    - Commodore Perry and the "opening" of Japan (1853–1854): US naval pressure ended sakoku
    - **Meiji Restoration (1868)**: young Emperor Meiji; overthrow of Tokugawa shogunate; Charter Oath — modernisation programme
    - Rapid industrialisation: government-led (zaibatsu — industrial conglomerates: Mitsubishi, Mitsui, Sumitomo); railways, telegraph, modern military (Prussian-model army, British-model navy)
    - Meiji Constitution (1889): modelled on Prussian constitution; limited parliamentary government; emperor as sovereign
    - Sino-Japanese War (1894–1895): Japan's first modern military victory; gained Taiwan, Liaodong (forced to return), and Korea as sphere of influence
    - **Russo-Japanese War (1904–1905)**: Japan defeated Russia — stunning first Asian victory over a European great power; reshaped global racial and geopolitical assumptions; Treaty of Portsmouth (Theodore Roosevelt mediated); Japan annexed Korea (1910)
    - Legacy: Japan as model for non-Western modernisation; but also as new imperial power with its own colonial subjects
  - **Southeast Asian colonialism**
    - French Indochina: Vietnam (Cochinchina, Annam, Tonkin), Cambodia, Laos — conquest 1858–1893
    - Dutch East Indies: expanded from VOC coastal holdings to full territorial control of Indonesian archipelago; Cultivation System (forced cropping for export, 1830–1870) — extracted enormous wealth
    - British: Burma (three Anglo-Burmese wars, 1824–1885); Malaya (tin, rubber); Singapore (Raffles, 1819); Borneo (Brooke rajahs of Sarawak)
    - US: Philippines (seized from Spain, 1898; Philippine-American War 1899–1902 — brutal counter-insurgency; often estimated 200,000–1,000,000 Filipino deaths)
    - Siam (Thailand): only Southeast Asian country to avoid colonisation; Chakri dynasty modernisation (Mongkut, Chulalongkorn); buffer state between British and French empires
  - **The Ottoman Empire — "the sick man of Europe"**
    - Tanzimat reforms (1839–1876): modernisation of law, military, education, administration; Gülhane Hatt-ı Şerif; equality of subjects regardless of religion
    - Crimean War (1853–1856): Ottoman, British, French vs. Russia; Battle of Balaclava (Charge of the Light Brigade); Treaty of Paris; demonstrated Ottoman dependence on European allies
    - Balkan crises: Greek independence (1829); Serbian, Romanian, Bulgarian autonomy/independence; Congress of Berlin (1878, Bismarck as "honest broker"); gradual retreat from the Balkans
    - Abdul Hamid II (r. 1876–1909): suspended constitution; Hamidian massacres of Armenians (1894–1896, ~100,000–300,000 killed)
    - Young Turks revolution (1908): Committee of Union and Progress; constitutional restoration
  - **Pacific imperialism**: Hawaii annexed by US (1898); New Zealand (Treaty of Waitangi 1840 — Maori sovereignty debate); Australia: colonisation, frontier violence against Aboriginal peoples; Pacific islands divided among European powers

- **Science and technology in the 19th century**
  - **Charles Darwin (1809–1882)**
    - On the Origin of Species (1859): natural selection as mechanism of evolution; perhaps the most consequential scientific book since Newton's Principia
    - HMS Beagle voyage (1831–1836): Galápagos observations; biogeography
    - The Descent of Man (1871): applied evolution to humans; sexual selection
    - Alfred Russel Wallace (1823–1913): independently conceived natural selection; Malay Archipelago
    - Darwin's theory initially controversial (Thomas Huxley as "Darwin's Bulldog"; Bishop Wilberforce debate, 1860); by century's end, evolution universally accepted among scientists (though mechanism debated until Modern Synthesis, 20th century)
  - **Germ theory and medicine**
    - Louis Pasteur (1822–1895): disproved spontaneous generation; pasteurisation; vaccines (anthrax, rabies); germ theory of disease
    - Robert Koch (1843–1910): Koch's postulates; identified tuberculosis bacillus (1882), cholera bacillus (1883)
    - Joseph Lister (1827–1912): antiseptic surgery (carbolic acid, inspired by Pasteur)
    - Florence Nightingale (1820–1910): nursing reform in Crimean War; pioneered statistical graphics for epidemiology; Notes on Nursing (1859)
    - Public health: sanitation engineering, clean water supply, sewage systems — dramatically reduced urban mortality
  - **Physics**
    - Thermodynamics: Sadi Carnot, James Prescott Joule, Rudolf Clausius (entropy), Lord Kelvin, Ludwig Boltzmann (statistical mechanics)
    - Electromagnetism: Michael Faraday (electromagnetic induction, 1831); **James Clerk Maxwell** (1831–1879): unified electricity, magnetism, and optics in Maxwell's equations — "the second great unification in physics"
    - Heinrich Hertz: demonstrated electromagnetic waves (1887) — basis for radio
    - End-of-century discoveries: Wilhelm Röntgen (X-rays, 1895), Henri Becquerel (radioactivity, 1896), Marie and Pierre Curie (radium, polonium), J.J. Thomson (electron, 1897)
  - **Chemistry**: Dmitri Mendeleev (periodic table, 1869); synthetic dyes (William Perkin, mauveine, 1856 — launched synthetic chemistry industry); Friedrich Wöhler (urea synthesis, 1828 — bridged organic/inorganic divide)
  - **Technology**: telegraph (Samuel Morse, 1837/1844; transatlantic cable 1866); telephone (Alexander Graham Bell, 1876); phonograph (Thomas Edison, 1877); electric light bulb (Edison, 1879; Joseph Swan); internal combustion engine (Nikolaus Otto, 1876; Karl Benz and Gottlieb Daimler — automobile); early cinema (Lumière brothers, 1895)

- **Culture and intellectual life in the 19th century**
  - **Romanticism (c. 1780–1850)**
    - Reaction against Enlightenment rationalism; emphasis on emotion, nature, imagination, individualism, the sublime
    - Literature: Goethe (Faust, The Sorrows of Young Werther), Wordsworth and Coleridge (Lyrical Ballads, 1798), Shelley, Keats, Byron, Victor Hugo (Les Misérables, Notre-Dame de Paris), Alexander Pushkin, Emily Brontë
    - Music: Beethoven (transitional figure), Schubert, Chopin, Schumann, Berlioz, Liszt, Wagner (Gesamtkunstwerk — total work of art), Verdi; opera as nationalist art form
    - Art: Caspar David Friedrich, J.M.W. Turner, Eugène Delacroix (Liberty Leading the People, 1830), Francisco Goya
    - Philosophy: German Idealism (Fichte, Schelling, Hegel); Schopenhauer (The World as Will and Representation); Kierkegaard (existentialist precursor)
  - **Realism and Naturalism (c. 1840–1900)**
    - Literature: Gustave Flaubert (Madame Bovary, 1857), Charles Dickens, George Eliot (Middlemarch), Honoré de Balzac (La Comédie Humaine), Leo Tolstoy (War and Peace, Anna Karenina), Fyodor Dostoevsky (Crime and Punishment, The Brothers Karamazov), Émile Zola (naturalism — Germinal, experimental novel), Henrik Ibsen (A Doll's House, Hedda Gabler), Anton Chekhov
    - Art: Gustave Courbet (The Stone Breakers — working-class subjects); Édouard Manet (bridge to Impressionism)
  - **Impressionism and Post-Impressionism**
    - Claude Monet (Impression, Sunrise, 1872), Pierre-Auguste Renoir, Edgar Degas, Camille Pissarro, Berthe Morisot, Mary Cassatt
    - Post-Impressionism: Paul Cézanne, Vincent van Gogh, Paul Gauguin, Georges Seurat — foundations of modern art
  - **Philosophy**
    - **Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel (1770–1831)**: dialectic (thesis-antithesis-synthesis); philosophy of history (Geist/Spirit progressing toward freedom); influence on Marx, existentialism, and virtually all subsequent continental philosophy
    - **Friedrich Nietzsche (1844–1900)**: "God is dead"; will to power; Übermensch; critique of Christian morality; eternal recurrence; Thus Spoke Zarathustra; Beyond Good and Evil — profoundly influential and profoundly contested
    - **Auguste Comte (1798–1857)**: positivism; sociology as "social physics"; law of three stages (theological, metaphysical, positive/scientific)
    - Utilitarianism: Bentham and **John Stuart Mill** (see above)
    - American Pragmatism: Charles Sanders Peirce, William James (Pragmatism, 1907) — truth as what works
  - **Sociology as discipline**: Comte coined the term; Émile Durkheim (The Division of Labour in Society, Rules of Sociological Method, Suicide); Max Weber (The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism, 1905); Georg Simmel — founding generation
  - **Psychology**: Wilhelm Wundt (first psychology laboratory, Leipzig, 1879); William James (Principles of Psychology, 1890); **Sigmund Freud** (The Interpretation of Dreams, 1899) — psychoanalysis emerging at century's end

- **Frontier and contemporary research directions**
  - **Global and transnational turn**: moving beyond national frames; Jürgen Osterhammel's global 19th century; Sebastian Conrad, Globalisation and the Nation in Imperial Germany (2010); "entangled histories" (histoire croisée)
  - **New history of capitalism**: linking slavery, empire, finance, and industrial development; Sven Beckert, Caitlin Rosenthal (Accounting for Slavery, 2018); Daron Acemoglu and James Robinson (Why Nations Fail, 2012)
  - **Subaltern studies**: Ranajit Guha and the Subaltern Studies collective (from 1982) — recovering experiences of colonised peoples, peasants, workers, women from imperial archives; post-Gramscian approach
  - **Environmental history**: John McNeill, Something New Under the Sun (2000); industrial pollution, deforestation, species extinction; "Anthropocene" debates often locate start in 19th-century industrialisation
  - **Digital history**: digitised newspaper archives (British Newspaper Archive, Gallica, Chronicling America); computational text analysis of 19th-century print culture; network analysis of correspondence and trade
  - **Material culture and consumption**: imperial commodities (sugar, cotton, tea, rubber, opium) as lenses for global history; museum studies and repatriation debates (Benin Bronzes provenance to 1897 British punitive expedition)
  - **Settler colonialism**: Patrick Wolfe (1949–2016): "settler colonialism is a structure, not an event"; application to Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, North America, Palestine
  - **Gender and empire**: Antoinette Burton, Burdens of History (1994); Margaret Strobel; Mrinalini Sinha — gender as constitutive category of imperial analysis
  - **Key reference texts**
    - Eric Hobsbawm, The Age of Revolution, The Age of Capital, The Age of Empire (trilogy)
    - Jürgen Osterhammel, The Transformation of the World (2014)
    - C.A. Bayly, The Birth of the Modern World 1780–1914 (2004)
    - Robert Allen, The British Industrial Revolution in Global Perspective (2009)
    - E.P. Thompson, The Making of the English Working Class (1963)
    - Adam Hochschild, King Leopold's Ghost (1998)
    - Mike Davis, Late Victorian Holocausts (2001)
    - Jonathan Sperber, Revolutionary Europe 1780–1850 (2000)
    - John Darwin, The Empire Project (2009) and After Tamerlane (2007)
    - Eric Foner, Reconstruction: America's Unfinished Revolution (1988)

#### Modern History — 20th Century Global Conflicts (1900–1945)

- **Scope and framing**
  - Covers the first half of the "short 20th century" (Hobsbawm's Age of Extremes): from the pre-1914 world through the end of World War II
  - Key analytical themes: total war and its transformation of society, the collapse of empires, the rise of ideological mass movements (communism, fascism, liberal democracy), genocide and mass atrocity, global depression, and the remaking of the international order
  - Eric Hobsbawm, The Age of Extremes (1994): the 20th century as age of catastrophe (1914–1945) followed by golden age and then crisis
  - Central question: how did the most "civilised" and technologically advanced era in history produce the most destructive wars and genocides?

- **The world before 1914**
  - **European great-power system**
    - Five great powers: Britain, France, Germany, Austria-Hungary, Russia; plus rising powers (USA, Japan, Italy)
    - Alliance systems: Triple Alliance (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy) vs. Triple Entente (France, Russia, Britain) — created rigid blocs
    - Arms race: Anglo-German naval rivalry (dreadnought battleships — HMS Dreadnought, 1906); continental army expansion; military planning (Schlieffen Plan, Plan XVII)
    - Balkans as "powder keg of Europe": Ottoman retreat, rival nationalisms (Serbian, Bulgarian, Greek, Romanian), Austrian and Russian competing interests; Balkan Wars (1912–1913)
  - **Imperial zenith**
    - European empires at greatest territorial extent: ~84% of world's land surface under European or European-derived control by 1914
    - Tensions: Fashoda Crisis (Britain-France, 1898), Moroccan Crises (Germany-France, 1905, 1911), Boer War (1899–1902)
  - **Social and cultural ferment**
    - Mass politics: expanded suffrage, labour parties, socialist internationalism, women's suffrage movements
    - Cultural modernism emerging: Picasso, Stravinsky, Schoenberg, Kafka, Proust, Freud
    - La Belle Époque (France) / Edwardian Era (Britain) / Gilded Age → Progressive Era (US): surface prosperity masking deep inequalities and gathering storms

- **World War I (1914–1918)**
  - **Origins and outbreak**
    - Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary in Sarajevo (28 June 1914) by Gavrilo Princip (Bosnian Serb nationalist, Black Hand network)
    - July Crisis: Austrian ultimatum to Serbia; Russian mobilisation in support of Serbia; German support for Austria ("blank cheque"); alliance chains activated
    - Historiographic debate on responsibility: Fritz Fischer, Germany's Aims in the First World War (1961) — blamed German expansionism; Christopher Clark, The Sleepwalkers (2012) — shared responsibility, "Europe stumbled into war"; ongoing debate between intention and miscalculation
    - Schlieffen Plan: German plan to defeat France quickly through Belgium, then turn east against Russia; violation of Belgian neutrality brought Britain into the war
  - **Western Front**
    - Race to the Sea (autumn 1914): after Marne (German advance halted), both sides dug trenches from Swiss border to English Channel
    - **Trench warfare**: ~700 km of fortified lines; mud, rats, disease, shell shock; millions lived and died in trenches for four years
    - Key battles:
      - **Verdun (February–December 1916)**: German attrition strategy; ~700,000 total casualties; "They shall not pass" (Pétain, though phrase attribution debated); defining French national trauma
      - **Somme (July–November 1916)**: British "Big Push"; 57,470 British casualties on first day (1 July 1916, worst day in British military history); ~1 million total casualties; tanks first used (15 September 1916); gained ~10 km
      - **Passchendaele / Third Ypres (1917)**: Belgian mud; ~475,000 total casualties for minimal gain
      - Second Marne (1918): turning point toward Allied victory
    - Technology of WWI: machine guns, artillery (dominant killer — ~60% of casualties), poison gas (chlorine, phosgene, mustard gas — first major use at Ypres, April 1915), tanks (British innovation 1916, decisive by 1918), aircraft (reconnaissance, then fighters and bombers), submarines
  - **Eastern Front**
    - Far more mobile than Western Front; vast distances
    - Tannenberg (1914): decisive German victory over Russia; Hindenburg and Ludendorff
    - Brusilov Offensive (1916): major Russian success against Austria-Hungary; ~1.6 million total casualties; last successful Russian offensive
    - Russian military collapse by 1917 → revolution (see below)
    - Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (March 1918): punitive German peace imposed on Bolshevik Russia; lost Poland, Baltic states, Ukraine, Finland — vast territorial concessions (annulled by Armistice)
  - **Other theatres**
    - **Ottoman theatre**: Gallipoli/Dardanelles Campaign (1915–1916, British/ANZAC/French vs. Ottoman — Ottoman victory; foundational national myth for Australia, New Zealand, and modern Turkey; Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's rise); Mesopotamian Campaign (British took Baghdad 1917); Sinai and Palestine Campaign (Allenby took Jerusalem 1917, Damascus 1918); **Armenian Genocide** (see below)
    - Italian front: Isonzo battles (11 battles, 1915–1917); Caporetto (1917, Austrian-German breakthrough; Italian near-collapse); Vittorio Veneto (1918)
    - African campaigns: German East Africa (Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck — undefeated guerrilla campaign); loss of other German colonies
    - War at sea: Jutland (1916, largest naval battle — inconclusive but British strategic dominance maintained); **unrestricted submarine warfare** — German U-boats targeted Allied and neutral shipping; sinking of Lusitania (1915, 1,198 dead including 128 Americans) — contributed to US entry
    - **T.E. Lawrence ("Lawrence of Arabia")**: British liaison with Arab Revolt against Ottomans (1916–1918); Faisal ibn Hussein; guerrilla warfare; Seven Pillars of Wisdom
  - **The Armenian Genocide (1915–1923)**
    - Ottoman government (Committee of Union and Progress / Young Turks) initiated systematic deportation and mass killing of Armenian population
    - Estimated 1–1.5 million Armenians killed through massacre, death marches, starvation, and forced labour
    - First major genocide of the 20th century; Raphael Lemkin (who coined "genocide") studied it as precedent
    - Turkey officially denies genocide classification; recognised by ~30 countries, EU Parliament, US (Biden formally recognised it, 2021); International Association of Genocide Scholars affirms genocide status
    - Assyrian and Greek genocide also occurred during same period (estimates: 250,000–750,000 Assyrians; 300,000–900,000 Greeks)
  - **Entry of the United States (April 1917)**
    - Woodrow Wilson: initially pursued neutrality; "He kept us out of war" (1916 campaign)
    - Triggers: unrestricted submarine warfare resumed (February 1917); Zimmermann Telegram (Germany proposed Mexican alliance against US); ideological affinity with Allies
    - American Expeditionary Forces (John J. Pershing): ~2 million troops deployed by 1918; fresh manpower decisive in final offensives
    - Wilson's **Fourteen Points** (January 1918): vision for post-war order — self-determination, open diplomacy, League of Nations, free trade, disarmament
  - **Home fronts and social transformation**
    - Total war mobilisation: entire economies directed toward war production; rationing, conscription, censorship, propaganda
    - **Women's roles**: entered factories, transport, agriculture, nursing in unprecedented numbers — "If the women in the factories stopped work for twenty minutes, the Allies would lose the war" (French Marshal Joffre); contributed to suffrage gains after war (Britain 1918 partial / 1928 full; US 19th Amendment 1920)
    - Shell shock / combat trauma: first mass recognition of psychological casualties of war; initially treated punitively; later recognition as genuine condition (now PTSD)
    - Colonial contributions: millions of colonial subjects served or laboured for European powers — Indian troops, African troops, Indochinese labourers, Chinese Labour Corps; their sacrifice raised expectations for post-war rights and self-determination
  - **Armistice and aftermath**
    - German Spring Offensives (March–July 1918): Ludendorff's last gamble; initial success then exhaustion
    - Hundred Days Offensive (August–November 1918): Allied counter-attacks; Amiens (8 August — "Black Day of the German Army"); continuous advance
    - German military collapse; revolution: Kaiser Wilhelm II abdicated (9 November 1918); republic declared
    - **Armistice: 11 November 1918, 11:00 AM** — "the eleventh hour of the eleventh day of the eleventh month"
    - Casualties: ~9–10 million military dead; ~7–8 million civilian dead; ~21 million wounded; empires destroyed (German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, Russian)
  - **Treaty of Versailles and the peace settlement (1919)**
    - **Paris Peace Conference**: dominated by "Big Four" — Wilson (US), Lloyd George (Britain), Clemenceau (France), Orlando (Italy)
    - Treaty of Versailles (28 June 1919):
      - **Article 231 (War Guilt Clause)**: Germany accepted responsibility for causing the war — deeply resented
      - Reparations: initially set at 132 billion gold marks (reduced and restructured multiple times; Dawes Plan 1924, Young Plan 1929; effectively ended 1932)
      - Territorial losses: Alsace-Lorraine to France, West Prussia and Posen to Poland (Polish Corridor), Danzig as free city, Saarland under League administration, all colonies
      - Military restrictions: army limited to 100,000; no air force, tanks, or submarines; Rhineland demilitarised
      - John Maynard Keynes, The Economic Consequences of the Peace (1919): argued reparations were economically catastrophic and would breed resentment; influential but debated (Sally Marks, Étienne Mantoux countered)
    - Other treaties: Saint-Germain (Austria), Trianon (Hungary — lost 2/3 of territory; lasting trauma in Hungarian politics), Neuilly (Bulgaria), Sèvres/Lausanne (Ottoman/Turkey)
    - **League of Nations**: created per Wilson's vision; headquartered in Geneva; US Senate rejected membership (isolationism); weakened from start; no enforcement mechanism; unable to prevent aggression in 1930s
    - Mandate system: former Ottoman and German territories administered by Allied powers under League oversight — de facto colonialism (Class A: Middle Eastern mandates — France: Syria/Lebanon; Britain: Iraq, Palestine, Transjordan; Class B/C: African and Pacific)
    - Sykes-Picot Agreement (1916, secret): British-French partition of Ottoman Middle East; Balfour Declaration (1917): British support for "national home for the Jewish people" in Palestine — foundations of Israeli-Palestinian conflict

- **The Russian Revolution (1917) and the Soviet Union**
  - **February Revolution (March 1917, Old Style calendar)**
    - Bread riots and strikes in Petrograd; soldiers refused to fire on crowds; Tsar Nicholas II abdicated (15 March)
    - Provisional Government formed (Prince Lvov, then Alexander Kerensky) — committed to continuing war; dual power with Petrograd Soviet (workers' and soldiers' council)
  - **October Revolution (7 November 1917, New Style)**
    - **Vladimir Ilyich Lenin (1870–1924)**: Bolshevik leader; returned from exile (sealed train through Germany); April Theses — "Peace, Land, Bread"; all power to soviets
    - Leon Trotsky (1879–1940): organised Military Revolutionary Committee; actual seizure of power
    - Bolsheviks seized key buildings in Petrograd; Winter Palace stormed (relatively bloodless initial coup; later mythologised by Eisenstein)
    - Constituent Assembly elected (November 1917): Social Revolutionaries won majority; Bolsheviks dissolved it after one day (January 1918) — end of democratic path
  - **Russian Civil War (1917–1922)**
    - Red Army (Bolsheviks, organised by Trotsky) vs. White Armies (diverse anti-Bolshevik forces: monarchists, liberals, socialists, nationalists) vs. Green armies (peasant bands) vs. foreign intervention (British, French, American, Japanese, Czech Legion)
    - War Communism: grain requisitioning, nationalisation, labour conscription — economic disaster but military necessity
    - Red Terror: Cheka (secret police, Felix Dzerzhinsky); execution of Romanov family (Yekaterinburg, July 1918)
    - White defeat by 1920; Red Army victory through organisation, central position, disunity of Whites
    - Famine of 1921–1922: ~5 million dead; partly consequence of war and requisitioning
    - New Economic Policy (NEP, 1921): partial market restoration; "strategic retreat" — mixed economy that produced recovery
  - **USSR established (1922)**
    - Union of Soviet Socialist Republics: Russia, Ukraine, Byelorussia, Transcaucasia — nominally federal, effectively centralised
    - Lenin's final years: strokes (1922–1923); "Testament" warned against Stalin; died January 1924
    - Succession struggle: Stalin vs. Trotsky vs. Zinoviev-Kamenev → **Joseph Stalin (1878–1953)** consolidated power by 1929
  - **Stalinism**
    - **Collectivisation of agriculture (1929–1933)**
      - Forced amalgamation of peasant farms into collective farms (kolkhozy)
      - "Dekulakisation": ~5–7 million "kulaks" (relatively prosperous peasants) deported, imprisoned, or killed
      - **Holodomor / Ukrainian Famine (1932–1933)**: estimated 3.5–7.5 million dead in Ukraine; debate over whether deliberate genocide or consequence of policies — recognised as genocide by Ukraine and ~20 countries; Timothy Snyder, Bloodlands (2010); Robert Conquest, The Harvest of Sorrow (1986); Andrea Graziosi's research
    - **Five-Year Plans (from 1928)**
      - Crash industrialisation: steel, coal, electricity, heavy machinery
      - Spectacular growth in heavy industry; USSR became industrial power within a decade
      - Human cost: forced labour, GULAG system, ecological destruction, consumer deprivation
    - **Great Terror / Great Purge (1936–1938)**
      - Show trials of Old Bolsheviks: Zinoviev, Kamenev, Bukharin — forced confessions, executed
      - Purge of military: ~35,000 officers dismissed or killed, including Marshal Tukhachevsky — devastating military capability on eve of WWII
      - Mass arrests: estimated ~750,000 executed, 1.3 million sentenced to GULAG (NKVD Order 00447 quotas); millions more affected
      - GULAG system: network of forced-labour camps across USSR; Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn, The Gulag Archipelago (1973, smuggled to West); Anne Applebaum, Gulag: A History (2003): estimated 18 million passed through GULAG system; ~1.5–1.8 million died in camps
    - **Stalinist culture**: socialist realism as mandatory artistic doctrine; cult of personality; Lysenkoist pseudo-science (genetics suppressed; Trofim Lysenko's Lamarckian theories favoured for ideological reasons — devastating for Soviet biology and agriculture)

- **The interwar world (1918–1939)**
  - **Weimar Republic (Germany, 1918–1933)**
    - Born of military defeat and revolution; democratic constitution (1919)
    - Crises: Spartacist Uprising (1919, Rosa Luxemburg and Karl Liebknecht — killed); Kapp Putsch (1920); hyperinflation (1923 — a loaf of bread cost billions of marks; wheelbarrows of money); Ruhr occupation by France (1923)
    - "Golden Age" (1924–1929): Stresemann's diplomacy (Locarno Treaties 1925; Germany admitted to League of Nations 1926); cultural efflorescence (Bauhaus, Expressionist cinema, Brecht, Weimar cabaret)
    - Structural weaknesses: proportional representation fragmented parliament; Article 48 emergency powers; army and judiciary hostile to republic; right-wing myth of "stab in the back" (Dolchstoßlegende — that Germany was undefeated militarily but betrayed by civilians/Jews/socialists)
  - **The Great Depression (1929–c. 1939)**
    - US stock market crash (29 October 1929, "Black Tuesday"): speculative bubble burst
    - Contraction: US GDP fell ~30%; unemployment reached ~25% (US), ~30% (Germany); industrial production collapsed; international trade shrank ~65%
    - Bank failures, farm foreclosures, Hoovervilles (US), mass unemployment queues
    - Global transmission: US recalled loans to Europe; Smoot-Hawley Tariff (1930) — protectionism worsened global trade
    - Keynesian response: **John Maynard Keynes, The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money (1936)**: argued for government deficit spending to stimulate demand during downturns; challenged classical economics; foundational for macroeconomics
    - **New Deal (US)**: Franklin D. Roosevelt (president 1933–1945); emergency banking reform, Social Security (1935), Works Progress Administration, Tennessee Valley Authority, Wagner Act (labour); "alphabet agencies"; partial recovery; debate over effectiveness continues
    - Political consequences: Depression radicalised politics worldwide; provided fertile ground for fascism in Europe and militarism in Japan; undermined faith in liberal capitalism
  - **Rise of fascism**
    - **Fascist Italy**
      - **Benito Mussolini (1883–1945)**: former socialist; founded fasci di combattimento (1919); March on Rome (October 1922); invited by King Victor Emmanuel III to form government
      - Fascist state: one-party dictatorship, cult of Il Duce, corporatism (state mediation of labour-capital relations); suppression of opposition (Matteotti murder, 1924; Aventine Secession); Lateran Treaty with Vatican (1929)
      - Imperialism: invasion of Ethiopia (1935–1936) — use of poison gas against Ethiopian forces; League of Nations sanctions ineffective; Haile Selassie's appeal to League
      - Model for other fascist movements: influence on Hitler, Salazar (Portugal), Franco (Spain)
    - **Nazi Germany**
      - **Adolf Hitler (1889–1945)**: Austrian-born; failed artist; WWI veteran; joined/took over Deutsche Arbeiterpartei → NSDAP (Nazi Party)
      - Mein Kampf (1925): racial ideology, antisemitism, Lebensraum (living space in the East), Führerprinzip (leader principle); initially dismissed
      - Beer Hall Putsch (1923): failed coup in Munich; Hitler imprisoned; Mein Kampf written in prison
      - Electoral rise: Nazis went from 2.6% (1928) to 18.3% (September 1930) to 37.3% (July 1932); Depression, political chaos, and propaganda (Joseph Goebbels) fuelled growth
      - **Hitler appointed Chancellor (30 January 1933)** by President Hindenburg under pressure from conservative elites who believed they could control him
      - Enabling Act (March 1933): effectively ended Weimar democracy; one-party state; Gleichschaltung (coordination) — every institution brought under Nazi control
      - **Nazi ideology**: racial supremacism (Aryan "master race"); virulent antisemitism (Jews as existential enemy of the race); Social Darwinism; anti-communism; anti-liberalism; Volksgemeinschaft (people's community); cult of violence and war
      - **Persecution escalation**:
        - Boycott of Jewish businesses (April 1933); Nuremberg Laws (1935): stripped Jews of citizenship and banned intermarriage
        - **Kristallnacht / November Pogrom (9–10 November 1938)**: organised nationwide attacks on Jewish businesses, synagogues, homes; ~91 killed, 30,000 arrested and sent to concentration camps; turning point toward open violence
        - Forced emigration, ghettoisation, and ultimately genocide (Holocaust — see below)
      - Rearmament: violated Versailles openly from 1935; conscription reintroduced; Luftwaffe created; military spending drove economic recovery (alongside Autobahn, labour service, women pushed from workforce)
    - **Other authoritarian/fascist regimes**: Hungary (Horthy), Romania (Iron Guard, Antonescu), Poland (Piłsudski), Baltic states, Yugoslavia (royal dictatorship); Portugal (Salazar, Estado Novo from 1933); Japan (military-dominated government from 1930s)
  - **Spanish Civil War (1936–1939)**
    - Republic vs. Nationalist rebels (General **Francisco Franco** and military conspirators); left-wing Popular Front government vs. right-wing coalition of army, Church, monarchists, Falangists, Carlists
    - International dimension: Germany and Italy supported Franco (Condor Legion — bombing of Guernica, 26 April 1937; Picasso's Guernica painting); Soviet Union supported Republic; International Brigades (~40,000 foreign volunteers, including George Orwell — Homage to Catalonia)
    - Britain and France: Non-Intervention Agreement — effectively helped Franco by denying arms to Republic
    - Republican defeat (1939); Franco dictatorship until 1975
    - Significance: testing ground for WWII weaponry and tactics; ideological polarisation; ~500,000 dead; ~500,000 refugees
  - **Japanese militarism and expansion**
    - Taishō Democracy (1912–1926): brief liberal parliamentary phase; undermined by economic crisis and military influence
    - Manchuria Incident (Mukden Incident, 18 September 1931): Japanese army fabricated pretext for invasion; established puppet state Manchukuo (1932); Japan left League of Nations (1933)
    - Military factionalism: assassinations and coup attempts (February 26 Incident, 1936); military effectively controlled government by late 1930s
    - Second Sino-Japanese War (from July 1937):
      - Full-scale invasion of China; Marco Polo Bridge Incident as trigger
      - **Nanjing Massacre / Rape of Nanjing (December 1937 – January 1938)**: Japanese troops perpetrated mass murder, rape, and destruction in Chinese capital Nanjing; estimated 200,000–300,000 killed (Chinese estimates higher; some Japanese revisionists contest figures; Iris Chang, The Rape of Nanking, 1997)
      - Chongqing as wartime Chinese capital; Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalists and Mao Zedong's Communists in uneasy cooperation against Japan
      - Unit 731: Japanese biological warfare research unit; horrific human experimentation; ~3,000–10,000 directly killed in experiments; biological weapons deployed in China
  - **Road to World War II**
    - **Appeasement**: British and French policy of conceding to Hitler's demands to avoid war
      - Remilitarisation of Rhineland (March 1936): Hitler's first major gamble; France and Britain did not respond
      - Anschluss (March 1938): annexation of Austria; plebiscite under coercion
      - **Munich Agreement (September 1938)**: Chamberlain, Daladier ceded Czechoslovak Sudetenland to Germany; "peace for our time" (Chamberlain); Winston Churchill: "a total and unmitigated defeat"
      - German occupation of rest of Czechoslovakia (March 1939): proved Hitler's promises worthless; ended appeasement
    - **Nazi-Soviet Non-Aggression Pact (Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, 23 August 1939)**: shocked the world; secret protocol divided Eastern Europe into spheres of influence; freed Hitler to attack Poland without two-front war

- **World War II (1939–1945)**
  - **European theatre**
    - **Invasion of Poland (1 September 1939)**: Blitzkrieg (combined arms — tanks, motorised infantry, air support); Britain and France declared war (3 September); Soviet Union invaded eastern Poland (17 September); Poland partitioned
    - "Phoney War" (September 1939 – April 1940): little fighting on Western Front
    - **Fall of France (May–June 1940)**: German forces attacked through Ardennes (bypassing Maginot Line); stunning victory in 6 weeks; Dunkirk evacuation (~338,000 Allied troops rescued); Vichy France (Pétain) collaborated with Germany; Free French under Charles de Gaulle continued from London
    - **Battle of Britain (July–October 1940)**: Luftwaffe vs. RAF; "The Few"; radar advantage; Hitler postponed invasion (Operation Sea Lion); the Blitz — bombing of London and British cities; Churchill: "their finest hour"
    - **North African Campaign (1940–1943)**: Italy's failed invasion of Egypt; Rommel's Afrika Korps; El Alamein (October–November 1942, Montgomery) — first major British land victory; Allied invasion of French North Africa (Operation Torch, November 1942); Axis surrender in Tunisia (May 1943)
    - **Invasion of the Soviet Union — Operation Barbarossa (22 June 1941)**
      - Largest military operation in history: ~3.8 million Axis troops invaded on ~2,900 km front
      - Initial devastating Soviet losses: millions of prisoners, vast territory lost
      - **Battle of Moscow (October 1941 – January 1942)**: German advance halted; Soviet counter-offensive; first major German setback
      - **Siege of Leningrad (September 1941 – January 1944)**: ~872 days; ~800,000–1,000,000 civilian dead (primarily starvation); one of history's deadliest sieges
      - **Battle of Stalingrad (August 1942 – February 1943)**: turning point of the war; German 6th Army (Friedrich Paulus) encircled and destroyed; ~2 million total casualties (both sides); "not one step back" (Order 227); decisive Soviet victory; Germany never regained strategic initiative in East
      - **Battle of Kursk (July 1943)**: largest tank battle in history (~6,000 tanks engaged); Soviet victory; permanent Soviet offensive initiative
      - Soviet advance westward (1943–1945): liberation of Ukraine, Byelorussia, Baltic states, Poland, Romania, Hungary, Czechoslovakia; massive casualties; Red Army reached Berlin
      - **Eastern Front scale**: ~80% of German military casualties occurred on the Eastern Front; ~27 million Soviet citizens died (military and civilian combined — largest national death toll of any belligerent); Soviet contribution to defeating Nazi Germany was primary and decisive
    - **Italian Campaign (1943–1945)**: Allied invasion of Sicily (July 1943); Mussolini overthrown; Italy switched sides; German defence; Anzio, Monte Cassino; slow grinding advance; Rome liberated (4 June 1944); Mussolini killed by partisans (April 1945)
    - **D-Day and the Western Front (1944–1945)**
      - **Operation Overlord / D-Day (6 June 1944)**: largest amphibious invasion in history; five beaches in Normandy (Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, Sword); ~156,000 troops landed on first day; fierce German resistance, especially at Omaha
      - Breakout from Normandy; Liberation of Paris (25 August 1944); advance toward Germany
      - **Battle of the Bulge (December 1944 – January 1945)**: last major German offensive; initial surprise; ultimately failed
      - Crossing the Rhine (March 1945); Allied advance into Germany; meeting with Soviet forces at Elbe River (Torgau, 25 April 1945)
      - **Hitler's suicide in Berlin bunker (30 April 1945)**; German unconditional surrender (8 May 1945, V-E Day)
  - **Pacific theatre**
    - **Pearl Harbor (7 December 1941)**: Japanese surprise attack on US naval base in Hawaii; ~2,403 Americans killed; 8 battleships damaged/sunk; "a date which will live in infamy" (Roosevelt); US declared war on Japan (8 December); Germany declared war on US (11 December)
    - Japanese expansion (December 1941 – mid-1942): Philippines (Fall of Bataan, Bataan Death March), Malaya, Singapore (15 February 1942 — largest British surrender in history), Dutch East Indies, Burma, Hong Kong, Wake Island, Guam
    - **Midway (4–7 June 1942)**: decisive naval battle; US (intelligence advantage — code-breaking) sank 4 Japanese carriers; turning point in Pacific war
    - Island-hopping strategy: Guadalcanal (1942–1943), Tarawa, Saipan, Guam, Peleliu, Iwo Jima (1945 — flag-raising photograph; ~7,000 US marines killed; ~18,000 Japanese killed), Okinawa (1945 — ~12,000 US, ~100,000 Japanese military, ~100,000+ Okinawan civilians dead)
    - Burma Campaign: British-Indian forces; Imphal and Kohima (1944) — "Stalingrad of the East"; defeated Japanese invasion of India
    - Philippines recaptured (1944–1945): Battle of Leyte Gulf — largest naval battle in history; MacArthur's "I have returned"
    - Strategic bombing of Japan: firebombing of Tokyo (9–10 March 1945 — ~100,000 killed, deadliest single air raid in history); systematic destruction of Japanese cities
    - **Atomic bombs**
      - **Manhattan Project**: US-British-Canadian secret programme to develop nuclear weapons; J. Robert Oppenheimer as scientific director; Trinity test (16 July 1945, New Mexico — "I am become death, the destroyer of worlds")
      - **Hiroshima** (6 August 1945, "Little Boy" uranium bomb): ~80,000 killed instantly, ~140,000 dead by end of 1945
      - **Nagasaki** (9 August 1945, "Fat Man" plutonium bomb): ~40,000 killed instantly, ~70,000 dead by end of 1945
      - Soviet declaration of war on Japan (8 August); invasion of Manchuria
      - Japan surrendered (15 August 1945, V-J Day; formal ceremony 2 September on USS Missouri)
      - Debate: whether atomic bombs were necessary to end the war, or whether Japan would have surrendered due to Soviet entry and conventional bombing alone; Tsuyoshi Hasegawa, Racing the Enemy (2005); Gar Alperovitz, The Decision to Use the Atomic Bomb (1995); debate is politically and morally charged and ongoing
  - **The Holocaust (Shoah)**
    - **Systematic genocide of European Jews (1941–1945)**
      - Preceded by years of escalating persecution: Nuremberg Laws, Kristallnacht, ghettoisation (Warsaw Ghetto — ~400,000 confined; Łódź, Kraków, Minsk, and hundreds more)
      - Mobile killing units (Einsatzgruppen): mass shootings following German advance into Soviet Union (1941); ~1.5 million Jews murdered in "Holocaust by bullets" (Babi Yar, September 1941 — ~34,000 Jews shot in two days near Kyiv; Father Patrick Desbois, Holocaust by Bullets, 2008)
      - **Wannsee Conference (20 January 1942)**: senior Nazi officials coordinated the "Final Solution to the Jewish Question" — administrative planning for industrial genocide
      - **Extermination camps (Vernichtungslager)**: Auschwitz-Birkenau (~1.1 million killed, ~90% Jews), Treblinka (~800,000–900,000), Sobibor (~200,000), Belzec (~600,000), Chelmno (~150,000–340,000), Majdanek — gas chambers and crematoria; industrial killing
      - **Auschwitz**: largest and most infamous; both concentration and extermination camp; selections on arrival (Dr. Josef Mengele — sadistic medical experiments); Arbeit Macht Frei gate; liberated by Soviet forces (27 January 1945 — now International Holocaust Remembrance Day)
      - Warsaw Ghetto Uprising (April–May 1943): doomed resistance by Jewish fighters; crushed by SS; ghetto destroyed
      - Death marches (1944–1945): as Allies advanced, prisoners force-marched westward; thousands died of exhaustion, cold, and shooting
    - **Total Jewish victims: approximately 6 million** — roughly 2/3 of European Jewry
      - Poland: ~3 million (90% of pre-war Jewish population); Soviet Union: ~1–1.5 million; Hungary: ~565,000; Romania: ~270,000–380,000; Germany: ~165,000; Netherlands: ~102,000; and throughout occupied Europe
    - **Other victims of Nazi genocide and mass killing**:
      - Roma and Sinti (Porajmos): estimated 220,000–500,000 killed
      - Soviet POWs: ~3.3 million died in German captivity (starvation, shooting, forced labour) — largest identifiable group of German war victims after Jews
      - Disabled persons: **Aktion T4** (1939–1941): ~70,000 killed in gas chambers; programme officially halted after public protest (Bishop Clemens August von Galen) but continued covertly — ~200,000+ total
      - Political prisoners, homosexuals (~5,000–15,000 in camps), Jehovah's Witnesses, Slavic civilians (millions of Soviet and Polish civilians deliberately killed)
    - **Key historiographic debates**
      - Intentionalist vs. functionalist: was the Holocaust planned from the start (Hitler's intention) or did it emerge through cumulative radicalisation (bureaucratic escalation)? Modern consensus: "twisted road" — radicalisation through policy evolution within a framework of Hitler's ideological obsession
      - Role of ordinary people: Daniel Goldhagen, Hitler's Willing Executioners (1996) — "eliminationist antisemitism"; Christopher Browning, Ordinary Men (1992) — situational factors; Hannah Arendt, Eichmann in Jerusalem (1963) — "banality of evil"
      - Bystanders, collaborators, rescuers: Yad Vashem recognises ~28,000 "Righteous Among the Nations"; collaboration across occupied Europe (Vichy France, Hungary, Baltic states); Catholic Church's role debated (Pius XII's "silence")
      - Holocaust memory and representation: Primo Levi (If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz, 1947); Elie Wiesel (Night, 1956); Art Spiegelman (Maus, 1980–1991); Claude Lanzmann's Shoah (1985, 9-hour documentary); oral history programs (USC Shoah Foundation, Steven Spielberg); Yad Vashem and USHMM as memorial institutions
    - Nuremberg Trials (1945–1946): International Military Tribunal; 24 major war criminals tried; 12 sentenced to death; established principles of individual criminal responsibility for crimes against humanity, war crimes, crimes against peace; foundation for international criminal law
  - **WWII casualties and scale**
    - **Total deaths: estimated 70–85 million** (including famine and disease)
    - Soviet Union: ~27 million; China: ~15–20 million; Poland: ~5.8 million (~17% of pre-war population — highest percentage); Germany: ~7–9 million; Japan: ~2.5–3.1 million; Yugoslavia: ~1–1.7 million; France: ~600,000; UK: ~450,000; US: ~420,000
    - Civilian deaths exceeded military deaths for the first time in a major conflict — strategic bombing, genocide, famine, occupation terror
    - Infrastructure destruction: much of Europe and East Asia devastated; millions of refugees and displaced persons

- **Decolonisation stirrings and anti-colonial movements (pre-1945)**
  - Indian independence movement: Mohandas K. **Gandhi** — nonviolent civil disobedience (satyagraha); Salt March (1930); Quit India Movement (1942); Indian National Congress and Muslim League
  - Chinese revolution: Sun Yat-sen (1911 revolution); warlord era; Chinese Communist Party (founded 1921); Long March (1934–1935, Mao Zedong); united front against Japan
  - Pan-Africanism: W.E.B. Du Bois; Pan-African Congresses (from 1900); Marcus Garvey (UNIA); Négritude movement (Aimé Césaire, Léopold Sédar Senghor)
  - Mandate system's contradictions: promised eventual independence but delivered colonial administration; Arab revolts in Palestine (1936–1939); Iraqi independence (1932)
  - Atlantic Charter (1941, Roosevelt and Churchill): affirmed "the right of all peoples to choose the form of government under which they will live" — colonised peoples noted the promise

- **1918 influenza pandemic**
  - "Spanish Flu" (misnamed — likely originated in US or China): three waves (spring 1918, autumn 1918, winter 1919)
  - Estimated 50–100 million dead worldwide (recent estimates trend higher); ~500 million infected (~1/3 of world population)
  - Disproportionately killed young adults (unusual for influenza); wartime censorship suppressed news; overcrowded troop ships and camps facilitated spread
  - Largely forgotten for decades; rediscovered as subject of historical study (Alfred Crosby, America's Forgotten Pandemic, 1989; John Barry, The Great Influenza, 2004); COVID-19 pandemic (2020) renewed interest

- **Frontier and contemporary research directions**
  - **Holocaust studies**: continued oral history collection (USC Shoah Foundation — 55,000+ testimonies); archaeological excavations at extermination camp sites (Sobibor, Treblinka); digital humanities applications to testimony analysis; debates over Holocaust uniqueness vs. comparative genocide studies
  - **WWI centennial scholarship (2014–2018)**: massive wave of new research; Christopher Clark's Sleepwalkers debate; cultural history of the war (Jay Winter, Sites of Memory, Sites of Mourning); environmental history of the trenches; colonial soldiers' experience
  - **WWII re-evaluation**: emphasis on global scope beyond European/Pacific binary; Asian and African theatres; Timothy Snyder, Bloodlands (2010) — zone of mass killing between Hitler and Stalin; new attention to Soviet contribution and Eastern Front
  - **Fascism studies**: comparative fascism (Robert Paxton, The Anatomy of Fascism, 2004); relations between interwar fascism and contemporary far-right movements; debate over applicability of "fascism" label today
  - **Economic history of the Depression**: Barry Eichengreen, Golden Fetters (1992) — gold standard as transmission mechanism; Ben Bernanke's research on Depression's banking dimension (influenced his Fed policy 2008–2014)
  - **Genocide studies**: Raphael Lemkin's legacy; prevention frameworks; International Criminal Court (est. 2002); comparative genocide analysis (Armenian, Holocaust, Rwandan, Bosnian, Cambodian)
  - **Gender and war**: women's military service, home-front transformation, sexual violence in wartime, gendered memory
  - **Digital and public history**: vast digitisation of WWII archives; Veterans History Project (Library of Congress); AI-assisted analysis of wartime photography and documents; 3D reconstruction of destroyed sites
  - **Key reference texts**
    - Christopher Clark, The Sleepwalkers: How Europe Went to War in 1914 (2012)
    - John Keegan, The First World War (1998) and The Second World War (1989)
    - Richard Overy, The Bombing War: Europe 1939–1945 (2013) and Russia's War (1997)
    - Timothy Snyder, Bloodlands: Europe Between Hitler and Stalin (2010)
    - Saul Friedländer, Nazi Germany and the Jews (2 vols., 1997/2007) — definitive integrated history
    - Ian Kershaw, Hitler (2 vols., 1998/2000) — definitive biography
    - Robert Paxton, The Anatomy of Fascism (2004)
    - Stephen Kotkin, Stalin (3 projected vols., 2014–) — monumental biography
    - Rana Mitter, Forgotten Ally: China's World War II, 1937–1945 (2013)
    - Adam Tooze, The Wages of Destruction: The Making and Breaking of the Nazi Economy (2006)
    - Eric Hobsbawm, The Age of Extremes: The Short Twentieth Century 1914–1991 (1994)

#### Modern History — Cold War, Decolonization, and Globalisation (1945–2000)

- **Scope and framing**
  - Covers the second half of the "short 20th century": from the post-WWII settlement through the end of the millennium
  - Key analytical themes: bipolar superpower rivalry, nuclear deterrence, decolonization and Third World emergence, ideological competition (liberal capitalism vs. Marxism-Leninism), human rights discourse, economic globalisation, and the unexpected collapse of communism
  - Periodisation debate: "Cold War" (1947–1991) vs. broader "postwar era" (1945–2000); Odd Arne Westad, The Cold War: A World History (2017): Cold War as global phenomenon, not merely US-Soviet standoff
  - Eric Hobsbawm's framework: "Golden Age" (1945–1973) of unprecedented growth → "Landslide" (1973–1991) of crisis and transformation

- **Post-war settlement and the origins of the Cold War (1945–1949)**
  - **Wartime conferences**
    - Yalta (February 1945): Roosevelt, Churchill, Stalin — agreed on Soviet entry against Japan, Polish borders, UN structure, "Declaration on Liberated Europe" (free elections — not honoured in Soviet sphere)
    - Potsdam (July–August 1945): Truman (replaced Roosevelt), Attlee (replaced Churchill mid-conference), Stalin — German occupation zones, reparations, Oder-Neisse line as Polish-German border, Potsdam Declaration demanding Japanese surrender
  - **United Nations**
    - Founded: San Francisco Conference (April–June 1945); UN Charter signed 26 June 1945; entered force 24 October 1945
    - Structure: General Assembly, Security Council (5 permanent members with veto: US, USSR/Russia, UK, France, China), Secretariat, International Court of Justice, Economic and Social Council, Trusteeship Council
    - Universal Declaration of Human Rights (10 December 1948): drafted by committee chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt; 30 articles; foundational document of international human rights law; not legally binding but enormously influential
    - Specialized agencies: WHO, UNESCO, FAO, UNICEF, ILO (pre-existing), IMF, World Bank (Bretton Woods institutions, 1944)
  - **Occupation and reconstruction of Germany and Japan**
    - Germany divided into four occupation zones (US, British, French, Soviet); Berlin divided similarly; denazification (Nuremberg Trials and broader processes — uneven); Bizone (1947), then Trizone; currency reform (June 1948) precipitated Berlin crisis
    - **Berlin Blockade (June 1948 – May 1949)**: Soviet blockade of West Berlin; US-British airlift (~2.3 million tons of supplies over 11 months — "Candy Bomber" Gail Halvorsen); Soviets lifted blockade; cemented division
    - Federal Republic of Germany (West, May 1949); German Democratic Republic (East, October 1949)
    - Japan: US occupation under General Douglas MacArthur (SCAP); new constitution (1947) — Article 9 renounced war; land reform; zaibatsu dissolution (partial); women's suffrage; Emperor Hirohito retained as symbolic figurehead; Tokyo War Crimes Tribunal (1946–1948)
  - **Origins of the Cold War — historiographic debate**
    - **Orthodox/traditional**: Soviet expansionism and communist ideology as primary cause; US responded defensively
    - **Revisionist** (1960s–70s): William Appleman Williams, Walter LaFeber — US economic imperialism and provocative policies drove Soviet reaction
    - **Post-revisionist**: John Lewis Gaddis — both sides bore responsibility; security dilemma, miscommunication, ideological incompatibility
    - **Post-Cold War**: archival revelations (Soviet, Chinese, Eastern European); Vladislav Zubok, A Failed Empire (2007); Melvyn Leffler, For the Soul of Mankind (2007) — more nuanced understanding of both sides' decision-making
  - **Key early Cold War developments**
    - Winston Churchill's "Iron Curtain" speech (Fulton, Missouri, March 1946): "From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent"
