    - **Truman Doctrine (March 1947)**: US pledged to support "free peoples who are resisting attempted subjugation" — immediate context: Greek civil war, Turkish straits; beginning of containment policy
    - **George Kennan's "Long Telegram" (February 1946) and "X Article" (Foreign Affairs, July 1947)**: architect of containment strategy — Soviet power contained would eventually mellow or collapse; profoundly influenced US Cold War policy
    - **Marshall Plan (European Recovery Program, 1948–1952)**: ~$13 billion in US aid to Western Europe (~$150 billion in 2024 dollars); accelerated recovery; cemented Western bloc; Soviets refused participation and pressured Eastern Europe to refuse
    - **NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization, 4 April 1949)**: collective defence alliance; Article 5 — attack on one is attack on all; founding members: US, Canada, UK, France, and 8 others; Lord Ismay's quip: purpose was "to keep the Russians out, the Americans in, and the Germans down"
    - **Soviet response**: Cominform (1947, Communist Information Bureau); COMECON (1949, Council for Mutual Economic Assistance); Warsaw Pact (1955, military alliance)
    - Soviet atomic bomb (29 August 1949): ended US nuclear monopoly; initiated arms race

- **The Cold War: confrontation and crises (1949–1962)**
  - **Chinese Revolution (1949)**
    - Chinese Civil War resumed (1946–1949) after WWII: Mao Zedong's Communist Party of China vs. Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalists (Kuomintang)
    - Communist victory: People's Republic of China proclaimed (1 October 1949); Nationalists fled to Taiwan
    - Mao Zedong (1893–1976): peasant-based revolution (unlike Soviet urban proletariat model); adapted Marxism-Leninism to Chinese conditions; established one-party state
    - US "loss of China" debate: political recrimination; contributed to McCarthyism; Sino-Soviet alliance (1950) alarmed the West
  - **Korean War (1950–1953)**
    - Korea divided at 38th parallel (1945): Soviet-backed North (Kim Il-sung) vs. US-backed South (Syngman Rhee)
    - North Korean invasion (25 June 1950); UN forces (primarily US, under MacArthur) intervened; Inchon landing (September 1950); advance to Yalu River; massive Chinese intervention (October 1950); retreat; stalemate near 38th parallel
    - MacArthur dismissed by Truman (April 1951) for insubordination (wanting to use nuclear weapons, attack China)
    - Armistice (27 July 1953): ceasefire along current DMZ; no peace treaty; technically still at war; ~2.5–3 million dead (majority Korean and Chinese civilians)
    - Significance: first "hot war" of Cold War; globalised containment; militarised NATO; established precedent for US military commitment in Asia
  - **McCarthyism and domestic Cold War (US)**
    - Senator Joseph McCarthy (1950–1954): accusations of communist infiltration of US government; House Un-American Activities Committee (HUAC); Hollywood blacklist; loyalty oaths; Rosenberg executions (1953, for passing nuclear secrets to Soviets)
    - Army-McCarthy hearings (1954): "Have you no sense of decency, sir?" (Joseph Welch); McCarthy censured; McCarthyism discredited but left lasting impact on civil liberties discourse
  - **Khrushchev era and de-Stalinisation**
    - Stalin's death (5 March 1953); power struggle; **Nikita Khrushchev** emerged as leader
    - **Secret Speech (February 1956)**: Khrushchev denounced Stalin's cult of personality and terror at 20th Party Congress; shockwaves through communist world; leaked to West
    - Hungarian Revolution (October–November 1956): popular uprising; Imre Nagy declared neutrality and withdrawal from Warsaw Pact; Soviet tanks crushed revolution; ~2,500 Hungarians killed; Nagy executed (1958); demonstrated limits of de-Stalinisation and Western non-intervention behind Iron Curtain
    - Polish October (1956): Gomułka returned to power; some liberalisation; Soviet restraint
  - **Space race**
    - **Sputnik (4 October 1957)**: first artificial satellite; launched by USSR; profound psychological shock in US; triggered "Sputnik crisis" — increased science education funding (National Defense Education Act), creation of NASA (1958), DARPA
    - Yuri Gagarin (12 April 1961): first human in space (Vostok 1); enormous Soviet propaganda victory
    - Alan Shepard (May 1961): first American in space (suborbital); John Glenn (February 1962): first American in orbit
    - **Kennedy's moon commitment (25 May 1961)**: "before this decade is out, of landing a man on the Moon and returning him safely to the Earth"
    - **Apollo 11 (20 July 1969)**: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon; "That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind"; Michael Collins orbited; defining moment of the century; 5 more successful landings through Apollo 17 (1972)
    - Soviet Luna programme, space stations (Salyut, Mir); Space Shuttle (US, 1981–2011)
  - **Berlin Wall (13 August 1961)**
    - East Germany hemorrhaging population (~3.5 million fled to West, 1945–1961); Berlin as escape route
    - Wall erected overnight; divided city for 28 years; symbol of Cold War division; attempted escapes and deaths (~140 killed at Wall)
    - JFK visit (26 June 1963): "Ich bin ein Berliner" speech
  - **Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962)**
    - Background: **Cuban Revolution** (1959): Fidel Castro overthrew Batista; aligned with USSR; Bay of Pigs invasion failure (April 1961, CIA-backed exiles routed)
    - Soviets secretly deployed nuclear missiles in Cuba; US aerial reconnaissance (U-2) discovered them (14 October 1962)
    - **13 days of crisis** (16–28 October): Kennedy imposed naval "quarantine" (blockade); ExComm deliberations; Khrushchev initially defiant; back-channel communications (Robert Kennedy – Anatoly Dobrynin); world on brink of nuclear war
    - Resolution: Khrushchev withdrew missiles; US secretly pledged not to invade Cuba and quietly removed Jupiter missiles from Turkey
    - Aftermath: "hotline" established (Moscow–Washington, 1963); Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (1963); both sides sobered; beginning of arms control era
    - Graham Allison, Essence of Decision (1971): classic analysis using three decision-making models (rational actor, organizational process, bureaucratic politics)

- **Decolonization: the end of European empires**
  - **South and Southeast Asia**
    - **Indian independence and partition (15 August 1947)**
      - Mountbatten Plan: British India divided into Hindu-majority India and Muslim-majority Pakistan (West and East)
      - Partition violence: ~1–2 million killed; ~10–15 million displaced; communal massacres; largest mass migration in history
      - Gandhi assassinated (30 January 1948) by Hindu extremist Nathuram Godse
      - **Jawaharlal Nehru**: first Prime Minister of India (1947–1964); non-alignment; Five-Year Plans; democratic socialism; Nehruvian vision shaped postcolonial India
      - Kashmir dispute: India-Pakistan wars (1947–48, 1965, 1971, 1999); unresolved; nuclear dimension after 1998 tests
    - **Indonesia**: declaration of independence (17 August 1945, Sukarno); Dutch "police actions" (1947, 1948); international pressure; sovereignty transferred (1949); Sukarno's Guided Democracy; 1965 anti-communist massacre (~500,000–1 million killed); Suharto's New Order (1966–1998)
    - **Indochina / Vietnam**
      - Ho Chi Minh declared Vietnamese independence (2 September 1945, quoting US Declaration of Independence)
      - First Indochina War (1946–1954): Viet Minh vs. France; **Dien Bien Phu** (1954, decisive French defeat); Geneva Accords — Vietnam divided at 17th parallel
      - → Vietnam War (see below)
    - Burma (Myanmar): independence 1948 (Aung San assassinated 1947); decades of military rule
    - Malaya: communist insurgency (Malayan Emergency, 1948–1960); independence 1957; Malaysia formed 1963
    - Philippines: independence from US (4 July 1946)
  - **Middle East**
    - **Creation of Israel (14 May 1948)**
      - Zionist movement (Theodor Herzl, 1897); British Mandate Palestine; Jewish immigration (especially post-Holocaust); UN Partition Plan (Resolution 181, November 1947)
      - David Ben-Gurion declared independence; immediate invasion by Arab armies (Egypt, Transjordan, Syria, Iraq, Lebanon)
      - 1948 Arab-Israeli War (Israeli War of Independence / Palestinian Nakba): Israel won; ~700,000 Palestinians displaced ("catastrophe" / Nakba); Palestinian refugee crisis — unresolved to present
      - Subsequent wars: Suez Crisis (1956), Six-Day War (1967 — Israel captured West Bank, Gaza, Sinai, Golan Heights), Yom Kippur War (1973), Lebanon Wars (1982, 2006); Intifadas (1987–1993, 2000–2005); peace process: Camp David Accords (1978, Egypt-Israel, Sadat-Begin-Carter), Oslo Accords (1993, PLO-Israel, Rabin-Arafat-Clinton)
    - **Iran**: Mossadegh nationalised oil (1951); CIA/MI6 coup (Operation Ajax, 1953) — restored Shah; decades of authoritarian modernisation → Iranian Revolution (see below)
    - **Egypt**: Free Officers' coup (1952); Gamal Abdel **Nasser** — Arab nationalism, Suez Crisis (1956, nationalised Suez Canal; Anglo-French-Israeli invasion; US and USSR forced withdrawal; humiliation of old empires, triumph of Third World nationalism); United Arab Republic (1958–1961, with Syria); Aswan High Dam (Soviet-funded)
    - **Algeria**: war of independence (1954–1962) — one of most violent decolonisation struggles; FLN vs. France; Battle of Algiers (1957); torture controversy; de Gaulle conceded; Évian Accords (1962); ~300,000–1.5 million Algerian dead (disputed figures); Frantz Fanon, The Wretched of the Earth (1961) — classic anti-colonial text
    - **Iraq**: monarchy overthrown (1958); Baathist rule from 1968; Saddam Hussein (1979–2003)
    - **Suez to oil**: OPEC founded (1960); oil as geopolitical weapon
  - **Africa**
    - **"Year of Africa" (1960)**: 17 African nations gained independence
    - **Ghana** (1957): first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence; Kwame **Nkrumah** — Pan-Africanism, African socialism, non-alignment; overthrown 1966
    - **Congo Crisis (1960–1965)**: Belgian Congo independence (30 June 1960); Patrice **Lumumba** elected PM; secession of Katanga (Tshombe, backed by Belgian mining interests); Lumumba assassinated (January 1961, with Belgian and CIA complicity — confirmed by 2001 Belgian parliamentary commission); Mobutu Sese Seko seized power (1965) — Cold War client dictator for 32 years
    - **Kenya**: Mau Mau Uprising (1952–1960) against British settlers; brutal British counterinsurgency (detention camps, torture — Caroline Elkins, Imperial Reckoning, 2005); independence (1963); Jomo Kenyatta
    - **French Africa**: mostly negotiated transitions; French maintained economic and military influence ("Françafrique"); Félix Houphouët-Boigny (Côte d'Ivoire), Léopold Sédar Senghor (Senegal)
    - **Portuguese Africa**: last European colonial wars; liberation wars in Angola, Mozambique, Guinea-Bissau (from 1961/1964); achieved independence only after Portuguese Carnation Revolution (1974)
    - **Southern Africa**:
      - **Apartheid South Africa (1948–1994)**: National Party — institutionalised racial segregation; pass laws, Bantustans, Group Areas Act; **African National Congress (ANC)**: Nelson Mandela, Walter Sisulu, Oliver Tambo; Sharpeville Massacre (1960, police killed 69 protesters); ANC banned; Mandela imprisoned (1962–1990, Robben Island); Soweto Uprising (1976 — students protesting Afrikaans-medium education); international sanctions and divestment campaign; F.W. de Klerk released Mandela (February 1990); negotiations; first democratic elections (27 April 1994); **Mandela** president (1994–1999); Truth and Reconciliation Commission (Desmond Tutu)
      - Rhodesia/Zimbabwe: Ian Smith's UDI (1965); guerrilla war (ZANU, ZAPU); Lancaster House Agreement (1979); Zimbabwe independent (1980); Robert Mugabe
    - Postcolonial challenges: arbitrary colonial borders, ethnic tensions, one-party states, military coups, Cold War proxy conflicts, structural adjustment, debt crisis; Basil Davidson, The Black Man's Burden: Africa and the Curse of the Nation-State (1992)
  - **Caribbean and Pacific**: wave of small-state independence (Jamaica, Trinidad 1962; Barbados 1966; Pacific islands 1960s–1980s; Hong Kong return to China 1997)

- **The Vietnam War (1955–1975)**
  - **Background**: divided Vietnam (North: Democratic Republic, Ho Chi Minh, communist; South: Republic of Vietnam, Ngo Dinh Diem, US-backed)
  - **US escalation**
    - Eisenhower: advisory role; domino theory
    - Kennedy: increased advisors (~16,000 by 1963); Strategic Hamlet Program (failed); Diem overthrown and killed in US-backed coup (1963)
    - **Gulf of Tonkin Incident (August 1964)**: alleged North Vietnamese attacks on US destroyer (second incident likely did not occur); Gulf of Tonkin Resolution — gave Johnson virtually unlimited war authority
    - **Lyndon B. Johnson**: escalation — Operation Rolling Thunder (sustained bombing of North Vietnam, 1965–1968); ground troops deployed (March 1965); troop levels peaked at ~543,000 (1969)
  - **The war**
    - Guerrilla warfare: Viet Cong (National Liberation Front) in South; Ho Chi Minh Trail (supply route through Laos and Cambodia); tunnels (Cu Chi); booby traps
    - Conventional warfare: North Vietnamese Army (NVA/PAVN) increasingly important
    - **Tet Offensive (January–March 1968)**: surprise attacks across South Vietnam including Saigon and Hue; military defeat for North but psychological/political victory — shattered US confidence; Walter Cronkite: "we are mired in stalemate"
    - My Lai Massacre (16 March 1968): US soldiers killed 347–504 unarmed civilians; covered up for over a year; Lieutenant William Calley convicted; shattered American moral narrative
    - **Anti-war movement**: largest anti-war movement in US history; campus protests, draft resistance, teach-ins; Kent State shootings (4 May 1970, Ohio National Guard killed 4 students); the war divided American society profoundly
  - **Nixon and Vietnamization**
    - Richard Nixon (president 1969–1974): "Vietnamization" — transfer fighting to South Vietnamese forces while withdrawing US troops
    - Secret bombing of Cambodia (1969–1973); Cambodian incursion (1970, sparked Kent State); Laotian incursion (1971)
    - Pentagon Papers (1971): leaked by Daniel Ellsberg; revealed government deception about war's progress; landmark press freedom case (New York Times Co. v. United States)
    - Paris Peace Accords (January 1973): ceasefire; US withdrawal; Kissinger and Le Duc Tho awarded Nobel Peace Prize (Le Duc Tho refused)
  - **Fall of Saigon (30 April 1975)**: North Vietnamese forces captured Saigon; iconic helicopter evacuation from US Embassy; Vietnam reunified under communist rule
  - **Aftermath and legacy**
    - ~2–3 million Vietnamese dead (both sides); ~58,000 Americans; ~300,000 Cambodians; ~60,000 Laotians
    - Agent Orange (chemical defoliant): ~20 million gallons sprayed; lasting health and environmental effects; birth defects in subsequent generations
    - Boat people: ~800,000 Vietnamese refugees fled by sea (1975–1995); thousands drowned
    - **Cambodia**: Khmer Rouge (Pol Pot) seized power (1975); Year Zero ideology; genocide of ~1.5–2 million (out of ~7 million population) through execution, starvation, forced labour; overthrown by Vietnamese invasion (1979); Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia (UN-backed tribunal, from 2006)
    - Vietnam syndrome: US reluctance to commit to foreign military interventions for a generation
    - Key texts: Neil Sheehan, A Bright Shining Lie (1988); Fredrik Logevall, Embers of War (2012) and Choosing War (1999); Viet Thanh Nguyen, Nothing Ever Dies: Vietnam and the Memory of War (2016)

- **Cold War: détente and its collapse (1963–1985)**
  - **Détente era (late 1960s – late 1970s)**
    - Arms control: Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT, 1968); Strategic Arms Limitation Talks — SALT I (1972, ABM Treaty); SALT II (1979, never ratified by US Senate)
    - Nixon-Kissinger diplomacy:
      - **Nixon in China (February 1972)**: historic visit; "opening to China"; exploited Sino-Soviet split; transformed geopolitics; normalisation of US-PRC relations (1979)
      - **Ostpolitik (West Germany)**: Willy Brandt — rapprochement with East Germany, Poland, USSR; Kniefall von Warschau (genuflecting at Warsaw Ghetto memorial, 1970); Nobel Peace Prize (1971)
      - Helsinki Accords (1975): recognised post-WWII European borders; human rights provisions (Basket III) — used by dissidents in Eastern bloc
    - **Sino-Soviet split**: ideological rivalry between Mao's China and Khrushchev's USSR (from late 1950s); border clashes (1969); China as third pole in Cold War
  - **1968 — year of global upheaval**
    - Paris May 1968: student protests and general strike; nearly toppled de Gaulle; worker-student alliance
    - Prague Spring (Czechoslovakia): Alexander Dubček — "socialism with a human face"; liberalisation; Soviet-led Warsaw Pact invasion (August 1968); crushed reform; Brezhnev Doctrine (USSR reserved right to intervene in socialist states)
    - Mexico City: Tlatelolco Massacre (2 October 1968, government troops killed ~300+ students days before Olympics)
    - US: Martin Luther King Jr. and Robert Kennedy assassinated; anti-Vietnam protests; Democratic Convention chaos (Chicago)
    - Cultural revolution: counterculture, civil rights, feminism, anti-colonial movements interconnected globally
  - **Third World Cold War**
    - Non-Aligned Movement (NAM): Bandung Conference (1955); founding leaders — Nehru, Nasser, Tito, Sukarno, Nkrumah; attempted neutrality between superpowers; G-77 bloc at UN; New International Economic Order demands (1970s)
    - Cold War proxy conflicts:
      - Angola civil war (1975–2002): MPLA (Soviet/Cuban-backed) vs. UNITA (US/South African-backed); Cuban troops (~50,000); devastating
      - Horn of Africa: Ethiopia (Derg, Soviet-backed after 1977) vs. Somalia; Ogaden War (1977–1978); Ethiopian famine (1983–1985, partly engineered)
      - Nicaragua: Sandinista Revolution (1979); Contra War (US-backed; Iran-Contra scandal, 1986)
      - Afghanistan (see below)
    - Latin American dictatorships and US intervention: Operation Condor (coordinated repression by Southern Cone military regimes — Argentina, Chile, Brazil, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia — CIA-supported); Chilean coup (11 September 1973, Pinochet overthrew Allende — elected socialist president; Allende died; 17 years of dictatorship; ~3,000 killed/disappeared); Argentine "Dirty War" (1976–1983, ~30,000 disappeared; Madres de Plaza de Mayo)
  - **Mao's China**
    - Great Leap Forward (1958–1962): forced collectivisation and industrialisation; catastrophic famine — estimated 15–55 million dead (most likely ~30–45 million; Frank Dikötter, Mao's Great Famine, 2010); one of the deadliest man-made disasters in history
    - **Cultural Revolution (1966–1976)**: Mao's campaign to reassert control and purge "capitalist roaders"; Red Guards; destruction of cultural heritage; persecution of intellectuals; millions killed or persecuted; chaos; economy and education disrupted; Gang of Four
    - Mao's death (1976); Deng Xiaoping rose to power (1978); launched economic reforms — "Reform and Opening Up"; market mechanisms within one-party state; Special Economic Zones (Shenzhen); spectacular economic growth; political control maintained
  - **Soviet stagnation under Brezhnev (1964–1982)**
    - Leonid Brezhnev: era of "developed socialism"; military-industrial complex; gerontocracy; corruption; declining economic performance; environmental degradation
    - Dissidents: Andrei Sakharov (physicist, Nobel Peace Prize 1975); Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn (exiled 1974); samizdat (underground publishing); Helsinki Watch Groups
    - **Soviet invasion of Afghanistan (December 1979)**: supported communist government against mujahideen insurgency; US, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia armed mujahideen (including Osama bin Laden's Arab volunteers); "Soviet Vietnam" — 10-year war; ~1 million Afghans dead; 5 million refugees; Soviet withdrawal (1989); contributed to Soviet collapse; CIA's Operation Cyclone
    - Andropov (1982–1984) and Chernenko (1984–1985): brief, ailing leaders; system in crisis

- **Civil rights and social movements (1945–2000)**
  - **African American civil rights movement (US)**
    - Background: Jim Crow segregation (since 1890s); NAACP (1909); A. Philip Randolph; Double V Campaign (WWII)
    - Brown v. Board of Education (1954): Supreme Court unanimously ruled segregation in public schools unconstitutional; overturned Plessy v. Ferguson (1896)
    - Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–1956): Rosa Parks's arrest; **Martin Luther King Jr.** (1929–1968) emerged as leader; nonviolent direct action, Christian social gospel, Gandhian philosophy
    - Little Rock Nine (1957); sit-ins (Greensboro, 1960); Freedom Riders (1961); Birmingham Campaign (1963, Bull Connor's fire hoses and dogs); March on Washington (28 August 1963, "I Have a Dream" speech — 250,000 participants)
    - **Civil Rights Act (1964)**: outlawed discrimination based on race, colour, religion, sex, national origin; Voting Rights Act (1965): outlawed discriminatory voting practices; 24th Amendment (1964): abolished poll taxes
    - **Malcolm X** (1925–1965): Nation of Islam; Black nationalism; later moderated; assassinated; The Autobiography of Malcolm X (1965)
    - Black Power movement (from 1966): Stokely Carmichael / Kwame Ture; Black Panther Party (Huey Newton, Bobby Seale); raised questions of structural racism beyond legal equality
    - King assassinated (4 April 1968, Memphis); Robert Kennedy assassinated (5 June 1968); urban riots; Fair Housing Act (1968)
    - Legacy and continuation: affirmative action debates; mass incarceration (Michelle Alexander, The New Jim Crow, 2010); Black Lives Matter (from 2013)
  - **Feminist movements**
    - Second-wave feminism (1960s–1980s): Betty Friedan, The Feminine Mystique (1963); NOW (1966); consciousness-raising; Simone de Beauvoir's The Second Sex (1949) as foundational text
    - Key gains: contraceptive pill (approved US 1960; Griswold v. Connecticut 1965); Roe v. Wade (1973, legalised abortion in US — overturned 2022); Title IX (1972, educational equality); anti-discrimination legislation
    - International: UN International Women's Year (1975); CEDAW (1979); Beijing Declaration (1995)
    - Third-wave feminism (1990s): intersectionality (Kimberlé Crenshaw, 1989); inclusivity of race, class, sexuality; Judith Butler, Gender Trouble (1990) — gender as performative
  - **LGBTQ+ rights movement**
    - Stonewall Riots (28 June 1969, New York): police raid on Stonewall Inn; resistance by LGBTQ+ patrons (led prominently by trans women of colour — Marsha P. Johnson, Sylvia Rivera); catalyst for modern LGBTQ+ rights movement
    - Gay liberation movement (1970s); AIDS crisis (1981– ): devastation; government neglect (especially Reagan administration); ACT UP (1987, "Silence = Death"); NAMES Project AIDS Memorial Quilt; antiretroviral therapy breakthroughs (1996)
    - Decriminalisation: gradual across Western countries (UK 1967, US: Lawrence v. Texas 2003)
    - Same-sex marriage: Denmark (civil partnerships, 1989); Netherlands (first same-sex marriages, 2001); spread globally
  - **Environmental movement**
    - Rachel Carson, Silent Spring (1962): pesticides and ecological damage; launched modern environmental movement
    - Earth Day (first: 22 April 1970); Environmental Protection Agency (US, 1970); Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act
    - Whole Earth Catalog; "Limits to Growth" (Club of Rome, 1972); oil crises (1973, 1979) raised energy consciousness
    - Greenpeace (1971); environmental parties (German Greens, entered parliament 1983); Brundtland Report — "sustainable development" (Our Common Future, 1987)
    - Climate change awareness: James Hansen's congressional testimony (1988); IPCC created (1988); Kyoto Protocol (1997); Rio Earth Summit (1992)
  - **Anti-apartheid, indigenous rights, and other movements**: global anti-apartheid solidarity; Australian Aboriginal land rights (Mabo decision, 1992); indigenous movements in Americas; disability rights (ADA 1990)

- **End of the Cold War and collapse of communism (1985–1991)**
  - **Mikhail Gorbachev (General Secretary, 1985–1991)**
    - **Glasnost** (openness): press freedom, public debate, revealing of Soviet crimes; dissidents released; open discussion of history
    - **Perestroika** (restructuring): economic reform; decentralisation; limited market mechanisms; unintended consequence — loosened political control without delivering economic improvement
    - **"New Thinking" in foreign policy**: reduced tensions; willingness to make asymmetric concessions
    - INF Treaty (1987): eliminated intermediate-range nuclear missiles — first actual reduction of nuclear arsenals
    - Withdrawal from Afghanistan (completed February 1989)
    - Reagan-Gorbachev summits (Geneva 1985, Reykjavik 1986, Washington 1987, Moscow 1988): transformed superpower relationship
  - **Revolutions of 1989**
    - Poland: Solidarity trade union (Lech Wałęsa, from 1980); martial law (Jaruzelski, 1981); Round Table Agreements (April 1989); first semi-free elections (June 1989); Solidarity won overwhelmingly; Tadeusz Mazowiecki, first non-communist PM in Eastern bloc
    - Hungary: reformist communists opened border with Austria (May 1989); East Germans fled through Hungary; multiparty elections (1990)
    - East Germany: mass protests (Leipzig Monday demonstrations, "Wir sind das Volk" — "We are the people"); Erich Honecker replaced; **Fall of the Berlin Wall (9 November 1989)**: confused press conference by Günter Schabowski; crowds surged to border crossings; jubilant destruction of Wall; defining image of the century's end
    - Czechoslovakia: **Velvet Revolution** (November–December 1989): mass protests; Václav Havel — playwright, dissident, president; peaceful transition
    - Romania: violent revolution; Nicolae Ceaușescu overthrown and executed (December 1989); only Eastern European revolution with significant bloodshed
    - Bulgaria, Albania: transitions followed
    - Timothy Garton Ash, The Magic Lantern (1990): eyewitness account of 1989 revolutions
  - **German reunification (3 October 1990)**: rapid unification process; Helmut Kohl; economic shock therapy for East; "blossoming landscapes" promised but slow to materialise; ongoing East-West disparities (Ostalgie phenomenon)
  - **Dissolution of the Soviet Union**
    - Baltic states: independence movements (1988–1991); Lithuanian declaration of independence (March 1990); Soviet crackdown in Vilnius (January 1991, 14 killed)
    - Nationality movements throughout USSR: Georgia, Armenia-Azerbaijan (Nagorno-Karabakh conflict), Ukraine, Central Asian republics
    - **August Coup (19–21 August 1991)**: hardliners attempted to overthrow Gorbachev; Boris Yeltsin rallied resistance (standing on tank outside Russian parliament); coup collapsed
    - Declarations of independence cascaded; Belavezha Accords (8 December 1991, Russia, Ukraine, Belarus dissolved USSR); Commonwealth of Independent States formed
    - **USSR ceased to exist (25 December 1991)**: Gorbachev resigned; Soviet flag lowered from Kremlin; Cold War over
    - Francis Fukuyama, "The End of History?" (1989 article, 1992 book): argued liberal democracy had triumphed as final form of government — enormously influential, much debated, later questioned

- **Post-Cold War world (1991–2000)**
  - **"New World Order" — unipolarity**
    - US as sole superpower; liberal internationalism; expansion of NATO eastward (Poland, Hungary, Czech Republic joined 1999); EU deepening (Maastricht Treaty 1992, Euro currency 1999)
    - "Washington Consensus": market liberalisation, privatisation, fiscal discipline as prescription for developing countries; IMF structural adjustment programmes; debate over effectiveness and social costs
  - **Yugoslav Wars (1991–2001)**
    - Breakup of Yugoslavia: Slovenia (Ten-Day War, 1991); Croatia (1991–1995, siege of Vukovar, ethnic cleansing); **Bosnia (1992–1995)**: multi-ethnic war; siege of Sarajevo (~1,400 days, longest modern siege); **Srebrenica massacre (July 1995)**: Bosnian Serb forces (Ratko Mladić) killed ~8,000 Bosnian Muslim men and boys — worst mass atrocity in Europe since WWII; classified as genocide by ICTY and ICJ; Dayton Accords (1995) ended war
    - Kosovo (1998–1999): Serbian repression of Albanian majority; NATO bombing campaign against Serbia (March–June 1999, without UN Security Council authorisation — controversial); Kosovo under UN administration; declared independence (2008)
    - International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia (ICTY, The Hague, 1993–2017): Slobodan Milošević tried (died during trial 2006); Radovan Karadžić and Ratko Mladić convicted of genocide; established precedents for international criminal justice
  - **Rwandan Genocide (April–July 1994)**
    - Hutu extremists systematically massacred Tutsi minority and moderate Hutus; ~800,000–1,000,000 killed in ~100 days (one of fastest rates of killing in history); machetes as primary weapon; Radio Télévision Libre des Mille Collines broadcast incitement
    - International failure: UN peacekeeping force (UNAMIR, Roméo Dallaire) present but under-resourced and restricted; US, France, and UN did not intervene effectively; Clinton later called it his "biggest regret"
    - RPF (Paul Kagame) halted genocide; massive refugee crisis in eastern Congo; Great Lakes conflicts
    - Gacaca community courts; ICTR (International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda); "never again" rhetoric questioned; Samantha Power, "A Problem from Hell": America and the Age of Genocide (2002)
  - **Gulf War (1990–1991)**
    - Iraq (Saddam Hussein) invaded and annexed Kuwait (August 1990); UN condemnation; massive US-led coalition (~700,000 troops, 34 nations)
    - Operation Desert Storm (January–February 1991): air campaign followed by 100-hour ground war; Iraqi forces routed; Kuwait liberated
    - Saddam remained in power; sanctions regime; no-fly zones; humanitarian catastrophe of sanctions debated; set stage for 2003 Iraq War
    - "Highway of Death"; Kurdish and Shia uprisings (encouraged by US, then not supported; brutally suppressed)
  - **Globalisation**
    - Acceleration of economic integration: world trade grew ~6% annually (1990s); foreign direct investment surged; global supply chains; containerisation
    - World Trade Organization (WTO, 1995, replacing GATT); NAFTA (1994); EU Single Market (1993)
    - Technology: personal computers, internet (World Wide Web — Tim Berners-Lee, 1991; Netscape 1994; dot-com boom), mobile phones; globalised communication
    - Financial crises: Mexican peso crisis (1994); **Asian Financial Crisis (1997–1998)** — Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea; currency collapses; IMF bailouts with austerity conditions; Russian financial crisis (1998); LTCM collapse; raised questions about financial deregulation
    - Anti-globalisation movement: WTO protests in Seattle (1999); World Social Forum (from 2001, Porto Alegre); Joseph Stiglitz, Globalization and Its Discontents (2002)
  - **Collapse and transition in Russia**
    - Boris Yeltsin (president 1991–1999): "shock therapy" economic liberalisation; hyperinflation; oligarchs acquired state assets at bargain prices (loans-for-shares scandal, 1995); GDP fell ~40% (1990s); life expectancy dropped; poverty surged
    - Constitutional crisis (1993): Yeltsin ordered tanks to shell parliament building; new constitution concentrated presidential power
    - First Chechen War (1994–1996): disastrous; Grozny destroyed; ceasefire; de facto Chechen autonomy; Second Chechen War (1999–2009): Putin (initially as PM) launched renewed campaign; Grozny destroyed again; Chechnya pacified under Kadyrov; horrific human rights abuses documented
    - **Vladimir Putin** became acting president (31 December 1999); elected 2000; began consolidation of power
  - **Other significant developments 1991–2000**
    - South African transition to democracy (1994, see above)
    - Northern Ireland peace process: Troubles (1968–1998, ~3,500 dead); Good Friday Agreement (1998); power-sharing; largely successful
    - Middle East peace process: Madrid Conference (1991); Oslo Accords (1993): PLO recognised Israel, Israel recognised PLO; Yitzhak Rabin assassinated by Israeli extremist (1995); Camp David Summit (2000) failed; Second Intifada (2000–2005)
    - Tiananmen Square massacre (4 June 1989): pro-democracy protests in Beijing crushed by military; hundreds to thousands killed (exact toll unknown, still suppressed in China); "Tank Man" photo; China continued economic reform without political liberalisation
    - Hong Kong handover to China (1 July 1997): "one country, two systems" for 50 years
    - European integration: Schengen Agreement (1985/1995); expansion; Euro adopted (1999/2002); debate over depth vs. breadth of integration
    - Genocide Convention — tested and found wanting (Rwanda, Srebrenica); International Criminal Court established (Rome Statute, 1998; operational 2002)
    - Latin America: democratic transitions ("Third Wave of democratization," Samuel Huntington); neoliberal reforms; Hugo Chávez in Venezuela (1999) — "Bolivarian Revolution"; "pink tide" emerging

- **Frontier and contemporary research directions**
  - **Cold War studies post-archives**: opening of Soviet, Chinese, Eastern European archives transformed understanding; Cold War International History Project (Wilson Center); ongoing declassification; Odd Arne Westad's emphasis on Global South as central to Cold War
  - **Decolonization studies**: from national narratives to transnational approaches; Frederick Cooper, Colonialism in Question (2005); "connected histories"; environmental dimensions of decolonization; postcolonial state formation; settler colonialism as framework
  - **1989 and the end of communism**: why did it happen? Structural explanations (economic failure, imperial overstretch) vs. contingent (Gorbachev's personality); Mary Elise Sarotte, The Collapse: The Accidental Opening of the Berlin Wall (2014); Stephen Kotkin, Uncivil Society (2009)
  - **Genocide prevention**: Responsibility to Protect (R2P) doctrine (2005 World Summit); implementation failures (Syria, Myanmar); AI and early warning systems for mass atrocity; forensic identification of victims (DNA matching at Srebrenica ongoing)
  - **Vietnam War historiography**: transnational turn — Vietnamese, Cambodian, Laotian perspectives; Lien-Hang Nguyen, Hanoi's War (2012); ongoing legacy of Agent Orange and unexploded ordnance; memory wars in both US and Vietnam
  - **Globalisation history**: debates over periodisation (when did globalisation begin?); Sebastian Conrad, What Is Global History? (2016); Amartya Sen vs. Jagdish Bhagwati on globalisation and inequality
  - **Post-Soviet studies**: Russia's authoritarian turn; comparative transitions (successful: Poland, Baltics; struggled: Ukraine, Georgia; authoritarian: Central Asia); "transitology" questioned
  - **Digital history methods**: network analysis of Cold War alliances; text mining declassified documents; GIS mapping of refugee flows; social media archives for 1990s–2000s
  - **Key reference texts**
    - Odd Arne Westad, The Cold War: A World History (2017) — definitive global survey
    - John Lewis Gaddis, The Cold War: A New History (2005)
    - Tony Judt, Postwar: A History of Europe Since 1945 (2005)
    - Pankaj Mishra, From the Ruins of Empire: The Revolt Against the West and the Remaking of Asia (2012)
    - Frederick Cooper, Africa Since 1940: The Past of the Present (2002)
    - Marilyn Young, The Vietnam Wars 1945–1990 (1991)
    - Timothy Garton Ash, The Magic Lantern (1990) and The File (1997)
    - Stephen Kotkin, Armageddon Averted: The Soviet Collapse 1970–2000 (2001)
    - Samuel Moyn, The Last Utopia: Human Rights in History (2010)
    - Daniel Sargent, A Superpower Transformed: The Remaking of American Foreign Relations in the 1970s (2015)

#### Contemporary History (2000–present)

- **Scope and framing**
  - Covers the period from the turn of the millennium through the present (2000–2026), a period often called the "21st-century disorder" or "Age of Acceleration" (Thomas Friedman, Thank You for Being Late, 2016)
  - Defining analytical themes: global terrorism and the security state; the rise of China as a peer competitor to the US; the 2008 Global Financial Crisis and neoliberalism's legitimacy collapse; the return of great-power competition; the populist-nationalist backlash against globalisation; digital transformation and the attention economy; pandemics and biosecurity; climate crisis as an existential structural force; the AI revolution
  - Periodisation still contested — too recent for settled historiography; "history of the present" (Timothy Garton Ash)
  - Key methodological challenges: source overload (digital archives, social media, leaked cables — WikiLeaks, Snowden files); classification and access restrictions; speed of events outpacing analysis; presentism bias
  - Core tension: Fukuyama's "End of History" thesis (1989/1992) vs. Huntington's "Clash of Civilizations" (1993/1996) — both partially validated, both partially refuted; the 21st century defies monocausal framing

- **9/11 and the Global War on Terror (2001–)**
  - **The September 11 attacks (2001)**
    - 11 September 2001: 19 al-Qaeda hijackers seized four commercial aircraft
      - American Airlines Flight 11 struck North Tower of World Trade Center, New York, 8:46 AM; United Airlines Flight 175 struck South Tower, 9:03 AM; American Airlines Flight 77 struck the Pentagon, Arlington, Virginia, 9:37 AM; United Airlines Flight 93 crashed in Shanksville, Pennsylvania (passengers revolted), 10:03 AM
      - Both WTC towers collapsed (North Tower 10:28 AM, South Tower 9:59 AM); 7 World Trade Center collapsed later that afternoon
      - **2,977 victims killed** (excluding 19 hijackers): 2,753 at WTC, 184 at Pentagon, 40 in Shanksville; citizens from 77 countries; deadliest terrorist attack in history; deadliest foreign attack on US soil
    - **al-Qaeda**: founded ~1988 by Osama bin Laden (1957–2011, Saudi-born) and other "Afghan Arabs" during Soviet-Afghan War; ideological roots in Sayyid Qutb's revolutionary Salafism and Abdullah Azzam's global jihad concept
      - 1998 fatwa against "Jews and Crusaders"; East Africa embassy bombings (7 August 1998, Nairobi and Dar es Salaam, 224 dead); USS Cole attack (Aden, Yemen, October 2000, 17 US sailors killed)
      - Khalid Sheikh Mohammed: operational architect of 9/11 plot; captured 2003 in Pakistan; Military Commission trial ongoing (plea deal announced 2024, then rescinded)
      - 9/11 Commission Report (2004): detailed failures of US intelligence; recommended reforms including Director of National Intelligence position (created 2004)
    - Lawrence Wright, The Looming Tower: Al-Qaeda and the Road to 9/11 (2006, Pulitzer Prize) — definitive pre-history of 9/11
    - Steve Coll, Ghost Wars: The Secret History of the CIA, Afghanistan, and Bin Laden (2004) — CIA involvement with mujahideen and blowback
  - **War in Afghanistan (October 2001 – August 2021)**
    - **Operation Enduring Freedom** (7 October 2001): US-led invasion with Northern Alliance; Taliban regime collapsed within weeks; Kandahar fell December 2001; Osama bin Laden escaped at Tora Bora (December 2001 — failure to commit sufficient ground forces; Dalton Fury, Kill Bin Laden, 2008)
    - Karzai government: Bonn Agreement (December 2001); Hamid Karzai as chairman then president (2004–2014); international recognition; weak state capacity; corruption endemic
    - Taliban insurgency resurgence (from ~2003): Quetta Shura; sanctuaries in Pakistan's Federally Administered Tribal Areas (FATA); Haqqani Network; narco-economy (opium financed insurgency — Afghanistan produced ~80–90% of world's illicit opium)
    - NATO/ISAF mission (2003–2014): ~130,000 troops at peak (2011, Obama surge of 30,000); counterinsurgency doctrine (COIN, General David Petraeus, FM 3-24; David Kilcullen, The Accidental Guerrilla, 2009); Provincial Reconstruction Teams; mixed results
    - **Osama bin Laden killed** (2 May 2011, Operation Neptune Spear): US Navy SEALs (DEVGRU) raided compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan; body buried at sea; Mark Bowden, The Finish (2012)
    - Drawdown under Obama (2014: ISAF ended, Resolute Support advisory mission); limited US presence maintained under Trump
    - **US-Taliban Doha Agreement** (29 February 2020): Trump administration negotiated directly with Taliban (Ahmad Baradar), excluding Afghan government; US committed to full withdrawal by May 2021; Taliban committed to not harbor terrorists and to negotiate with Afghan government — negotiations largely failed
    - **Fall of Kabul (15 August 2021)**: Taliban swept across Afghanistan in weeks as US withdrew; Ashraf Ghani fled; chaotic evacuation from Hamid Karzai International Airport (~124,000 evacuated in 17 days); Abbey Gate bombing (26 August 2021, ISIS-K, 13 US service members and ~170 Afghans killed); last US forces left 30 August 2021 — 20-year war ended
    - Taliban government: rolled back women's education and employment rights; banned secondary and higher education for girls (December 2022); international isolation; humanitarian crisis; no country has formally recognised Taliban government as of 2026
    - Casualties: ~2,461 US military killed; ~3,846 US contractors; ~1,144 allied military; ~46,319 Afghan civilians killed directly (Watson Institute, Brown University Costs of War Project); ~176,000 total dead including combatants; estimated cost: >$2.3 trillion (US)
    - Carter Malkasian, The American War in Afghanistan: A History (2021) — comprehensive military and political history
  - **Iraq War (2003–2011)**
    - **Justification**: Bush administration asserted Iraq possessed weapons of mass destruction (WMD) and had links to al-Qaeda; Colin Powell's UN Security Council presentation (5 February 2003) — satellite photos, vials; subsequent investigation found both claims false
      - UN weapons inspectors (Hans Blix, UNMOVIC) found no WMD; IAEA (Mohamed ElBaradei) contested nuclear claims; France, Germany, Russia opposed invasion; largest global anti-war protests in history (15 February 2003, estimated 6–10 million worldwide)
      - "Downing Street Memo" (July 2002, leaked 2005): British intelligence assessment that "the intelligence and facts were being fixed around the policy"; Chilcot Report (UK, July 2016, 2.6 million words): concluded invasion was "not the last resort" and based on "flawed intelligence"
    - **Invasion (20 March – 1 May 2003)**: "Shock and Awe" air campaign; rapid ground advance; Baghdad fell (9 April); Saddam's statue toppled (staged by Marines, later revealed as psyop — Peter Maass, The Toppling, 2021, *The New Yorker*); Bush declared "Mission Accomplished" (1 May 2003, aboard USS Lincoln)
    - **Occupation and insurgency (2003–2011)**
      - Coalition Provisional Authority (Paul Bremer): disbanded Iraqi army (CPA Order 2, 23 May 2003 — widely considered catastrophic error; ~400,000 armed men suddenly unemployed); de-Baathification purge; alienated Sunni population
      - Sunni insurgency, Shia militias (Mahdi Army, Muqtada al-Sadr), al-Qaeda in Iraq (AQI, Abu Musab al-Zarqawi — killed June 2006), foreign fighters
      - **Abu Ghraib torture scandal** (photos leaked April 2004): US military police abused and humiliated Iraqi detainees; photographs published worldwide; devastating to US moral authority; Seymour Hersh investigative reporting; low-ranking soldiers convicted; systemic nature debated (Philip Zimbardo cited in defense; Senate Armed Services Committee report, December 2008, found responsibility at senior levels)
      - Sectarian civil war (2006–2008): al-Askari mosque bombing (February 2006, Samarra — destroyed Shia shrine; triggered massive sectarian violence); Baghdad morgues overwhelmed; ethnic cleansing of mixed neighborhoods; ~3,000+ civilian deaths per month at peak
      - **"Surge" (2007–2008)**: 30,000 additional US troops (General David Petraeus); combined with Sunni Awakening (Sahwa) — Sunni tribal militias turned against AQI; violence significantly reduced; success debated as temporary vs. structural
    - Saddam Hussein captured (13 December 2003, "spider hole" near Tikrit); tried by Iraqi tribunal; executed (30 December 2006)
    - US withdrawal (completed December 2011, Obama)
    - **WMD findings**: Iraq Survey Group (Charles Duelfer, 2004 final report): Iraq had no stockpiles of WMD and no active WMD programs after 1991; "one of the greatest intelligence failures in modern history"
    - Casualties: ~4,431 US military killed; ~31,994 wounded; Iraqi civilian deaths: estimates range from ~150,000 (Iraq Body Count, documented) to ~500,000+ (Lancet surveys, 2004/2006) to ~405,000 (PLOS Medicine, Hagopian et al., 2013); estimated cost: >$3 trillion (Joseph Stiglitz and Linda Bilmes, The Three Trillion Dollar War, 2008)
    - Thomas Ricks, Fiasco: The American Military Adventure in Iraq (2006); Dexter Filkins, The Forever War (2008); Rajiv Chandrasekaran, Imperial Life in the Emerald City (2006)
  - **Rise of ISIS / Islamic State (2013–2019)**
    - Emerged from remnants of AQI; Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi proclaimed "caliphate" (29 June 2014) spanning Mosul (Iraq) to Raqqa (Syria); controlled territory ~size of UK at peak (~8 million people under ISIS rule)
    - Ideology: ultra-extremist Salafi-jihadism; apocalyptic theology; persecution and genocide of Yazidis (August 2014, Sinjar — mass killings, sexual enslavement; UN Commission classified as genocide, 2016; Nadia Murad, Nobel Peace Prize 2018); mass executions of Shia; destruction of Palmyra, Nineveh, Nimrud (cultural heritage destruction); beheading videos as propaganda
    - Territorial defeat: US-led coalition (Operation Inherent Resolve, from 2014); Kurdish forces (YPG/SDF) in Syria; Iraqi army; Iranian-backed militias; Russian airstrikes (from 2015); Mosul recaptured (July 2017, after 9-month battle; massive destruction of Old City); Raqqa fell (October 2017); Baghouz (last territorial holdout, March 2019)
    - al-Baghdadi killed in US special operations raid (26 October 2019, Barisha, Syria); ISIS continued as insurgent and terrorist network; affiliates globally (ISIS-K in Afghanistan, ISIS-West Africa, ISIS-Mozambique)
    - Joby Warrick, Black Flags: The Rise of ISIS (2015, Pulitzer Prize); Jessica Stern and J.M. Berger, ISIS: The State of Terror (2015)
  - **The broader "War on Terror"**
    - **Drone warfare**: Predator and Reaper UAVs; targeted killings; Obama administration dramatically expanded drone strikes (Pakistan, Yemen, Somalia); Anwar al-Awlaki (US citizen, killed September 2011 in Yemen — constitutional controversy); civilian casualties disputed (Bureau of Investigative Journalism estimated 8,858–16,901 killed in US drone strikes, 2004–2020, including 910–2,200 civilians); Jeremy Scahill, Dirty Wars (2013)
    - **Guantánamo Bay detention facility** (opened January 2002): ~780 total detainees; military commissions; enhanced interrogation techniques (waterboarding — described as torture by Senate Intelligence Committee Report, December 2014, 6,700-page study); Obama pledged closure but failed; ~30 detainees remained as of 2024
    - **NSA mass surveillance**: revealed by **Edward Snowden** (June 2013): NSA's PRISM, Upstream collection, telephony metadata programs; bulk surveillance of Americans and foreign leaders; Snowden fled to Hong Kong then Russia; asylum granted; Glenn Greenwald, No Place to Hide (2014); Barton Gellman, Dark Mirror (2020); prompted USA FREEDOM Act (2015), partial reforms; EU-US Privacy Shield debates
    - **Domestic counterterrorism**: Department of Homeland Security created (2002); USA PATRIOT Act (October 2001) — expanded surveillance authorities; TSA and airport security transformation; Islamophobia and hate crimes surge; domestic lone-wolf attacks (Fort Hood 2009, San Bernardino 2015, Orlando Pulse nightclub 2016, New York truck attack 2017)
    - Global spread of jihadist groups: Boko Haram (Nigeria, 2002–; Chibok schoolgirls kidnapping 2014); al-Shabaab (Somalia); AQAP (Yemen); AQIM and affiliates (Sahel); counter-terrorism partnerships with authoritarian regimes raised human rights concerns

- **The rise of China**
  - **Economic ascent**
    - GDP growth: China's GDP (PPP) surpassed US in 2014 (IMF data); became world's largest manufacturer (~2010); largest trading nation (~2013); second-largest nominal GDP
    - "World's factory": Special Economic Zones → integrated into global supply chains; Shenzhen transformed from fishing village to 17.5-million tech megacity; Pearl River Delta as manufacturing hub
    - WTO accession (11 December 2001): integrated China into global trading system; dramatic export growth; "China shock" — David Autor, David Dorn, and Gordon Hanson, "The China Syndrome" (American Economic Review, 2013): estimated ~2.4 million US manufacturing jobs lost 1999–2011 due to Chinese import competition
    - Belt and Road Initiative (BRI, announced 2013): massive infrastructure investment across ~150 countries; estimated $1 trillion in projects; "debt-trap diplomacy" critique (Hambantota port, Sri Lanka, 99-year lease to China — though Anna Gelpern and others contest "debt trap" framing); geopolitical instrument; new Silk Road
    - Technology firms: Huawei, Tencent, Alibaba, ByteDance (TikTok), Baidu; "Made in China 2025" industrial policy for high-tech sectors; AI national strategy (2017); semiconductor competition (US export controls on advanced chips, October 2022 — Bureau of Industry and Security rules targeting NVIDIA A100/H100); 5G network deployment rivalry
  - **Xi Jinping era (General Secretary from November 2012, President from March 2013)**
    - Consolidated power: anti-corruption campaign (targeted ~1.5 million officials; Bo Xilai, Zhou Yongkang purged); abolished presidential term limits (March 2018, constitutional amendment — enabling indefinite rule); "Xi Jinping Thought" enshrined in party constitution (2017); most powerful Chinese leader since Mao
    - **Xinjiang**: mass internment of Uyghurs and other Turkic Muslims (from ~2017); estimated 1–1.8 million detained in "re-education" centres (Adrian Zenz research, ASPI investigations, leaked Xinjiang Police Files 2022); cultural suppression; surveillance state; forced labor allegations; US, EU, and others declared "genocide" or "crimes against humanity" (Uyghur Tribunal, December 2021, chaired by Sir Geoffrey Nice QC, concluded genocide through birth prevention)
    - **Hong Kong**: Umbrella Movement (2014); massive pro-democracy protests (2019–2020, triggered by extradition bill; at peak ~2 million marched, 25% of population); **National Security Law** (30 June 2020): imposed by Beijing; criminalised secession, subversion, terrorism, collusion with foreign forces; mass arrests of pro-democracy figures (Jimmy Lai, Joshua Wong, media outlets shut — Apple Daily forced closure June 2021); effectively ended "one country, two systems" a generation early
    - **Taiwan**: increased military pressure; regular PLA air incursions into Taiwan's ADIZ (scaled up dramatically after Nancy Pelosi's visit, August 2022 — largest Chinese military exercises around Taiwan since 1996 crisis); Xi stated "reunification must be fulfilled" (2022); semiconductor dependency (TSMC produces ~90% of world's most advanced chips); potential flashpoint for great-power conflict; global strategic importance
    - **South China Sea**: artificial island construction (from ~2013); Spratly and Paracel Islands; militarised despite Xi's 2015 pledge; Permanent Court of Arbitration ruling (Philippines v. China, 12 July 2016): rejected China's "nine-dash line" claims; China rejected ruling; US "freedom of navigation operations"; regional tensions with Philippines, Vietnam, Malaysia, Brunei, Indonesia
    - "Wolf warrior diplomacy" (from ~2019): assertive and combative diplomatic style; named after 2015 Chinese action film; exemplified by Zhao Lijian and others; COVID-19 origin debates heightened tensions
    - Elizabeth Economy, The Third Revolution: Xi Jinping and the New Chinese State (2018); Rush Doshi, The Long Game: China's Grand Strategy to Displace American Order (2021)
  - **US-China strategic competition**
    - Obama "pivot to Asia" (2011); Trans-Pacific Partnership (TPP, signed 2016 — US withdrew under Trump 2017, remainder became CPTPP)
    - Trump era: trade war (tariffs imposed 2018–2019 on ~$370 billion of Chinese goods); tech decoupling; Huawei sanctions; "great power competition" framework (National Security Strategy 2017 and National Defense Strategy 2018)
    - Biden era: maintained most Trump tariffs and tech restrictions; CHIPS and Science Act (August 2022, ~$280 billion including ~$52.7 billion for semiconductor manufacturing); AUKUS pact (September 2021, US-UK-Australia — nuclear-powered submarines for Australia); Quad (US, Japan, India, Australia) strengthened
    - Graham Allison, Destined for War: Can America and China Escape Thucydides's Trap? (2017)

- **The 2008 Global Financial Crisis and its aftermath**
  - **Origins**
    - US housing bubble: subprime mortgage lending; mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and collateralised debt obligations (CDOs); credit rating agency failures (Moody's, S&P, Fitch rated toxic assets AAA); deregulation (repeal of Glass-Steagall by Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act 1999; Commodity Futures Modernization Act 2000 — exempted derivatives from regulation)
    - Michael Lewis, The Big Short (2010): profiles investors who bet against housing market; Bethany McLean and Joe Nocera, All the Devils Are Here (2010)
  - **Crisis (2007–2009)**
    - BNP Paribas froze three funds (9 August 2007): "credit crunch" began; Northern Rock bank run (September 2007, UK — first in 150 years)
    - **Bear Stearns** rescued via sale to JPMorgan (March 2008, Fed-facilitated, $2/share initially)
    - **Lehman Brothers bankruptcy** (15 September 2008): largest bankruptcy in US history ($639 billion in assets); triggered global panic; credit markets froze; interbank lending collapsed
    - **AIG bailout** ($85 billion initially, ultimately $182 billion): insurer of CDOs through credit default swaps; "too interconnected to fail"
    - Global contagion: stock markets crashed ~50% (October 2007–March 2009); Iceland's banking system collapsed entirely (three major banks failed — 2008; GDP fell ~7%); Ireland, Spain, Greece, Portugal severely hit
    - US unemployment: rose from 4.7% (November 2007) to 10.0% (October 2009); ~8.7 million jobs lost; median household wealth fell ~39% (2007–2010, Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances)
  - **Policy response**
    - US: TARP (Troubled Asset Relief Program, $700 billion, October 2008); Federal Reserve slashed interest rates to near zero (December 2008); quantitative easing (QE1, QE2, QE3 — Fed purchased ~$4.5 trillion in assets by 2014); American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (February 2009, $787 billion stimulus)
    - Ben Bernanke, Timothy Geithner, Hank Paulson: key crisis managers (Bernanke, The Courage to Act, 2015; Geithner, Stress Test, 2014)
    - UK: bank bailout (RBS, Lloyds-HBOS); Bank of England QE; Gordon Brown coordinated G20 response
    - **G20 London Summit (April 2009)**: $1.1 trillion in stimulus commitments; G20 elevated to primary forum for economic coordination; Financial Stability Board created
    - Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform Act (July 2010): created Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB, Elizabeth Warren's concept); Volcker Rule (restricted proprietary trading); stress tests for banks; partially rolled back by Economic Growth Act 2018 (Trump)
  - **European sovereign debt crisis (2010–2015)**
    - Greece: revealed massive debt (budget deficit revised from 3.7% to 12.7% of GDP); three bailout packages (2010, 2012, 2015; total ~€289 billion); extreme austerity; GDP fell ~25%; unemployment peaked ~28% (youth: ~60%); Alexis Tsipras / Syriza (2015); "Grexit" threat; OXI referendum (July 2015) rejected austerity but Tsipras accepted terms; IMF later acknowledged austerity was excessive (Independent Evaluation Office, 2016)
    - Ireland, Portugal: bailouts (2010, 2011); austerity and recovery
    - Spain: banking crisis; ~25% unemployment; "Indignados" movement (15-M, from 15 May 2011)
    - Italy: Berlusconi resignation (2011); Mario Monti technocratic government; Matteo Renzi reforms; persistent low growth
    - **Eurozone structural debate**: lack of fiscal union; asymmetric adjustment (debtor countries bore disproportionate burden); moral hazard vs. solidarity; Mario Draghi (ECB President): "whatever it takes" speech (26 July 2012) — saved euro; Outright Monetary Transactions programme
    - Adam Tooze, Crashed: How a Decade of Financial Crises Changed the World (2018) — definitive account
  - **Long-term consequences**
    - Secular stagnation hypothesis (Larry Summers, 2013): structurally low interest rates, insufficient demand, weak investment
    - Inequality widened: recovery disproportionately benefited asset owners; Thomas Piketty, Capital in the Twenty-First Century (2013, English 2014): r>g (rate of return on capital exceeds growth rate); demonstrated rising wealth concentration since 1980; most cited economics book of the decade
    - Loss of trust in institutions: banks, governments, experts; fueled populism (see below)
    - Austerity debate: Mark Blyth, Austerity: The History of a Dangerous Idea (2013) vs. Alberto Alesina's "expansionary austerity" thesis (largely discredited after Herndon, Ash, and Pollin found spreadsheet errors in Reinhart-Rogoff's influential 2010 paper on debt thresholds)

- **The Arab Spring and its aftermath (2010–)**
  - **Spark**: Mohamed Bouazizi, Tunisian street vendor, self-immolated (17 December 2010, Sidi Bouzid) after police humiliation; protests spread across Tunisia
  - **Tunisia**: President Ben Ali fled (14 January 2011, after 23 years in power); democratic transition — only Arab Spring "success story"; new constitution (2014); Beji Caid Essebsi elected; but democratic backsliding under Kais Saied (suspended parliament July 2021, ruled by decree; new constitution 2022 concentrating presidential power)
  - **Egypt**: Tahrir Square protests (25 January 2011 – 11 February 2011): 18 days of mass demonstrations; Hosni Mubarak resigned after 30 years; Mohamed Morsi (Muslim Brotherhood) elected 2012; military coup by General **Abdel Fattah el-Sisi** (3 July 2013); Rabaa al-Adawiya massacre (14 August 2013: security forces killed ~817–1,000+ Morsi supporters in single day — Human Rights Watch, "All According to Plan," 2014, called it "one of the largest killings of demonstrators in a single day in recent history"); Sisi consolidated authoritarian control
  - **Libya**: uprising against Muammar Gaddafi (February 2011); NATO intervention (UN Security Council Resolution 1973; air campaign, March–October 2011); Gaddafi captured and killed by rebels (20 October 2011); state collapse; civil war (two rival governments 2014–2020; involvement of Russia/Wagner Group, Turkey, UAE, Egypt); ongoing instability and arms proliferation throughout Sahel
  - **Syria**: peaceful protests (March 2011, Daraa — sparked by detention and torture of teenagers who wrote anti-regime graffiti); Bashar al-Assad regime's violent crackdown → civil war
    - Multiple factions: Assad regime (backed by Russia, Iran, Hezbollah), Free Syrian Army, Kurdish YPG, jihadist groups (Jabhat al-Nusra → HTS), ISIS
    - **Russian military intervention** (30 September 2015): airstrikes; turned tide in Assad's favor; Aleppo siege and fall to government forces (December 2016 — massive humanitarian catastrophe)
    - **Chemical weapons**: Ghouta sarin attack (21 August 2013, ~1,400 killed — Obama "red line" declared but did not act militarily; Russia brokered chemical weapons deal); Khan Shaykhun (April 2017, sarin — Trump ordered cruise missile strike on Shayrat airbase); Douma (April 2018, chlorine — US/UK/France strikes); OPCW Investigation and Identification Team confirmed Syrian government responsibility for multiple attacks
    - **Refugee crisis**: ~6.8 million Syrian refugees (UNHCR 2023, largest refugee population globally); ~6.9 million internally displaced; total displacement ~13.7 million out of pre-war population of ~22 million
    - **Fall of Assad regime (December 2024)**: HTS-led opposition launched offensive from Idlib (27 November 2024); government forces rapidly collapsed; Damascus fell (8 December 2024); Assad fled to Russia; Ahmed al-Sharaa (formerly Abu Mohammed al-Julani, ex-HTS leader) led transitional government; international community engaged cautiously; uncertain transition
    - ~500,000+ killed (Syrian Observatory for Human Rights estimate); Caesar photographs (smuggled images of ~6,800+ regime torture victims; led to Caesar Syria Civilian Protection Act, US sanctions 2019)
    - Robin Yassin-Kassab and Leila Al-Shami, Burning Country: Syrians in Revolution and War (2016); Sam Dagher, Assad or We Burn the Country (2019)
  - **Yemen**: Houthi takeover of Sanaa (2014); Saudi-led coalition intervention (March 2015); proxy war (Iran-backed Houthis vs. Saudi/UAE-backed government); ~150,000+ direct conflict deaths; ~227,000 total excess deaths by 2021 (UN estimate); "worst humanitarian crisis in the world" (UN); blockade-induced famine; Houthi Red Sea shipping attacks (from November 2023 — disrupted ~15% of global shipping; US/UK Operation Prosperity Guardian)
  - **Bahrain**: Pearl Roundabout protests (February 2011); crushed with Saudi military support (GCC Peninsula Shield Force); sectarian framing (Sunni monarchy vs. Shia majority population)
  - Legacy: Marc Lynch, The New Arab Wars: Uprisings and Anarchy in the Middle East (2016); Shadi Hamid, Islamic Exceptionalism (2016); debate over structural causes vs. contingent triggers; "Arab Winter" as counter-narrative

- **Russia under Putin and the Russia-Ukraine War**
  - **Putin's consolidation (2000–2021)**
    - Vladimir Putin (b. 1952): KGB career, St. Petersburg politics; PM under Yeltsin; president 2000–2008; PM 2008–2012 (Dmitry Medvedev as placeholder president); president 2012–present; 2020 constitutional amendments reset term limits (potentially allowing rule until 2036)
    - Domestic: brought oligarchs to heel (Mikhail Khodorkovsky arrested 2003, Yukos dismembered; Roman Abramovich compliant); media controlled (NTV takeover 2001; Anna Politkovskaya murdered 2006; Novaya Gazeta editor Dmitri Muratov, Nobel Peace Prize 2021); civil society restricted (foreign agent law 2012, expanded; Memorial International forced to close December 2021)
    - Opposition suppressed: **Alexei Navalny** — anti-corruption campaigner; poisoned with Novichok nerve agent (August 2020; treated in Berlin; Bellingcat investigation identified FSB team; Navalny himself called FSB agent who admitted poisoning — dramatic phone call leaked December 2020); returned to Russia and arrested (January 2021); sentenced; died in Arctic penal colony (16 February 2024 — circumstances disputed; international condemnation)
    - Economy: oil and gas dependency; "resource curse" dynamics; periods of growth (2000s commodity boom); stagnation and inequality; corruption pervasive (Transparency International ranked Russia ~136th)
    - Georgia War (August 2008): Russia invaded Georgia after Georgian operation in South Ossetia; Russian forces advanced deep into Georgian territory; recognised South Ossetia and Abkhazia as independent; first post-Soviet Russian military aggression against a neighbour
    - **Crimea annexation (February–March 2014)**: Russian special forces ("little green men") seized key sites in Crimea; sham referendum (16 March 2014, ~97% "voted" to join Russia under military occupation); formal annexation (18 March); violated international law (UN General Assembly Resolution 68/262 affirmed Ukraine's territorial integrity, 100–11); Western sanctions imposed
    - Donbas conflict (2014–2022): Russian-backed separatists in Donetsk and Luhansk; hybrid warfare (Igor Girkin/Strelkov); **MH17 shoot-down (17 July 2014)**: Malaysia Airlines flight shot down over eastern Ukraine by Buk missile from Russian 53rd Anti-Aircraft Missile Brigade; 298 killed (all aboard); Joint Investigation Team (Netherlands) identified suspects; Russia denied involvement
    - Minsk Agreements (Minsk I September 2014, Minsk II February 2015): ceasefire frameworks; never fully implemented; later both Ukrainian and Russian officials admitted they used Minsk to buy time
    - Mark Galeotti, We Need to Talk About Putin (2019); Catherine Belton, Putin's People: How the KGB Took Back Russia and Then Took on the West (2020)
  - **Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine (24 February 2022–)**
    - **Buildup**: ~190,000+ Russian troops massed on Ukraine's borders (October 2021–February 2022); US intelligence publicly warned of invasion (unusual strategy — declassified intelligence shared); diplomatic efforts failed; Putin demanded NATO withdrawal from Eastern Europe and Ukrainian neutrality guarantee
    - **Invasion (24 February 2022)**: multi-axis attack from north (toward Kyiv), east (Donbas), south (from Crimea); Putin declared "special military operation" citing "denazification" and "demilitarisation" — pretext widely rejected internationally
    - **Battle of Kyiv (February–March 2022)**: Russian advance stalled; fierce Ukrainian resistance; Hostomel Airport battle; 64-km Russian convoy stalled on highway; **Bucha massacre** discovered (late March–early April 2022): evidence of Russian war crimes — extrajudicial killings of civilians; bound bodies in streets; UN, ICC investigations; prompted international outrage and weapons deliveries acceleration
    - **President Volodymyr Zelensky** (b. 1978; former comedian/actor; elected April 2019): refused US evacuation offer ("I need ammunition, not a ride"); emerged as wartime leader; addressed parliaments globally via video; galvanised Western support; Time Person of the Year 2022
    - **Key military phases**
      - Russian withdrawal from Kyiv axis (April 2022): refocused on Donbas and south
      - Siege of Mariupol (February–May 2022): Azovstal steelworks last holdout; devastating destruction; ~90% of city damaged; final surrender 17 May 2022
      - **Kharkiv counteroffensive** (September 2022): Ukraine recaptured ~12,000 km² in days; stunned Russia
      - **Kherson liberation** (November 2022): only regional capital Russia captured; Russian forces withdrew across Dnipro River
      - Bakhmut/Artemovsk battle (August 2022–May 2023): grinding attrition; Wagner Group (Yevgeny Prigozhin's private military company) spearheaded assault; heavy casualties both sides; Bakhmut fell May 2023 after months of urban combat
      - **Prigozhin mutiny (23–24 June 2023)**: Wagner forces seized Rostov military headquarters; marched column toward Moscow; stood down after Belarus's Lukashenko mediated; Prigozhin killed in plane crash (23 August 2023 — widely attributed to Putin)
      - Ukrainian counteroffensive (summer 2023): limited territorial gains; heavily mined Russian defensive lines; Western-supplied armour (Leopard 2, Bradley IFV) used; fell short of objectives; exposed challenges of combined-arms offensive against prepared defenses
      - Kursk incursion (August 2024): Ukrainian forces crossed into Russian territory in Kursk Oblast; captured ~1,300 km² of Russian territory; first foreign ground invasion of Russia since WWII; strategic rationale debated; North Korean troops deployed by Russia in Kursk (confirmed late 2024 — ~10,000–12,000 soldiers, unprecedented development)
      - As of early 2026: war continues; ~1,200 km front line; both sides sustaining heavy casualties; entrenched positional warfare reminiscent of WWI in places
    - **International dimensions**
      - Western response: massive sanctions (SWIFT exclusion of major Russian banks; asset freezes; oil price cap; ~$300 billion in Russian Central Bank reserves frozen; debate over confiscation/transfer to Ukraine — G7 agreed October 2024 to use interest from frozen assets for $50 billion Ukraine loan); arms deliveries (Javelin ATGMs, HIMARS, Patriot air defense, F-16s committed; ATACMS long-range missiles; debate over each escalation step)
      - **NATO reinvigoration**: Finland joined NATO (4 April 2023); Sweden joined (7 March 2024); major shift — both abandoned decades of neutrality; NATO defense spending spurred; alliance purpose clarified after decades of questioning
      - Russia: turned to Iran (Shahed drones), North Korea (ammunition, troops), China (diplomatic support; "no limits partnership" — Xi-Putin joint statement February 2022; but China careful about sanctions-busting); deepened isolation from West; BRICS expansion as alternative framework
      - Energy war: Europe rapidly reduced Russian gas dependency (Nord Stream pipelines sabotaged September 2022 — investigation ongoing; Germany went from ~55% gas from Russia to near zero); LNG imports from US surged; energy price crisis winter 2022–2023; accelerated European energy transition
    - **War crimes and accountability**: ICC arrest warrant for Putin (17 March 2023, for unlawful deportation of Ukrainian children — first sitting head of state of UNSC permanent member with ICC warrant); documented atrocities: Bucha, Irpin, Izium mass graves, strikes on Kramatorsk station (8 April 2022, ~60 killed), Mariupol drama theatre (16 March 2022, "дети"/children written outside, ~300–600 killed); Russian targeting of civilian infrastructure (systematic attacks on energy grid, winter 2022–2023 and 2023–2024)
    - **Casualties and damage**: extremely difficult to confirm; Ukrainian government claims ~300,000+ Russian casualties (killed and wounded); US intelligence estimates ~315,000 Russian casualties by end 2024; Ukrainian casualties acknowledged as "significant" — possibly comparable; ~10,000+ Ukrainian civilians confirmed killed (OHCHR, likely undercount); millions displaced; economic damage to Ukraine estimated at >$400 billion (World Bank, 2024 assessment)
    - Serhii Plokhy, The Russo-Ukrainian War: The Return of History (2023); Timothy Snyder, "The War in Ukraine Is a Colonial War" (essays and lectures, 2022–2024); Phillips O'Brien and others on industrial attritional warfare in 21st century

- **Populism, nationalism, and democratic backsliding**
  - **Conceptual framework**: Cas Mudde, "The Populist Zeitgeist" (Government and Opposition, 2004): populism as "thin-centered ideology" — the "pure people" vs. "corrupt elite"; Jan-Werner Müller, What Is Populism? (2016): anti-pluralism as defining feature; Pippa Norris and Ronald Inglehart, Cultural Backlash: Trump, Brexit, and Authoritarian Populism (2019): post-materialist cultural shift generating reaction
  - **V-Dem Institute (Varieties of Democracy)**: annual Democracy Report tracks democratic quality globally; 2024 report: "level of democracy for the average global citizen in 2023 is back to 1985"; 72% of world's population live in autocracies; autocratisation wave ongoing since ~2010
  - **Hungary**: Viktor Orbán's Fidesz (returned to power 2010): constitutional capture; media takeover; "illiberal democracy" (Orbán's own term, 2014 speech); EU Article 7 proceedings; asylum policy hardline; model for right-wing populists globally
  - **Poland**: Law and Justice (PiS, 2015–2023, Jarosław Kaczyński): judiciary capture; constitutional tribunal packed; media law targeting independent outlets; anti-LGBTQ+ rhetoric; EU rule-of-law disputes; lost power 2023 elections (Donald Tusk returned as PM — partial democratic restoration, ongoing legal tangles)
  - **Turkey**: Recep Tayyip Erdoğan (PM 2003–2014, President 2014–): early modernisation and EU accession hopes; post-2013 authoritarian turn (Gezi Park protests May–June 2013 — brutally suppressed); 2016 coup attempt (15 July 2016, attributed to Gülenist network); massive purge (~150,000 detained or dismissed); 2017 constitutional referendum shifted to presidential system; control of judiciary and media; 2023 election — Erdoğan narrowly won against opposition alliance (Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu)
  - **Brazil**: Jair Bolsonaro (president 2019–2022): far-right populist; Amazon deforestation surge; COVID-19 denialism; attacked democratic institutions; lost 2022 election to Lula da Silva; **8 January 2023 insurrection**: Bolsonaro supporters stormed Congress, Supreme Court, and Presidential Palace in Brasília (consciously modeled on US January 6); Bolsonaro indicted (2024) for attempted coup
  - **India**: Narendra Modi (PM from 2014), BJP: Hindu nationalist (Hindutva) agenda; economic reform; CAA (Citizenship Amendment Act, 2019 — critics argued discriminated against Muslims); Kashmir autonomy revoked (Article 370, August 2019); press freedom declining (India 159th in RSF Press Freedom Index 2024); electoral dominance; increasingly described as "electoral autocracy" (V-Dem) rather than liberal democracy
  - **Italy**: Giorgia Meloni (PM from October 2022): Brothers of Italy (post-fascist lineage); first far-right PM since WWII; moderated on EU/NATO policy in office
  - **Philippines**: Rodrigo Duterte (2016–2022): drug war (~12,000–30,000 extrajudicial killings); Ferdinand Marcos Jr. won 2022 election; historical revisionism regarding Marcos dictatorship
  - **Israel**: Benjamin Netanyahu — longest-serving PM; judicial overhaul crisis (2023, attempted to limit Supreme Court power; massive protests, 29 weeks of demonstrations); far-right coalition partners (Itamar Ben-Gvir, Bezalel Smotrich — settler movement representatives in government)
  - Steven Levitsky and Daniel Ziblatt, How Democracies Die (2018); Anne Applebaum, Twilight of Democracy (2020); David Runciman, How Democracy Ends (2018)

- **Brexit**
  - Referendum (23 June 2016): 51.9% voted Leave; David Cameron resigned; Theresa May became PM; "Brexit means Brexit"
  - Negotiations (2017–2020): Article 50 triggered (29 March 2017); Irish border backstop dispute; three defeats of May's deal in Parliament; Boris Johnson became PM (July 2019); "Get Brexit Done"
  - **Withdrawal Agreement** (enacted 31 January 2020): transition period until 31 December 2020; Trade and Cooperation Agreement (December 2020) — thin free trade agreement
  - Consequences: disruption to UK-EU trade; Northern Ireland Protocol disputes (post-Brexit regulatory border in Irish Sea — Windsor Framework 2023 partial resolution); Scottish independence pressure; immigration policy shift; debate over economic impact (Office for Budget Responsibility estimated 4% GDP reduction long-term compared to remaining); UK rejoining debate emerging (2024–2025 polling)
  - Key figures: Nigel Farage (UKIP/Brexit Party), Boris Johnson, Michael Gove, Dominic Cummings; Remain campaign (led by Cameron, George Osborne — "Project Fear" criticism)
  - Tim Shipman, All Out War (2016) and Fall Out (2017); Fintan O'Toole, Heroic Failure: Brexit and the Politics of Pain (2018)

- **Trump and US political polarisation**
  - **Donald Trump (president 2017–2021, re-elected 2024)**
    - 2016 election: defeated Hillary Clinton; won Electoral College (306–232) while losing popular vote by ~2.9 million; Russian interference (Mueller investigation — established Russian interference but did not conclude criminal conspiracy; Mueller Report, March 2019, 448 pages)
    - Presidency (2017–2021): tax cuts (Tax Cuts and Jobs Act 2017); trade wars; immigration policy (family separation, border wall); withdrawal from Paris Agreement (2017), Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA, 2018), WHO (2020); three Supreme Court justices appointed (Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett); Abraham Accords (2020, normalisation agreements: Israel-UAE, Bahrain, Morocco, Sudan); COVID-19 response (see below)
    - First impeachment (December 2019): Ukraine aid withholding / pressure to investigate Biden; acquitted by Senate (February 2020)
    - 2020 election: lost to Joe Biden (306–232 Electoral College, ~7 million popular vote margin); refused to concede; alleged widespread voter fraud without evidence ("The Big Lie" — over 60 court cases dismissed, including by Trump-appointed judges)
    - **January 6 Capitol attack (2021)**: Trump rally at Ellipse ("We fight like hell, and if you don't fight like hell, you're not going to have a country anymore"); supporters stormed US Capitol during electoral vote certification; Vice President Mike Pence sheltered; 5 deaths associated with the event; ~140 police officers injured; Congress evacuated; first breach of Capitol since War of 1812; second impeachment (13 January 2021, most bipartisan impeachment vote in history, 57–43 but acquitted — 67 needed); January 6 Select Committee investigation (final report, December 2022: documented multi-part effort to overturn election; criminal referrals)
    - **2024 election**: Trump won (announced November 2024); returned to presidency January 2025; first president to serve non-consecutive terms since Grover Cleveland (1885–1889, 1893–1897); campaigned on immigration, economic populism, and "retribution"
    - Broader significance: democratic norms erosion; "constitutional hardball"; information ecosystem fragmentation; existential debate about American democratic institutions
    - Bob Woodward, Rage (2020) and War (2024); staff memoirs (John Bolton, Mark Esper, Cassidy Hutchinson); legal proceedings (~91 criminal charges across 4 indictments (2023) — Georgia election interference, federal election interference, classified documents, Manhattan business records conviction)

- **The COVID-19 pandemic (2019–2023)**
  - **Origins and emergence**
    - SARS-CoV-2 virus: novel coronavirus; first cluster identified in Wuhan, China (December 2019); Wuhan Institute of Virology proximity fueled lab-leak hypothesis; WHO declared Public Health Emergency of International Concern (30 January 2020); declared pandemic (11 March 2020)
    - Origin debate: zoonotic spillover (Huanan Seafood Market — Worobey et al., Science, 2022, geo-spatial analysis pointed to market as epicenter; Pekar et al., Science, 2022, suggested two separate zoonotic jumps) vs. laboratory leak (US Department of Energy assessment, "low confidence" lab-leak, February 2023; FBI "moderate confidence" lab-leak; Alina Chan and Matt Ridley, Viral: The Search for the Origin of COVID-19, 2021; debate continues without definitive resolution)
    - Genomic sequencing: Zhang Yongzhen's team published viral genome (11 January 2020, GenBank); enabled rapid diagnostic and vaccine development
  - **Global spread and first wave (2020)**
    - Italy (first major European outbreak, February 2020): Bergamo overwhelmed; military trucks carrying caskets; healthcare system collapse; nationwide lockdown (9 March 2020); ~130,000+ Italian deaths ultimately
    - US: first confirmed case 20 January 2020; exponential growth; New York City epicenter (March–April 2020, overwhelmed hospitals; Javits Center, USNS Comfort; Governor Andrew Cuomo's daily briefings); national emergency declared 13 March; CDC guidelines; state-level variation in response
    - Global lockdowns: ~3.9 billion people under stay-at-home orders at peak (April 2020); unprecedented peacetime restrictions on movement; education disrupted (UNESCO estimated 1.6 billion students affected); remote work transformation
  - **Deaths and waves**
    - Official WHO reported deaths: ~7 million (as of 2023 wave subsidence)
