  - 20th-century diplomacy: secret vs. open diplomacy (Wilson's Fourteen Points demanded open covenants); League of Nations; UN system; summit diplomacy (Yalta, Potsdam, Camp David); shuttle diplomacy (Kissinger); track-two diplomacy; public diplomacy; digital diplomacy
  - Henry Kissinger, Diplomacy (1994); Harold Nicolson, Diplomacy (1939); Paul Sharp, Diplomatic Theory of International Relations (2009)

- **History of Espionage and Intelligence**
  - Ancient: Sun Tzu, The Art of War (ch. 13, "Use of Spies"); Roman speculatores and frumentarii; Byzantine intelligence; Walsingham's spy network (Elizabethan England); Enlightenment cryptography
  - Modern intelligence agencies: British Secret Service Bureau (1909 → MI5, MI6); US OSS (WWII, William Donovan) → CIA (1947); KGB (1954–1991, evolved from Cheka/NKVD/MGB); Mossad (Israel, 1949); BND (Germany); DGSE (France); Five Eyes alliance (US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand — signals intelligence partnership)
  - Key operations: Bletchley Park codebreaking (Enigma — Turing; Colossus — first programmable electronic computer; Ultra intelligence — possibly shortened war by 2+ years); Cambridge Five (Philby, Burgess, Maclean, Blunt, Cairncross); Rosenberg case; U-2 incident (1960, Gary Powers); Cuban Missile Crisis intelligence; Aldrich Ames (CIA mole for Soviets); Robert Hanssen (FBI); Stuxnet (US-Israeli cyber weapon against Iranian centrifuges, ~2010 — first known cyber weapon causing physical destruction)
  - Christopher Andrew, The Secret World: A History of Intelligence (2018); Tim Weiner, Legacy of Ashes: The History of the CIA (2007); Ben Macintyre's works (Agent Zigzag, A Spy Among Friends)

- **History of Labor**
  - Pre-industrial: guild system (medieval, regulated crafts, apprentice-journeyman-master); serfdom and corvée; artisan traditions; putting-out system (domestic system)
  - Industrial labor: factory system and labor discipline (E.P. Thompson, "Time, Work-Discipline, and Industrial Capitalism," Past & Present, 1967); child labour (factory acts: UK 1833, 1844; Mining Act 1842); trade unions (Combination Acts repeal, UK, 1824; Tolpuddle Martyrs, 1834; AFL founded 1886, Gompers); strikes (Great Railroad Strike, US, 1877; Pullman Strike, 1894; UK General Strike, 1926); International Labour Organization (1919, oldest UN specialized agency)
  - Marxism and labor: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, Communist Manifesto (1848); Das Kapital (1867); class struggle; surplus value; First International (1864); Second International (1889); Russian Revolution (1917); trade union movements worldwide
  - 20th century: Fordism ($5 day, assembly line); New Deal legislation (Wagner Act, minimum wage); civil rights and labor (A. Philip Randolph, Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters); CIO sit-down strikes (Flint, 1936–1937); post-war social compact; deindustrialisation; Solidarity (Poland, 1980); neoliberal labour market deregulation; gig economy (Uber, 2009; classification debates); Amazon warehouse organizing (2022–); global supply chain labor conditions (Rana Plaza collapse, Bangladesh, 24 April 2013 — 1,134 dead; garment industry reform)
  - E.P. Thompson, The Making of the English Working Class (1963); David Montgomery, The Fall of the House of Labor (1987); Marcel van der Linden, Workers of the World (2008)

- **History of Colonialism and Decolonization**
  - Ancient and early empires as colonial: Roman provinces; Chinese expansion; Aztec tribute system
  - European colonialism: Portuguese (15th century–); Spanish (Americas, Philippines); Dutch (VOC, Indonesia, Cape Colony); French (North America, Caribbean, Africa, Indochina); British (largest empire in history — ~25% of world's land area by 1920); Belgian (Congo — King Leopold II's Congo Free State 1885–1908, rubber terror; Adam Hochschild, King Leopold's Ghost, 1998); German, Italian (late arrivals)
  - Settler colonialism: distinct form — settlers come to stay, eliminate/replace indigenous population (Patrick Wolfe, Settler Colonialism and the Transformation of Anthropology, 1999); US, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, Algeria, Palestine (debated); Lorenzo Veracini, Settler Colonialism: A Theoretical Overview (2010)
  - Decolonization (see Cold War section for detailed narrative): first wave (Americas, 18th–19th century); second wave (Africa, Asia, 20th century); third wave (Pacific, remaining territories); neo-colonialism concept (Kwame Nkrumah, Neo-Colonialism: The Last Stage of Imperialism, 1965)
  - Postcolonial theory: Edward Said, Orientalism (1978) — foundational text; Gayatri Spivak, "Can the Subaltern Speak?" (1988); Homi Bhabha, The Location of Culture (1994); Frantz Fanon, The Wretched of the Earth (1961) and Black Skin, White Masks (1952); Dipesh Chakrabarty, Provincializing Europe (2000); Achille Mbembe, On the Postcolony (2001)
  - Jürgen Osterhammel, Colonialism: A Theoretical Overview (1997); John Darwin, The Empire Project (2009) and Unfinished Empire (2012)

- **Military History**
  - **Scope**: the study of warfare, strategy, tactics, military institutions, technology, and the experience of combat; one of oldest historical sub-fields; Carl von Clausewitz, On War (1832) — "war is the continuation of politics by other means"
  - Ancient warfare: phalanx (Greek hoplites; Philip II/Alexander's Macedonian sarissa phalanx); Roman legions (manipular system → cohort system; engineering: roads, fortifications, siege works); Parthian mounted archery; Chinese crossbow armies; Warring States military treatises (Sun Tzu, The Art of War, ~5th century BCE — most influential military text; Sun Bin)
  - Medieval warfare: heavy cavalry (knights, cataphracts); castle-siege-countermeasure cycle; gunpowder weapons adoption (cannons at siege of Constantinople, 1453; arquebus → musket); Mongol warfare (mobility, composite bow, intelligence network; Timothy May, The Mongol Conquests in World History, 2012); longbow (English, Crécy 1346, Agincourt 1415); samurai traditions (Japan)
  - Early modern: military revolution thesis (Michael Roberts 1956; Geoffrey Parker elaborated — infantry firearms, drill, fortifications, larger armies; debated by Jeremy Black and others); Gustavus Adolphus (Swedish innovations); Vauban (fortification); Frederick the Great (oblique order); Napoleon (corps system, mass conscription — levée en masse; decisive battle; culmination at Waterloo 1815)
  - Industrial-age warfare: rifled weapons; machine gun; railroad logistics; telegraph; ironclad navies (Monitor vs. Merrimack/Virginia, 1862); trenchworks (American Civil War anticipates WWI); Mahan's sea power theory (The Influence of Sea Power upon History, 1890)
  - 20th–21st century: see WWI and WWII sections above; nuclear strategy (deterrence, MAD — mutually assured destruction); counterinsurgency (COIN — Galula, Counterinsurgency Warfare, 1964; Petraeus FM 3-24, 2006); Revolution in Military Affairs (RMA — precision-guided munitions; Gulf War 1991 showcase); asymmetric warfare (terrorism, guerrilla); drone warfare; cyber operations (Stuxnet; SolarWinds); AI-enabled warfare (autonomous weapons debate — Campaign to Stop Killer Robots; LAWS — lethal autonomous weapons systems); space militarisation (US Space Force, 2019)
  - John Keegan, The Face of Battle (1976) and A History of Warfare (1993); Victor Davis Hanson, The Western Way of War (1989); Hew Strachan, Clausewitz's On War: A Biography (2007)

- **Environmental History**
  - **Scope**: the interaction between human societies and the natural environment through time; emerged as distinct field 1970s
  - Key founding works: Alfred Crosby, The Columbian Exchange (1972) — biological exchange between Old and New Worlds (maize, potato, tomato → Europe; wheat, horses, smallpox, measles → Americas); William Cronon, Changes in the Land (1983, New England ecology); Richard White, The Organic Machine (1995, Columbia River); Donald Worster, Dust Bowl (1979)
  - Themes: deforestation (Mediterranean antiquity; tropical forests 20th–21st century); soil degradation; water management (irrigation civilizations — Karl Wittfogel, Oriental Despotism, 1957, "hydraulic hypothesis"; largely discredited as monocausal); disease ecology (William McNeill, Plagues and Peoples, 1976); animal history (Virginia DeJohn Anderson, Creatures of Empire, 2004)
  - Climate history: Little Ice Age (~1300–1850; Brian Fagan, The Little Ice Age, 2000); medieval warm period; volcanic forcings (Tambora 1815 → "Year Without a Summer" 1816; Gillen D'Arcy Wood, Tambora, 2014); dendroclimatology; ice core records (Vostok, EPICA — 800,000 years of temperature and CO₂)
  - Anthropocene: Paul Crutzen and Eugene Stoermer proposed term (2000); start date debated (Neolithic? Industrial Revolution? 1945? 1950s Great Acceleration?); Anthropocene Working Group proposed ~1950s start, golden spike at Crawford Lake, Ontario (plutonium from nuclear tests) — IUGS Subcommission on Quaternary Stratigraphy voted against formal adoption (2024); concept remains influential regardless of formal geological status
  - J.R. McNeill, Something New Under the Sun: An Environmental History of the Twentieth-Century World (2000); Dipesh Chakrabarty, "The Climate of History: Four Theses" (Critical Inquiry, 2009 — landmark essay linking climate change and historical thought)

- **History of Gender and Sexuality**
  - Foundational: Joan Wallach Scott, "Gender: A Useful Category of Historical Analysis" (American Historical Review, 1986) — gender as social construction, relational, embedded in power; transformed historical practice
  - Women's history → gender history: from "history of women" (1970s, recovery project) to analytical category; Gerda Lerner, The Creation of Patriarchy (1986); Natalie Zemon Davis, "Women's History in Transition" (Feminist Studies, 1976); Judith Bennett, History Matters: Patriarchy and the Challenge of Feminism (2006)
  - History of sexuality: Michel Foucault, History of Sexuality (3 vols., 1976–1984) — "homosexuality" as 19th-century category; shift from acts to identity; John Boswell, Christianity, Social Tolerance, and Homosexuality (1980); Thomas Laqueur, Making Sex: Body and Gender from the Greeks to Freud (1990); history of heterosexuality (Jonathan Ned Katz, The Invention of Heterosexuality, 1995)
  - Queer history: George Chauncey, Gay New York (1994); Lillian Faderman, Odd Girls and Twilight Lovers (1991); trans history (Susan Stryker, Transgender History, 2008; Jules Gill-Peterson, Histories of the Transgender Child, 2018)
  - Masculinity studies: R.W. Connell, Masculinities (1995); hegemonic masculinity concept; military masculinity; crisis of masculinity narratives
  - Global perspectives: third gender traditions (hijra in South Asia, Two-Spirit in Indigenous North America, fa'afafine in Samoa); colonialism's imposition of binary sex/gender norms; Oyèrónkẹ́ Oyěwùmí, The Invention of Women (1997, Yoruba gender before colonialism)

- **History of Childhood**
  - Philippe Ariès, Centuries of Childhood (1960/1962): argued "childhood" as modern invention; medieval children treated as small adults; controversial thesis — critiqued but generative
  - Childhood and labour: children as workers throughout most of history; child labor reform (factory acts, compulsory schooling); children's rights (UN Convention on the Rights of the Child, 1989 — most widely ratified human rights treaty)
  - Hugh Cunningham, The Invention of Childhood (2006); Steven Mintz, Huck's Raft: A History of American Childhood (2004)

- **Postcolonial Historiography**
  - Subaltern Studies: founded by Ranajit Guha (1982, collective based in South Asian history); "history from below" for colonial and postcolonial subjects; Gayatri Spivak, "Can the Subaltern Speak?" (1988); influenced by Gramsci's concept of subaltern
  - Key debates: Eurocentrism in historical method; provincial vs. universal history (Chakrabarty, Provincializing Europe, 2000); connected and entangled histories (Sanjay Subrahmanyam); global history vs. area studies; indigenous knowledge systems; decolonization of the curriculum (Rhodes Must Fall, 2015; broader university debates); archive and silencing (Michel-Rolph Trouillot, Silencing the Past, 1995)
  - Frontier: digital decolonization of archives; repatriation of cultural objects (Sarr-Savoy Report, 2018 — recommended return of African artefacts from French museums; Benin Bronzes restitution from Germany 2022, Smithsonian 2022); climate justice historiography; reparative history

#### Historiography: Methods and Schools of Historical Thought

- **Scope and definition**
  - Historiography = the study of the writing of history and the methods, interpretations, and theoretical frameworks historians use; "the history of history"
  - Distinct from history itself: concerns how knowledge of the past is produced, evaluated, and contested
  - Metahistory: philosophy of history (speculative — Hegel, Marx, Toynbee) vs. analytical philosophy of history (logic of historical explanation — Hempel, Dray, Collingwood)
  - Ernst Breisach, Historiography: Ancient, Medieval, and Modern (3rd ed., 2007) — standard textbook survey
  - Georg G. Iggers, Historiography in the Twentieth Century: From Scientific Objectivity to the Postmodern Challenge (1997)

- **Ancient and medieval historiography**
  - **Herodotus (~484–425 BCE)**: Histories — "the Father of History" (Cicero's phrase); inquiry (historia) into Greek-Persian Wars; included ethnographic digressions (Egypt, Scythia); narrative, wide-ranging, sometimes credulous; criticized by Thucydides but now valued for cultural history method; Arnaldo Momigliano argued Herodotus founded ethnographic tradition
  - **Thucydides (~460–400 BCE)**: History of the Peloponnesian War — rationalist, contemporary-event focus; excluded gods/myth; Thucydidean speeches (recreated but "as close as possible to the overall purport"); sought general truths about human nature and power; foundational for political-military history; "possession for all time" (ktēma es aei)
    - Contrast: Herodotus → cultural, inclusive, narrative; Thucydides → political, exclusionary, analytical; both foundational but create rival traditions
  - **Polybius (~200–118 BCE)**: universal history (Mediterranean world); pragmatic history (useful for statesmen); theory of anacyclosis (constitutional cycle); eyewitness principle; critique of armchair historians
  - **Sima Qian (~145–86 BCE)**: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) — foundational Chinese historiography; comprehensive form (basic annals, chronological tables, treatises, hereditary houses, ranked biographies); ~130 chapters; personal suffering (castration for defending a general) infused work with moral passion; established the Chinese historiographic tradition followed by dynastic histories (Ershisi Shi, Twenty-Four Histories)
  - **Roman historiography**: Livy (Ab Urbe Condita — from founding of Rome; patriotic; moral exempla); Tacitus (Annals, Histories — dark, penetrating analysis of imperial power; psychology of tyranny; prose style among finest in Latin; "sine ira et studio" — without anger or partiality — though deeply partisan); Ammianus Marcellinus (late Roman, last great Latin historian)
  - **Islamic historiography**: Ibn Khaldun (1332–1406): Muqaddimah (Prolegomena, 1377) — philosophy of history; theory of asabiyyah (social cohesion/group solidarity); cyclical rise and fall of dynasties; proto-social science; considered by many the first sociologist or philosopher of history; al-Tabari (839–923): Ta'rikh al-Rusul wa'l-Muluk (History of Prophets and Kings) — isnad method (chain of transmission); comprehensive universal chronicle
  - **Medieval Christian historiography**: providentialist — history as unfolding of divine plan; Augustine, City of God (~413–426 CE, two cities — earthly and divine); Bede, Ecclesiastical History of the English People (~731, careful source criticism within Christian framework; introduced AD dating to English historiography); chronicle tradition (Anglo-Saxon Chronicle; Ordericus Vitalis; Geoffrey of Monmouth — pseudohistory; William of Malmesbury); Jean Froissart (Chronicles of the Hundred Years' War)
  - **Chinese dynastic historiography**: Bureau of Historiography (Shiguan) — official historians attached to courts; Standard Histories format initiated by Sima Qian and Ban Gu (Hanshu, ~80 CE); Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government, Sima Guang, 1084) — 294 volumes, political history for rulers; Liu Zhiji, Shitong (Understanding History, 710) — first Chinese work of historiographic criticism; Zhang Xuecheng, Wenshi Tongyi (General Principles of Literature and History, 1790s) — "six classics are all history"

- **Renaissance and early modern historiography**
  - Humanist historiography: return to classical models (Thucydides, Livy); rhetoric as essential component; Lorenzo Valla (1407–1457): exposed the Donation of Constantine as forgery (1440, De falso credita et ementita Constantini donatione) using philological and historical criticism — landmark of source criticism; Leonardo Bruni, History of the Florentine People (~1416–1442) — secular causal explanation; Niccolò Machiavelli, Discourses on Livy (1531) — history as source of political wisdom
  - Antiquarianism: distinct tradition alongside narrative history; collection and classification of physical remains, inscriptions, coins, manuscripts; William Camden, Britannia (1586); Ole Worm (Denmark); Mabillon, De Re Diplomatica (1681) — founded diplomatics (authentication of documents); Momigliano, "Ancient History and the Antiquarian" (Journal of the Warburg and Courtauld Institutes, 1950) — classic essay on the two traditions
  - Erudite (critical) tradition: Bollandists (Jesuit hagiographers — critical editions of saints' lives, Acta Sanctorum, 1643–); Maurists (Benedictine monks — critical editions of church fathers; Mabillon); Richard Simon (1638–1712) — pioneered biblical criticism; Pierre Bayle, Historical and Critical Dictionary (1697) — sceptical, footnote-heavy; foundation for Enlightenment critical history
  - **Enlightenment historiography**: "philosophical history" — secular, progress-oriented; Voltaire, The Age of Louis XIV (1751) and Essay on the Customs and Spirit of Nations (1756) — cultural history, anti-providentialist; Edward Gibbon, The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire (6 vols., 1776–1789) — masterpiece of narrative and analysis; sceptical of Christianity's role; David Hume, History of England (1754–1761); William Robertson (History of Scotland, History of America); Montesquieu, The Spirit of the Laws (1748) — comparative-structural approach; Giambattista Vico, Scienza Nuova (New Science, 1725) — cyclical history; "verum factum" (truth is made); precursor to historicism; largely ignored until 19th-century rediscovery

- **19th century: the professionalization of history**
  - **Leopold von Ranke (1795–1886)**: "wie es eigentlich gewesen" ("how it actually was" — often mistranslated as "what actually happened"); seminar method (primary sources, archival research); diplomatic/political history focus; Berlin school; trained generations of professional historians; established history as academic discipline with critical source methodology; Archives-based research as gold standard
    - Rankean legacy: source criticism (Quellenkritik); external criticism (authenticity) and internal criticism (reliability); footnotes as evidential apparatus; monograph as scholarly form
    - Limitations: narrowly political; nation-state as natural unit; pretension to objectivity masked ideological commitments (Prussian conservative)
  - German historicism (Historismus): each epoch has its own character and must be understood on its own terms; Johann Gustav Droysen, Historik (lectures, 1857–1882) — hermeneutics of historical understanding (Verstehen); Wilhelm Dilthey (1833–1911) — Geisteswissenschaften (human sciences) vs. Naturwissenschaften (natural sciences); understanding vs. explanation; empathic re-experience (Nacherleben); influenced Weber, Gadamer
  - **Positivism**: Auguste Comte applied natural-science model to history; Henry Thomas Buckle, History of Civilization in England (1857–1861) — statistical regularities; Hippolyte Taine — race, milieu, moment; scientific history aspirations; largely abandoned as naïve by early 20th century but empiricist impulse persisted
  - National/Romantic historiography: Jules Michelet (1798–1874): "the people" (le peuple) as protagonist; passionate, literary; Le Peuple (1846), Histoire de la Révolution française (7 vols.); Thomas Carlyle, The French Revolution: A History (1837) — dramatic, vivid; George Bancroft (United States); nationalist histories across Europe served nation-building projects
  - **Marx and historical materialism**: Karl Marx (1818–1883) and Friedrich Engels: modes of production (primitive communism → slavery → feudalism → capitalism → socialism); base/superstructure model; class conflict as engine of change; historical materialism as "science of history"; The Eighteenth Brumaire of Louis Bonaparte (1852) — "Men make their own history, but they do not make it as they please"; Georg Lukács, History and Class Consciousness (1923); debate over determinism vs. agency within Marxism
  - Jacob Burckhardt (1818–1897): The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy (1860) — cultural history masterwork; defined Renaissance as "discovery of the world and of man"; art, culture, individuality; rejected Hegelian philosophy of history; influence on Aby Warburg and Huizinga
  - Lord Acton (1834–1902): planned Cambridge Modern History; "power tends to corrupt, and absolute power corrupts absolutely"; liberal Catholic; universal history aspiration

- **Early 20th century: new directions**
  - **The "New History" movement (US)**: James Harvey Robinson, The New History (1912) — history should draw on all social sciences; break from mere political narrative; Charles Beard, An Economic Interpretation of the Constitution (1913) — economic interests drove founding fathers; progressive historiography; Frederick Jackson Turner, "The Significance of the Frontier in American History" (1893) — frontier thesis; all immensely influential in American historiography
  - **The Annales School**
    - Founded by Marc Bloch (1886–1944) and Lucien Febvre (1878–1956): journal Annales d'histoire économique et sociale (1929); rejected narrow political-event history (histoire événementielle)
    - First generation: Bloch — The Royal Touch (1924, mentalités); Feudal Society (1939–1940); comparative method; member of French Resistance, shot by Nazis 1944, The Historian's Craft (posthumous, 1949); Febvre — The Problem of Unbelief in the Sixteenth Century (1942, Rabelais — history of mentalities, "mental equipment")
    - **Second generation: Fernand Braudel (1902–1985)**: The Mediterranean and the Mediterranean World in the Age of Philip II (1949) — masterwork; **three levels of historical time**: (1) longue durée (geographical time — structures, slowly changing); (2) conjonctures (medium-term social/economic cycles); (3) événements (events — "surface disturbances, crests of foam that the tides of history carry on their strong backs"); paradigm-shifting; Civilization and Capitalism (3 vols., 1979); dominated French historiography; sixth section of EPHE (later EHESS)
    - Third generation: Emmanuel Le Roy Ladurie, Montaillou (1975) — medieval village microhistory using Inquisition records; bestseller; Jacques Le Goff (medieval mentalités); Georges Duby (feudal society, chivalry)
    - Fourth generation / later: Roger Chartier (cultural history, history of reading); shifts toward cultural turn; debates about Annales relevance in age of cultural/linguistic turn
    - Legacy: transformed historical practice globally; longue durée concept endures; inspired world-systems theory, environmental history, historical sociology
  - **British Marxist historians**: E.P. Thompson, The Making of the English Working Class (1963) — "history from below"; agency of working people; culture and consciousness; Eric Hobsbawm (Age of Revolution, Age of Capital, Age of Empire, Age of Extremes — magisterial tetralogy); Christopher Hill (English Civil War as bourgeois revolution; The World Turned Upside Down, 1972); Rodney Hilton (medieval peasants); Raphael Samuel (History Workshop movement, 1967 — democratic, community history); Past & Present journal (1952–); Raymond Williams, The Long Revolution (1961) — culture and society approach
  - R.G. Collingwood (1889–1943): The Idea of History (posthumous, 1946) — re-enactment theory: historian must re-think the thoughts of historical agents; idealist philosophy of history; "all history is the history of thought"; influenced hermeneutic tradition
  - Benedetto Croce (1866–1952): "all history is contemporary history" — presentist orientation; idealist; anti-positivist; influenced Italian historiography

- **Social history and "history from below"**
  - Definition: study of the experiences and structures of everyday life, ordinary people, social groups, and social processes — as opposed to political/diplomatic/military/elite-focused history
  - Origins: French Revolution-era interest in "the people"; Michelet; Marx; but flourished as distinct movement from ~1960s
  - E.P. Thompson's influence: Making of the English Working Class ("class is a relationship, not a thing"); moral economy concept; custom and culture as historical forces; enormous influence
  - History Workshop movement (Raphael Samuel, Ruskin College, Oxford, 1967–): "people's history"; oral history; community history; History Workshop Journal (1976–); democratization of historical practice
  - Social history of medicine (Roy Porter); social history of crime (Douglas Hay, Albion's Fatal Tree, 1975); social history of the family (Lawrence Stone, The Family, Sex and Marriage in England, 1977); social history of food (Jack Goody, Cooking, Cuisine and Class, 1982)
  - Historical demography: Cambridge Group for the History of Population and Social Structure (Peter Laslett, E.A. Wrigley, R.S. Schofield, 1964–); parish register analysis; The Population History of England (1981); family reconstitution method; Louis Henry (French historical demography pioneer)

- **Quantitative history and cliometrics**
  - Cliometrics (named for Clio, muse of history): application of economic theory and quantitative methods to history; Robert Fogel and Douglass North (Nobel Prize in Economics, 1993); Fogel, Railroads and American Economic Growth (1964) — counterfactual: what if no railroads?; Time on the Cross: The Economics of American Negro Slavery (Fogel and Engerman, 1974 — controversial: argued slavery was "profitable and efficient"; critiqued by Herbert Gutman and others for methodology and implications)
  - Quantitative social history: Charles Tilly, The Vendée (1964); collective biography / prosopography (Lawrence Stone); serial history (Annales quantitative tradition — Pierre Chaunu, Seville and the Atlantic; Ernest Labrousse, price history)
  - Historical statistics: compilations (B.R. Mitchell, International Historical Statistics; Maddison Project — Angus Maddison, historical GDP estimates); census analysis
  - Debates: "counting vs. understanding"; quantification can obscure meaning; narrative vs. analysis; cultural turn partly a reaction against quantification; convergence in 21st century (digital humanities combines both)

- **Microhistory (microstoria)**
  - Definition: intensive study of a small unit — a single event, individual, village, trial — to illuminate larger structures, mentalities, and social processes
  - **Carlo Ginzburg (b. 1939)**: The Cheese and the Worms (Il formaggio e i vermi, 1976) — Menocchio, a 16th-century Italian miller tried by Inquisition; reconstructed his cosmology from trial records; "history from below" through a unique individual; conjectural paradigm ("Clues: Roots of an Evidential Paradigm," 1979 — detective-like method linking Morelli, Freud, Sherlock Holmes)
  - Giovanni Levi: Inheriting Power (1985); "On Microhistory" (in New Perspectives on Historical Writing, ed. Peter Burke, 1991) — programmatic statement
  - Natalie Zemon Davis, The Return of Martin Guerre (1983) — impostor case in 16th-century France; narrative reconstruction; possibility and plausibility in historical method
  - Robert Darnton, The Great Cat Massacre (1984) — cultural history meets microhistory; "the otherness of the past"
  - Emmanuel Le Roy Ladurie, Montaillou (1975) — often considered foundational microhistory (though emerging from Annales tradition)
  - Debate: is the micro generalizable?; relationship between micro and macro; Ginzburg's "normal exception" — the exceptional case reveals the norm; Jacques Revel, Jeux d'échelles (Games of Scales, 1996) — micro-macro relationship
  - New directions: global microhistory (Francesca Trivellato, The Familiarity of Strangers, 2009 — Sephardic merchants; Tonio Andrade); microhistory of objects; digital microhistory

- **Oral history**
  - Definition: collection and analysis of spoken testimony from participants in or witnesses to historical events; democratizes who "counts" as historical source
  - Origins: WPA Federal Writers' Project (1930s, US — slave narratives); Allan Nevins (1948, Columbia University Oral History Research Office — first formal program, elite interviews); "democratic" turn: Studs Terkel, Working (1974), The Good War (1984, Pulitzer — WWII oral histories); Paul Thompson, The Voice of the Past (1978 — foundational British text; "oral history gives history back to the people")
  - Methods: interview techniques; recording technology (cassette → digital); transcription and editing (verbatim vs. cleaned); ethical issues (informed consent, memory reliability, interviewer influence, power dynamics; Institutional Review Board requirements)
  - Memory and oral history: Alessandro Portelli, "The Peculiarities of Oral History" (History Workshop Journal, 1981) — memory is not a passive repository but an active process of meaning-making; "what informants believe is indeed a historical fact"; the errors and silences in testimony are themselves evidence
  - Oral history of trauma: Holocaust testimony (USC Shoah Foundation, Steven Spielberg, 1994 — ~52,000 testimonies; Yale Fortunoff Archive; Claude Lanzmann, Shoah, 1985 — 9-hour film); oral histories of slavery descendants; oral history of partition (India/Pakistan, 1947 — Urvashi Butalia, The Other Side of Silence, 1998); oral history of war (Vietnam, Iraq/Afghanistan)
  - Indigenous oral traditions: oral history as primary record for pre-literate and partly-literate societies; legal recognition (Delgamuukw v. British Columbia, 1997, Canada — Supreme Court ruled oral history admissible as evidence of Aboriginal title); Australian Aboriginal oral histories (deep time — stories matching geological/sea-level records ~7,000–10,000 years; Patrick Nunn, The Edge of Memory, 2018); debate over reliability and historicity
  - Digital oral history: online archives (StoryCorps, US; British Library Sound Archive; Oral History Society, UK); searchable transcripts; AI transcription and analysis; COVID-19 oral history projects (rapid-response collecting)

- **Cultural history and the cultural turn**
  - Definition: study of cultural practices, representations, meanings, and symbolic systems as central to historical experience — not mere "decoration" added to social or political history
  - Origins: Burckhardt, Huizinga (Autumn of the Middle Ages, 1919 — sensory, aesthetic); Annales mentalités tradition (Febvre, Le Goff); intellectual history overlap
  - **The "cultural turn" (~1980s–1990s)**: influenced by literary theory, anthropology (Clifford Geertz, thick description), poststructuralism (Foucault, Derrida); language as constitutive of reality, not merely reflective; "the linguistic turn" (Richard Rorty coined phrase, 1967) applied to history
  - Key practitioners: Lynn Hunt, ed., The New Cultural History (1989) — programmatic manifesto; Roger Chartier (history of reading, The Cultural Origins of the French Revolution, 1991); Robert Darnton (cultural history of Enlightenment); Peter Burke, What Is Cultural History? (2004, 2nd ed. 2008 — accessible overview); Natalie Zemon Davis (rituals of violence, gift-giving)
  - Specific subfields: history of the book and reading (Robert Darnton, Elizabeth Eisenstein, The Printing Press as an Agent of Change, 1979; Adrian Johns, The Nature of the Book, 1998); history of material culture (objects, consumption — Beverly Lemire, Daniel Miller); history of the body (body as historical and cultural construct — Roy Porter, Alain Corbin); history of emotions (or "emotionology" — Peter and Carol Stearns; William Reddy, The Navigation of Feeling, 2001; Barbara Rosenwein, "emotional communities"; Jan Plamper, The History of Emotions, 2012 — overview); history of the senses (Mark Smith, Sensing the Past, 2007; Alain Corbin, The Foul and the Fragrant, 1982); history of memory (see below)
  - Debates: did the cultural turn go "too far"? — lost sight of material conditions, power, economics; "the social in the cultural" (William Sewell, Logics of History, 2005); post-cultural turn → return to materiality, political economy

- **Postmodern / linguistic turn in historiography**
  - **Hayden White (1928–2018)**: Metahistory: The Historical Imagination in Nineteenth-Century Europe (1973) — analysed major 19th-century historians (Michelet, Ranke, Tocqueville, Burckhardt) and philosophers of history (Hegel, Marx, Nietzsche, Croce) as literary constructions; four "emplotments" (Romance, Comedy, Tragedy, Satire); four "tropes" (Metaphor, Metonymy, Synecdoche, Irony); argued historical texts are literary artefacts shaped by narrative and rhetorical conventions; deeply controversial — challenged the distinction between history and fiction; The Content of the Form (1987)
  - **Keith Jenkins**: Re-thinking History (1991); argued "history is a discourse about the past, not the past itself"; radical scepticism about objectivity; "the past and history are two different things"
  - **Frank Ankersmit**: Narrative Logic (1983); postmodern sublime; irony in historiography; Sublime Historical Experience (2005)
  - Debate: most practising historians rejected strong postmodernism (if history is just narrative, how do we distinguish it from fiction?); Richard J. Evans, In Defence of History (1997) — polemical response; Gabrielle Spiegel, "History, Historicism, and the Social Logic of the Text" (Speculum, 1990); Carlo Ginzburg critiqued White's relativism (argued evidence constrains interpretation); Georg Iggers: "moderate constructivism" — most historians accept representation is shaped by language/culture but insist on referential relationship to past reality
  - Legacy: heightened reflexivity about narrative, representation, language; source criticism now includes analysis of rhetorical/literary dimensions; "the text" as object of scrutiny; but most historians remain realists — the postmodern challenge was absorbed, not accepted wholesale

- **World-systems theory**
  - **Immanuel Wallerstein (1930–2019)**: The Modern World-System (4 vols., 1974–2011) — "world-system" as unit of analysis (not nation-state); capitalist world-economy emerged ~16th century; core-periphery-semiperiphery structure; unequal exchange; longue durée influence (Braudel was mentor and inspiration); Fernand Braudel Center, Binghamton
  - André Gunder Frank, ReOrient: Global Economy in the Asian Age (1998) — challenged Eurocentric world-systems narrative; argued Asia dominated until ~1800
  - Giovanni Arrighi, The Long Twentieth Century (1994) — systemic cycles of accumulation (Genoa → Dutch → British → American hegemony)
  - Janet Abu-Lughod, Before European Hegemony (1989) — 13th-century world system centered on trade routes from NW Europe through Middle East to China; no single hegemon
  - Critiques: teleological? Eurocentrism within anti-Eurocentrism? Neglects culture and agency? Too structuralist? — but influenced global history, dependency theory, postcolonial thought

- **Big History**
  - **David Christian (b. 1946)**: coined "Big History" (1991); Maps of Time: An Introduction to Big History (2004); places human history within cosmic history — from Big Bang (~13.8 billion years ago) through formation of stars, elements, solar system, Earth, life, Homo sapiens, agriculture, modern era; identifies "thresholds of increasing complexity"; Fred Spier, Big History and the Future of Humanity (2010, 2nd ed. 2015)
  - Institutional base: Big History Project (with Bill Gates, aimed at high school students); International Big History Association (2010)
  - Influences: Annales longue durée (extended to cosmic scale); evolutionary biology; complexity science; ecology
  - Critiques: too grand? Deterministic? Marginalises human agency and meaning? Risk of "just-so stories"? William McNeill (historiographical eminence) broadly supportive; others sceptical; tension between scientific and humanistic approaches

- **Transnational and global history**
  - Transnational history: examines flows (people, ideas, goods, diseases, organisms) across national boundaries; rejects "methodological nationalism" (using nation-state as default unit); Akira Iriye, Global and Transnational History: The Past, Present, and Future (2013); Pierre-Yves Saunier, Transnational History (2013); Sebastian Conrad, What Is Global History? (2016) — accessible introduction
  - Global history: not merely "history of the whole world" but analysis of connections, comparisons, and interactions across regions; Distinguished from "world history" (survey/textbook) and "universal history" (philosophically driven — Hegel, Condorcet)
  - Key practitioners: Kenneth Pomeranz, The Great Divergence (2000) — why did Europe industrialise, not China?; Sanjay Subrahmanyam, "Connected Histories" (Modern Asian Studies, 1997); C.A. Bayly, The Birth of the Modern World (2004); Jürgen Osterhammel, The Transformation of the World: A Global History of the Nineteenth Century (2009/2014); Timothy Brook, Vermeer's Hat (2008 — global connections through 17th-century Delft paintings)
  - Debates: is global history just rehashed Eurocentrism? Who has access to multiple archives/languages required?; provincial vs. global; area studies vs. global history tension; digital tools (corpus analysis, network visualisation) enabling new scales
  - Oceanic and environmental framings: Atlantic history (Bernard Bailyn, David Armitage); Indian Ocean history (Michael Pearson, K.N. Chaudhuri); Pacific history; Mediterranean (Braudel's original)

- **Memory studies**
  - **Pierre Nora (b. 1931)**: Les Lieux de Mémoire (Realms of Memory, 7 vols., 1984–1992) — "sites of memory" (lieux de mémoire): places, monuments, symbols, texts, practices where collective memory crystallises when organic memory fades; distinction between memory (lived, embodied) and history (reconstructed, critical); enormously influential
  - Maurice Halbwachs (1877–1945): On Collective Memory (1925/1950, posthumous — died in Buchenwald); social frameworks of memory; memory as social construction; foundational
  - Jan Assmann and Aleida Assmann: "communicative memory" (living, ~80-year horizon) vs. "cultural memory" (institutionalised, formalised — texts, rituals, monuments); Das kulturelle Gedächtnis (Cultural Memory, 1992); Aleida Assmann, Shadows of Trauma: Memory and the Politics of Postwar Identity (2016)
  - Holocaust memory: paradigmatic case; Yad Vashem; Holocaust museums (US Holocaust Memorial Museum, 1993); Spielberg testimonies; "memory boom" (~1980s–2000s); James Young, The Texture of Memory (1993) — memorials as cultural practices; Marianne Hirsch, "postmemory" concept (The Generation of Postmemory, 2012 — memory transmitted to those who did not directly experience events)
  - Memory politics: memory laws (France: Gayssot Act 1990, criminalising Holocaust denial; Poland's 2018 law on WWII responsibility — controversial; Spain's Historical Memory Law 2007/2022); transitional justice (Truth and Reconciliation Commission, South Africa, 1996 — Desmond Tutu); contested monuments (Confederate statues removal debate, US; Rhodes Must Fall); reparative memory projects
  - Digital memory: internet as memory medium; social media and collective remembering/forgetting; "right to be forgotten" (EU CJEU ruling, 2014); digital archives and their gaps; Andrew Hoskins, Digital Memory Studies (2018)

- **Digital history and digital humanities**
  - Emergence: digitisation of archives and sources (~1990s–); "the digital turn" in humanities
  - Text mining and computational text analysis: topic modelling (LDA — Latent Dirichlet Allocation; applied to historical corpora; Cameron Blevins); word embeddings for tracking semantic change (Google Ngrams; Culturomics, Michel et al., Science, 2011); named entity recognition; stylometry
  - Historical GIS: spatial history (Richard White, Spatial History Project, Stanford); mapping historical phenomena; HGIS of the Low Countries; reconstructing landscape change
  - Network analysis: social network analysis of historical correspondence (Republic of Letters project, Stanford; Mapping the Republic of Letters); political networks; trade networks
  - Digital archives: mass digitization (Google Books; HathiTrust; Internet Archive; Europeana; Gallica); born-digital sources (social media, email as future primary sources; web archiving — Internet Archive Wayback Machine); "too big to know" — overabundance after 500 years of archival scarcity
  - Debate: digital dividends vs. digital divides; "more data" ≠ "better history"; Franco Moretti, Distant Reading (2013) — computational analysis of literary corpora; Matthew Jockers; digital methods supplement but do not replace close reading/archival work; AI and LLMs in historical research (language model analysis of historical texts; risks of hallucination and false pattern detection; Lara Putnam, "The Transnational and the Text-Searchable," American Historical Review, 2016 — how keyword search transforms research practice)
  - Key centres: Roy Rosenzweig Center for History and New Media (George Mason University — Omeka, Zotero); Stanford Literary Lab; Luxembourg Centre for Contemporary and Digital History (C²DH)

- **Counterfactual history**
  - Definition: asking "what if?" — structured consideration of alternative scenarios to illuminate contingency, causation, and the weight of individual events/decisions
  - Niall Ferguson, Virtual History: Alternatives and Counterfactuals (1997) — edited volume; argued counterfactuals are logically necessary for any causal claim; defended against E.P. Thompson's derision ("unhistorical shit")
  - Philosophical basis: possible-worlds semantics (David Lewis); causal inference (counterfactual theory of causation — Lewis, Judea Pearl); social sciences use counterfactuals routinely (Fogel's "what if no railroads?")
  - Critiques: can never be tested; too speculative; serves ideological purposes (what if Hitler had won?); E.H. Carr dismissed counterfactuals as intellectual parlour games; Richard Evans, Altered Pasts (2014) — critical of the genre
  - Distinguished from "alternate history" as fiction genre (Philip K. Dick, The Man in the High Castle, 1962; Philip Roth, The Plot Against America, 2004)

- **Historical theory: explanation, narrative, causation**
  - **Carl Hempel, "The Function of General Laws in History" (Journal of Philosophy, 1942)**: covering law model — historical explanation requires subsumption under general laws (just as in natural sciences); "deductive-nomological" model; deeply contested by historians and philosophers
  - **William Dray, Laws and Explanation in History (1957)**: rebutted Hempel; historical explanations are rational reconstructions of agents' reasoning; "continuous series" model
  - R.G. Collingwood: re-enactment; historical understanding is sui generis
  - Louis Mink: narrative as "cognitive instrument" — narratives are not just ways of presenting history but ways of comprehending it; "History and Fiction as Modes of Comprehension" (New Literary History, 1970)
  - Paul Ricoeur, Time and Narrative (3 vols., 1984–1988): phenomenology of temporality and narrative; history mediates between lived time and cosmic time; emplotment; bridge between Continental philosophy and Anglo-American historical theory
  - Recent: causal pluralism in history (multiple types of causes — structural, contingent, agential); mechanism-based explanation (social mechanisms — Hedström, Swedberg); computational models (agent-based modelling of historical processes); experimental history (natural experiments — Jared Diamond and James Robinson, Natural Experiments of History, 2010)

- **Frontier: contemporary debates and emerging approaches (2020–present)**
  - History and climate crisis: Dipesh Chakrabarty, "The Climate of History in a Planetary Age" (2021, book expansion of 2009 essay) — humans as geological agents; collapse of distinction between natural and human history; "Anthropocene histories"; deep history meets environmental crisis
  - Decolonising historical practice: reexamination of archives as colonial instruments; incorporating indigenous, oral, and non-textual sources; "archival silences" (Trouillot, Silencing the Past, 1995 — power shapes what enters the archive); repatriation debates; community-based research; Linda Tuhiwai Smith, Decolonizing Methodologies (1999) — indigenous research ethics
  - History of capitalism: resurgent field — Sven Beckert, Empire of Cotton (2014); Edward Baptist, The Half Has Never Been Told (2014); "new history of capitalism" (Harvard/Cornell programs); connects slavery, colonialism, financialisation, inequality; debates with economic historians over methodology
  - Emotional turn: growing institutionalisation — Australian Research Council Centre of Excellence for the History of Emotions (2011–2018); Queen Mary Centre for the History of Emotions; neuroscience dialogue
  - Spatial turn: persistence and deepening; GIS; landscape archaeology; "deep mapping" (Least Heat-Moon, PrairyErth, 1991; creative non-fiction meets historical topography)
  - Animal turn / multispecies history: Harriet Ritvo, The Animal Estate (1987); Erica Fudge; Donna Haraway, When Species Meet (2008); history beyond the human; microbiome history
  - Sensory history: expansion of what counts as historical evidence; soundscapes (Emily Thompson, The Soundscape of Modernity, 2002); smell, taste, touch as historical topics
  - AI and history: large language models trained on historical corpora; automated transcription of handwritten documents (Transkribus — HTR, Handwritten Text Recognition); machine learning for palaeography; ethical concerns over AI-generated pseudo-historical content; potential for pattern detection across massive archives; debates in American Historical Review and History and Theory (2023–)
  - Georg G. Iggers, Q. Edward Wang, and Supriya Mukherjee, A Global History of Modern Historiography (2nd ed., 2017); John Burrow, A History of Histories (2007); Daniel Woolf, A Global History of History (2011); Aviezer Tucker, ed., A Companion to the Philosophy of History and Historiography (2009)

- Art History
- Cultural History
- Economic History
- Intellectual History
- Political History
- History of Science & Technology
- Environmental History
- Military History
- Social History
- Oral History & Historiography

### Languages & Literature

#### Linguistics — Full Research Depth

- **Scope and definition**
  - Linguistics = the scientific study of language: its structure, use, acquisition, change, and biological/cognitive/social foundations
  - Descriptive (how language is) vs. prescriptive (how language "should" be); modern linguistics is descriptive
  - Competence vs. performance (Chomsky, 1965): knowledge of language vs. actual use
  - Ferdinand de Saussure (1857–1913): Course in General Linguistics (1916, posthumous, compiled by students Charles Bally and Albert Sechehaye): langue (language system) vs. parole (speech); synchronic vs. diachronic analysis; the sign = signifier (sound image) + signified (concept); arbitrariness of the sign; foundational for structuralism
  - Major traditions: European structuralism (Saussure → Prague School → Copenhagen School) vs. American structuralism (Boas → Sapir → Bloomfield) vs. generative grammar (Chomsky, 1957–)
  - Key professional organisations: Linguistic Society of America (LSA, 1924); Linguistic Society of Europe (SLE); International Phonetic Association (IPA, 1886)
  - Core reference: Victoria Fromkin, Robert Rodman, and Nina Hyams, An Introduction to Language (12th ed., 2022); Mark Aronoff and Janie Rees-Miller, eds., The Handbook of Linguistics (2nd ed., 2017)

- **Phonetics**
  - **Definition**: study of the physical properties of speech sounds (phones); distinct from phonology (which studies sound systems)
  - **Articulatory phonetics**: production of speech sounds by the vocal tract
    - Vocal tract anatomy: lungs (airstream source), larynx (voicing — vocal fold vibration), pharynx, oral cavity, nasal cavity; articulators: tongue (tip, blade, body, root), lips, teeth, alveolar ridge, hard palate, soft palate (velum), uvula, glottis
    - Consonants classified by: **place of articulation** (bilabial, labiodental, dental, alveolar, post-alveolar, retroflex, palatal, velar, uvular, pharyngeal, glottal); **manner of articulation** (stop/plosive, fricative, affricate, nasal, lateral, approximant, trill, tap/flap); **voicing** (voiced vs. voiceless)
    - Vowel classification: tongue height (high/close, mid, low/open); tongue frontness/backness (front, central, back); lip rounding (rounded vs. unrounded); tense vs. lax; IPA vowel trapezoid (based on Daniel Jones's cardinal vowels, 1917)
    - Suprasegmentals: stress, tone, intonation, length, nasalisation; tone languages (Mandarin: 4 tones; Cantonese: 6–9; Vietnamese: 6; many West African and East Asian languages)
    - **International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)**: devised 1888 by Paul Passy and others; revised multiple times (latest: 2005 chart with 2015/2020 updates); goal: one symbol per distinctive sound; ~107 letters, ~31 diacritics, ~19 additional symbols; universally used in linguistics; based on Latin alphabet with additions from Greek and purpose-designed symbols
    - Key figures: Alexander Melville Bell (Visible Speech, 1867 — pre-IPA articulatory notation; father of Alexander Graham Bell); Daniel Jones (1881–1967, UCL — cardinal vowels; English Pronouncing Dictionary, 1917); Peter Ladefoged (1925–2006, UCLA — A Course in Phonetics, standard textbook; fieldwork on endangered-language phonetics; Ladefoged and Maddieson, The Sounds of the World's Languages, 1996 — comprehensive phonetic typology)
  - **Acoustic phonetics**: physical properties of speech sounds as sound waves
    - Sound spectrography: spectrogram (frequency × time × intensity visualization); formants (resonant frequencies of the vocal tract — F1, F2, F3; key for vowel identification: F1 correlates with height, F2 with frontness); voice onset time (VOT — distinguishes voiced/voiceless stops)
    - Source-filter theory (Gunnar Fant, Acoustic Theory of Speech Production, 1960): glottal source (periodic vibration) filtered by vocal tract resonances; foundational for speech synthesis
    - Speech analysis software: Praat (Paul Boersma and David Weenink, University of Amsterdam, 1995– ; free, ubiquitous in phonetics labs); spectral analysis; pitch tracking
    - Key figures: Harvey Fletcher (Bell Labs, 1920s–1950s — speech signal analysis); Kenneth Stevens (MIT, acoustic phonetics; Acoustic Phonetics, 1998); Gunnar Fant
  - **Auditory/perceptual phonetics**: how the ear and brain process speech sounds
    - Categorical perception: listeners perceive speech sounds as belonging to discrete categories despite continuous acoustic variation (Liberman et al., 1957; magnet effect — Patricia Kuhl); language-specific perceptual tuning in infancy (Kuhl et al., 1992 — "perceptual magnet effect")
    - McGurk effect (McGurk and MacDonald, 1976): visual information overrides auditory — "ba" heard as "da" when lip movements show "ga"; demonstrates multimodal speech perception
  - **Laboratory phonetics / experimental phonetics**: instrumental methods — ultrasound tongue imaging; electromagnetic articulography (EMA); electropalatography (EPG); aerodynamic measurements; MRI of vocal tract (real-time MRI — Narayanan Lab, USC); advances in articulatory modelling
  - Frontier: real-time MRI speech imaging (5-D vocal tract models; Bresch et al., 2020); ultrasound biofeedback for speech therapy; deep learning for automatic phonetic transcription (wav2vec 2.0; Whisper — OpenAI, 2022; XLS-R); articulatory synthesis; cross-linguistic phonetic databases (PHOIBLE — Moran and McCloy, 2019: 3,020 inventories; UCLA Phonological Segment Inventory Database)

- **Phonology**
  - **Definition**: study of the sound systems of languages — which sounds are distinctive (phonemes), how they pattern, and what rules/constraints govern their distribution
  - Phoneme: minimal distinctive unit; established by minimal pairs (e.g., English /p/ vs. /b/: "pat" vs. "bat"); allophones = contextually determined variants of a phoneme (e.g., English aspirated [pʰ] in "pin" vs. unaspirated [p] in "spin")
  - **Structuralist phonology**: Prague School — Nikolai Trubetzkoy, Grundzüge der Phonologie (Principles of Phonology, 1939/posthumous) — systematic opposition and markedness; Roman Jakobson (1896–1982): distinctive features (binary — [±voice], [±nasal], etc.); The Sound Pattern of Russian (1948, with Fant and Halle); Preliminaries to Speech Analysis (Jakobson, Fant, Halle, 1952); enormously influential on structuralism broadly
  - **Generative phonology**: Noam Chomsky and Morris Halle, The Sound Pattern of English (SPE, 1968) — ordered rewrite rules mapping underlying representations to surface forms; distinctive features (articulatory basis); rule ordering; dominated 1970s–1980s; highly productive but criticised for excessive power
  - **Natural Phonology**: David Stampe (1979) — phonological processes are natural, innate responses to phonetic difficulty; acquisition involves suppression of innate processes
  - **Autosegmental phonology**: John Goldsmith (1976 MIT dissertation) — tones and features on separate "tiers" (autosegments) linked to skeletal positions by association lines; solved problems of tone spreading, contour tones; generalised to all features (feature geometry — Clements, 1985; Sagey, 1986)
  - **Metrical phonology**: Mark Liberman and Alan Prince (1977) — stress as hierarchical rhythmic structure (strong/weak); foot structure; metrical grids; stress clash and lapse
  - **Lexical phonology**: Paul Kiparsky (1982) — phonological rules apply in ordered strata corresponding to morphological levels (word formation feeds phonology cyclically)
  - **Optimality Theory (OT)**: Alan Prince and Paul Smolensky (1993, published as Optimality Theory: Constraint Interaction in Generative Grammar, 2004) — output constraints (markedness and faithfulness) ranked in language-specific hierarchy; Gen produces candidates, Eval selects optimal output via ranked constraints; replaced rule-based derivation with constraint-based competition; revolutionised phonology (and morphology, syntax); John McCarthy and Alan Prince extended to prosodic morphology (reduplication, infixation); OT-Learn; Stochastic OT (Boersma and Hayes, 2001); Harmonic Grammar (weighted constraints — Legendre, Miyata, Smolensky, 1990; revived ~2005+ as alternative to OT strict ranking)
  - **Laboratory phonology**: integration of phonology with experimental phonetics; Phonology Laboratory at UC Berkeley (John Ohala); LabPhon conference series (1987–); Journal of Laboratory Phonology (2010–); Mary Beckman; Janet Pierrehumbert, The Phonology and Phonetics of English Intonation (1980 MIT dissertation) — autosegmental-metrical (AM) model of intonation; ToBI (Tones and Break Indices) transcription system
  - Prosodic phonology / prosodic hierarchy: syllable, foot, prosodic word, phonological phrase, intonational phrase, utterance; Elisabeth Selkirk (1984, 1986); Marina Nespor and Irene Vogel, Prosodic Phonology (1986/2007)
  - Frontier: articulatory phonology (Catherine Browman and Louis Goldstein — gestures as primitives, not segments; dynamical systems approach); exemplar theory (Joan Bybee; Pierrehumbert — rich memory of tokens; probabilistic phonology); computational phonology (neural network models of phonological learning; Rule-Based vs. connectionist debate); learning models (Hayes and Wilson, Maximum Entropy learner, 2008); phonological typology and universals (Hyman, 2008 — "Where's phonology in typology?")

- **Morphology**
  - **Definition**: study of the internal structure of words; morpheme = smallest unit of meaning or grammatical function
  - Types of morphemes: free (can stand alone: "cat") vs. bound (must attach: "-s," "un-"); roots, stems, affixes (prefix, suffix, infix, circumfix); derivational (create new words: "kind" → "unkindly") vs. inflectional (grammatical marking: "walk" → "walks," "walked")
  - **Morphological typology**: isolating/analytic (e.g., Mandarin — few affixes, word boundaries clear); agglutinative (e.g., Turkish, Swahili — transparent, regular affixation); fusional/inflectional (e.g., Latin, Russian — single morphemes encode multiple features); polysynthetic (e.g., Mohawk, Yup'ik — single "word" can express entire sentence); typology originally from Wilhelm von Humboldt (1836) and August Schleicher; refined by Edward Sapir (Language, 1921); now understood as continuum, not discrete types
  - **Morphological processes**: affixation; compounding (English: "blackboard"; German: Rindfleischetikettierungsüberwachungsaufgabenübertragungsgesetz); reduplication (Indonesian: "orang" → "orang-orang" = people; Tagalog); ablaut/apophony (English: "sing-sang-sung"); suppletion (English: "go" → "went"); conversion/zero-derivation ("to bottle"; "a run"); back-formation ("edit" from "editor"); blending ("smog" from "smoke"+"fog"); clipping ("exam" from "examination"); acronyms (NATO, laser)
  - **Item-and-Arrangement (IA)** vs. **Item-and-Process (IP)** vs. **Word-and-Paradigm (WP)**: three models of morphological analysis (Charles Hockett, 1954); IA: morphemes as items in fixed slots; IP: morphemes derived by processes/rules; WP: whole word-forms in paradigms (Peter Matthews, Morphology, 1974/2nd ed. 1991; Gregory Stump, Inflectional Morphology, 2001)
  - **Lexicalist vs. constructionist approaches**: lexicalism (word formation in lexicon, separate from syntax — Chomsky, Remarks on Nominalization, 1970; Lieber, Morphology and Lexical Semantics, 2004); anti-lexicalism/constructionism (morphology in syntax — Distributed Morphology: Halle and Marantz, 1993; all word formation syntactic; Nanosyntax: Starke, 2009)
  - **Paradigm Function Morphology (PFM)**: Stump (2001) — inflectional paradigms determined by functions mapping a root and set of features to word-forms; handles syncretism, overabundance, defectivity
  - Key figures: Leonard Bloomfield (Language, 1933 — American structuralist morphophonemics); Eugene Nida (Morphology, 1949 — distributional and substitutional methods); Mark Aronoff (Word Formation in Generative Grammar, 1976; Morphology by Itself, 1994 — autonomy of morphology); Rochelle Lieber; Andrew Spencer; Geert Booij (Construction Morphology, 2010)
  - Frontier: computational morphology (finite-state transducers — Beesley and Karttunen, 2003; neural morphological analysis: UniMorph project — Kirov et al., 2018; SIGMORPHON shared tasks); morphological complexity metrics; morphological typology at scale (World Atlas of Language Structures — WALS — Feature 26A); usage-based morphology (Bybee)

- **Syntax**
  - **Definition**: study of sentence structure — how words combine into phrases and sentences; rules/principles governing grammaticality
  - **Constituency**: sentences have hierarchical structure (phrases within phrases); evidence: substitution (pronoun replacing NP), movement (question formation), coordination; phrase structure rules (S → NP VP; NP → Det N; etc.)
  - **Grammatical relations**: subject, object, indirect object; ergative-absolutive vs. nominative-accusative alignment; topic-comment structure
  - **Chomskyan generative grammar**: dominant paradigm from 1957
    - **Syntactic Structures (1957)**: formal grammar; phrase structure grammar + transformations; review of Skinner's Verbal Behavior (1959) — devastating critique of behaviorist language learning
    - **Aspects of the Theory of Syntax (1965)**: Standard Theory; deep structure and surface structure; competence/performance distinction; base component generates deep structures, transformational component produces surface structures; subcategorisation; selectional restrictions
    - **Government and Binding Theory (GB, ~1981)**: Lectures on Government and Binding — modular architecture; principles and parameters; X-bar theory (all phrases have uniform hierarchical structure: XP → Spec, X'; X' → X, Complement); movement (Move-α); Case theory; Theta theory; Binding theory (Condition A: anaphors bound locally; Condition B: pronouns free locally; Condition C: R-expressions free); Control theory; Empty Category Principle; Subjacency/bounding theory
    - **Minimalist Program (1995–)**: The Minimalist Program (Chomsky, 1995) — reduce syntax to bare minimum: Merge (binary, recursive structure-building) and Agree (feature checking); Merge as possibly language's fundamental operation; phases (CP, v*P — Chomsky, 2000, 2001); labeling (Chomsky, 2013, 2015); interface conditions (syntax produces structures interpreted by Sensorimotor and Conceptual-Intentional systems); "third factor" (computational efficiency, not innate and not experience); Merge as species-unique operation hypothesized to have emerged ~80,000–100,000 years ago (Berwick and Chomsky, Why Only Us, 2016)
    - **Parameters**: head-directionality (head-initial: English, Arabic; head-final: Japanese, Turkish); pro-drop (null subject: Italian, Spanish vs. obligatory subject: English, French — *pro-drop parameter*); Wh-movement (English: overt movement — "What did you see?"; Chinese, Japanese: in-situ — "Nǐ kàn-le shénme?"); Polysynthesis Parameter (Mark Baker, 1996); debate on whether parameters are macro (few, UG-specified) or micro (many, lexically specified); recent: parameters maybe an artefact — Richard Kayne's microparametric approach; Ian Roberts and Anders Holmberg
  - **Alternative syntactic theories**
    - **Lexical-Functional Grammar (LFG)**: Joan Bresnan (1982, 2001); parallel structures: c-structure (constituency, phrase structure) and f-structure (functional — grammatical relations, agreement); non-transformational; computationally tractable; strong typological coverage
    - **Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG)**: Carl Pollard and Ivan Sag (1987, 1994); constraint-based; typed feature structures; no transformations; sign-based; computationally precise; implemented grammars (English Resource Grammar — Dan Flickinger)
    - **Construction Grammar**: Charles Fillmore, Paul Kay, Adele Goldberg (Constructions: A Construction Grammar Approach to Argument Structure, 1995); no sharp lexicon/syntax division; constructions (form-meaning pairings) at all levels; William Croft, Radical Construction Grammar (2001); Sign-Based Construction Grammar (Sag, 2012); usage-based construction grammar (Tomasello, 2003); Fluid Construction Grammar (Luc Steels, computational)
    - **Role and Reference Grammar (RRG)**: Robert Van Valin and William Foley (1980); Robert Van Valin, Exploring the Syntax-Semantics Interface (2005); functionally-oriented; macro-roles (actor, undergoer); layered structure of the clause; language universals with cross-linguistic applicability
    - **Dependency Grammar**: Lucien Tesnière, Éléments de syntaxe structurale (1959); words connected by dependency relations (head–dependent); no constituency; computationally influential (dependency parsing standard in NLP — Universal Dependencies project, Nivre et al., 2016; ~150 languages)
    - **Categorial Grammar**: Kazimierz Ajdukiewicz (1935); Yehoshua Bar-Hillel; Combinatory Categorial Grammar (CCG, Mark Steedman, 2000) — type-logical; operations: function application, composition, type-raising; elegant interface with semantics
  - Frontier: computational syntax (neural parsers — constituency and dependency; GPT-like models' implicit syntactic knowledge — Linzen, Dupoux, Goldberg, 2016; Marvin and Linzen, 2018; syntactic evaluation of LLMs); cross-linguistic syntactic typology at scale (WALS; Autotyp); argument structure and verb classes (Beth Levin, English Verb Classes and Alternations, 1993 — 3,024 verbs classified; VerbNet); information structure (focus, topic — Vallduví, Büring, Krifka); syntactic change and grammaticalization; minimalist syntax vs. alternatives debate continues vigorously

- **Semantics**
  - **Definition**: study of meaning in language — word meaning, sentence meaning, truth conditions, compositionality
  - **Lexical semantics**: word meaning
    - Sense relations: synonymy, antonymy (gradable, complementary, converse), hyponymy/hypernymy, meronymy, polysemy vs. homonymy
    - Prototype theory: Eleanor Rosch (1975) — category membership graded; "robin" more prototypical bird than "penguin"; challenges classical (necessary and sufficient conditions) definition
    - Frame semantics: Charles Fillmore ("The Case for Case," 1968; "Frame Semantics," 1982; FrameNet project — Baker, Fillmore, and Lowe, 1998 — lexical database of ~1,200 semantic frames, ~13,000 lexical units; computationally useful for NLP)
    - WordNet: George Miller (Princeton, 1995) — large lexical database; synsets (synonym sets); hierarchical; ~117,000 synsets for English; widely used in NLP; multilingual versions (Open Multilingual Wordnet)
    - Conceptual Metaphor Theory: George Lakoff and Mark Johnson, Metaphors We Live By (1980): metaphor is fundamental to conceptual structure, not merely decorative language; ARGUMENT IS WAR, LIFE IS A JOURNEY, etc.; cognitive linguistics foundation; critiqued for unfalsifiability by some (McGlone, 2007)
  - **Formal semantics / truth-conditional semantics**
    - **Gottlob Frege (1848–1925)**: sense (Sinn) vs. reference (Bedeutung) (1892); compositionality principle ("the meaning of a complex expression is a function of the meanings of its parts and the way they are combined"); predicate logic; quantification; the "Frege-Russell" tradition
    - **Richard Montague (1930–1971)**: "Universal Grammar" (1970) and "The Proper Treatment of Quantification in Ordinary English" (PTQ, 1973) — demonstrated that natural language could be given the same rigorous, formal semantic treatment as logical languages; Montague Grammar: model-theoretic semantics; lambda calculus; type theory; compositionality implemented; Barbara Partee: "Montague's bold claim: natural language can be described as a formal system" — paradigm shift
    - Possible worlds semantics: Saul Kripke (1959, 1963 — modal logic semantics); applied to modality (necessity, possibility), counterfactuals (David Lewis, Counterfactuals, 1973); Angelika Kratzer (modal semantics for natural language; "Modality," 1991)
    - Generalized Quantifier Theory: Jon Barwise and Robin Cooper (1981) — determiners as functions from sets to sets; "every," "some," "no," "most" as generalized quantifiers; enabled rigorous treatment of NP semantics
    - Event semantics: Donald Davidson, "The Logical Form of Action Sentences" (1967) — events as individuals; neo-Davidsonian approach (Parsons, 1990 — thematic roles as separate predicates); widely adopted
    - Dynamic semantics: file change semantics (Irene Heim, 1982 — indefinites "introduce" discourse referents); Discourse Representation Theory (DRT, Hans Kamp, 1981 — discourse representation structures for anaphora, donkey sentences, presupposition); Dynamic Predicate Logic (Groenendijk and Stokhof, 1991)
    - Type-logical semantics: Carpenter (1997); Jacobson (2014); continuation-based semantics
    - Situation semantics: Barwise and Perry, Situations and Attitudes (1983); alternative to possible-worlds
  - **Cognitive semantics**: meaning as conceptualization (not truth conditions); George Lakoff (Women, Fire, and Dangerous Things, 1987); Ronald Langacker, Cognitive Grammar (1987/1991 — language as symbolization of conceptualizations; schema-instance; construal; figure-ground); Leonard Talmy (force dynamics, fictive motion, 2000); part of the broader cognitive linguistics movement (Lakoff, Langacker, Talmy, Fillmore, Croft, Goldberg)
  - Frontier: distributional semantics and word embeddings (word2vec — Mikolov et al., 2013; GloVe; contextual embeddings: BERT, GPT — meaning from usage, resonant with Firth's "you shall know a word by the company it keeps," 1957); formal semantics + neural models interface; cross-linguistic semantics at scale (Concepticon; List et al., 2016); experimental semantics (psycholinguistic and neurolinguistic testing of semantic theories); probabilistic/Bayesian semantics (Goodman and Frank, 2016 — Rational Speech Act model)

- **Pragmatics**
  - **Definition**: study of meaning in context — how context contributes to meaning beyond the literal, semantic content; what speakers mean vs. what sentences mean
  - **Paul Grice (1913–1988)**: "Logic and Conversation" (1975, from 1967 William James Lectures at Harvard); **Cooperative Principle** and four **maxims**: Quality (truthfulness), Quantity (informativeness), Relation (relevance), Manner (clarity); **implicature**: conversational implicatures arise when maxims are flouted; scalar implicatures ("some" → "not all"); conventional vs. conversational implicature; enormously influential
  - **Speech act theory**: J.L. Austin, How to Do Things with Words (1962, posthumous — William James Lectures, 1955) — locutionary, illocutionary, perlocutionary acts; performatives ("I pronounce you husband and wife") vs. constatives; John Searle, Speech Acts (1969) — taxonomy of illocutionary acts (assertives, directives, commissives, expressives, declaratives); felicity conditions; indirect speech acts ("Can you pass the salt?" = request, not question)
  - **Relevance Theory**: Dan Sperber and Deirdre Wilson, Relevance: Communication and Cognition (1986, 2nd ed. 1995) — communication guided by presumption of optimal relevance; cognitive effort vs. cognitive effect; rejects Gricean maxims as separate principles — replaces with single principle; highly influential in pragmatics and cognitive science
  - **Presupposition**: what is taken for granted; triggers (definite descriptions, factive verbs, cleft constructions, change-of-state verbs); projection problem (how presuppositions of embedded clauses project to matrix clause); Stalnaker (1974, common ground — presuppositions update the context set); Heim (1983, presupposition as context change potential)
  - **Deixis**: spatial (here/there), temporal (now/then), person (I/you), discourse (this/that), social (tu/vous — Brown and Gilman, 1960, "The Pronouns of Power and Solidarity"); Charles Fillmore, Santa Cruz Lectures on Deixis (1971/1997)
  - **Discourse analysis and conversation analysis**: discourse structure (Grosz and Sidner, 1986 — intentional, attentional, linguistic structure); conversation analysis (Harvey Sacks, Emanuel Schegloff, Gail Jefferson — turn-taking system, 1974; adjacency pairs; repair; preference organization; ethnomethodological roots — Harold Garfinkel); politeness theory (Penelope Brown and Stephen Levinson, 1978/1987 — face-threatening acts, positive and negative face; universal politeness strategies; critiqued for Western bias by Sachiko Ide, 1989, and others)
  - **Experimental pragmatics**: testing implicature processing (Ira Noveck; Huang and Snedeker, 2009 — scalar implicature timing); developmental pragmatics (children's understanding of implicature — typically by age 5–7, but slower than adults); eye-tracking; ERP studies of pragmatic processing (N400 for pragmatic anomalies)
  - Frontier: Rational Speech Act (RSA) models (Noah Goodman, Michael Frank — Bayesian pragmatics; models of scalar implicature, vagueness, politeness, metaphor as rational inference); computational pragmatics in dialogue systems; LLMs and pragmatic competence (do GPT models understand implicature? — Hu et al., 2023; Ruis et al., 2023); cross-cultural pragmatics; pragmatics of emoji and digital communication

- **Historical linguistics**
  - **Definition**: study of language change over time — sound change, morphological change, syntactic change, semantic change, language birth and death
  - **The comparative method**: systematic comparison of cognate words across related languages to reconstruct proto-languages; developed in 19th century (the "golden age" of historical linguistics)
    - Sir William Jones (1786): observed similarities among Sanskrit, Greek, Latin, Gothic, Celtic, Old Persian → hypothesised common ancestor (Proto-Indo-European); Third Anniversary Discourse to the Asiatic Society of Bengal
    - Rasmus Rask (1818), Jacob Grimm (1822): Grimm's Law — systematic consonant correspondences between Germanic and other IE languages (PIE *p → Germanic f; *t → þ; *k → h; etc.); first formulation of a sound law
    - Karl Verner (1875): Verner's Law — explained apparent exceptions to Grimm's Law (voicing conditioned by PIE accent position); demonstrated regularity of sound change
    - **Neogrammarians (Junggrammatiker)**: Karl Brugmann, Hermann Osthoff, August Leskien, et al. (Leipzig, from ~1876); slogan: "sound laws suffer no exceptions" (Ausnahmslosigkeit der Lautgesetze); regularity principle foundational for modern historical linguistics; analogy as mechanism for apparent exceptions
    - August Schleicher: family tree model (Stammbaumtheorie, 1861); Indo-European as tree of descent
    - Johannes Schmidt: wave model (Wellentheorie, 1872): innovations spread like waves from centers; accounts for overlapping change patterns, dialect continua; tree and wave models complementary
  - **Reconstruction**: comparative reconstruction (proto-forms via systematic correspondences); internal reconstruction (using alternations within a single language to infer earlier stages); Proto-Indo-European reconstucted phonology (laryngeal theory — Saussure, 1879, confirmed by Hittite /ḫ/ discovery, Kuryłowicz, 1927); PIE morphology (ablaut, case system, verb system); PIE syntax (debated: SOV?); PIE homeland debate: Pontic-Caspian steppe (Kurgan hypothesis — Marija Gimbutas, 1956; supported by aDNA: Haak et al., Nature, 2015; Allentoft et al., Nature, 2015; Yamnaya migrations) vs. Anatolian hypothesis (Colin Renfrew, 1987; minority position post-aDNA revolution)
  - **Language families of the world** (major; ~7,000 languages; Ethnologue 27th ed., 2024; Glottolog)
    - **Indo-European** (~445 languages; ~3.2 billion L1 speakers): branches — Indo-Iranian (Sanskrit, Hindi, Urdu, Bengali, Persian, Kurdish; largest branch), Hellenic (Greek), Italic (Latin → Romance: Spanish, Portuguese, French, Italian, Romanian), Celtic (Irish, Welsh, Breton), Germanic (English, German, Dutch, Swedish, Norwegian, Icelandic), Balto-Slavic (Russian, Polish, Czech, Lithuanian, Latvian), Armenian, Albanian, Tocharian (extinct), Anatolian (Hittite, extinct — oldest attested IE); reconstructed PIE; Watkins, The American Heritage Dictionary of Indo-European Roots (3rd ed., 2011); Fortson, Indo-European Language and Culture (2nd ed., 2010)
    - **Sino-Tibetan** (~400+ languages; ~1.3 billion speakers): Sinitic (Mandarin, Wu, Cantonese, Min, Hakka); Tibeto-Burman (Tibetan, Burmese, ~400 languages); tonal; Proto-Sino-Tibetan reconstruction ongoing and difficult (Laurent Sagart; Guillaume Jacques)
    - **Niger-Congo** (~1,500 languages; ~700 million speakers; some classifications): includes Bantu (Swahili, Zulu, Shona; ~500 languages; Bantu expansion ~3000 BCE from Cameroon-Nigeria borderlands); Atlantic-Congo debate; noun classes (gender systems — Bantu: up to ~18 classes); tone
    - **Afroasiatic** (~300+ languages; ~500 million speakers): Semitic (Arabic, Hebrew, Amharic, Tigrinya), Berber, Egyptian (Ancient Egyptian → Coptic, extinct as L1), Cushitic (Somali, Oromo), Chadic (Hausa), Omotic; Proto-Afroasiatic homeland debated (Africa — Ehret; Levant — some others)
    - **Austronesian** (~1,200 languages; ~385 million speakers; largest language family by language count): Formosan (Taiwan — highest diversity, indicating homeland; ~16 indigenous groups), Malayo-Polynesian (Malay/Indonesian, Tagalog, Javanese, Malagasy, Hawaiian, Maori, Samoan, Tongan, Fijian); Robert Blust (The Austronesian Languages, 2013); expansion from Taiwan ~5,000 BP → Philippines → Indonesia → Pacific → Madagascar
    - **Dravidian** (~85 languages; ~220 million speakers): Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam; concentrated in South India; Brahui in Pakistan; Proto-Dravidian (Bhadriraju Krishnamurti, The Dravidian Languages, 2003); Indus Valley link debated
    - **Turkic** (~40 languages; ~200 million speakers): Turkish, Azerbaijani, Uzbek, Kazakh, Kyrgyz, Turkmen, Uyghur; agglutinative; vowel harmony
    - **Uralic** (~38 languages; ~25 million speakers): Finno-Ugric (Finnish, Estonian, Hungarian, Sami) and Samoyedic (Nenets); agglutinative; vowel harmony; long-range proposals linking to other families (Ural-Altaic — largely rejected; Nostratic — hypothetical macro-family; highly controversial)
    - Other families: Japonic, Koreanic, Tai-Kadai, Austroasiatic (Vietnamese, Khmer), Nilo-Saharan (debated grouping), Algic, Athabaskan/Na-Dene, Uto-Aztecan, Mayan, Arawakan, Tupian, Pama-Nyungan (Australian), Trans–New Guinea, Kartvelian (Georgian), Northeast/Northwest Caucasian
    - Language isolates: Basque, Burushaski, Ainu, Korean (sometimes Koreanic family if Jeju included), Sumerian (extinct), Elamite (extinct)
    - Macro-family hypotheses: Nostratic (Illich-Svitych, 1960s — connecting IE, Uralic, Altaic, Kartvelian, Dravidian, Afroasiatic; mostly rejected by mainstream but debated); Joseph Greenberg's mass comparison: Amerind (1987 — nearly universally rejected by specialists); Eurasiatic (Greenberg, 2000); Proto-Human / Proto-World (Merritt Ruhlen — rejected); Dené–Yeniseian (Edward Vajda, 2008 — Na-Dene + Ket; most widely accepted long-range proposal)
  - **Mechanisms of change**
    - Sound change: regular and exception-free (Neogrammarian principle); conditioned vs. unconditioned; chain shifts (Great English Vowel Shift ~1400–1700); lenition, fortition, assimilation, dissimilation, metathesis, epenthesis, deletion, palatalization, tonogenesis (how tone systems arise — e.g., Vietnamese, Chinese: onset consonant features → tone)
    - Grammaticalization: Lehmann (1982); Hopper and Traugott, Grammaticalization (1993/2003); independent content words → grammatical function words → affixes → zero; unidirectionality hypothesis (mostly accepted; some contested counterexamples); "going to" → "gonna" (future marker)
    - Semantic change: metaphor, metonymy, narrowing, broadening, amelioration, pejoration, bleaching; Traugott and Dasher, Regularity in Semantic Change (2002)
    - Syntactic change: word order change (Latin SOV → Romance SVO); analytic to synthetic and vice versa; parameter change (V2 loss in English)
    - Contact-induced change: borrowing (lexical, phonological, structural); code-switching → mixed languages (Media Lengua, Michif); areal features (Sprachbund — Balkan Sprachbund: shared features across unrelated Bulgarian, Romanian, Albanian, Greek; South Asian Sprachbund)
