  - **Creole and pidgin studies**: pidgin (contact language with no native speakers) → creole (nativised pidgin); Derek Bickerton, Language Bioprogram Hypothesis (1981 — children create creole grammar from pidgin input using innate linguistic capacity; controversial); John McWhorter, The Missing Spanish Creoles (2000); Michel DeGraff (Haitian Creole; critiques colonial attitudes toward creoles); Salikoko Mufwene, The Ecology of Language Evolution (2001); Language Creolization and Language Change (2008)
  - Frontier: phylogenetic methods borrowed from biology applied to language classification (Gray and Atkinson, Nature, 2003 — Bayesian dating of IE tree; ASJP — Automated Similarity Judgment Program; Glottolog phylogenetic tools); aDNA + linguistic correlations (Lazaridis et al.; concordance of Yamnaya expansion and IE spread); computational historical linguistics (cognate detection algorithms — List, 2012; LingPy; CLDF — Cross-Linguistic Data Formats); language birth and language creation (Nicaragua Sign Language as natural experiment; Al-Sayyid Bedouin Sign Language; emerging sign languages)

- **Sociolinguistics**
  - **Definition**: study of the relationship between language and society — variation, identity, power, contact, policy, attitudes
  - **Variationist sociolinguistics**: William Labov (b. 1927) — founder; The Social Stratification of English in New York City (1966, 2nd ed. 2006); "fourth floor" study (Saks, Macy's, S. Klein — /r/ stratification by social class and formality); Martha's Vineyard study (1963 — vowel centralization as identity marker); **the sociolinguistic variable**: linguistic variable correlated with social factors (class, age, gender, ethnicity, style); apparent-time method (age groups as proxy for change in progress); real-time studies (return after decades)
  - **Dialectology**: study of regional language variation; dialect atlas tradition (Georg Wenker, Deutschen Sprachatlas, 1888–; Linguistic Atlas of New England — Kurath, 1939–1943; Survey of English Dialects, Harold Orton and Eugen Dieth, 1962–1971); isoglosses; dialect continua; perceptual dialectology (Dennis Preston — "mental maps" of dialect regions); online projects (Yale Grammatical Diversity Project of American English; Dialektometrie / dialectometry — quantitative comparison)
  - **Language and gender**: Robin Lakoff, Language and Woman's Place (1975 — hedging, tag questions, politeness; pioneering but critiqued for overgeneralization); Deborah Tannen (You Just Don't Understand, 1990 — difference model); Penelope Eckert and Sally McConnell-Ginet (1992, communities of practice approach); gendered variation (vocal fry, uptalk); non-binary language (singular "they"; Mx; Swedish "hen," 2015; gender-neutral innovations across languages)
  - **Language and ethnicity/race**: African American Vernacular English (AAVE) / African American Language (AAL): systematic grammar (habitual "be," remote past "been," copula deletion, negative concord); John Rickford (2006); Geneva Smitherman; William Labov (Language in the Inner City, 1972); Oakland Ebonics controversy (1996); H. Samy Alim, Articulate While Black (2012); linguistic profiling (John Baugh); Latinx English; World Englishes (Braj Kachru, three circles model: Inner, Outer, Expanding, 1985; critiqued but influential)
  - **Code-switching and translanguaging**: switching between languages within conversation or sentence; Carol Myers-Scotton (Matrix Language Frame model, 1993/2002); Ofelia García (translanguaging — fluid bilingual practice; Translanguaging: Language, Bilingualism, and Education, 2014); Li Wei (2018); challenges monolingual bias in linguistics
  - **Language and power**: Pierre Bourdieu, Language and Symbolic Power (1991) — linguistic market, symbolic capital; Norman Fairclough, Critical Discourse Analysis (1989/1995); Teun van Dijk (discourse and racism); Ruth Wodak (Discourse-Historical Approach); language ideologies (Silverstein, 1979; Woolard and Schieffelin, 1994)
  - **Language policy and planning**: status planning (which languages get official/national status); corpus planning (standardisation, orthography); acquisition planning (language in education); endangered language revitalisation (Hebrew revival — most successful case; Irish, Welsh, Māori, Hawaiian revitalisation programs; Joshua Fishman, Reversing Language Shift, 1991; immersion schooling — kura kaupapa Māori); linguistic human rights (Skutnabb-Kangas and Phillipson, 1995)
  - **Language endangerment and death**: ~7,000 languages; ~43% endangered (UNESCO Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger); one language dies every ~2 weeks; factors: colonialism, globalisation, education policy, urbanisation, prestige; language documentation response (ELDP — Endangered Languages Documentation Programme; ELAR; DoBeS — Dokumentation Bedrohter Sprachen); David Crystal, Language Death (2000); K. David Harrison, When Languages Die (2007)
  - Frontier: digital sociolinguistics (social media as corpus — Twitter/X, YouTube; Jack Grieve, computational dialectology; using geotagged tweets to map dialectal features); language and AI bias (word embeddings encode social biases — Bolukbasi et al., 2016; Caliskan et al., Science, 2017); real-time language change tracking; COVID-era linguistic innovation; sociolinguistics of signed languages

- **Language acquisition**
  - **First language acquisition (L1)**
    - Stages: babbling (~6 months); first words (~12 months); two-word stage (~18–24 months); telegraphic speech; inflectional morphology emerges; complex syntax by ~4–5 years; remarkable uniformity across cultures and languages
    - **Nativist/UG approach**: Chomsky — poverty of the stimulus argument (input underdetermines linguistic knowledge; children don't receive enough data to learn grammar by induction alone); Universal Grammar (UG) as innate linguistic endowment; parameter setting (input triggers specification of parameters); language acquisition device (LAD)
    - Evidence for innateness: critical period hypothesis (Eric Lenneberg, Biological Foundations of Language, 1967; Genie case study; Newport — age effects on ASL acquisition; Johnson and Newport, 1989); creolization (children grammaticize pidgins); uniformity of acquisition stages; specific language impairment (SLI — now Developmental Language Disorder, DLD); Williams syndrome (relatively preserved language with cognitive deficits); FOXP2 gene (KE family — Gopnik, 1990; Lai et al., 2001 — implicated in speech and language; not "the language gene" but affects orofacial motor control and grammar); Merge as biological adaptation
    - **Usage-based/emergentist approach**: Michael Tomasello (Constructing a Language, 2003) — language learned from input through general cognitive mechanisms (pattern recognition, analogy, intention-reading, statistical learning); no need for innate UG; construction grammar approach; item-based learning → abstract schemas; Elizabeth Bates (competition model); Joan Bybee; Nick Ellis; statistical learning (Saffran, Aslin, Newport, 1996 — 8-month-olds track transitional probabilities; foundational finding)
    - **Connectionist models**: Rumelhart and McClelland (1986 — past tense learning: overregularization ("goed") modelled by neural network without rules; Pinker and Prince, 1988, critique); modern deep learning and language acquisition modelling
    - Child-directed speech (CDS) / "motherese": higher pitch, exaggerated intonation, simplified vocabulary, shorter utterances; cross-culturally widespread but not universal (Schieffelin and Ochs, 1986 — Kaluli, Samoa); role debated: facilitating vs. necessary?
  - **Second language acquisition (SLA)**
    - Contrastive analysis (Robert Lado, 1957 — L1 interference predicts difficulty); Error Analysis (S. Pit Corder, 1967 — errors as evidence of learning strategies, not just interference); Interlanguage (Larry Selinker, 1972 — learner's developing system is a language in its own right)
    - **Stephen Krashen**: Monitor Model (5 hypotheses: acquisition-learning distinction; monitor; natural order; input — i+1; affective filter); hugely influential in language teaching despite heavy criticism (weak empirical support; unfalsifiable)
    - **Processability Theory**: Manfred Pienemann (1998) — learners can only acquire structures they are developmentally ready to process; implicational hierarchy; predicts acquisition sequences
    - **Sociocultural Theory**: Lev Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development applied to SLA; James Lantolf; scaffolding; language socialization (Ochs and Schieffelin)
    - **Usage-based SLA**: Nick Ellis; frequency effects; constructions as units of acquisition; emergentism
    - Critical period for L2: gradual decline in ultimate attainment with age of onset; no sharp boundary; "age effects" vs. "critical period proper" debate; Hartshorne, Tenenbaum, and Pinker (2018, Cognition — 669,498 participants; critical period for grammar ~17.4 years)
  - **Bilingualism and multilingualism**: types (simultaneous, sequential, heritage); cognitive effects (bilingual advantage debate: Bialystok, 2001 — executive function advantage in bilinguals; challenged by de Bruin, Treccani, and Della Sala, 2015 — publication bias; meta-analyses mixed; ongoing); code-switching (see Sociolinguistics); bilingual first language acquisition (BFLA — Annick De Houwer, 2009); attrition (first language loss in immigrants — Monika Schmid, 2011)
  - Frontier: computational models of acquisition (Yang, 2002 — variational learning; Perfors et al., 2011 — Bayesian; neural network models); neuroimaging of language development (fNIRS in infants); individual differences in L2 aptitude (LLAMA test); heritage language research; LLMs as models of language learning (debate: are LLMs evidence for/against poverty of stimulus? — Warstadt and Bowman, 2022; Wilcox et al., 2023)

- **Neurolinguistics**
  - **Definition**: study of the neural bases of language — how the brain processes, produces, and acquires language
  - Classical model: Paul Broca (1861 — patient "Tan" Leborgne; left inferior frontal gyrus → "Broca's area" → speech production/syntax); Carl Wernicke (1874 — left posterior superior temporal gyrus → "Wernicke's area" → comprehension); Lichtheim (1885 — connectionist model linking Broca's and Wernicke's areas via arcuate fasciculus); classical aphasiology: Broca's aphasia (non-fluent, agrammatic, relatively preserved comprehension); Wernicke's aphasia (fluent but meaningless, impaired comprehension); conduction aphasia (impaired repetition); global aphasia
  - Modern revision: classical model oversimplified; language engages distributed bilateral networks; dual-stream model (Gregory Hickok and David Poeppel, 2004, 2007 — dorsal stream: auditory-motor mapping, articulation, repetition; ventral stream: sound-to-meaning mapping, comprehension); Friederici (2012, 2017 — neurocognitive model of sentence processing; three-phase model)
  - **Neuroimaging methods**: EEG/ERP (event-related potentials: N400 — Kutas and Hillyard, 1980 — semantic anomaly; P600 — syntactic violations; LAN/ELAN — morphosyntactic violations; mismatch negativity — MMN — phonological); fMRI (spatial resolution ~1mm; blood oxygenation level dependent — BOLD; identifies activated brain regions during language tasks); MEG (magnetoencephalography — millisecond temporal resolution); PET; fNIRS (portable, infant-friendly); intracranial electrodes (ECoG — high spatiotemporal resolution; Fedorenko et al., Chang et al.); lesion-deficit studies (classic and modern: voxel-based lesion-symptom mapping — VLSM)
  - **Key findings**
    - Language network: Evelina Fedorenko (MIT) — "language-selective" regions in left frontal and temporal cortex, distinct from domain-general executive function; language network is remarkably selective (responds to language but not music, math, or executive tasks; Fedorenko et al., 2011; Fedorenko and Blank, 2020)
    - Lateralization: language predominantly left-lateralized (~95% of right-handers; ~70% of left-handers; Wada test; fMRI laterality indices); prosody more bilateral; signed languages also left-lateralized → language lateralization is about language not modality
    - Bilingual brain: shared vs. separate representations; proficiency and age of acquisition effects; Abutalebi and Green, 2007 (Adaptive Control Hypothesis); dense code-switching as neural flexibility
    - Reading: visual word form area (VWFA — left fusiform gyrus; Dehaene et al., 2002; Stanislas Dehaene, Reading in the Brain, 2009; "neuronal recycling" hypothesis — VWFA repurposes object recognition circuits for reading)
  - **Aphasia**: see classical types above; modern classification more nuanced (fluent/nonfluent; acute/chronic); primary progressive aphasia (PPA — Mesulam, 1982; three variants: non-fluent/agrammatic, semantic, logopenic); recovery and neuroplasticity; aphasia therapy (constraint-induced language therapy — Pulvermüller et al., 2001; intensive comprehensive aphasia programs)
  - Frontier: intracranial recording during language (Edward Chang, UCSF — neural basis of speech production; high-gamma mapping); brain-computer interfaces for speech (BCI: Willett et al., Nature, 2023 — decoded attempted speech at 62 words/min; Moses et al., NEJM, 2021 — BRAVO neuroprosthesis); computational neurolinguistics (linking neural network models to brain data — Schrimpf et al., 2021 — GPT-2 representations predict neural responses; Caucheteux and King, 2022; debate on what this means); language and prediction (predictive processing framework — Kuperberg and Jaeger, 2016); connectome studies of language circuits (Human Connectome Project); neuroscience of sign language; neurobiology of tone perception

- **Computational linguistics and NLP**
  - **Definition**: computational approaches to language analysis, understanding, and generation; natural language processing (NLP) = applied computational linguistics and AI
  - **History**: machine translation (Georgetown-IBM experiment, 1954; ALPAC report, 1966 — pessimistic; rule-based MT → statistical MT → neural MT); ELIZA (Weizenbaum, 1966 — pattern-matching chatbot); SHRDLU (Winograd, 1972 — natural language understanding in blocks world); ATN parsers (Woods, 1970); Augmented Transition Networks; conceptual dependency (Schank, 1970s)
  - **Statistical and machine learning era (~1990–2012)**: corpus-based methods; Brown Corpus (1961 — first machine-readable corpus, 1 million words; Francis and Kučera); Penn Treebank (Marcus, Marcinkiewicz, and Santorini, 1993 — syntactically annotated; foundational); hidden Markov models (HMMs — speech recognition, POS tagging); statistical machine translation (Brown et al., IBM, 1990 — "the mathematics of statistical machine translation"; Koehn, Och, Marcu — phrase-based SMT); maximum entropy models; conditional random fields (CRFs — Lafferty, McCallum, Pereira, 2001); SVM and other classifiers; feature engineering
  - **Deep learning era (~2012–present)**
    - Word embeddings: word2vec (Mikolov, Chen, Corrado, Dean, 2013 — skip-gram, CBOW; distributional semantics; king − man + woman = queen); GloVe (Pennington, Socher, Manning, 2014)
    - Recurrent neural networks and LSTMs: sequence modelling; Sutskever, Vinyals, Le (2014 — seq2seq); attention mechanism (Bahdanau, Cho, Bengio, 2015)
    - **Transformer architecture**: Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need" (NeurIPS, 2017) — self-attention mechanism; parallelizable; replaced RNNs as dominant architecture; foundational for everything that followed
    - **BERT** (Devlin et al., Google, 2018): bidirectional encoder representations from transformers; masked language model pre-training; dominated NLP benchmarks 2019–2020; transfer learning paradigm (pre-train on massive data, fine-tune on tasks)
    - **GPT series** (OpenAI): GPT-1 (2018, Radford et al.); GPT-2 (2019 — 1.5B parameters; initially withheld due to misuse concerns); GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020 — 175B parameters; few-shot learning); GPT-4 (2023, multimodal); in-context learning; chain-of-thought prompting; instruction tuning; RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — Ouyang et al., 2022)
    - Other LLMs: PaLM (Google, 2022); LLaMA (Meta, 2023); Claude (Anthropic); Gemini (Google DeepMind, 2024); Mistral; open-source movement; scaling laws (Kaplan et al., 2020; Chinchilla — Hoffmann et al., 2022)
    - Neural machine translation: Google Neural Machine Translation (GNMT, Wu et al., 2016); NLLB (No Language Left Behind, Meta, 2022 — 200 languages); quality approaching human for high-resource pairs; low-resource challenges persist
  - **Core NLP tasks**: tokenization, sentence segmentation; part-of-speech tagging; named entity recognition (NER); syntactic parsing (constituency and dependency); semantic role labeling; coreference resolution; sentiment analysis; question answering; text summarization (extractive, abstractive); information extraction; text classification; relation extraction; machine reading comprehension; dialogue systems
  - **Speech technology**: automatic speech recognition (ASR — HMMs → hybrid DNN-HMM → end-to-end: CTC, attention-based; Whisper, OpenAI, 2022; conformer architecture); text-to-speech (TTS — concatenative → parametric → neural: WaveNet, DeepMind, 2016; Tacotron; VALL-E, Microsoft, 2023 — 3s voice cloning); speaker identification/verification
  - **Evaluation and benchmarks**: BLEU (machine translation — Papineni et al., 2002); ROUGE (summarization); GLUE/SuperGLUE (general language understanding — Wang et al., 2018/2019); SQuAD (reading comprehension); WMT shared tasks; BIG-Bench; MMLU; human evaluation challenges
  - Frontier: multimodal models (vision + language — GPT-4V; LLaVA); retrieval-augmented generation (RAG); AI agents (tool use, planning); alignment and safety research (RLHF, constitutional AI, scalable oversight); LLMs and linguistic theory (do LLMs learn syntax? — Warstadt et al., BLiMP benchmark, 2020; Hewitt and Manning, structural probe, 2019; syntactic generalization tests); LLMs for endangered language documentation; interpretability of language models (probing classifiers; mechanistic interpretability; Elhage et al., 2022); energy cost and environmental impact of training

- **Typology and universals**
  - **Definition**: cross-linguistic comparison to discover what is universal about language and what varies, and the limits on variation
  - **Joseph Greenberg (1915–2001)**: "Some Universals of Grammar with Particular Reference to the Order of Meaningful Elements" (1963 — 45 universals; "Universal 1: In declarative sentences with nominal subject and object, the dominant order is almost always one in which the subject precedes the object"; correlations among word order features); founded modern typological research; also morphological typology; number systems; mass comparison (controversial for classification but productive for typology)
  - **Types of universals**: absolute universals (no exceptions: "all languages have vowels") vs. statistical universals/tendencies; implicational universals ("if language has X, then it also has Y" — e.g., "if a language has fricatives, it has stops"; vowel inventory hierarchies); unrestricted vs. implicational
  - **Word order typology**: SOV (~41%) vs. SVO (~35%) vs. VSO (~9%) vs. VOS, OVS, OSV; Dryer's large-sample studies (WALS — World Atlas of Language Structures, Haspelmath, Dryer, Gil, and Comrie, eds., 2005; online 2013+); correlations: prepositions/postpositions, adjective order, genitive order, relative clause position; head-directionality parameter
  - **Morphological typology**: analytic-synthetic continuum; agglutinative-fusional; polysynthesis; Greenberg's index of synthesis; quantitative typology
  - **Alignment typology**: nominative-accusative vs. ergative-absolutive vs. active-stative (split-S) vs. tripartite; split ergativity (conditioned by tense, aspect, person — Dixon, 1994; split-S in Georgian, Dyirbal); grammatical relations across languages
  - **Semantic typology**: Berlin and Kay, Basic Color Terms (1969 — claimed universal hierarchy of color term development; critiqued for Anglocentrism but influential; World Color Survey — Kay and Maffi, 2009; 110 languages); kinship terminology (Kroeber, Murdock); spatial reference frames (absolute, intrinsic, relative — Stephen Levinson, Space in Language and Cognition, 2003; Tzeltal absolute frame); body part terminology; numeral systems (Comrie, 2013); cutting and breaking events (Majid et al.)
  - **Key resources**: WALS (World Atlas of Language Structures Online — 2,662 languages, 192 features); Autotyp (Zürich); Grambank (Max Planck, 2023 — largest grammatical features database, 2,467 languages, 195 features; Skirgård et al., Science Advances, 2023); CLICS (Database of Cross-Linguistic Colexifications — colexification patterns across 3,156 languages); Leipzig Glossing Rules
  - Major typologists: Greenberg; Bernard Comrie (Language Universals and Linguistic Typology, 1981/1989); Matthew Dryer; Martin Haspelmath; William Croft (Typology and Universals, 2nd ed., 2003); R.M.W. Dixon (Basic Linguistic Theory, 3 vols., 2010–2012); Balthasar Bickel; Johanna Nichols (Linguistic Diversity in Space and Time, 1992)
  - Frontier: phylogenetic typology (correlating typological features with language phylogenies; Dunn et al., Nature, 2011; Grambank analysis — Skirgård et al., 2023); areal typology; causal explanation of universals (functional, cognitive, processing-based — Hawkins, Processing Typology, 2004; efficiency in communication — Gibson et al., PNAS, 2019); large-scale quantitative typology using databases; computational typology; typological features of sign languages

- **Writing systems**
  - **Types**: logographic (Chinese characters — each character represents a morpheme/word; ~50,000+ characters; ~3,000–4,000 for literacy; simplified vs. traditional; Japanese uses kanji + kana); syllabic (Japanese kana — hiragana, katakana; Cherokee syllabary — invented by Sequoyah, ~1821; Vai syllabary — Liberia, ~1833); alphabetic (Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic, Hebrew, Georgian, Armenian, Hangul, etc.); abugida/alphasyllabary (Devanagari, Ge'ez/Ethiopic, Thai — consonant base with vowel modification); abjad (Arabic, Hebrew — consonants written, vowels partially or optionally marked)
  - **History of writing**: proto-writing (Jiahu symbols ~6600 BCE; Vinča symbols ~5500 BCE; debate over whether they are true writing); **cuneiform** (~3400–3200 BCE, Uruk — earliest definitively writing; evolved from accounting tokens → pictographs → abstract wedge-shaped signs; Sumerian, Akkadian, Hittite, Elamite, Old Persian; deciphered: Henry Rawlinson, Edward Hincks, 1850s — Behistun inscription); **Egyptian hieroglyphs** (~3200 BCE; logographic/consonantal; hieratic and demotic cursive scripts; deciphered: Jean-François Champollion, 1822, Rosetta Stone; recently: Ilona Regulski, Nature, 2023 — earliest royal inscriptions database); **Chinese** (oracle bones ~1200 BCE, earliest; but Erlitou pottery marks debated); **Indus script** (~2600–1900 BCE — undeciphered; ~400 signs; too few texts too short for decipherment? Rao et al., Science, 2009 — statistical argument for linguistic content; Farmer, Sproat, Witzel, 2004 — argue non-linguistic; ongoing debate)
  - **Alphabet origins**: proto-Sinaitic script (~1800 BCE, Egyptian turquoise mines; acrophonic principle — each sign's value = first sound of the word the sign depicted; 'aleph = ox head → /ʔ/; bet = house → /b/); Phoenician alphabet (~1050 BCE — 22 consonantal letters; abjad; spread by trade); Greek alphabet (~800 BCE — added vowels, first true alphabet; adapted from Phoenician — alpha, beta, gamma from 'aleph, bet, gimel); Etruscan → Latin alphabet; Aramaic → Hebrew, Arabic, Syriac, South Asian scripts (Brahmi → Devanagari, Tamil, Thai, Tibetan, etc.)
  - **Korean Hangul**: invented by King Sejong the Great (1443, promulgated 1446 — Hunminjeongeum); designed to be scientifically rational; consonant shapes mirror articulatory place/manner; vowel shapes encode yin-yang cosmology; featural alphabet; celebrated as "perhaps the most scientific writing system in general use" (Geoffrey Sampson); UNESCO King Sejong Literacy Prize
  - Orthographic depth: shallow/transparent orthographies (Finnish, Spanish, Italian — close grapheme-phoneme correspondence) vs. deep/opaque (English, French — many irregularities; historical reasons: Great Vowel Shift, Norman French influence, printing standardized earlier spellings); affects reading acquisition rate
  - Peter Daniels and William Bright, eds., The World's Writing Systems (1996 — definitive reference); Florian Coulmas, Writing Systems: An Introduction to Their Linguistic Analysis (2003); Andrew Robinson, The Story of Writing (1995/2007)
  - Frontier: undeciphered scripts (Indus; Linear A — Minoan, ~1800–1450 BCE; Proto-Elamite ~3100 BCE; Rongorongo, Easter Island; Cypro-Minoan); computational decipherment (Luo et al., 2019 — neural models for lost languages; Jiaming Luo and Regina Barzilay, MIT; Google DeepMind collaboration); endangered writing systems; digital encoding (Unicode Consortium — ~150,000 characters; Script Encoding Initiative, UC Berkeley; emoji as quasi-writing)

- **Sign languages**
  - **Definition**: natural human languages expressed through the visual-gestural modality (hand shape, movement, location, palm orientation, non-manual markers — facial expression, mouthing, head/body movement); NOT manual codes for spoken languages
  - Linguistic status: sign languages are full natural languages with phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, pragmatics; demonstrated by William Stokoe (Sign Language Structure, 1960 — established ASL as a genuine language; identified cheremes, later "parameters" — handshape, location, movement; analogous to phonemes); Stokoe faced ridicule from Gallaudet colleagues initially; vindicating research followed
  - **American Sign Language (ASL)**: most-studied sign language; ~500,000 L1 users in US/Canada; derived from French Sign Language (LSF) via Laurent Clerc (deaf educator from Paris) and Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet (founded first permanent school for the deaf in US, Hartford, 1817); not mutually intelligible with British Sign Language (BSL — different language family)
  - Major sign languages: BSL; LSF; Deutsche Gebärdensprache (DGS); Langue des Signes Québécoise (LSQ); Japanese Sign Language (JSL/Nihon Shuwa); Chinese Sign Language; Auslan (Australian); hundreds of village/community sign languages (Al-Sayyid Bedouin Sign Language, Kata Kolok — Bali)
  - **Phonology of sign**: Stokoe's parameters → expanded (Battison, 1978; Brentari, 1998 — Prosodic Model); movement and hold models; phonological processes (assimilation, metathesis in sign); syllable structure in sign
  - **Morphology and syntax**: rich spatial grammar; verb agreement (indicating verbs modify movement path to agree with subject/object locations); classifiers (handshape classifiers representing objects, actions — handling, surface, extent); role shifting (body shift to represent different discourse participants — direct quotation); topic-comment structure; OSV common in many sign languages; wh-questions with non-manual markers (brow furrow); negation (headshake simultaneous with manual signs — simultaneous morphology; contrast with linear/sequential morphology in spoken languages)
  - **Emerging sign languages**: Nicaraguan Sign Language (Idioma de Signos Nicaragüense — ISN; emerged ~1980s when deaf children brought together in schools with no sign instruction; spontaneously created a language; Ann Senghas, Marie Coppola: documented creolization-like process in real time; evidence for innate language capacity; Senghas, Kita, and Özyürek, Science, 2004 — spatial segmentation); Al-Sayyid Bedouin Sign Language (ABSL — emerged ~1930s in Negev village with high hereditary deafness; Sandler et al., PNAS, 2005 — SOV order emerging without external model)
  - Deaf culture and Deaf history: uppercase "Deaf" = cultural identity (Padden and Humphries, Deaf in America: Voices from a Culture, 1988); oralism vs. manualism debate (Milan Conference, 1880 — voted to ban sign languages in deaf education; devastating for deaf communities; lasted ~100 years; now recognized as wrong); Gallaudet University (founded 1864, Washington DC — only university in world designed for deaf and hard-of-hearing); cochlear implant debate (deaf community perspectives — not universally welcomed; identity vs. medical model of deafness)
  - Key figures: William Stokoe; Ursula Bellugi (1931–2022 — neurolinguistics of sign; The Signs of Language, with Klima, 1979); Diane Lillo-Martin (syntax of ASL); Karen Emmorey (Language, Cognition, and the Brain: Insights from Sign Language Research, 2002); Mark Aronoff (morphology of ABSL); Wendy Sandler (Sign Language and Linguistic Universals, with Lillo-Martin, 2006)
  - Frontier: sign language AI (automatic sign language recognition — computer vision + NLP; SignAll; Google's Sign Language research; challenges: continuous sign recognition vs. isolated sign; non-manual markers essential but hard to capture); sign language corpora (Auslan Corpus; BSL Corpus; NGT Corpus — Crasborn, 2008); cross-linguistic sign language typology (Zeshan, 2004, 2006 — interrogatives, negation across sign languages); brain imaging of sign language (left-lateralized, like spoken language — strong evidence against modality-dependence of language lateralization; Emmorey et al., 2014); machine translation between sign and spoken languages (still very difficult)

- **Language documentation and field linguistics**
  - **Definition**: systematic recording, annotation, and archiving of languages, especially endangered ones; fieldwork: going to speech communities, eliciting data, recording naturalistic speech
  - Methods: wordlists/questionnaires (Swadesh list — Morris Swadesh, 1950s, 100/200 basic vocabulary items for comparison; SIL questionnaires); grammatical elicitation; recordings (audio, video → transcription → annotation → archiving); community collaboration (increasingly: community-driven documentation; Participatory Action Research; ELAR — Endangered Languages Archive, SOAS; PARADISEC — Pacific and Regional Archive for Digital Sources in Endangered Cultures)
  - **Nikolaus Himmelmann**: "Documentary and Descriptive Linguistics" (Linguistics, 1998) — distinguished documentation (creation of a lasting, multipurpose record) from description (grammatical analysis); foundational for modern language documentation
  - Ethical frameworks: informed consent; community ownership of data; benefit to communities; Open Language Archives Community (OLAC); FAIR data principles; CARE principles for indigenous data governance (Collective benefit, Authority to control, Responsibility, Ethics — Carroll et al., 2020)
  - Major documentation programs: ELDP (Endangered Languages Documentation Programme, SOAS, Hans Rausing — funded ~400+ projects); DoBeS (Dokumentation Bedrohter Sprachen, Volkswagen Foundation); NSF DEL (Documenting Endangered Languages, NSF/NEH); RNLD (Research Network on Linguistic Diversity)
  - Technology: ELAN (annotation tool, Max Planck — time-aligned transcription of audio/video; ubiquitous in fieldwork); FLEx (FieldWorks Language Explorer, SIL — lexicon + interlinear text); SayMore (recording management); language apps for communities (First Voices — Canadian indigenous languages; Aikuma — mobile recording; Living Tongues Institute)
  - Frontier: AI-assisted documentation (automatic transcription of under-resourced languages — Elpis, Persephone; wav2vec fine-tuned on small datasets; challenges: minimal training data, tonal languages; Michaud et al., 2018); computational field linguistics; community-based archiving; revitalization through digital tools (language apps, social media in endangered languages — Facebook/Meta supports Quechua, Guaraní); machine learning for morphological analysis of polysynthetic languages

- **Cognitive linguistics**
  - **Definition**: approach viewing language as integral part of general cognition — not a separate module; meaning central; language reflects and shapes conceptual structure
  - Foundational rejection of Chomskyan autonomy thesis: language not independent of cognition; grammar emerges from usage; no sharp syntax-semantics division; no separate "language faculty"
  - **Key figures and ideas**:
    - George Lakoff: Metaphors We Live By (1980, with Johnson); Women, Fire, and Dangerous Things (1987 — prototype theory, idealized cognitive models, radial categories); conceptual metaphor theory; embodied cognition → embodied language
    - Ronald Langacker: Cognitive Grammar (Foundations, 1987; 2 vols., 1987/1991; Cognitive Grammar: A Basic Introduction, 2008) — language as symbolization: form-meaning pairings; schemas, prototypes, construal operations (profiling, trajector-landmark, scope, perspective); usage-based model
    - Leonard Talmy: force dynamics, fictive motion, cognitive semantics of motion and space (Toward a Cognitive Semantics, 2 vols., 2000)
    - Adele Goldberg: Constructions: A Construction Grammar Approach to Argument Structure (1995); constructions as form-meaning pairings at all levels; usage-based; central constructional polysemy
    - William Croft: Radical Construction Grammar (2001); typological and cross-linguistic construction grammar
    - Gilles Fauconnier and Mark Turner: conceptual blending (The Way We Think, 2002 — mental space integration)
  - Embodied cognition: language grounded in bodily experience; Bergen, Louder Than Words (2012); Barsalou (perceptual symbol systems); motor simulation during language comprehension (Glenberg and Kaschak, 2002 — action-sentence compatibility; Pulvermüller, 2005 — somatotopic motor cortex activation for action verbs); debated: how deep is embodiment?
  - Construction Grammar family (broadly cognitive-functional): Berkeley Construction Grammar (Fillmore, Kay); Cognitive Construction Grammar (Goldberg); Radical Construction Grammar (Croft); Usage-Based Construction Grammar (Tomasello); Sign-Based Construction Grammar (Sag); Fluid Construction Grammar (Steels)
  - Frontier: quantitative cognitive linguistics (corpus methods + experimental methods; Stefan Gries; Dirk Geeraerts); cognitive sociolinguistics; cognitive linguistics and AI (LLMs as distributional learners — resonance with usage-based theory, but disembodied; can distributional representations capture meaning? — continued debate)

- **Frontier: the state of linguistics 2020–present**
  - LLMs and linguistic theory: do transformer models learn linguistic structure? Syntactic evaluations (BLiMP — Warstadt et al., 2020; SyntaxGym); "BabyLM" shared task (Warstadt et al., 2023 — train models on child-scale data; how much data is enough?); arguments for (distributional information may suffice) and against (no grounding, no social interaction, no embodiment) UG/poverty of stimulus
  - Language diversity and inclusion: linguistics traditionally biased toward European languages; ~10% of world's languages well-documented; Grambank and WALS expanding coverage; calls for inclusive linguistics (Charity Hudley et al., 2020); linguistic justice; dialect discrimination and "standard language ideology"
  - Computational linguistic typology: estimating universals from large databases; Grambank-enabled studies (language structure correlates with demographic, geographic, ecological variables — Skirgård et al., Science Advances, 2023); automated language comparison
  - Language and climate change: languages of communities displaced by climate; linguistic diversity correlated with biodiversity ("biocultural diversity" — Gorenflo et al., PNAS, 2012); biolinguistic diversity loss intertwined
  - Neuroscience-linguistics convergence: predictive processing in language (Kuperberg, Jaeger, Levy); neural oscillations and language (Giraud and Poeppel, 2012 — theta-gamma coupling); brain-computer interfaces (see Neurolinguistics)
  - Language technology for all: NLP for low-resource languages (Masakhane NLP — grassroots African NLP community, Adelani et al., 2021; AmericasNLP; Lacuna Fund); Whisper ASR multi-language; machine translation for more languages; digital divide remains
  - Key handbooks: Mark Aronoff and Janie Rees-Miller, eds., The Handbook of Linguistics (2nd ed., 2017); Bernd Heine and Heiko Narrog, eds., The Oxford Handbook of Linguistic Analysis (2nd ed., 2015); Song, ed., The Oxford Handbook of Linguistic Typology (2011); Cambridge Handbooks series; Oxford Handbooks series; Annual Review of Linguistics (2015–)

#### Literature — Full Canonical Survey

- **Scope and definition**
  - Literature as aesthetic verbal art — distinct from utilitarian or purely informational writing; boundaries contested (scriptures? journalism? oral performance? graphic novels? rap lyrics?)
  - Canon formation: who decides what "counts"? Harold Bloom, The Western Canon (1994) — 26 authors as "center"; critiqued for Eurocentrism, gender bias; counter-canon movements (women's literature, postcolonial literature, queer literature); "opening the canon" since ~1980s
  - Relationship to orality: most world literatures begin oral — Homer, Vedas, Epic of Gilgamesh, griots; Walter Ong, Orality and Literacy (1982); oral-formulaic theory (Milman Parry and Albert Lord, The Singer of Tales, 1960)
  - Periodization always contested and Eurocentric when applied globally; survey below moves roughly chronologically within traditions, then addresses comparative and theoretical frameworks

- **Ancient literatures**
  - **Mesopotamian literature**
    - Epic of Gilgamesh (~2100 BCE earliest Sumerian poems; Standard Babylonian version ~1200 BCE, Sin-leqi-unninni): earliest great literary work; themes of friendship, mortality, quest for immortality; flood narrative (Tablet XI — parallels Genesis); Andrew George, critical edition (2003, Penguin)
    - Enuma Elish (Babylonian creation epic); Atra-Hasis (flood); Descent of Inanna/Ishtar; Sumerian love poetry (Inanna and Dumuzi); Ludlul bēl nēmeqi ("Babylonian Job"); lament literature
  - **Egyptian literature**
    - Story of Sinuhe (~1875 BCE, Middle Kingdom — masterpiece of narrative; exile and return); Tale of the Shipwrecked Sailor; Eloquent Peasant; Wisdom of Ptahhotep (~2400 BCE); love poetry (New Kingdom); funerary literature (Pyramid Texts, Coffin Texts, Book of the Dead)
    - Richard Parkinson, The Tale of Sinuhe and Other Ancient Egyptian Poems (2009, Oxford World's Classics)
  - **Hebrew Bible / Old Testament as literature**
    - Genesis (creation narratives, patriarchal narratives); Exodus (liberation narrative); poetry: Psalms (150 poems — lament, praise, wisdom, royal; attributed to David); Song of Songs / Song of Solomon (erotic poetry; allegorical vs. literal reading debate); Ecclesiastes/Qoheleth ("Vanity of vanities" — existential wisdom); Job (theodicy — dramatic dialogue; one of world literature's greatest achievements); Prophetic literature (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel — rhetorical power)
    - Robert Alter, The Art of Biblical Narrative (1981) and The Art of Biblical Poetry (1985) — literary-critical approach; Alter's solo translation of the Hebrew Bible (2018)
  - **Ancient Indian literature**
    - **Vedas** (~1500–500 BCE): Rigveda (1,028 hymns — oldest Indian literary text; invocations, cosmogony: "Nasadiya Sukta" — Creation Hymn, Rig Veda 10.129 — "Who really knows? Who will here proclaim it?"); Samaveda, Yajurveda, Atharvaveda
    - **Sanskrit epics**: Mahabharata (attributed to Vyasa; ~100,000 verses — longest epic poem in any language; Kurukshetra war, Pandavas vs. Kauravas; contains Bhagavad Gita — philosophical dialogue between Arjuna and Krishna; dharma, duty, action, devotion); Ramayana (attributed to Valmiki; ~24,000 verses; Rama's quest to rescue Sita; dharma, ideal kingship; enormously influential across Southeast Asia — Thai Ramakien, Javanese Kakawin Ramayana, Cambodian Reamker)
    - Sanskrit court poetry (kavya): Kalidasa (~4th–5th century CE) — greatest Sanskrit poet; Shakuntala (drama — Goethe: "If you want to name one work to compass the bloom of early days and the fruit of later days… I name thee, O Sakuntala!"); Meghaduta (The Cloud Messenger — lyric); Kumarasambhava; Raghuvamsha; Bhartrhari (love, renunciation, worldly wisdom — Shatakatrayam)
    - Tamil Sangam literature (~300 BCE–300 CE): akam (interior/love) and puram (exterior/war/heroism) poetry; anthologies (Ettuttokai, Pattuppattu); Tolkappiyam (grammar and poetics); Tirukkural (Tiruvalluvar — ethical aphorisms, ~5th century CE; "Tamil Veda")
    - Pali literature: Jataka tales (Buddhist birth stories); Therigatha (Verses of the Elder Nuns, ~6th century BCE — earliest known collection of women's spiritual literature)
  - **Ancient Chinese literature**
    - Five Classics: Shijing (Book of Songs/Odes, ~11th–7th century BCE — 305 poems; folk songs, court hymns, ritual odes; Confucius reportedly edited; foundational; "Poetry can be used to stimulate the mind, to observe society, to cultivate sociability, to express grievances"); Shujing (Book of Documents); Yijing (Book of Changes); Liji (Book of Rites); Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals)
    - Chuci (Songs of the South): Qu Yuan (~340–278 BCE) — "Li Sao" (Encountering Sorrow; China's first named poet; patriotic exile); shamanistic imagery; David Hawkes translation (1959/1985)
    - Historical prose: Sima Qian, Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian — also masterpiece of narrative prose); Zuozhuan (commentary on Spring and Autumn Annals — vivid battle and diplomatic narratives)
    - Han fu (rhapsody/prose-poem): Sima Xiangru, Jia Yi; elaborate descriptions
  - **Ancient Greek literature**
    - **Homer** (fl. ~8th century BCE?): Iliad (24 books; Trojan War; wrath of Achilles; mortality, honour, war; "Homeric Question" — single author or oral tradition? Milman Parry demonstrated oral-formulaic composition, 1930s; Albert Lord, The Singer of Tales, 1960); Odyssey (24 books; Odysseus's return; adventure, cunning, homecoming, recognition)
    - **Hesiod** (fl. ~700 BCE): Theogony (divine genealogy, creation); Works and Days (farming, justice, myth of the ages)
    - **Lyric poetry**: Sappho (~630–570 BCE, Lesbos — Fragment 31 "He seems to me equal to the gods"; nine books in antiquity, mostly lost; fragments; "the Tenth Muse" — Plato; recent papyrus discoveries: "Brothers Poem" and "Kypris Poem," 2014, Dirk Obbink); Pindar (victory odes — elaborate, mythological); Alcaeus, Anacreon, Archilochus (iambic invective), Simonides, Bacchylides
    - **Tragedy**: Aeschylus (~525–456 BCE — Oresteia: Agamemnon, Libation Bearers, Eumenides; Prometheus Bound; introduced second actor; cosmic justice); Sophocles (~496–406 BCE — Oedipus Rex, Antigone, Electra, Ajax, Philoctetes; tragic irony; Aristotle called Oedipus ideal tragedy; introduced third actor); Euripides (~480–406 BCE — Medea, Bacchae, Trojan Women, Hippolytus, Alcestis; psychological realism; innovative; ~18 surviving plays out of ~90; "most tragic of poets" — Aristotle)
    - **Comedy**: Aristophanes (~446–386 BCE — Old Comedy: Clouds, Birds, Lysistrata, Frogs, Wasps; political satire, obscenity, fantastical plots); Menander (~342–290 BCE — New Comedy: Dyscolos; social comedy; influenced Roman comedy)
    - **Historiography as literature**: Herodotus, Thucydides (see Historiography section); Xenophon (Anabasis)
    - **Philosophy as prose art**: Plato's dialogues — among greatest Greek prose (Republic, Symposium, Phaedrus, Apology — dramatic, literary, philosophical); Aristotle's Poetics (~335 BCE — foundational literary theory; tragedy defined: "imitation of an action that is serious, complete, and of a certain magnitude"; catharsis — purgation/purification of pity and fear; plot as "soul" of tragedy; hamartia, peripeteia, anagnorisis; incomplete — lost book on comedy?)
    - **Hellenistic literature**: Callimachus (erudite, witty — Aetia, Hymns; "a big book is a big evil"); Theocritus (Idylls — pastoral poetry, invented the genre); Apollonius of Rhodes (Argonautica — Hellenistic epic; Jason and Medea); epigram tradition (Greek Anthology, compiled ~10th century CE from earlier collections; ~4,000 poems; Meleager, Leonidas, Asclepiades)
    - **Greek novel**: Longus, Daphnis and Chloe (~2nd century CE); Heliodorus, Aethiopica; Achilles Tatius; genre prefigures modern romance/adventure novel
  - **Roman/Latin literature**
    - **Comedy**: Plautus (~254–184 BCE — The Brothers Menaechmi, Pseudolus; boisterous, farcical; influenced Shakespeare's Comedy of Errors); Terence (~185–159 BCE — six plays; "I am human, and nothing human is alien to me" — Heauton Timorumenos; refined, humanistic)
    - **Late Republic prose and poetry**: Cicero (106–43 BCE — orations, philosophical works, letters; master of Latin prose style; model for Renaissance humanists; De Oratore; De Republica); Lucretius, De Rerum Natura (~55 BCE — Epicurean philosophy in hexameter verse; atoms, void, mortality; rediscovered 1417 by Poggio Bracciolini — Stephen Greenblatt, The Swerve, 2011); Catullus (~84–54 BCE — passionate lyric; "Odi et amo" — I hate and I love; love poems to "Lesbia")
    - **Augustan Age (Golden Age of Latin literature)**: Virgil (70–19 BCE — Eclogues, Georgics; Aeneid: Rome's national epic, 12 books; Aeneas from Troy to Italy; "Arms and the man I sing"; pietas, fate, empire; rival to Homer; "a classic is a book that never finishes what it has to say" — Calvino, channeling Virgil's endlessness); Horace (65–8 BCE — Odes, Satires, Epistles, Ars Poetica; "carpe diem"; "dulce et decorum est pro patria mori"; lyric master; "I have raised a monument more lasting than bronze"); Ovid (43 BCE–17/18 CE — Metamorphoses: 15 books, ~250 myths woven into continuous narrative; Amores, Ars Amatoria; exiled by Augustus; immeasurably influential on Western art and literature); Propertius, Tibullus (elegists); Livy (Ab Urbe Condita — monumental history)
    - **Silver Age**: Seneca (tragedies — Medea, Thyestes, Phaedra; Stoic philosophy; advisor to Nero); Petronius, Satyricon (~60s CE — comic novel, Trimalchio's feast; picaresque); Lucan, Pharsalia (epic on civil war; anti-Virgilian); Martial (epigrams — wit, obscenity, social observation); Juvenal (Satires — "bread and circuses"; savage indignation); Tacitus (see Historiography); Pliny the Younger (Letters — including account of Vesuvius eruption, 79 CE); Apuleius, The Golden Ass (Metamorphoses, ~2nd century CE — only Latin novel to survive complete; Cupid and Psyche inset tale; Isis mysteries)
    - **Late Antiquity**: Augustine, Confessions (~397–400 CE — spiritual autobiography; foundational for Western autobiography and introspection; "Late have I loved you"); Boethius, Consolation of Philosophy (~524 CE — prosimetrum, prison, Fortuna's wheel; most widely read secular book in medieval Europe)
  - **Classical Arabic literature**
    - Pre-Islamic poetry (Jahiliyyah): Mu'allaqat ("Suspended Odes," 7 celebrated pre-Islamic qasidas — Imru' al-Qays, Antarah ibn Shaddad; desert imagery, boasting, elegiac); qasida form (nasib — erotic prelude; journey; panegyric/boast)
    - Quran (~610–632 CE): Arabic literary masterpiece regardless of theological status; inimitable style (i'jaz); saj' (rhymed prose); influenced all subsequent Arabic literature, linguistics, law
    - Abbasid-era prose: al-Jahiz (~776–868/869, Kitab al-Hayawan, Kitab al-Bukhala' — natural history and literary entertainment); Maqamat (picaresque sessions — al-Hamadhani, ~968; al-Hariri, ~1100; rhymed prose; virtuosic wordplay)
    - One Thousand and One Nights (Alf Layla wa-Layla): compiled over centuries (Indian, Persian, Arabic sources; earliest fragment 9th century; Scheherazade frame narrative; Aladdin and Ali Baba added by Antoine Galland in European translation, 1704–1717; Robert Irwin, The Arabian Nights: A Companion, 1994)
    - Arabic poetry: Abu Nuwas (~756–814, wine poetry, khamriyyat; homoerotic verse); al-Mutanabbi (915–965 — "greatest Arab poet"; panegyric master; "I am the one whose literature can be seen by the blind"); Abu al-Ala al-Ma'arri (973–1057 — blind, sceptical, proto-existentialist; Luzumiyyat); Andalusian poetry (Ibn Hazm, The Ring of the Dove, ~1022 — treatise on love)
  - **Persian literature**
    - Ferdowsi, Shahnameh (Book of Kings, ~1010 CE — ~50,000 couplets; Iranian national epic; mythological and historical kings; Rostam and Sohrab; preserved Persian language and identity after Arab conquest)
    - Sufi poetry: Rumi / Jalal ad-Din Muhammad Balkhi (1207–1273 — Masnavi ("spiritual couplets," ~25,000 verses; "Ocean of Meaning"); Divan-i Shams-i Tabrizi; best-selling poet in America, though translations (Coleman Barks) heavily adapted/criticized for de-Islamization); Hafiz (~1315–1390 — ghazals; Divan; "Tongue of the Hidden"; Goethe's inspiration for West-östlicher Divan); Sa'di (~1210–1291/92 — Bustan, Gulistan; "All human beings are members of one body" — inscribed at UN entrance); Omar Khayyam, Rubaiyat (quatrains; Edward FitzGerald translation, 1859 — Victorian bestseller; very free adaptation)
    - Attar, Conference of the Birds (~1177 — Sufi allegory; birds seek the Simorgh); Nizami, Khamsa (Five Poems — Layla and Majnun; a Persian Romeo and Juliet)
  - **Japanese literature**
    - Man'yoshu (~759 CE — "Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves"; oldest Japanese poetry anthology; ~4,500 poems; all social classes represented; Kakinomoto no Hitomaro; tanka form — 5-7-5-7-7 syllables)
    - Heian period (~794–1185): Kokinwakashu (905 CE, imperially commissioned; Ki no Tsurayuki, preface — foundational poetics); **Murasaki Shikibu, The Tale of Genji (Genji Monogatari, ~1000–1012 CE)**: often called the world's first novel; psychological depth; court life; aesthetics of mono no aware (pathos of things); Sei Shōnagon, The Pillow Book (~1002 — zuihitsu/essay form; witty, observant); women dominated Heian prose literature (wrote in kana, while men wrote official texts in Chinese)
    - Noh drama (Zeami Motokiyo, ~1363–1443 — codified Noh aesthetics; Fūshikaden; yugen — profound grace/mystery; masks; slow, ritualistic)
    - Haiku: Matsuo Bashō (1644–1694 — greatest haiku master; "Furu ike ya / kawazu tobikomu / mizu no oto" — "Old pond / a frog jumps in / the sound of water"; Narrow Road to the Deep North/Oku no Hosomichi, 1702; travel literature + haiku); Yosa Buson; Kobayashi Issa; haiku form: 5-7-5 morae (not strictly "syllables"); kigo (seasonal reference); kireji (cutting word)
    - Edo prose: Ihara Saikaku (The Life of an Amorous Man, 1682; ukiyo-zōshi — "floating world" fiction); Chikamatsu Monzaemon (~1653–1725 — puppet/bunraku theater; The Love Suicides at Sonezaki; "Japanese Shakespeare")
  - **Medieval European literature**
    - **Old English**: Beowulf (~8th–11th century, single manuscript Cotton Vitellius A.xv; heroic epic; Grendel, Grendel's mother, dragon; alliterative verse; Tolkien, "Beowulf: The Monsters and the Critics," 1936 — transformative critical essay; Seamus Heaney translation, 1999, bestseller); The Wanderer, The Seafarer (elegiac poems); Caedmon's Hymn (earliest attested English poem)
    - **Old Norse / Icelandic**: Eddas: Prose Edda (Snorri Sturluson, ~1220 — Norse mythology manual; Gylfaginning); Poetic Edda (anonymous — Völuspá, Hávamál); Icelandic sagas (Njáls saga, Egils saga, Laxdæla saga — 13th century prose narratives; realistic, laconic, psychological; "prose Homer of the North"; Jesse Byock, Viking Age Iceland, 2001)
    - **Troubadour and trouvère poetry** (~11th–13th century): Provençal/Occitan love lyric; courtly love (fin'amors); William IX of Aquitaine (earliest known troubadour, ~1071–1126); Bernart de Ventadorn; Countess of Dia (trobairitz — female troubadour); influenced all European lyric poetry; trouvères (Northern French): Chrétien de Troyes (Lancelot, Yvain, Perceval — Arthurian romance, ~1170–1190); Minnesang (German: Walther von der Vogelweide)
    - **Dante Alighieri (1265–1321)**: Divina Commedia (Divine Comedy, ~1308–1321): Inferno, Purgatorio, Paradiso; 14,233 lines in terza rima; allegorical journey through afterlife guided by Virgil then Beatrice; Italian vernacular as literary language (De Vulgari Eloquentia argued for vernacular); "the chief imagination of Christendom" — Eliot; Vita Nuova (love for Beatrice); foundational for Italian language
    - **Geoffrey Chaucer (~1343–1400)**: Canterbury Tales (~1387–1400; unfinished; 24 tales, plan for 120; frame narrative of pilgrimage; social panorama — Knight, Miller, Wife of Bath, Pardoner; Middle English vernacular poetry; "the father of English literature"; rhyming couplets (heroic couplet); Troilus and Criseyde)
    - **Giovanni Boccaccio (1313–1375)**: Decameron (1353): 100 tales told by 10 young people fleeing Black Death; earthly, comic, erotic, tragic; frame narrative; prose masterwork; influenced Chaucer; Margaret of Navarre (Heptameron, 1558, French counterpart)
    - **Romance**: genre of medieval narrative (chivalry, adventure, love, quest); Arthurian romance (Chrétien de Troyes; Wolfram von Eschenbach, Parzival; Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, ~14th century — alliterative, Anonymous, Pearl-poet; Malory, Le Morte d'Arthur, 1485); Roman de la Rose (Guillaume de Lorris + Jean de Meun, ~1230–1275; allegorical, encyclopedic)
    - **Medieval drama**: mystery/miracle plays (York cycle, Chester cycle, Wakefield Master — Second Shepherds' Play); morality plays (Everyman, ~1485); liturgical drama origins

- **Renaissance and early modern literature (~1400–1700)**
  - **Italian Renaissance**: Petrarch (1304–1374): Canzoniere (366 poems to Laura; Petrarchan sonnet form — octave + sestet; blazon tradition; influenced all European love poetry for centuries); Ludovico Ariosto, Orlando Furioso (1516/1532 — chivalric epic, 46 cantos; ironic, exuberant); Torquato Tasso, Gerusalemme Liberata (1581 — First Crusade epic; Counter-Reformation seriousness); Niccolò Machiavelli, The Prince and Mandragola (comedy); Baldassare Castiglione, The Book of the Courtier (1528 — ideal Renaissance gentleman; sprezzatura)
  - **French Renaissance**: François Rabelais, Gargantua and Pantagruel (1532–1564 — comic giants; grotesque body; Mikhail Bakhtin, Rabelais and His World, 1965 — "carnivalesque"); Michel de Montaigne, Essais (1580–1592 — invented the essay form; "Que sais-je?"; self-examination; skepticism; enormous influence: "It is myself that I portray"); Pléiade poets (Pierre de Ronsard, Joachim du Bellay — Du Bellay, La Deffence et Illustration de la Langue Françoyse, 1549)
  - **Spanish Golden Age (Siglo de Oro)**:
    - **Miguel de Cervantes (1547–1616)**: Don Quixote (Part I 1605, Part II 1615): "the first modern novel"; romance vs. reality; metafiction; encyclopedic; Don Quixote and Sancho Panza as archetypes; Harold Bloom: "contains within itself all the novels that have followed in its wake"; Novelas Ejemplares (1613)
    - Picaresque novel: Lazarillo de Tormes (1554, anonymous — foundational picaresque; rogue narrator); Mateo Alemán, Guzmán de Alfarache (1599)
    - Drama: Lope de Vega (~1,500 plays attributed; Arte Nuevo de Hacer Comedias, 1609); Tirso de Molina (El Burlador de Sevilla — first Don Juan); Pedro Calderón de la Barca, Life Is a Dream (La Vida es Sueño, 1635 — philosophical drama)
    - Poetry: Garcilaso de la Vega (Petrarchist, Renaissance poetry); Luis de Góngora (Culteranismo/Gongorism — elaborate, Latinate; Soledades); Francisco de Quevedo (Conceptismo — wit, conceit; satirist; Buscón)
  - **English Renaissance and early modern**
    - **William Shakespeare (1564–1616)**: ~37 plays, 154 sonnets, narrative poems; tragedies: Hamlet (~1600 — "To be or not to be"; consciousness, revenge, play-within-play; most performed play in the world), Othello, King Lear, Macbeth, Antony and Cleopatra; comedies: A Midsummer Night's Dream, Twelfth Night, As You Like It, The Merchant of Venice; histories: Henry IV Parts 1&2 (Falstaff), Henry V, Richard III; romances: The Tempest, The Winter's Tale; sonnets (addressed to "Fair Youth" and "Dark Lady"; Sonnet 18 "Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?"; Sonnet 130 anti-blazon); language: invented/popularized ~1,700 words; Shakespeare as secular scripture (Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human, 1998); First Folio (1623, compiled by Hemmings and Condell; 36 plays; ~750 copies printed, ~235 survive; most valuable printed book)
    - Christopher Marlowe (1564–1593 — Doctor Faustus, Tamburlaine; mighty line — blank verse pioneer; murdered, age 29); Ben Jonson (Volpone, The Alchemist; comedy of humours; Timber; critic and rival of Shakespeare)
    - **Elizabethan and Jacobean poetry**: Edmund Spenser, The Faerie Queene (1590/1596 — allegorical epic; Spenserian stanza; Queen Elizabeth as Gloriana); Philip Sidney, Astrophil and Stella (1591 — sonnet sequence; An Apology for Poetry/Defence of Poesie); John Donne (1572–1631 — Metaphysical poetry: "The Flea," "A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning," Holy Sonnets — "Death, be not proud"; conceits, wit, intellectual passion; T.S. Eliot rehabilitated Metaphysicals in "The Metaphysical Poets," 1921); George Herbert (The Temple); Andrew Marvell ("To His Coy Mistress"); Henry Vaughan
    - **John Milton (1608–1674)**: Paradise Lost (1667/1674 — blank verse epic; 12 books; Fall of Satan and Fall of Man; "Of Man's first disobedience, and the fruit"; Satan as compelling character — "Better to reign in Hell than serve in Heaven"; Romantic poets saw Satan as hero — Blake: "Milton was of the Devil's party without knowing it"; arguably the greatest English poem); Paradise Regained, Samson Agonistes; Areopagitica (1644 — argument for press freedom)
  - **17th-century French classicism**: Molière (Jean-Baptiste Poquelin, 1622–1673 — Tartuffe, Le Misanthrope, L'Avare, Le Bourgeois Gentilhomme; comedy as social criticism; died shortly after performing Le Malade Imaginaire); Jean Racine (1639–1699 — Phèdre, Andromaque, Bérénice; Alexandrine verse; psychological intensity; classical tragedy perfected); Pierre Corneille (Le Cid, 1637; heroic tragedy); La Fontaine (Fables); La Rochefoucauld (Maximes); Madame de Lafayette, La Princesse de Clèves (1678 — often called first French novel / first psychological novel)

- **Enlightenment and 18th-century literature**
  - **The rise of the novel**: Ian Watt, The Rise of the Novel (1957) — formal realism; individual experience; middle-class readership; printing press made books accessible
    - Daniel Defoe, Robinson Crusoe (1719 — castaway; economic individualism; colonialism); Samuel Richardson, Pamela (1740) and Clarissa (1748 — epistolary; sentiment; 1 million words — longest English novel of its era); Henry Fielding, Tom Jones (1749 — comic epic in prose; narrator's ironic commentary); Laurence Sterne, Tristram Shandy (1759–1767 — antinarrative; digression as method; proto-postmodern; "I will persist in recovering the lost thread"); Tobias Smollett; Fanny Burney, Evelina (1778 — woman's entry into society)
    - French: Voltaire, Candide (1759 — satirical conte; "we must cultivate our garden"; critique of Leibnizian optimism); Denis Diderot (Jacques le fataliste, Le Neveu de Rameau); Jean-Jacques Rousseau (Confessions, 1782/1789 — autobiographical frankness; Julie, ou la nouvelle Héloïse, 1761 — bestselling novel of the century); Marquis de Sade (120 Days of Sodom, Justine — transgressive; debated: pornography or philosophy?); Choderlos de Laclos, Les Liaisons dangereuses (1782)
    - German: Goethe, The Sorrows of Young Werther (1774 — Sturm und Drang; European sensation; suicide wave attributed to it "Werther effect")
  - **Augustan / neoclassical poetry**: Alexander Pope (1688–1744 — The Rape of the Lock, 1712/1714; An Essay on Criticism; The Dunciad; heroic couplets; "The proper study of mankind is man"; most quoted English poet after Shakespeare); Jonathan Swift, Gulliver's Travels (1726 — satire; four voyages; Houyhnhnms and Yahoos; "savage indignation"); Samuel Johnson (1709–1784 — Dictionary of the English Language, 1755; Lives of the Poets; Rasselas; Boswell's Life of Samuel Johnson, 1791; "the Great Cham of Literature")
  - **Gothic novel**: Horace Walpole, The Castle of Otranto (1764 — founded the genre); Ann Radcliffe, The Mysteries of Udolpho (1794); Matthew Lewis, The Monk (1796); Mary Shelley, Frankenstein (1818 — Gothic + Romantic + science fiction; written at 18; published at 20; "Modern Prometheus")

- **Romanticism (~1780–1850)**
  - **German Romanticism**: Goethe, Faust (Part I 1808, Part II 1832 — life's work; Faust's pact with Mephistopheles; striving; "Verweile doch, du bist so schön"; "the greatest work of German literature"; completes Sturm und Drang → Weimar Classicism → Romanticism); Friedrich Schiller (Die Räuber; "Ode to Joy"; Wallenstein; aesthetic education); Novalis (Heinrich von Ofterdingen — "blue flower" as Romantic symbol); E.T.A. Hoffmann (The Sandman, The Golden Pot — uncanny/fantastic); Brothers Grimm (Kinder- und Hausmärchen, 1812 — fairy tales collected, edited, influential)
  - **English Romanticism**
    - First generation: William Wordsworth and Samuel Taylor Coleridge, Lyrical Ballads (1798 — revolutionary; Wordsworth's Preface 1800 = Romantic manifesto: "poetry is the spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings"; common language; nature; imagination; "Tintern Abbey"); Coleridge ("The Rime of the Ancient Mariner"; "Kubla Khan"; Biographia Literaria, 1817 — imagination as esemplastic power; organic form; foundational criticism); William Blake (Songs of Innocence and Experience, 1789/1794; prophetic books; "Tyger Tyger"; visionary; engraved his own books)
    - Second generation: George Gordon, Lord Byron, (1788–1824 — Don Juan, 1819–1824, unfinished — comic epic; Childe Harold; Byronic hero; scandalous; died in Greek War of Independence); Percy Bysshe Shelley (1792–1822 — "Ozymandias," "Ode to the West Wind," Prometheus Unbound; "Defence of Poetry" — "poets are the unacknowledged legislators of the world"); John Keats (1795–1821 — "Ode to a Nightingale," "Ode on a Grecian Urn," "To Autumn"; "negative capability" — "capable of being in uncertainties, mysteries, doubts"; died at 25 of tuberculosis)
    - Prose: Jane Austen (1775–1817 — Pride and Prejudice, 1813; Sense and Sensibility; Emma; Persuasion; irony; free indirect discourse; marriage plot as vehicle for social analysis; "the most perfect artist among women" — Virginia Woolf; endlessly adapted; D.A. Miller, Jane Austen, or the Secret of Style, 2003); Mary Shelley, Frankenstein (see Gothic); Walter Scott (Waverley novels — historical novel genre established)
  - **French Romanticism**: Victor Hugo (1802–1885 — Les Misérables, 1862; Notre-Dame de Paris, 1831; Hernani, 1830 — "battle of Hernani" against classicists; poetry: Les Contemplations; exile; "the most powerful French literary mind of the 19th century"); Alexandre Dumas (The Three Musketeers, The Count of Monte Cristo); Stendhal (Henri Beyle — The Red and the Black, 1830; The Charterhouse of Parma, 1839; realism emerging from Romanticism); Alfred de Musset; Gérard de Nerval
  - **American Romanticism / Transcendentalism**: Ralph Waldo Emerson ("Nature," 1836; "Self-Reliance"; transcendentalist philosophy); Henry David Thoreau, Walden (1854 — "I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately"); Walt Whitman (1819–1892 — Leaves of Grass, 1855, continuously revised; "Song of Myself"; free verse; democratic vision; the body; "I contain multitudes"; "the good gray poet"); Edgar Allan Poe (1809–1849 — "The Raven," "The Tell-Tale Heart," "The Fall of the House of Usher"; detective fiction pioneer: "The Murders in the Rue Morgue," 1841; "The Philosophy of Composition," 1846; Gothic American); Emily Dickinson (1830–1886 — ~1,800 poems, mostly unpublished in lifetime; slant rhyme, dashes, compression; "Because I could not stop for Death"; "I'm Nobody! Who are you?"; reclusiveness; radical posthumous editorial history — Thomas H. Johnson, 1955; Franklin variorum, 1998); Nathaniel Hawthorne (The Scarlet Letter, 1850); Herman Melville (Moby-Dick, 1851 — "Call me Ishmael"; whaling encyclopedia + metaphysical quest; commercial failure, posthumous rediscovery ~1920s; F.O. Matthiessen, American Renaissance, 1941)

- **Realism and Naturalism (~1850–1900)**
  - **French Realism**: Gustave Flaubert, Madame Bovary (1857 — prosecuted for immorality; le mot juste; free indirect discourse mastered; "Madame Bovary, c'est moi"; Nabokov: "the best novel ever written"); Honoré de Balzac, La Comédie Humaine (~90 novels/stories, 1829–1850 — panoramic social anatomy of France; Père Goriot, Illusions Perdues; Marx admired as sociological observer; 2,000+ recurring characters)
  - **French Naturalism**: Émile Zola (1840–1902 — Les Rougon-Macquart, 20-novel cycle: Germinal, 1885 — miners; Nana; L'Assommoir; scientific/experimental method applied to fiction — Le Roman expérimental, 1880; Dreyfus affair — "J'accuse," L'Aurore, 13 January 1898); Guy de Maupassant (short stories — "The Necklace," "Boule de Suif")
  - **Russian literature's golden age**
    - Alexander Pushkin (1799–1837 — Eugene Onegin, 1825–1832: novel in verse; "Russian Shakespeare"; founded modern Russian literary language); Mikhail Lermontov (A Hero of Our Time, 1840)
    - **Nikolai Gogol** (1809–1852 — Dead Souls, 1842; "The Overcoat"; "The Nose"; satirical, absurdist; "We all came out from under Gogol's overcoat" — attributed to Dostoevsky)
    - **Ivan Turgenev** (Fathers and Sons, 1862 — generational conflict; nihilism)
    - **Fyodor Dostoevsky (1821–1881)**: Crime and Punishment (1866 — Raskolnikov; freedom, guilt, redemption); The Brothers Karamazov (1880 — "Grand Inquisitor" chapter; theodicy; Alyosha, Ivan, Dmitri; "the supreme achievement of world literature" — Freud); Notes from Underground (1864 — anti-rationalist; "I am a sick man…"; existential precursor); The Idiot; Demons; polyphonic novel (Mikhail Bakhtin, Problems of Dostoevsky's Poetics, 1929/1963 — multiple voices, none authoritative; dialogism)
    - **Leo Tolstoy (1828–1910)**: War and Peace (1865–1869 — Napoleonic Wars panorama; Pierre, Andrei, Natasha; philosophy of history; ~580,000 words; "the greatest novel ever written" — many); Anna Karenina (1877 — "Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way"; adultery, society, land, faith); late works: The Death of Ivan Ilyich (1886 — novella; mortality); moral/religious crisis; Tolstoy vs. Dostoevsky — central comparison in Russian literary culture
    - **Anton Chekhov (1860–1904)**: short stories ("The Lady with the Dog," "Ward No. 6," "The Steppe"); plays: The Seagull (1896), Uncle Vanya (1899), Three Sisters (1901), The Cherry Orchard (1904); subdued drama; subtext; "If in the first act you have hung a pistol on the wall…" (Chekhov's gun); Stanislavski's Moscow Art Theatre
  - **English Victorian literature**: Charles Dickens (1812–1870 — Great Expectations, Bleak House, David Copperfield, Oliver Twist, A Tale of Two Cities; serial publication; social criticism; memorable characters; "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times"); George Eliot (Mary Ann Evans, 1819–1880 — Middlemarch, 1871–1872: "the finest English novel" — Virginia Woolf; moral seriousness; psychological realism; Daniel Deronda); Charlotte Brontë, Jane Eyre (1847 — "Reader, I married him"); Emily Brontë, Wuthering Heights (1847 — Heathcliff and Catherine; Gothic Romantic); Thomas Hardy (Tess of the d'Urbervilles, 1891; Jude the Obscure, 1895; fate, nature, society); Robert Louis Stevenson; Oscar Wilde (The Picture of Dorian Gray; The Importance of Being Earnest; wit; aestheticism; imprisoned 1895)
  - **American literature**: Mark Twain (Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, 1884 — Hemingway: "All modern American literature comes from one book by Mark Twain"; vernacular language; Mississippi; race); Henry James (1843–1916 — The Portrait of a Lady, 1881; The Turn of the Screw; The Ambassadors; international theme; psychological intricacy; late style); Stephen Crane (The Red Badge of Courage); Kate Chopin (The Awakening, 1899 — feminist, suppressed); Edith Wharton
  - **Scandinavian literature**: Henrik Ibsen (1828–1906 — A Doll's House, 1879; Hedda Gabler; Ghosts; The Wild Duck; "father of modern drama"; realist social drama; prose drama replacing verse; influenced Shaw, O'Neill, Miller); August Strindberg (Miss Julie, 1888; The Father; Dream Play; expressionistic; bitter rivalry with Ibsen); Knut Hamsun (Hunger, 1890 — stream-of-consciousness precursor; Nobel 1920; later Nazi collaboration)

- **Modernism (~1890–1945)**
  - **Defining features**: formal experimentation; fragmentation; stream of consciousness; unreliable narration; mythical method; subjectivity; rupture with tradition; "Make it new" (Ezra Pound); reaction against Realist conventions; influenced by WWI, Freud, Marx, Nietzsche, Einstein
  - **Poetry**
    - **W.B. Yeats (1865–1939)**: "The Second Coming" ("Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold"); "Easter 1916"; "Sailing to Byzantium"; "Among School Children"; Irish mythology; symbolism; occultism; Nobel 1923; greatest 20th-century poet in English (or one of two — with Eliot)
    - **T.S. Eliot (1888–1965)**: "The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock" (1915 — "Do I dare to eat a peach?"); The Waste Land (1922 — modernist masterpiece; "April is the cruellest month"; Ezra Pound edited; fragmented, allusive, polyphonic); Four Quartets (1935–1942 — metaphysical, time, stillness; "In my beginning is my end"); literary criticism: "Tradition and the Individual Talent" (1919 — impersonal theory of poetry; "objective correlative"); Nobel 1948
    - Ezra Pound (1885–1972 — Imagism: "In a Station of the Metro"; Cantos (1917–1969, unfinished, ~800 pages — encyclopedic; Chinese history, economics, Provençal poetry; fascist sympathies; imprisoned by US military; St. Elizabeths; Bollingen Prize controversy 1949)
    - Wallace Stevens ("The Emperor of Ice-Cream," "Sunday Morning," "Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird"; imagination and reality); William Carlos Williams ("The Red Wheelbarrow"; "no ideas but in things"; Paterson; local/American vs. Pound/Eliot's cosmopolitan); Rainer Maria Rilke (Duino Elegies, 1923; Sonnets to Orpheus; Letters to a Young Poet; German-language modernism); Federico García Lorca (Romancero gitano; Poeta en Nueva York; duende; assassinated by Francoists, 1936); Paul Valéry; Giuseppe Ungaretti; Fernando Pessoa (heteronyms — Álvaro de Campos, Alberto Caeiro, Ricardo Reis; Book of Disquiet; Portuguese modernism)
  - **Prose fiction**
    - **Marcel Proust (1871–1922)**: À la recherche du temps perdu (In Search of Lost Time, 7 vols., 1913–1927): ~3,000 pages; madeleine dipped in tea; involuntary memory; time, art, love, jealousy, homosexuality, society; "the definitive modern novel" — many critics; revolutionized the novel's treatment of consciousness and time
    - **James Joyce (1882–1941)**: Dubliners (1914 — short stories; "epiphany"; "The Dead"); A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man (1916 — Bildungsroman; Stephen Dedalus); Ulysses (1922 — 18 episodes corresponding to Homer's Odyssey; one day in Dublin, 16 June 1904 — "Bloomsday"; Leopold Bloom, Stephen Dedalus, Molly Bloom; stream of consciousness; parody of literary styles; encyclopedic; "the most important novel of the 20th century" — many; banned in US/UK for obscenity until 1930s); Finnegans Wake (1939 — portmanteau language; circular structure; "riverrun, past Eve and Adam's"; resists conventional reading)
    - **Virginia Woolf (1882–1941)**: Mrs. Dalloway (1925 — one day in London; Clarissa Dalloway and Septimus Warren Smith; interiority, time); To the Lighthouse (1927 — Ramsay family; "Time Passes"; lighthouse as symbol); Orlando (1928 — gender fluidity; biographical fantasy); The Waves (1931); A Room of One's Own (1929 — feminist criticism; "a woman must have money and a room of her own if she is to write fiction"); Bloomsbury Group
    - **Franz Kafka (1883–1924)**: The Metamorphosis (1915 — "As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams, he found himself transformed into a monstrous vermin"; alienation, bureaucracy, absurdity); The Trial (1925, posthumous — Josef K.; unpublished by design — friend Max Brod disobeyed wish to burn manuscripts); The Castle (unfinished); "Kafkaesque" as adjective
    - **Thomas Mann (1875–1955)**: Buddenbrooks (1901 — family decline); The Magic Mountain (1924 — European civilization in a sanatorium); Doctor Faustus (1947); Nobel 1929
    - William Faulkner (1897–1962 — The Sound and the Fury, 1929; As I Lay Dying; Absalom, Absalom!; Yoknapatawpha County; Southern Gothic; multiple narrators; stream of consciousness; Nobel 1949); Ernest Hemingway (1899–1961 — The Sun Also Rises; A Farewell to Arms; The Old Man and the Sea; iceberg theory — omission; short declarative sentences; Nobel 1954; "Paris is a moveable feast"); F. Scott Fitzgerald (The Great Gatsby, 1925 — American Dream; green light; Jazz Age; "So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past")
  - **Drama**: Luigi Pirandello (Six Characters in Search of an Author, 1921 — metatheatre); Bertolt Brecht (1898–1956 — Mother Courage; Threepenny Opera; Verfremdungseffekt — alienation/estrangement effect; epic theatre; anti-Aristotelian; "the purpose of art is not to hold a mirror to nature but to hammer it into shape"); Eugene O'Neill (Long Day's Journey into Night; Nobel 1936 — first American dramatist); Samuel Beckett (see post-WWII)
  - **Harlem Renaissance (~1920s–1930s)**: Langston Hughes ("The Negro Speaks of Rivers"; "I, Too, Sing America"; jazz poetry); Zora Neale Hurston, Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937 — Janie Crawford; Black vernacular; rediscovered by Alice Walker); Claude McKay; Jean Toomer, Cane (1923); Countee Cullen; Alain Locke, The New Negro (1925 — manifesto)

- **Post-WWII and contemporary literature (~1945–present)**
  - **Existentialist literature**: Albert Camus, The Stranger (1942 — "Mother died today. Or maybe yesterday, I don't know"; Meursault; absurdity); The Plague (1947); The Myth of Sisyphus; Nobel 1957; Jean-Paul Sartre, Nausea (1938); No Exit (1944 — "Hell is other people"); declined Nobel 1964; Simone de Beauvoir, The Second Sex (1949 — foundational feminist philosophy; "One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman"); The Mandarins
  - **Theatre of the Absurd**: Samuel Beckett, Waiting for Godot (1953 — "Nothing happens, nobody comes, nobody goes, it's awful"; Vladimir and Estragon; Nobel 1969); Endgame; Eugène Ionesco (The Bald Soprano; Rhinoceros); Harold Pinter (The Birthday Party; The Homecoming; "Pinteresque" — pauses, menace; Nobel 2005); Edward Albee (Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?, 1962)
  - **American post-war fiction**: J.D. Salinger, The Catcher in the Rye (1951 — Holden Caulfield; adolescent alienation); Saul Bellow, The Adventures of Augie March; Herzog; Nobel 1976; Ralph Ellison, Invisible Man (1952 — "I am an invisible man"; race, identity); James Baldwin (Go Tell It on the Mountain; "Notes of a Native Son"; Giovanni's Room; The Fire Next Time); Flannery O'Connor, (A Good Man Is Hard to Find — Southern Gothic; grace and violence); Jack Kerouac, On the Road (1957 — Beat Generation; "the only people for me are the mad ones"); Allen Ginsberg, "Howl" (1956 — "I saw the best minds of my generation destroyed by madness"; obscenity trial); Vladimir Nabokov, Lolita (1955 — "Lolita, light of my life, fire of my loins"; unreliable narrator par excellence; linguistic brilliance; published in Paris then US)
  - **Postmodernism**: Thomas Pynchon (Gravity's Rainbow, 1973 — encyclopedic; paranoia; entropy; V.; The Crying of Lot 49); Don DeLillo (White Noise, 1985; Underworld, 1997); John Barth ("The Literature of Exhaustion," 1967); Robert Coover; Italo Calvino (If on a winter's night a traveler, 1979 — reader as character; metafiction; Invisible Cities); Jorge Luis Borges (1899–1986 — "The Garden of Forking Paths," "The Library of Babel," "Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius," "Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote"; ficciones; labyrinths; philosophical fictions; arguably most influential short story writer of 20th century; precursor of postmodernism); Umberto Eco, The Name of the Rose (1980 — medieval detective fiction + semiotics)
  - **Latin American Boom and Magical Realism**: **Gabriel García Márquez (1927–2014)** — One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967 — Macondo; Buendía family; magical realism — "the marvelous real"; Latin American history as mythical cycle; Nobel 1982); Julio Cortázar (Hopscotch, 1963 — read in any order); Carlos Fuentes (The Death of Artemio Cruz); Mario Vargas Llosa (The War of the End of the World; Nobel 2010); Isabel Allende (The House of the Spirits); Juan Rulfo (Pedro Páramo, 1955 — "a murmur of souls"; precursor; García Márquez: "I could recite the whole of Pedro Páramo by heart")
  - **African literature**: Chinua Achebe (1930–2013 — Things Fall Apart, 1958: most widely read African novel; response to colonial narratives; Igbo society; critique of Conrad's Heart of Darkness); Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o (Decolonising the Mind, 1986 — argued for writing in African languages; Gĩkũyũ novels); Wole Soyinka (Death and the King's Horseman; Nobel 1986 — first African Nobel laureate in literature); Bessie Head; Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie (Half of a Yellow Sun; Americanah; "The Danger of a Single Story," TED talk); NoViolet Bulawayo
  - **South Asian literature**: Rabindranath Tagore (1861–1941 — Gitanjali, 1912; Nobel 1913 — first non-European Nobel in literature; Bengali literature; songs, poems, novels, plays; composed national anthems of India and Bangladesh); Salman Rushdie (Midnight's Children, 1981 — Booker Prize; partition; magical realism; The Satanic Verses, 1988 — fatwa); R.K. Narayan (Malgudi); Arundhati Roy, The God of Small Things (1997 — Booker Prize)
  - **East Asian literature**: Lu Xun (1881–1936 — "Diary of a Madman," 1918; "The True Story of Ah Q"; foundational modern Chinese fiction); Yasunari Kawabata (Snow Country; Nobel 1968); Yukio Mishima (The Temple of the Golden Pavilion; ritual suicide 1970); Kenzaburo Oe (A Personal Matter; Nobel 1994); Haruki Murakami (Norwegian Wood; Kafka on the Shore; 1Q84; global readership); Mo Yan (Red Sorghum; Nobel 2012); Han Kang (The Vegetarian; Human Acts; Nobel 2024)
  - **Caribbean literature**: Derek Walcott, Omeros (1990 — Homeric epic of Caribbean; Nobel 1992); V.S. Naipaul (A House for Mr Biswas; Nobel 2001); Édouard Glissant (creolization theory; Poetics of Relation); Jean Rhys, Wide Sargasso Sea (1966 — postcolonial rewriting of Jane Eyre); Jamaica Kincaid
  - **Australian, Canadian, New Zealand literature**: Patrick White (Voss; Nobel 1973); Alice Munro (short stories — Nobel 2013; "our Chekhov"); Margaret Atwood (The Handmaid's Tale, 1985; The Blind Assassin; speculative fiction); Michael Ondaatje (The English Patient); Janet Frame; Keri Hulme (The Bone People, 1984 — Booker Prize)
  - **Contemporary trends (2000–present)**: autofiction (Karl Ove Knausgård, My Struggle, 6 vols.; Ben Lerner; Rachel Cusk); climate fiction / "cli-fi" (Richard Powers, The Overstory, 2018, Pulitzer; Amitav Ghosh, The Great Derangement, 2016); digital literature (hypertext fiction, electronic literature, Twitter fiction); genre fiction's literary ascendance (Cormac McCarthy, Blood Meridian; The Road; Kazuo Ishiguro — Nobel 2017 — Never Let Me Go; Colson Whitehead, The Underground Railroad, 2016, Pulitzer); diverse voices amplified (Abdulrazak Gurnah — Nobel 2021; Jon Fosse — Nobel 2023; translated literature (Three-Body Problem — Liu Cixin, Hugo 2015); AI-generated text debates; book-to-screen adaptation economy (streaming era); global Anglophone literature

- **Literary theory and criticism**
