  - **Classical**: Aristotle's Poetics (see above); Horace, Ars Poetica (~19 BCE — "dulce et utile"; teach and delight); Longinus, On the Sublime (~1st century CE — elevation, transport, ecstasy in literature; "sublimity is the echo of a great mind")
  - **Renaissance–18th century**: Sidney, Defence of Poesie (1595); Boileau, L'Art Poétique (1674); Samuel Johnson (Preface to Shakespeare; Lives of the Poets); neoclassical rules (unities of time, place, action — derived from Aristotle via Italian and French codification)
  - **Romantic criticism**: Wordsworth, Preface to Lyrical Ballads; Coleridge, Biographia Literaria (imagination, organic form); Shelley, "Defence of Poetry"; Keats's letters (negative capability); Friedrich Schlegel (irony, fragment, progressive universal poetry)
  - **Victorian / 19th century**: Matthew Arnold, "The Function of Criticism at the Present Time" (1864; "the best that has been thought and said"); Hippolyte Taine (race, milieu, moment); Sainte-Beuve (biographical criticism)
  - **Russian Formalism (~1915–1930)**: Viktor Shklovsky, "Art as Technique" (1917 — ostranenie/defamiliarization: "art exists to make one feel things, to make the stone stony"); Boris Eichenbaum; Roman Jakobson (linguistics + poetics; "the poetic function"); Vladimir Propp, Morphology of the Folktale (1928 — 31 narrative functions; narrative grammar); Mikhail Bakhtin (dialogism, polyphony, chronotope, carnival, heteroglossia — The Dialogic Imagination, 1981; Problems of Dostoevsky's Poetics)
  - **New Criticism (~1930s–1960s, Anglo-American)**: close reading of text; autonomy of the literary work; I.A. Richards, Practical Criticism (1929 — reader responses to poems stripped of author/title); Cleanth Brooks, The Well Wrought Urn (1947 — "the heresy of paraphrase"; paradox, irony, ambiguity); William Empson, Seven Types of Ambiguity (1930); W.K. Wimsatt and Monroe Beardsley: "The Intentional Fallacy" (1946 — author's intention irrelevant) and "The Affective Fallacy" (1949 — reader's emotional response irrelevant); John Crowe Ransom coined "New Criticism" (1941)
  - **Structuralism and semiotics**: Ferdinand de Saussure (langue/parole — applied to literature); Claude Lévi-Strauss (mytheme analysis); Roland Barthes: early structuralist (S/Z, 1970 — five codes; reading Balzac); "The Death of the Author" (1967 — "the birth of the reader must be at the cost of the Death of the Author"); Gérard Genette, Narrative Discourse (1972/1980 — narratological categories: order, duration, frequency, mood, voice; foundational); Tzvetan Todorov (coined "narratology"; fantastic literature theory); A.J. Greimas (actantial model); Jonathan Culler, Structuralist Poetics (1975)
  - **Post-structuralism and deconstruction**: **Jacques Derrida (1930–2004)**: Of Grammatology (1967) — deconstruction; différance; "there is nothing outside the text" (il n'y a pas de hors-texte — often misunderstood); logocentrism; binary opposition undermined; Paul de Man (Blindness and Insight; Allegories of Reading; Yale School; posthumous collaboration controversy); J. Hillis Miller; Geoffrey Hartman; Barbara Johnson, The Critical Difference (1980)
  - **Reader-response theory**: Wolfgang Iser, The Act of Reading (1976) — implied reader; gaps and indeterminacies; Hans Robert Jauss, "horizon of expectations" (reception aesthetics, Constance School); Stanley Fish, Is There a Text in This Class? (1980) — interpretive communities determine meaning; "the reader makes the text"
  - **Psychoanalytic criticism**: Freud himself on literature (Oedipus; "The Uncanny," 1919; Leonardo; Hamlet); Jacques Lacan (mirror stage applied to literature; Seminar on "The Purloined Letter" — Poe; signifier/signified; the Real, Symbolic, Imaginary); Julia Kristeva (abjection; intertextuality coined — from Bakhtin; Revolution in Poetic Language, 1974); Slavoj Žižek (Lacanian culture critique); Harold Bloom, The Anxiety of Influence (1973 — poets wrestle with strong precursors; six "revisionary ratios": clinamen, tessera, kenosis, daemonization, askesis, apophrades)
  - **Marxist / materialist criticism**: Georg Lukács (The Historical Novel, 1937; realism as progressive; Balzac, Tolstoy); Frankfurt School: Theodor Adorno (Aesthetic Theory; "to write poetry after Auschwitz is barbaric"); Walter Benjamin ("The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction," 1935 — aura, reproducibility; "Theses on the Philosophy of History"); Raymond Williams, Culture and Society (1958); The Long Revolution (1961); Keywords (1976); Terry Eagleton, Literary Theory: An Introduction (1983 — most widely read introduction to theory); Fredric Jameson, Postmodernism, or, The Cultural Logic of Late Capitalism (1991); Pierre Macherey, A Theory of Literary Production (1966)
  - **Feminist literary criticism**: Virginia Woolf, A Room of One's Own (precursor); Simone de Beauvoir (precursor); Kate Millett, Sexual Politics (1970); Elaine Showalter, A Literature of Their Own (1977 — three phases: feminine, feminist, female; "gynocriticism"); Sandra Gilbert and Susan Gubar, The Madwoman in the Attic (1979 — 19th-century women writers; Bertha Mason); Hélène Cixous, "The Laugh of the Medusa" (1975 — écriture féminine); Luce Irigaray; Gayatri Spivak; Judith Butler, Gender Trouble (1990 — performativity; "there is no gender identity behind the expressions of gender"); bell hooks, Feminist Theory: From Margin to Center (1984)
  - **Postcolonial criticism**: Edward Said, Orientalism (1978) — foundational; Gayatri Spivak, "Can the Subaltern Speak?" (1988); Homi Bhabha, The Location of Culture (1994 — hybridity, mimicry, third space); Frantz Fanon (Black Skin, White Masks; The Wretched of the Earth); Chinua Achebe, "An Image of Africa: Racism in Conrad's Heart of Darkness" (1977); postcolonial rewriting/revision (Jean Rhys, Coetzee, Rushdie)
  - **New Historicism and Cultural Materialism**: Stephen Greenblatt, Renaissance Self-Fashioning (1980); "the circulation of social energy"; literature in/as history; anecdote as method; Louis Montrose ("the textuality of history, the historicity of texts"); Cultural Materialism (British counterpart): Jonathan Dollimore and Alan Sinfield, Political Shakespeare (1985); Raymond Williams's influence
  - **Ecocriticism**: Cheryll Glotfelty and Harold Fromm, The Ecocriticism Reader (1996); Lawrence Buell, The Environmental Imagination (1995); Timothy Morton (ecology without nature; "hyperobjects"); Rob Nixon, Slow Violence and the Environmentalism of the Poor (2011); posthumanism and literature
  - **Digital humanities and computational literary studies**: Franco Moretti, "Conjectures on World Literature" (2000); Distant Reading (2013 — graphs, maps, trees; computational analysis of 1,000+ novels); Matthew Jockers, Macroanalysis (2013); Ted Underwood, Distant Horizons (2019); Stanford Literary Lab pamphlets; topic modelling of literary corpora; stylometry; network analysis of character interactions

- **Rhetoric and poetics**
  - Classical rhetoric: Aristotle, Rhetoric (three modes of persuasion: ethos, pathos, logos); Cicero, De Oratore (ideal orator); Quintilian, Institutio Oratoria (education of the orator; "the good man speaking well"); the five canons: inventio, dispositio, elocutio, memoria, actio
  - Figures and tropes: metaphor, simile, metonymy, synecdoche, irony, hyperbole, litotes, anaphora, chiasmus, antithesis, paradox, oxymoron, personification, apostrophe; Aristotle: "the greatest thing by far is to be a master of metaphor" (Poetics); George Lakoff and Mark Turner, More Than Cool Reason (1989)
  - Modern rhetoric: Kenneth Burke, A Rhetoric of Motives (1950 — identification as key; dramatism; pentad); Chaïm Perelman and Lucie Olbrechts-Tyteca, The New Rhetoric (1958/1969); Wayne Booth, The Rhetoric of Fiction (1961 — reliable/unreliable narrator; implied author); rhetorical genre studies (Carolyn Miller, "Genre as Social Action," 1984)
  - Poetics: prosody (meter — iambic pentameter, hexameter, alexandrine; foot: iamb, trochee, dactyl, anapest, spondee; scansion; free verse — from Whitman onward; vers libre); rhyme (end, internal, slant, eye); stanza forms (couplet, tercet, quatrain, sestet, octave); fixed forms (sonnet — Petrarchan/Shakespearean/Spenserian; villanelle, sestina, ghazal, haiku, pantoum, rondeau); narratology (Genette, Prince, Bal); Derek Attridge, Poetic Rhythm (1995); Timothy Steele, All the Fun's in How You Say a Thing (1999)

- **Comparative and world literature**
  - Comparative literature: Goethe coined "Weltliteratur" (world literature, 1827 — "national literature is now a rather unmeaning term; the epoch of world literature is at hand"); discipline institutionalized in 19th century (Hugo Meltzl de Lomnitz, first journal 1877); René Wellek and Austin Warren, Theory of Literature (1949 — foundational textbook); comparative method: cross-cultural, cross-linguistic, inter-art, inter-disciplinary; structuralism and narratology deeply comparative
  - Franco Moretti, "Conjectures on World Literature" (New Left Review, 2000) — distant reading; world-systems approach to literary history; "trees" of genre diffusion; Pascale Casanova, The World Republic of Letters (1999 — literary world-system; Paris as center; unequal exchange of literary capital); David Damrosch, What Is World Literature? (2003 — "a mode of circulation and reading"); Emily Apter, Against World Literature (2013 — untranslatability; critique of flattening)
  - Translation studies: Eugene Nida (formal vs. dynamic equivalence); Lawrence Venuti, The Translator's Invisibility (1995 — foreignizing vs. domesticating); "world literature is writing that gains in translation" (Damrosch); "Three Percent" problem (only ~3% of books published in US are translations); International Booker Prize effect
  - Frontier: digital world literature (computational comparison of literary traditions); literature and AI (creative writing by LLMs; authorship debate; genre detection across languages); climate literature as global genre; planetary literature beyond national paradigms
